activities.text: Hike around Tre Cime from Rifugio Auronzo – 9.5-kilometer loop with 350 meters of elevation gain, passing Rifugio Locatelli (2,405m) with views of the north faces of the three towers.

practical_info.conseil: Toll road closed to cars after 9am in July-August, shuttle from Lago di Misurina

activities.text: Walk around Lago di Braies – marked 3.5-kilometer trail around the lake with wooden rowboat rentals from the hotel on the south shore.

practical_info.conseil: Arrive before 8am or after 5pm to avoid summer crowds

seo.h1_title: Dolomites Loop: Alpine Lakes, Mountain Pastures & Limestone Peaks
seo.intro_paragraph: This loop begins in Merano, the Austro-Hungarian spa town where Sissi stayed in 1870, before climbing toward Val Gardena and the Alpe di Siusi—56 square kilometers of car-free alpine pastures. The passes of the Great Dolomites Road (1909) connect Ortisei to Cortina d'Ampezzo, Olympic capital in 1956 and 2026. The final days lead to Tre Cime di Lavaredo, whose 500-meter north face was first climbed in 1933, and Lago di Braies, where 139 hostages were liberated in May 1945.
seo.seo_hook: Sissi's spa retreat, Europe's largest alpine plateau, Great War trenches at 2,300 meters, turquoise glacial lakes: the Dolomites where history meets high mountains.
featured_list: Merano: Empress Sissi's spa town with 12-hectare botanical gardens
Alpe di Siusi: Europe's largest alpine plateau spanning 56 km² at 1,680-2,350m
Seceda: ridge at 2,518m with sweeping views of the Odle peaks and Val Gardena
Cinque Torri: open-air museum in 1915-1918 trenches at 2,300m altitude
Tre Cime di Lavaredo: three iconic dolomite towers with 500m north face first climbed in 1933

places_taglines.IT::merano: Where Empress Sissi took her cure among palms and thermal gardens
places_taglines.IT::ortisei: Val Gardena's woodcarving capital beneath the pink dolomite spires
places_taglines.IT::cortina-dampezzo: Twice-Olympic alpine resort cradled by 3,000-meter limestone walls
places_taglines.IT::dobbiaco: Gateway to the legendary Tre Cime and mirror-still Lago di Braies

highlights: Alpe di Siusi: mandatory shuttle access in July-August, cars prohibited on the plateau
Tre Cime di Lavaredo: toll road closed to cars after 9am in season, shuttle from Lago di Misurina
Lago di Braies: arrive before 8am or after 5pm to avoid summer crowds
Seceda: last cable car around 5:30pm, verify seasonal schedules
Great Dolomites Road: certain passes (Sella, Pordoi) closed November to May depending on snow

itineraries.title: From the Po Delta to the Romagna Riviera: Ferrara, Ravenna and Rimini
itineraries.subtitle: A 6-day road trip along Emilia-Romagna's eastern coast, through Renaissance palaces, flamingo-filled lagoons, Byzantine mosaics and Roman ruins.
itineraries.notes: Access to Comacchio's salt pans and the Po Delta nesting areas requires a guide. Entry to San Marino is free, but parking is paid and often full in summer—park in Borgo Maggiore and take the cable car. A vehicle is essential for the entire route.

visits.text: Ferrara is one of the great cities of the Italian Renaissance, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1995. Start with the Castello Estense, the imposing red-brick castle that dominates the city centre. Built in 1385 by the Marquis Niccolò II d'Este following a violent popular uprising, it's the only castle in Italy still surrounded by water-filled moats. Inside, you'll discover the sumptuous ducal apartments with painted ceilings, the kitchens and—in a darker vein—the underground dungeons where the Este family imprisoned their enemies. Climb to the top of the Torre dei Leoni for exceptional panoramic views across the entire city.

A stone's throw from the castle, the cathedral of San Giorgio blends Romanesque and Gothic styles. Its marble façade, begun in the 12th century, is adorned with elaborate sculptures depicting biblical scenes, including a striking Last Judgement. Notice that the lower section is Romanesque, with rounded arches, while the upper levels are Gothic, with pointed arches. The stone lions guarding the main entrance are copies—the originals are preserved inside.

Don't miss the Palazzo Schifanoia, whose name literally means 'escape boredom'—this was the residence where the Este came to be entertained. Built in 1385 by Alberto V d'Este and then enlarged by Borso d'Este in the 1470s, it houses the Hall of the Months, a fresco cycle painted by the finest artists of the Ferrara school, including Francesco del Cossa. Each panel represents a month of the year with its zodiac sign, fieldwork and court entertainments. It's one of the most beautiful ensembles of secular painting from the Italian Renaissance.

Lose yourself in the Via delle Volte, one of the most atmospheric medieval streets in Italy. About 2 km long, it's punctuated by brick arches that connect buildings above your head—these passages once linked merchants' warehouses to their homes on the other side of the street. The atmosphere is unique, especially late in the day. If you have time, push on to the Palazzo dei Diamanti (8,500 stone points on the façade, designed by Biagio Rossetti in 1493)—it mainly hosts temporary exhibitions, so check the programme before going.

activities.text: Ferrara is the cycling city par excellence—they say every resident owns three bikes. Rent a bicycle to ride the 9 km of Renaissance ramparts that encircle the old town. This green circuit, almost entirely intact, offers changing views of interior gardens, tiled roofs and the surrounding countryside. It's the best way to understand why UNESCO described Ferrara as 'an outstanding example of a planned Renaissance city'.

practical_info.tip: Bike rentals are available near the station and in the historic centre

activities.text: Ferrara's cuisine has its own specialities, distinct from the rest of Emilia-Romagna. Try cappellacci di zucca—ravioli stuffed with pumpkin, a local classic—and salama da sugo, a long-simmered sausage served with mashed potato. Wash it down with a glass of Fortana, the local red wine from the delta. The trattorias in the medieval centre, around Via delle Volte, are your best bet.

practical_info.tip: The covered market near Via Garibaldi is ideal for discovering local products

visits.text: Comacchio will surprise you. Nicknamed 'Little Venice', this fishing town is built on 13 islands linked by canals and bridges, at the heart of the Po Delta. Start with the Ponte Trepponti, the city's emblem: this monumental bridge with five staircases and two towers, built in the 17th century, is Comacchio's historic gateway. From there, let yourself drift along the canals lined with colourful houses—fishermen used to paint their façades with leftover boat paint. An hour is enough to tour the centre, but allow two or three to savour the atmosphere.

Push on to the Manifattura dei Marinati, the old eel-pickling factory turned eco-museum. Eel fishing has regulated life in Comacchio for centuries—a Capitulary from 715 already mentions the city's exclusive right to preserve and trade this fish. You'll discover ancestral fishing and pickling techniques, with the great fires where eels are smoked according to a method dating back to the 17th century. Comacchio's pickled eel is recognised by the Slow Food Presidium.

The Museo Delta Antico, housed in the Palazzo Bellini near the Ponte degli Sbirri, is well worth a visit. It contains the treasures of a Roman ship from the Augustan era, discovered in 1981 while digging a drainage canal, with its entire cargo intact: 102 lead ingots, amphorae, ceramics and everyday objects. It's a fascinating dive into the delta's ancient maritime trade.

Walking back along the canal, don't miss the Porticato dei Cappuccini, a covered promenade of 143 arches built in 1647 as an ex-voto. It leads to the church of Santa Maria in Aula Regia. The cathedral of San Cassiano, whose foundations date back to the 8th century but rebuilt in the 17th, and the Torre dell'Orologio complete the centre's architectural panorama.

activities.text: In the afternoon, set off to explore the Valli di Comacchio by boat. These brackish lagoons, the largest in Italy, stretch over about 13,000 hectares between Ferrara and the Adriatic Sea. Operators offer guided excursions that cross the canals, pass traditional fishermen's huts and lead to the 'lavorieri'—the eel trap systems still used today. You'll encounter grey herons, kingfishers, egrets and, with a bit of luck, the graceful silhouettes of the flamingos that have populated the lagoons since the 1990s.

practical_info.tip: Booking recommended. Bring binoculars and sunscreen—there's no shade on the lagoons

activities.text: If you prefer cycling, a signposted trail of about 20 km circles the lagoon. The route is flat and crosses breathtaking landscapes—water mirrors, reeds, fishermen's huts and flocks of birds. Halfway, a small café allows for a break and a taste of grilled eel, Comacchio's unmissable speciality.

practical_info.tip: Bike rental available at the Manifattura dei Marinati. Flat route, accessible to all

map_keywords: Basilica di Sant'Apollinare in Classe Ravenna

visits.text: Ravenna is the world capital of mosaics, and that's no exaggeration. Eight of its monuments are UNESCO World Heritage Sites—all built between the 5th and 6th centuries, when the city was successively capital of the Western Roman Empire, the Ostrogothic Kingdom and the Byzantine Exarchate. Begin with the Basilica of San Vitale, consecrated in 548 under Archbishop Maximian. Its octagonal architecture blends Roman and Byzantine traditions. The mosaics in the presbytery will leave you speechless: Emperor Justinian and Empress Theodora, haloed in gold, have been gazing at you for 1,500 years. Take time to sit and observe the details—the embroidery on the garments, the expressions on the faces, the changing reflections in the light.

Just behind San Vitale, the Mausoleum of Galla Placidia is one of Ravenna's most striking experiences. From the outside, this small cruciform brick building is nothing special—12 metres by 10. But push open the door and you enter a starry sky: the dome, covered in mosaics on a midnight-blue background with golden stars and a Latin cross, dates from the 5th century. These are Ravenna's oldest mosaics, stunningly well preserved. Galla Placidia, daughter of Emperor Theodosius I, had this mausoleum built, but her remains probably never rested here. Arrive early or late in the day—the space is small and queues can be long.

Continue to the Neonian Baptistery (or Baptistery of the Orthodox), the oldest monument still standing in Ravenna. Built in the 5th century, it preserves beneath its dome a mosaic depicting the baptism of Christ surrounded by the twelve apostles, on a golden background. The contrast between the austere brick exterior and the rich interior is characteristic of Ravenna.

The Basilica of Sant'Apollinare Nuovo, built by the Ostrogothic king Theodoric in the 6th century as a palatine chapel, features two long mosaic processions running along the nave: 26 martyrs on one side, 22 virgins on the other, advancing towards Christ and the Virgin. Byzantine influence mingles here with the Roman tradition in fascinating ways.

Make a detour to Dante's Tomb, a small neoclassical marble temple built in 1780 on Via Dante Alighieri. The father of the Italian language, exiled from Florence in 1302, spent his final years in Ravenna under the protection of the da Polenta family. It was here that he completed the Divine Comedy, and here that he died in 1321. Every 14 September, the anniversary of his death, the city of Florence offers the olive oil that fuels the tomb's votive lamp—a gesture of reparation for the exile they inflicted on him. Entry is free.

Late in the day, take the road (or bike—5 km on a pleasant cycle path) to the Basilica of Sant'Apollinare in Classe, Ravenna's eighth UNESCO monument. This imposing 6th-century edifice stands alone in the middle of the plain, with its characteristic cylindrical bell tower. The apse mosaic is of rare poetry: the Transfiguration of Christ is represented in a flowery meadow setting with sheep symbolising the apostles, on a golden background. Don't miss it—it's one of Ravenna's jewels, often overlooked because it's off-centre.

activities.text: Between visits, treat yourself to a break on Piazza del Popolo, Ravenna's living room. Lined with elegant buildings and its Venetian columns, it's the perfect spot for a coffee or aperitivo. The pedestrian streets around the square house interesting shops—notably contemporary mosaic workshops where craftspeople perpetuate the city's thousand-year tradition. Some offer a few hours' introduction to create your own mosaic.

practical_info.tip: Ravenna is very compact—everything is walkable from Piazza del Popolo

activities.text: Romagna cuisine in Ravenna deserves dedicated time. Try cappelletti in brodo (small stuffed pasta in broth), piadina romagnola (filled wheat flatbread), passatelli (breadcrumb and parmesan pasta in broth) and grilled fish from the Adriatic. The best tables are around Piazza del Popolo and in the adjacent pedestrian alleys. A glass of Sangiovese or Albana DOCG pairs perfectly.

practical_info.tip: Romagna trattorias often close between 2:30pm and 7pm—plan your meals accordingly

visits.text: Before heading to Cesenatico, make a stop in Cervia, 10 km north along the road. This small seaside town hides an unexpected treasure: its historic salt pans, worked without interruption since Etruscan times. Cervia's salt pans produce a renowned 'sweet' salt—less bitter than ordinary sea salt—thanks to a traditional hand-harvesting method. The visitor centre explains the process and shows the evaporation basins where salt crystallises between June and September. Operators offer boat or bike excursions through the salt pans, which are also a protected wetland—you'll see flamingos, avocets and black-winged stilts.

In Cervia's centre, the Torre San Michele, a 17th-century defensive tower, dominates Piazza Garibaldi. Right next door, the MUSA—Museo del Sale traces Cervia's thousand-year history of salt production, from the Etruscans to current techniques. It's a small but well-done museum that explains why salt was for centuries the white gold of this coast and the object of wars between Comacchio, Cervia and Venice.

Arriving in Cesenatico, head for the canal-port, the city's heart. In 1502, Leonardo da Vinci, then in the service of Cesare Borgia, drew from the top of the fortress a perspective of the city and the construction plan for two jetties to combat silting—a recurring problem for Adriatic ports. Leonardo's drawing, dated 6 September 1502, is preserved in the National Library of Paris. Stroll along the quays: this is Cesenatico's real 'piazza', lined with colourful fishing boats, restaurants and bars.

The Museo della Marineria is Cesenatico's flagship attraction. This maritime museum, unique in Italy, unfolds in two parts: an open-air section on the canal, where about ten traditional Adriatic fishing boats are moored with their coloured sails—spectacular when hoisted—and an indoor section tracing the Romagna coast's maritime history. Allow a good hour for both.

Push on to the Piazza delle Conserve, a little architectural gem: these circular pits dug into the ground once served to preserve fish in ice, before refrigeration was invented. The recently restored square often hosts exhibitions and cultural events. Right next door, the Piazza Pisacane houses the monument to Garibaldi, erected in 1884—on 2 August 1849, Cesenatico supplied the fleeing national hero with provisions and 13 ships to reach Venice by sea.

activities.text: Cesenatico is an active fishing port with 90 vessels. In season, operators offer sea trips of 2 to 5 hours: you board a traditional fishing boat, witness fishing with nets between two boats and taste freshly fried sardines or anchovies on deck, accompanied by local wine. An authentic and convivial experience.

practical_info.tip: Trips available May to October, early morning departure. Booking advised

activities.text: Late in the day, enjoy Cesenatico's beach for a swim in the Adriatic. The Romagna coast is renowned for its fine sandy beaches and shallow waters, ideal for families. In the evening, return to the canal-port quays: as the sun sets, the coloured sails of the maritime museum boats reflect in the water—it's Cesenatico's most photogenic moment.

practical_info.tip: The beaches are organised into numbered 'bagni' with deckchairs and umbrellas, but free zones exist too

visits.text: Devote the morning to the Republic of San Marino, the world's oldest sovereign state and the oldest still-functioning republic. According to tradition, a Dalmatian stonemason named Marinus founded a Christian community atop Mount Titano in 301 AD to escape the persecutions of Emperor Diocletian. Since then, this micro-republic of 33,000 inhabitants has never lost its independence. The historic centre and Mount Titano (750 m) have been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2008. Park in Borgo Maggiore and take the cable car—the 2-minute ascent offers spectacular panoramas and saves you the traffic jams at the summit.

San Marino's Three Towers are the republic's symbol and stand on the three peaks of Mount Titano. La Guaita (first tower), the oldest, dates from the 11th century and served as a prison until 1970. Climb it for a panorama embracing all of Romagna to the Adriatic Sea—on clear days, you can glimpse the Croatian coast. La Cesta (second tower), perched on Titano's highest point, houses a small museum of antique weapons. The path linking the two towers follows the ridge and offers vertiginous views.

Head down to Piazza della Libertà, San Marino's heart, dominated by the Palazzo Pubblico—seat of government since 1894. The changing of the guard takes place every half-hour in summer. The Basilica of San Marino (19th century, neoclassical) preserves the relics of the founding saint. Wander through the cobbled alleys that climb and descend between the crenellated ramparts before heading down towards Rimini.

Arriving in Rimini in early afternoon, start with the Arch of Augustus, erected in 27 BC—it's the oldest triumphal arch still standing in Rome. Its Corinthian half-columns and rounded arch mark the southern entrance to the ancient city. From there, walk up Corso d'Augusto, the main street that follows the route of the ancient Via Flaminia, towards the historic centre.

At the centre's other end, the Bridge of Tiberius spans the Marecchia river with its five massive Istrian stone arches. Begun under Augustus and completed under Tiberius in 21 AD, it has been in continuous service for 2,000 years—cars still cross it daily. It's one of the best-preserved Roman bridges in the world. Come in the evening when it's illuminated and reflected in the water.

Just beyond the Bridge of Tiberius, the Borgo San Giuliano is Rimini's old fishermen's quarter, transformed into one of the city's most charming corners. Its brightly coloured low houses, cobbled alleys and murals pay homage to Fellini and his films—you'll recognise scenes from Amarcord and La Dolce Vita on the walls. The atmosphere is intimate, working-class and perfect for a terrace dinner.

activities.text: On the road between San Marino and Rimini, stop at one of the Romagna hills' vineyards. The region produces Sangiovese di Romagna, a full-bodied red, and Albana DOCG, the first Italian white to obtain this designation. Operators offer tastings with views over the hills—a beautiful transition between mountain and sea.

practical_info.tip: Booking advised. The hill road between San Marino and Rimini is very pleasant

activities.text: Enjoy your first evening in Rimini in the Borgo San Giuliano. The terrace tables in the cobbled alleys, overlooking the illuminated Bridge of Tiberius, are the best place to taste Romagna cuisine: piadina, passatelli in brodo, grilled Adriatic fish. The neighbourhood's atmosphere is warm and working-class, far from the seaside bustle of the waterfront.

practical_info.tip: Borgo San Giuliano has the best tables for an authentic dinner

visits.text: Devote this morning to Rimini's Renaissance and cinematic heritage. The Tempio Malatestiano is far more than a simple cathedral. Sigismondo Malatesta, the powerful lord of Rimini, had Leon Battista Alberti transform a Gothic church into a sumptuous Renaissance temple from 1450. The white marble exterior is inspired by Roman triumphal arches. Inside, you'll find a crucifix painted by Giotto and the Malatesta family's funeral chapels. Sigismondo wanted to make it the Renaissance's most beautiful tomb—Pope Pius II excommunicated him for his pride.

The Castel Sismondo, the imposing fortress built in 1437 by Sigismondo Malatesta himself (he personally supervised the works), dominates the city centre. Since 2021, it has housed the Fellini Museum, an immersive and multimedia journey dedicated to the director born in Rimini in 1920. Fellini's visionary universe unfolds between the castle, Piazza Malatesta and the Fulgor cinema—the theatre where young Federico discovered cinema. Even if you're not a Fellini devotee, the experience is worthwhile.

Piazza Cavour is Rimini's medieval heart, an elegant urban salon bordered by the Palazzo dell'Arengo (13th century), the Palazzo del Podestà and the Teatro Galli, faithfully reconstructed after war damage and reopened in 2018. At the centre stands the Fontana della Pigna, a 14th-century white marble fountain that was long the city's only source of drinking water.

If you have a little time, detour to the remains of the Roman amphitheatre, located between the historic centre and the sea. Built in the 2nd century AD, it could accommodate about 12,000 spectators. Only the foundations and a few arches remain, but it's a powerful reminder of Rimini's importance in imperial Rome—the city stood at the crossroads of the Via Emilia and Via Flaminia, two of the Empire's most strategic roads.

activities.text: In the afternoon, if you fancy some relaxation before departure, Rimini has 15 km of fine sandy beaches along the Adriatic. The coast is organised into numbered 'bagni' with deckchairs, umbrellas and sometimes beach volleyball courts or beach bars. Even out of season, a stroll along the seafront with views of the Grand Hotel—immortalised by Fellini in Amarcord—remains a pleasant moment.

practical_info.tip: Free beach zones exist between the paid bagni. The water is shallow, ideal for families

activities.text: To close your itinerary, do your last shopping at Rimini's covered market, near Piazza Cavour, or treat yourself to a final espresso in one of the square's cafés. If you're leaving late in the day, Rimini station offers direct connections to Bologna (1h15), Ravenna (1h) and beyond. Federico Fellini Airport is a few minutes from the centre.

practical_info.tip: The station and airport are very close to the centre—no stress for departure

seo.h1_title: From the Po Delta to the Romagna Riviera: Ferrara, Ravenna and Rimini
seo.intro_paragraph: This 6-day itinerary follows the eastern coast of Emilia-Romagna, from Ferrara and its Renaissance palaces to the flamingo-filled Po Delta, through Ravenna's Byzantine mosaics—8 UNESCO monuments—and the Romagna Riviera to Rimini and its Roman remains. A detour through the Republic of San Marino, the world's oldest republic perched on Mount Titano, completes this journey between land and sea.
seo.seo_hook: A 6-day road trip between Renaissance Ferrara, the lagoons of the Po Delta, Ravenna's UNESCO mosaics, the Republic of San Marino and the beaches of Rimini.
featured_list: Ferrara: the Este castle of 1385 surrounded by moats, 9 km of Renaissance ramparts and frescoes of the Palazzo Schifanoia
Comacchio: 'Little Venice' of the Po Delta with its canals, the Ponte Trepponti and flamingos in the lagoons
Ravenna: 8 UNESCO monuments including the Basilica of San Vitale and the Mausoleum of Galla Placidia with 5th-century mosaics
Cervia and Cesenatico: historic salt pans and canal-port designed by Leonardo da Vinci in 1502
San Marino: the world's oldest republic founded in 301 AD, three medieval towers on Mount Titano
Rimini: Arch of Augustus from 27 BC, Bridge of Tiberius from 21 AD, Malatesta Temple and Fellini Museum

places_taglines.it::ferrare: Where the Este dynasty built a Renaissance jewel wrapped in 9 km of ramparts
places_taglines.it::comacchio: A fishing town of canals and bridges where flamingos glide over lagoons
places_taglines.it::ravenne: Eight UNESCO monuments holding the world's finest Byzantine mosaics
places_taglines.it::cesenatico: Where Leonardo sketched harbour plans and fishing boats still sail
places_taglines.it::rimini: Two thousand years of history between Roman ruins and Fellini's dreams

highlights: Ferrara, a UNESCO site since 1995, preserves 9 km of Renaissance ramparts and the Castello Estense surrounded by water-filled moats
The Valli di Comacchio form Italy's largest wetland with over 300 bird species including flamingos
Ravenna possesses 8 UNESCO monuments with the world's finest Byzantine mosaics, dating from the 5th and 6th centuries
The Bridge of Tiberius in Rimini, built between 14 and 21 AD, is one of the oldest Roman bridges still in use

itineraries.title: Gourmet Emilia: From Parma to Bologna Through the Hills
itineraries.subtitle: A 6-day road trip through Italy's Food Valley, where UNESCO-listed Romanesque cathedrals meet medieval hilltop villages, aged Parmigiano Reggiano, cured Parma ham, and traditional balsamic vinegar.
itineraries.notes: This itinerary follows the Via Emilia, the ancient Roman road that crosses the entire region. Tours of balsamic vinegar cellars and cheese dairies often require advance booking. A car is essential for reaching Maranello, Brisighella, and Dozza.

map_keywords: Basilica Santa Maria della Steccata Parma

visits.text: Begin your discovery of Parma at Piazza del Duomo, one of Italy's most beautiful squares. The cathedral, built between 1060 and 1073 in pure Romanesque style, welcomes you with its simple facade in pale stone. Look up inside to discover the dome painted by Correggio in the early 16th century—an Assumption of the Virgin so breathtaking it seems to open onto the heavens themselves. Entry is free, so take your time soaking it all in.

Right beside the cathedral stands the octagonal baptistery in pink Verona marble, the work of Benedetto Antelami, begun in 1196. This monument bridges Romanesque and Gothic styles and ranks among Italy's finest baptisteries. Step inside: the frescoes covering the walls and dome are remarkable, and the light filtering through the windows creates an extraordinary atmosphere. Allow about 30 minutes; a combined ticket includes the adjacent diocesan museum.

Head next to Palazzo della Pilotta, just a hundred meters from the main square. This vast 16th-century complex, built by the Farnese family, houses the National Gallery, the archaeological museum, and above all the Teatro Farnese. Built in 1618 by Giovan Battista Aleotti on orders from Duke Ranuccio I, this entirely wooden theater could seat 4,000 spectators—in a city that had only 20,000 residents. It was used just nine times over a century. Almost completely destroyed by bombing on May 13, 1944, it was reconstructed identically in the 1950s. It's the world's first theater with a permanent proscenium arch. They even staged mock naval battles here thanks to a system that could flood the pit.

Don't miss the Basilica of Santa Maria della Steccata, erected in 1521. This Renaissance church shelters an image of the Virgin venerated for centuries in Parma. You'll also see frescoes by Parmigianino in the apse. Entry is free and the visit is quick—allow 20 minutes.

Finish with a stroll through Piazza Garibaldi, the lively heart of the historic center. Entirely paved, it's bordered by the Palazzo del Comune and the Governor's Palace with its clock tower. This is where locals gather for aperitivo. From here, Via Farini—a pedestrian shopping street—leads you to the Teatro Regio, built in 1829 on the initiative of Marie Louise of Austria, Napoleon's wife and Duchess of Parma.

activities.text: Parma is the capital of Italy's Food Valley, and you can't leave town without tasting its two treasures: Parmigiano Reggiano and Prosciutto di Parma. Tour operators offer combined visits to the city outskirts to discover a cheese dairy and a ham curing facility—allow half a day. Alternatively, head to the covered market or gourmet food shops in the center for a more flexible tasting. Pair everything with a glass of local Lambrusco.

practical_info.tip: Book cheese dairy visits in advance, especially during high season

activities.text: Treat yourself to a walk in the Ducal Park, Parma's grand Italian-style garden located just across the Torrente Parma. Its tree-lined paths, fountains, and small lake make it the perfect spot to digest after all those tastings. The Ducal Palace, once Marie Louise's residence, can be viewed from outside—it now houses the Carabinieri headquarters.

practical_info.tip: Free access, lovely in late afternoon

visits.text: Leaving Parma, stop in Reggio Emilia, the city that gave birth to the Italian flag. Head straight to Piazza Prampolini, the city's heart, where the cathedral and town hall stand. Inside this communal palace is the Sala del Tricolore, where on January 7, 1797, representatives from Reggio, Modena, Bologna, and Ferrara adopted the green-white-red flag as the emblem of the Cispadane Republic—forerunner of unified Italy. The room, designed by architect Lodovico Bolognini in 1774, is free to visit. The small adjacent Tricolore Museum traces the flag's history with period documents.

A few steps from Piazza Prampolini, step into the Basilica della Ghiara, one of the region's finest Baroque buildings. Built between 1597 and 1619, it preserves remarkable frescoes and a miraculous image of the Virgin. The contrast with the Romanesque austerity of the neighboring cathedral is striking. Allow 20 minutes.

Arriving in Modena by midday, make your way to Piazza Grande. Modena's cathedral, begun in 1099, is a masterpiece of Lombard Romanesque art, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List since 1997 along with its bell tower, the Ghirlandina, and the square itself. Inside, don't miss the richly sculpted 13th-century rood screen that separates the nave from the elevated choir above the crypt—quite rare in Italian churches. Cathedral entry is free.

The Torre Ghirlandina, the cathedral's bell tower, rises 86 meters and dominates all of Modena. If you have the energy to climb, the view over the city's terracotta rooftops and surrounding hills is worth the effort. Allow 30 minutes for the ascent and descent.

The Palazzo Ducale, imposing residence of the Este dukes who ruled Modena from 1598 to 1859, sits just a few minutes' walk away. The palace now houses the Military Academy—you can't visit inside, but the facade and courtyard merit a detour. The Este family had made Modena a refined little capital after being forced to leave Ferrara.

activities.text: Modena is the capital of traditional balsamic vinegar PDO, a product that bears no resemblance to the industrial balsamic vinegar sold in supermarkets. Here, grape must (from Trebbiano and Lambrusco) is slowly cooked then aged for 12 to 25 years in batteries of small barrels made from oak, cherry, juniper, and mulberry, stored in house attics. Local producers open their doors to reveal this fascinating process. Book ahead—it's an experience you won't forget.

practical_info.tip: Booking essential, especially in high season. Some acetaie are in the historic center

activities.text: For lunch or late afternoon, visit the Mercato Albinelli, Modena's historic covered market open since 1931. Under its beautiful halls, you'll find stalls selling cured meats, cheeses, fresh pasta, and of course balsamic vinegar. You can buy on the spot and sit down to eat—a very local way to discover Modenese gastronomy. Try the tortellini, the region's specialty.

practical_info.tip: Open mornings and early afternoon, closed Sundays

visits.text: Before leaving Modena province, devote the morning to the Ferrari Museum in Maranello, located 20 km south of Modena. Opened in 1990, this museum traces the history of the Scuderia and the Prancing Horse through legendary Formula 1 cars, prototypes, and GTs. You can also try a driving simulator. If you're a true enthusiast, combine it with the Enzo Ferrari Museum in central Modena, housed in the founder's birthplace—a combined ticket lets you visit both within 48 hours.

Arriving in Bologna by early afternoon, head to Piazza Maggiore, the city's heart. The Basilica of San Petronio dominates the square with its deliberately unfinished facade—construction began in 1390 and it remains the world's fifth-largest church. Inside, you'll find a meridian line traced on the floor in 1655 by astronomer Cassini, stretching 66 meters long. Entry is free.

The Two Towers are Bologna's symbol. The Torre degli Asinelli, rising 97 meters, is Italy's tallest leaning medieval tower still standing. Climb its 498 steps: the panorama over Bologna's red rooftops, the hills, and on clear days the Alps is exceptional. Beside it, the Torre Garisenda, smaller at 48 meters, leans even more than the Tower of Pisa—it's not accessible for safety reasons. In the 12th century, Bologna had over a hundred such towers; about twenty remain.

activities.text: Bologna is the only city in the world where you can walk for kilometers without ever being exposed to rain or sun. Its porticoes, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2021, extend over 40 km in the historic center. Let yourself wander beneath these arcades, from Via dell'Indipendenza to Strada Maggiore. For the more athletic, the San Luca portico climbs 3.8 km and 666 arches up to the sanctuary perched on the hill—allow 1h30 round trip.

practical_info.tip: The San Luca climb is more pleasant in late afternoon when the heat subsides

activities.text: Bologna earned the nickname "la Grassa" (the fat one) for good reason. Lose yourself in the Quadrilatero, the old medieval guild quarter, now a permanent market where the aromas of cheeses, cured meats, and fresh fish mingle. Taste tortellini in brodo—tiny pasta parcels served in capon broth—and mortadella Bologna IGP, far more refined than the deli meat you might know as "bologna." Pair it all with a glass of Pignoletto, the local sparkling white wine.

practical_info.tip: The Quadrilatero is just steps from Piazza Maggiore; visit mornings for the best products

visits.text: Bologna deserves a second day. Start at Palazzo dell'Archiginnasio, the former seat of the University of Bologna—founded in 1088, it's the oldest university in the Western world. Dante, Petrarch, and Copernicus studied here. Inside, don't miss the Teatro Anatomico, a room entirely clad in fir wood adorned with sculptures, where medical professors gave anatomy lectures before tiers of students. The atmosphere is both scholarly and slightly macabre.

Continue to Piazza Santo Stefano, one of Bologna's most beautiful squares. The Basilica of Santo Stefano is actually a complex of several interlocking churches and cloisters, built between the 5th and 13th centuries—sometimes called "the seven churches." The medieval cloister, with its Romanesque columns and central basin, radiates absolute serenity. Take time to sit for a moment.

Pass through Piazza Nettuno to admire the Neptune Fountain, sculpted in bronze by Giambologna between 1563 and 1567. The sea god, trident in hand, dominates a basin adorned with mermaids and dolphins. Just behind stands Palazzo Re Enzo, where Emperor Frederick II's son was imprisoned for 23 years after being captured by the Bolognese in 1249.

Wander through the university quarter around Via Zamboni. With 100,000 students—a quarter of the population—Bologna pulses with distinctive energy. Independent bookshops, lively cafes, walls covered in posters: this is where you'll feel why Bologna also bears the nickname "la Dotta" (the learned one). Continue to Palazzo Poggi, the university's current seat, which houses fascinating scientific collections.

If you're seeking a quiet moment, the Baroque church of San Domenico is worth the detour. It houses the Arca di San Domenico, the monumental tomb of Saint Dominic, worked on by renowned sculptors including the young Michelangelo—three statuettes on the tomb are attributed to him. The wooden choir is also remarkable.

activities.text: Bologna is a city best discovered by bike. The historic center is largely flat and pedestrianized, and operators offer rentals by the hour or day. It's the best way to cover the 40 km of porticoes without exhausting yourself, and to easily reach outlying neighborhoods like the Giardini Margherita, the large public park south of the center.

practical_info.tip: Bike-sharing stations are available throughout the center

activities.text: For complete immersion in Bolognese gastronomy, join a cooking class to learn how to make tagliatelle al ragù—the real ragù Bolognese, a thick and savory sauce served with egg pasta, which bears no resemblance to the "spaghetti Bolognese" found abroad. Operators offer 2- to 3-hour workshops, often in welcoming settings in the historic center.

practical_info.tip: Booking essential, often full on weekends

visits.text: Brisighella greets you with an unforgettable silhouette: three rocky pinnacles of selenite dominate the village, each crowned by a monument—the Rocca Manfrediana fortress, the Clock Tower, and the Sanctuary of Monticino. Ranked among Italy's most beautiful villages, this medieval hamlet in Ravenna province has remained remarkably intact. Start with the Via degli Asini, the village's most famous attraction. This covered, elevated street, built in the 12th century, originally served as a defensive rampart before housing the stables of carters who transported gypsum from nearby quarries with their donkeys. Its arched windows, wooden beams, and irregular paving make it unique in Italy.

Climb up to the Rocca Manfrediana, the 14th-century fortress that dominates the village. From up here, the view over the Lamone valley and hills covered with olive groves and vineyards is superb. The fortress houses the Museum of Man and Gypsum, which traces the thousand-year history of mining this mineral that shaped the village and its landscape. Allow 45 minutes for the climb, visit, and panorama.

Descend and take the path leading to the Torre dell'Orologio, perched on the second rocky pinnacle opposite the fortress. This 13th-century tower has a peculiarity: its clock shows only six hours, a historical detail that intrigues every visitor. The view from its summit complements that from the Rocca.

If you have time, make a detour to the Pieve del Tho (Pieve di San Giovanni in Ottavo), a Romanesque church located a few minutes from the center. Erected between the 8th and 10th centuries, it contains reused Roman fragments and ancient frescoes. It can be visited freely on Sunday afternoons; other days require booking.

activities.text: Brisighella sits within the Regional Park of the Vena del Gesso Romagnola, a striking landscape of white gypsum ridges, caves, and forests. Several hiking trails start directly from the village—the most accessible connects the three hills in a loop of about 1h30. For the more motivated, longer paths wind through the gypsum formations with spectacular views over the Romagna hinterland.

practical_info.tip: Bring good shoes; some passages are steep

activities.text: Brisighella is also a village of flavors. Its extra virgin olive oil PDO "Brisighello," obtained from the Nostrana variety cultivated only on these hills, was the first in Italy to obtain PDO certification in 1996. Local producers offer mill visits with tastings. Pair the oil with cured meats from Mora Romagnola, a local pig breed, and a glass of Sangiovese DOC.

practical_info.tip: Mills operate from November to December, but tastings are possible year-round

visits.text: Dozza is a museum-village in the literal sense. Ranked among Italy's most beautiful villages, this small medieval hamlet perched on a hill between Bologna and Imola has become an open-air art gallery thanks to the Painted Wall Biennial, launched in 1960. Over 200 Italian and international artists have painted house facades, arches, and walls over the decades. The works are everywhere: look up, peer into corners, venture down alleys. Among the best-known are "The Angel of Dozza" by Giuliana Bonazza (1993) and "The Rainbow" by Alfonso Frasnedi (2000). The village consists of two parallel streets, Via XX Settembre and Via De Amicis—you can't get lost.

At the top of the village rises the Rocca Sforzesca, a fortress built in 1245 whose current appearance dates from work by Caterina Sforza starting in 1480. She added the two large round corner towers with machicolations. Inside, you'll discover rooms furnished with furniture from the Campeggi and Malvezzi families who inhabited the fortress until 1960, dungeons and oubliettes, and a picture gallery preserving sketches of the wall frescoes. From the rampart walk, the view over the valley vineyards and hills is remarkable.

In the Rocca's cellars, don't miss the Enoteca Regionale dell'Emilia-Romagna, installed here since 1970. It's the showcase for wines from across the region—over 1,000 labels from 200 producers. You can taste on-site using a self-service system, or simply buy a bottle to take home. The setting of vaulted cellars adds to the pleasure. Try the Sangiovese di Romagna, Albana DOCG (the first Italian white to obtain DOCG status), and Pignoletto.

activities.text: If you visit Dozza in September of an even year, you'll catch the Painted Wall Biennial in full swing: over a weekend, artists paint live on the village facades, before the public's eyes. It's a unique moment when you can watch a work being born and chat with its creator. Even in odd years, the village hosts cultural events and wine tastings.

practical_info.tip: Download the "Muro Dipinto" app to identify each fresco with its artist and year

activities.text: To close this itinerary in style, treat yourself to lunch at one of the village's restaurants. Romagna cuisine takes center stage: fresh pasta, grilled meats, piadina (Romagna flatbread), and local cheeses. Tables are set on terraces as soon as good weather arrives, with views over the hills. Pair the meal with a glass of regional wine—you're in the heart of the Strada dei Vini e Sapori of the Colli d'Imola.

practical_info.tip: The village is small; restaurants fill up fast on Sundays—arrive early

seo.h1_title: Gourmet Emilia: From Parma to Bologna Through the Hills
seo.intro_paragraph: Emilia-Romagna is Italy's Food Valley, and this 6-day itinerary takes you through its most delicious treasures. From Parma, capital of prosciutto and Parmesan, to Bologna with its 40 km of UNESCO-listed porticoes, passing through Modena's balsamic vinegar cellars and the perched medieval villages of Brisighella and Dozza, every stop blends Romanesque heritage, tastings, and the Italian art of living.
seo.seo_hook: A 6-day gourmet road trip through Italy's Food Valley: Parma, Modena, Bologna, Brisighella, and Dozza, where UNESCO cathedrals meet traditional balsamic vinegar and medieval hilltop villages.
featured_list: Parma: 11th-century Romanesque cathedral and the 1618 wooden Teatro Farnese that could seat 4,000 spectators
Modena: cathedral-Ghirlandina-Piazza Grande UNESCO ensemble and traditional balsamic vinegar aged 12 to 25 years
Bologna: 40 km of UNESCO porticoes, university founded in 1088, and leaning medieval towers
Brisighella: perched village ranked among Italy's most beautiful, with the unique 12th-century Via degli Asini
Dozza: open-air museum village with over 100 painted murals and regional wine cellar in the Rocca Sforzesca

places_taglines.it::parme: Capital of Italy's Food Valley, where Romanesque splendor meets the Teatro Farnese's wooden grandeur.
places_taglines.it::modene: UNESCO cathedral, PDO balsamic vinegar, and birthplace of Ferrari in nearby Maranello.
places_taglines.it::bologne: La Dotta, la Grassa, la Rossa: where UNESCO porticoes shelter Europe's oldest university.
places_taglines.it::brisighella: Three-peaked village where the medieval Via degli Asini winds beneath arched windows.
places_taglines.it::dozza: Where 200 artists turned ancient walls into an open-air canvas beneath the Rocca Sforzesca.

highlights: Modena's cathedral, its Ghirlandina bell tower, and Piazza Grande form a UNESCO-listed ensemble since 1997
Bologna boasts over 40 km of medieval porticoes inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2021
Traditional balsamic vinegar of Modena PDO ages for a minimum of 12 years in precious wooden barrels
Brisighella, ranked among Italy's most beautiful villages, shelters the Via degli Asini, a unique covered and elevated medieval street

itineraries.title: Castles of the Piacenza Hills: A 2-Day Medieval Road Trip Through Northern Italy

map_keywords: Basilica di Santa Maria di Campagna Piacenza

visits.text: Admire Piazza Cavalli, the beating heart of Piacenza dominated by two majestic Baroque equestrian statues of Alessandro and Ranuccio Farnese. Commissioned in 1600 and crafted by Mochi da Montevarchi over 13 years of work, they represent the third and fourth Duke of Parma and Piacenza in a breathtaking masterpiece of bronze casting.

Visit the Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta and Santa Giustina, one of the best-preserved examples of Romanesque architecture in Italy. Built between the 11th and 12th centuries, it conceals behind its sober façade a richly frescoed interior, crowned by the extraordinary dome painted by Guercino in the 17th century. Climb the 136 steps to reach the 27-meter-high dome.

Explore the Museums of Palazzo Farnese, a sumptuous period building housing seven different exhibition areas: the Picture Gallery, the Carriage Museum, the Civic Museum, the Rizzi Vaccari Collection, the Ceramics Museum, the Risorgimento Museum, and the Archaeological Museum. A journey through centuries of Piacenza's history and art.

Discover the Ricci Oddi Modern Art Gallery, a museum established in 1931 that houses the famous Portrait of a Lady by Gustav Klimt. The painting, stolen in 1997 and found over twenty years later in a hidden trapdoor within the museum itself, has been visible again since 2020 and is one of the most mysterious masterpieces in art history.

activities.text: Stroll beneath the arcades of the historic center to the Basilica of Santa Maria di Campagna, a jewel of Renaissance art with its frescoed chapel blending religious and pagan figures. Since 2024, visitors can climb to the dome to admire the details of the masterpieces from a unique perspective.

Visit the Municipal Theater, inaugurated in the early 19th century and considered among the most beautiful in Italy, as Stendhal himself proclaimed. The three-quarter ellipse auditorium, revolutionary for its time, offers extraordinary acoustics and interior decorations of meticulous elegance.

map_keywords: Castello di Grazzano Visconti Grazzano Visconti
Chiesa dei Santi Cosma e Damiano Grazzano Visconti

visits.text: Explore the Castle and Historic Park of Grazzano Visconti, the most precious part of this unique village. The 14th-century castle, completely restored by Giuseppe Visconti di Modrone in eclectic style, offers guided tours through the courtyard, the richly furnished reception rooms, and the bedrooms on the first floor.

Wander through the Castle Park, 15 hectares of English and Italian gardens designed by Giuseppe Visconti. Statues, vases, columns, fountains, and an extraordinary variety of trees and shrubs create a fairytale atmosphere, completed by the delightful little girls' cottage that seems straight out of a storybook.

Visit the Wax Museum with statues of figures connected to Piacenza's history, and the Torture Museum in the Palazzetto dell'Istituzione, small but complete, with torture instruments accompanied by explanatory panels. Adjacent is an Escape Room themed around the Inquisition.

Admire the Parish Church of Saints Cosmas and Damian, the oldest building in the village with 17th-century renovations. Inside, the Chapel of Our Lady of Lourdes houses numerous ex-votos, testimony to popular devotion through the centuries.

activities.text: Lose yourself in the white lanes of the village, admiring the cottages with frescoed façades, the arcades, and the statues. In the Cortevecchia you'll find the statue of Aloisa, the famous ghost of Grazzano Visconti, and a small museum of agricultural tools under the long portico.

Explore the numerous craft shops and souvenir stores, open in every season on weekends. This rare example of revivalist architecture, recognized as a City of Art by the Emilia Romagna Region in 1986, will make you feel catapulted back in time 700 years.

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta Castell'Arquato

visits.text: Explore the Rocca Viscontea, an imposing medieval fortress built between 1342 and 1349 at the behest of Luchino Visconti of Milan. With its four square corner towers, double defensive walls, and deep moat, it represents one of the best-preserved castles in Italy. Today it houses a fascinating Museum of Medieval Life.

Admire the Palazzo del Podestà in Piazza del Borgo, emblem of civic power built at the end of the 13th century by Alberto Scotti, lord of Piacenza. The elegant exposed brick façade dominates the village's main square, creating one of the most photographed medieval views in Emilia Romagna.

Visit the Collegiate Church of Santa Maria, a place of worship with ancient origins dating back to the 7th century. The sober Romanesque gabled façade decorated with blind arches hides naves adorned with precious 15th-century frescoes of extraordinary beauty.

Discover the Geological Museum housed in the ancient Hospital of Santo Spirito, a 13th-century building that once offered hospitality to pilgrims on the Via Francigena. The paleontological collections bear witness to millions of years of natural history in the Piacenza territory.

activities.text: Stroll through the Garden of Poetry, a large green expanse where you can find a silent refuge among evergreen trees and romantic benches. From here you can admire the entire Val d'Arda and several monuments dedicated to illustrious figures such as the luthier Antonio Stradivari.

Lose yourself in the cobblestone lanes of the village, one of the best-preserved windows onto medieval life in Italy, framed by noble palaces and Romanesque churches expressing the power of the Scotti, Visconti, and Sforza families.

visits.text: Explore the fortified village of Vigoleno, certified among the Most Beautiful Villages in Italy and Orange Flag of the Italian Touring Club. This perfect example of medieval residential logic is characterized by the elegance of its forms, with imposing crenellated walls traversed by a panoramic patrol walkway.

Climb the quadrangular keep equipped with arrow slits, machicolations, and Ghibelline merlons, with four floors of visits offering spectacular views over the surrounding countryside and hills. Inside the castle, the small theater with oriental images is a hidden gem not to be missed.

Visit the Romanesque Church of San Giorgio from the second half of the 12th century, reachable by continuing east from the main square where the 16th-century fountain stands. The intimate and spiritual atmosphere of this sacred place transports you back in time.

activities.text: Taste the Vin Santo di Vigoleno, a DOC passito wine whose vinification and aging operations must be carried out exclusively in the territory of the municipality of Vernasca. A golden nectar representing the oenological excellence of the Piacenza Hills.

Walk the Brigands' Ring, a circuit of about 21 km that starts from Vigoleno and arrives in Vernasca passing through Monte Ciocca. During flowering season, the golden broom creates an unforgettable natural spectacle.

seo.h1_title: Castles of the Piacenza Hills: A 4-Day Medieval Road Trip Through Northern Italy
seo.intro_paragraph: Embark on a journey through time in the Piacenza Hills, where imposing medieval fortresses rise above perfectly preserved fairytale villages. This 4-day itinerary takes you to discover masterpieces of Romanesque architecture, hidden artistic treasures, and vineyard landscapes of rare beauty in one of Emilia Romagna's most enchanting regions.
seo.seo_hook: An immersion into the Italian Middle Ages among castles, fairytale villages, and the wine excellence of the Piacenza Hills.
featured_list: Piacenza, city of art with Piazza Cavalli and the rediscovered Klimt
Grazzano Visconti, fairytale village recreated in medieval style
Castell'Arquato, one of the best-preserved medieval centers in Italy
Vigoleno, Orange Flag fortified village and its DOC Vin Santo

places_taglines.IT::piacenza: City of art with Baroque statues and the mysterious rediscovered Klimt
places_taglines.IT::grazzano-visconti: Fairytale village with 14th-century castle and medieval atmosphere
places_taglines.IT::castell-arquato: Visconti fortress and medieval square among the most photogenic in Italy
places_taglines.IT::vigoleno: Perfect fortified village with walkable walls and DOC Vin Santo

itineraries.title: Romagna Circuit: Medieval Fortresses and Hilltop Villages
itineraries.subtitle: From Rimini's Roman ruins to the Republic of San Marino, a journey back through time among Malatesta fortresses, Villanovan necropolises, and Montefeltro citadels
specialties: Extra virgin olive oil from Valconca hills

visits.text: Arch of Augustus - Erected in 27 BC by decree of the Roman Senate to honor Emperor Augustus returning victorious from the Battle of Actium, this monumental arch marked the arrival point of the Via Flaminia at Ariminum, where it intersected with the Via Emilia. Its rounded arch is flanked by Corinthian half-columns; the crenellated crown is a 10th-century medieval addition. It stands as the oldest surviving honorary Roman gate in northern Italy.

Tiberius Bridge - Begun under Augustus and completed in 21 AD under Tiberius, this five-arched bridge in Istrian stone has spanned the Marecchia River for over two thousand years without interruption. Locals call it the 'Devil's Bridge' because its resilience seems supernatural: it has survived earthquakes, floods, and World War II bombings. It marks the starting point of the Via Emilia toward Piacenza.

Tempio Malatestiano - Rimini's cathedral, this church is the signature monument of Renaissance Rimini. Sigismondo Pandolfo Malatesta, lord of Rimini from 1432 to 1468, launched construction in 1447 over the former church of San Francesco. He commissioned Leon Battista Alberti to design the white marble façade, which draws direct inspiration from the Arch of Augustus and the Tiberius Bridge. Inside: a Crucifix by Giotto (early 14th century), Sigismondo before Saint Sigismund by Piero della Francesca, and chapels adorned with bas-reliefs by Agostino di Duccio. The building remained unfinished at Sigismondo's death.

Domus of the Surgeon - Discovered in the late 1980s beneath Piazza Ferrari during urban development, this 2nd-century AD Roman house was destroyed by fire around the mid-3rd century, probably during a Germanic incursion. The sudden collapse sealed mosaics, frescoes, and furnishings in situ—a 'little Pompeii' of Rimini. The major treasure: a collection of over 150 surgical instruments in iron and bronze (scalpels, probes, forceps, trepans, dental tools), the richest ancient surgical kit to survive to our day. Its owner was likely a military surgeon of Greek origin.

Piazza Cavour - The medieval heart of Rimini, this elegant square is dominated by the Palazzo dell'Arengo (13th century), the Palazzo del Podestà, and the neoclassical façade of Teatro Galli, faithfully reconstructed after war damage and reopened in 2018. At the center stands the Fontana della Pigna, a 14th-century fountain in white marble that served for centuries as the city's sole source of drinking water.

Piazza Tre Martiri - The ancient Roman forum of Ariminum, this square preserves the rectangular plan of the intersection of the cardo and decumanus. Tradition holds that Julius Caesar addressed his legions here after crossing the Rubicon in 49 BC. A small temple commemorates this episode.

Castel Sismondo - Fortress-residence built between 1437 and 1446 by Sigismondo Pandolfo Malatesta, who drew the plans himself. The building now houses part of the Museo Fellini, an immersive, multimedia journey dedicated to Rimini's legendary filmmaker.

Cinema Fulgor - This is the theater where young Fellini discovered the magic of cinema. The restored hall, with its red-and-gold Art Deco interior, forms part of the Fellini Museum experience. The façade has been faithfully recreated.

City Museum - Housed in the former Jesuit convent on Via Tonini, this museum brings together 1,500 works spanning from the region's geological formation to the present day: archaeological section (linked to the Domus), paintings from the 14th-century Rimini school, and medieval and Renaissance art collections.

activities.text: Stroll through Borgo San Giuliano - This former fishermen's quarter across the Tiberius Bridge is a maze of narrow lanes covered in colorful murals paying homage to Fellini and his films. Each fresco tells a scene from Amarcord, La Dolce Vita, or the director's iconic characters. Low houses with pastel façades, tiny squares, and wisteria spilling over walls create a wonderfully photogenic setting.

Tasting piadina romagnola - The piadina is Romagna's emblematic specialty: a flatbread cooked on a testo (terracotta or cast-iron griddle) and filled with squacquerone (creamy fresh cheese), arugula, and prosciutto crudo. Stalls throughout the historic center uphold this thousand-year-old tradition.

map_keywords: Pieve di San Michele Arcangelo Santarcangelo

visits.text: Rocca Malatestiana - Towering over the village from the summit of Colle Giove, this fortress was built in the 14th century by the Malatesta family and remodeled in the 15th century by Sigismondo Pandolfo Malatesta. The crenellated walls and square tower offer sweeping views across the Romagna plain to the Adriatic Sea. The rocca is now privately owned but open for guided tours.

Tufa Caves - Beneath the historic center lies a network of underground galleries carved into the calcareous tufa, whose origin remains debated: some archaeologists date them to Roman times (cisterns or silos), others to the Middle Ages (storage cellars). Part of the network is accessible by guided tour; the cool, constant temperature and rounded vaults create a unique atmosphere.

Parish Church of San Michele Arcangelo - One of the oldest Romanesque churches in Romagna, dating back to the 6th–7th century. It reuses ancient Roman columns and capitals. The simplicity of the single nave and semicircular apse contrasts with the medieval additions.

Historic Center - Sloping lanes climb from Piazza Ganganelli (with its neoclassical triumphal arch dedicated to Pope Clement XIV, a native son) toward the rocca. Ochre and brick-faced palaces, artisan workshops practicing mangano (printing on fabric with carved wooden blocks, a centuries-old technique still alive here), and shaded piazzas compose a village of real character.

activities.text: Wandering among palaces and artisan workshops - Santarcangelo's mangano is one of the last workshops in Italy still printing fabrics using rust-based dyes and hand-carved pear-wood blocks, a technique inherited from the 17th century. The production of fabrics with motifs of roosters, grape clusters, and foliage is a living craft tradition.

visits.text: Rocca Malatestiana di Verucchio - Perched on a rocky spur at 330 meters above sea level commanding the entire Marecchia valley to the sea, this fortress is known as the 'Rocca del Sasso' (Rock Fortress). This is where the Malatesta dynasty was born—Dante cites in the Inferno the 'Mastin Vecchio' of Verucchio, father of Gianciotto and Paolo, protagonists of the tragedy of Paolo and Francesca. Sigismondo Pandolfo Malatesta renovated the rocca between 1442 and 1449, turning it into a defensive stronghold facing the Montefeltro of Urbino.

Castle of Montebello (Poggio Torriana) - A ten-minute drive from Verucchio, this rocca perched at 436 meters dominates the valleys of the Marecchia and Uso rivers. Built in the 12th century around a 3rd-century BC Roman tower (the Latin name Mons Belli means 'mountain of war'), the fortress passed from the Malatesta to the Guidi di Bagno and then to the Montefeltro. It owes its fame to the legend of Azzurrina: on June 21, 1375, the summer solstice, little Guendalina, daughter of lord Uguccione, born albino with bluish reflections, disappeared into the castle's icehouse and was never found. A 17th-century account recorded the story and gave her the nickname 'Azzurrina' (Little Azure). Tradition holds that her ghost manifests every five years on the summer solstice. Period furnishings (14th–18th century) and nighttime ghost tours make this one of Italy's most famous haunted castles.

Civic Archaeological Museum - Installed since 1985 in the former Augustinian monastery (13th century), this museum displays across three floors and eight rooms the treasures from the Villanovan necropolises excavated at the foot of Verucchio. Over 600 burials dating from the 10th to the 7th century BC yielded objects of exceptional preservation: a wooden throne carved with human figures (a unique piece in the world), textiles, baskets of plant fibers, jewelry in gold and Baltic amber. Verucchio controlled the Adriatic trade routes for amber between Greece, the Orient, and northern Europe.

Convent of San Francesco at Villa Verucchio - At the foot of the village, this convent houses in its cloister a monumental cypress tree estimated to be about 700 years old, planted according to tradition by Saint Francis of Assisi himself. The church preserves a 14th-century Crucifix.

activities.text: Walk through the medieval village - Stone lanes climbing toward the rocca offer views over the valley and, on clear days, to the nearby Republic of San Marino. The route passes the church of Sant'Agostino, the monastery of the Poor Clares, and several 15th–16th-century palaces.

visits.text: Rocca Guaita (First Tower) - The oldest and largest of San Marino's three towers, built directly on the rock of Monte Titano in the 11th century, without foundations. Encircled by the first of the city's three fortified walls, it houses an interior courtyard, the chapel of Santa Barbara, and former prisons. The view from the battlements sweeps across the Adriatic coast, the Romagna plain, and the Apennines.

Torre Cesta (Second Tower) - Erected in the 13th century at the highest point of Monte Titano (755 m), this pentagonal tower houses the Museum of Ancient Weapons: over 700 pieces (crossbows, swords, armor, firearms) from the Middle Ages to the 19th century. The path linking Guaita to Cesta, carved along the rocky ridge, offers vertiginous panoramas on both sides of the mountain.

Torre del Montale (Third Tower) - The smallest of the three, connected to Cesta by a woodland path. Not accessible inside, it contains a prison 8 meters deep called the 'fondo della torre' (tower pit). Its strategic role was crucial during wars against the Malatesta of Rimini. Large stones primitively stacked around its base testify to very ancient construction.

Piazza della Libertà and Palazzo Pubblico - The political heart of the Republic, this compact square overlooks the valley. The Palazzo Pubblico (1894), designed by Roman architect Francesco Azzurri and inaugurated with a speech by Giosuè Carducci on 'perpetual liberty,' is the seat of government and the Consiglio Grande e Generale. At the center of the square, the statue of Liberty in Carrara marble brandishes a lance shaped like a halberd (symbol of San Marino) and a crown adorned with the three towers. The square rests on ancient cisterns that once supplied the city with water.

Basilica of San Marino - Neoclassical church housing beneath the main altar the relics of the founding saint, rediscovered on March 3, 1586. The small adjacent Chiesetta di San Pietro preserves, according to tradition, the niches carved in the rock where Saint Marinus and his companion Saint Leo slept.

State Museum - On Piazza Titano, this museum gathers about 5,000 pieces: archaeology (including fine ancient Greek vases), paintings (Guercino, Pompeo Batoni, Elisabetta Sirani), and the Gothic goldwork jewel of the Domagnano Treasure, the only piece of this ensemble still preserved in the Republic (the others are scattered in the world's great museums).

activities.text: Path of the Three Towers (Passo delle Streghe) - The ridge path linking the three towers of Monte Titano (about 2 km) winds between cliffs, oak woods, and viewpoints. The 'Witches' Passage,' between Cesta and Montale, crosses steep rocky terrain. On clear days, the view extends to the Croatian coast.

La Cava dei Balestrieri - This pit carved in the rock at the foot of the ramparts still serves as a competition ground for San Marino's crossbowmen. Every year on September 3 (national holiday, anniversary of the Republic's foundation in 301 AD), a crossbow tournament in medieval costume takes place here.

map_keywords: Pieve di Santa Maria Assunta San Leo

visits.text: Fortress of San Leo - Rising atop a limestone spur with vertical walls, this citadel has dominated the Valmarecchia from a position renowned as impregnable. A first Roman fortification already occupied the site. Between 961 and 963, King Berengar II of Italy withstood a siege here against Otto I of Saxony. The Malatesta and Montefeltro fought over the stronghold for two centuries; in 1441, the young Federico da Montefeltro retook it by a daring climb up the sheer walls. He entrusted its reconstruction to Sienese architect Francesco di Giorgio Martini, who designed circular bastions and crossfire adapted to firearms—a masterpiece of Renaissance military engineering.

Cagliostro's Cell - From 1631 onward, the fortress became a papal prison. Its most famous prisoner, Count Cagliostro (Giuseppe Balsamo, born in Palermo in 1743), alchemist, Freemason, and adventurer, was imprisoned here in 1791 after his condemnation by the Roman Inquisition. Initially held in a standard cell, he was transferred after an escape attempt to the 'cella del pozzetto': a dungeon of about 3 by 3 meters, accessible only through a trapdoor in the ceiling, with a single opening to the outside. He died there in 1795 of apoplexy.

Parish Church of Santa Maria Assunta - Considered the oldest place of worship in the Montefeltro, this small pre-Romanesque sanctuary reuses columns and capitals from Roman times. Its single, low, austere nave exudes an archaic atmosphere of contemplation.

Cathedral of San Leone - Built of cut stone in Lombard Romanesque style, this cathedral was consecrated to the patron saint Leone, who came from Dalmatia in the 3rd century to evangelize the region. The austere façade and cul-de-four apse dominate the main piazza. Dante Alighieri, struck by the vertiginous aspect of the place, cites San Leo in Canto IV of Purgatory: 'Vassi in Sanleo e discendesi in Noli...'

Piazza Dante and Historic Center - The small square is surrounded by the Palazzo Mediceo (1517–1523), the residence of the Severini-Nardini counts (13th–16th century), and the Palazzo Della Rovere. Together they form a condensed showcase of Montefeltro civic architecture. The sole access road to the village is carved into the rock—a feature that reinforced its impregnable character.

activities.text: Climb on foot to the fortress - From the piazza, a paved path of a few hundred meters climbs to the fort's entrance. The view from the ramparts embraces the Marecchia valley, wooded hills, and, on clear days, the Adriatic coast. Wear comfortable shoes.

Alchimia Alchimie Festival - Every late August, San Leo celebrates the memory of Cagliostro with a festival dedicated to mystery and the occult sciences: markets, street performances, lectures, and fireworks animate the village for three days.

visits.text: Mondaino - This fortified village conceals a rare architectural curiosity: a semicircular arcaded square (Piazza Maggiore), shaped like a half-moon, designed in the 18th century. Surrounded by brick porticoes, it served as a covered market and gathering place. The Rocca Malatestiana, at the summit, offers a viewpoint over the wooded hills of the Valconca. The small Paleontological Museum displays marine fossils from the Miocene and Pliocene found in the surrounding hills.

Montegridolfo - This tiny fortified village, enclosed by almost intact walls, can be visited in a few minutes but concentrates a dense charm: flowered lanes, clock tower, ochre stone walls, and open views over the hills. The Museum of the Gothic Line, installed in a former church, traces the passage of the German defensive line Gothic Line in 1944 through objects, photographs, and World War II testimonies.

Saludecio - Perched at 343 meters altitude, this walled medieval village has become an open-air museum: colorful murals painted on façades represent the great inventions of the 19th century (the telephone, the vaccine, cinema, the automobile). The Chiesa di San Biagio, the parish church, preserves sacred art works. The Torre dell'Orologio marks the entrance to the historic center.

activities.text: Circuit among the three villages - The rolling road linking Mondaino, Montegridolfo, and Saludecio crosses landscapes of hills cultivated with olive groves and vineyards, with panoramas over the Romagna coast on one side and the ridges of the Montefeltro on the other. Total distance of about 25 km.

Tasting olive oil and Sangiovese - The hills between Saludecio and Mondaino produce a full-bodied, fruity Sangiovese di Romagna DOC. Farms offer tastings combining local extra virgin olive oil, formaggio di fossa (cheese aged in pits since the Middle Ages), and red wine.

seo.h1_title: Romagna Circuit: Medieval Fortresses and Hilltop Villages
seo.intro_paragraph: Step back through time in Rimini between the 27 BC Arch of Augustus and Alberti's Tempio Malatestiano, then climb toward Santarcangelo di Romagna and its caves carved into the tufa. In Verucchio, cradle of the Malatesta dynasty, a 2,700-year-old wooden throne awaits in the archaeological museum; the haunted castle of Montebello guards the memory of Azzurrina. Cross the border to discover San Marino, the world's oldest republic perched on Monte Titano. San Leo commands the valley from its impregnable rocky spur, where Cagliostro ended his days in a 3-by-3-meter dungeon. The circuit closes among the hilltop villages of Mondaino, Montegridolfo, and Saludecio.
seo.seo_hook: A 27 BC Roman arch at Rimini's gateway, Francesco di Giorgio Martini's fortress commanding the Valmarecchia, a 2,700-year-old Villanovan throne, Cagliostro's dungeon perched on a vertical spur. Medieval Romagna concentrates its treasures between plain and hills.
featured_list: Rimini: Roman arch, Malatesta cathedral, and Surgeon's Domus
Santarcangelo: caves in the tufa and centuries-old mangano workshop
Verucchio: birthplace of the Malatesta and 2,700-year-old Villanovan throne
San Marino: three medieval towers of the world's oldest republic
San Leo: Francesco di Giorgio Martini's citadel and Cagliostro's dungeon
Saludecio: hilltop villages with murals and Romagna panoramas

places_taglines.IT::rimini: Where Augustus's arch meets Alberti's Renaissance cathedral
places_taglines.IT::santarcangelo-di-romagna: A hilltop village carved with tufa caves and artisan workshops
places_taglines.IT::verucchio: Cradle of the Malatesta dynasty commanding the valley from 330 meters
places_taglines.IT::san-leo: An impregnable citadel reimagined by Francesco di Giorgio Martini
places_taglines.IT::saludecio: Romagna's inland hilltop villages painted with 19th-century murals
places_taglines.IT::san-marino: The world's oldest republic founded in 301 AD on UNESCO Monte Titano

highlights: Arch of Augustus and Tiberius Bridge: two thousand years of Roman history in Rimini
Surgeon's Domus: the world's richest ancient surgical kit
Tempio Malatestiano: cathedral-mausoleum with Giotto crucifix and Piero della Francesca fresco
San Marino: three UNESCO towers on Monte Titano, world's oldest republic
Verucchio Archaeological Museum: unique Villanovan wooden throne
Fortress of San Leo: Renaissance military engineering masterpiece and Cagliostro's dungeon
Three inland hilltop villages: Mondaino, Montegridolfo, and Saludecio

itineraries.title: Udine & Friuli Circuit: Hidden Treasures
itineraries.subtitle: From Palmanova's star fortress to Venzone's mummies, a journey through Venetian squares, world-class mosaics, emerald torrents, and prosciutto traditions

visits.text: Piazza Grande di Palmanova - At the precise geometric center of this star-shaped fortress city founded on October 7, 1593 by the Venetian Republic, the hexagonal plaza forms the heart of an architectural masterpiece. The foundation date commemorates the 1571 Battle of Lepanto victory over the Ottomans. The original name "Palma" symbolized that triumph; Napoleon later added "nova" two centuries on. Stand on the central platform to grasp the perfect symmetry of six radiating streets leading toward nine defensive bastions.

Duomo del Santissimo Redentore - Dominating Piazza Grande, this church traditionally attributed to Vincenzo Scamozzi, Palladio's pupil, features a deliberately squat bell tower: it couldn't be visible from outside the ramparts, denying potential attackers a targeting reference point. This single detail encapsulates Palmanova's dual nature as both Renaissance utopian city and military machine.

Fortezza di Palmanova - Three concentric rings form three nested nine-pointed stars. The first two were built by Venice between 1593 and 1690, the third added by Napoleon between 1806 and 1813 under General Chasseloup's direction. Each curtain wall measured roughly 400 meters—exactly matching the cannon range of the era, so a single bastion could defend the entire adjacent front. UNESCO-listed since 2017, Palmanova remains the only perfectly preserved star-shaped Renaissance fortress city in existence.

Museo della Grande Guerra e della Fortezza - Housed in the dungeons of Porta Cividale, this museum chronicles the fortress's military history from its 1593 founding through the 20th century. Weapons, uniforms, and documents illustrate the Venetian, Napoleonic, and Austro-Hungarian periods.

Bastioni e Gallerie di Contromina - Descend into the underground galleries of Baluardo Donato: these secret tunnels connected the bastions to the 4-kilometer outer moat, allowing soldiers to move under cover. The pointed bastion design deflected cannonballs, reducing impact force. Above, the rampart walkway offers a 4.3-kilometer circuit on foot or by bike around the entire fortress.

Porta Aquileia, Porta Udine, and Porta Cividale - The three monumental gates facing Aquileia, Udine, and Cividale were the fortress's only entry points. Each was protected by counter-gates and drawbridges. Push through to appreciate the wall thickness and the perspectives opening toward Piazza Grande.

activities.text: Complete rampart circuit on foot or by bike - The Anello delle Fortificazioni (4.3 km) traces all three Venetian and Napoleonic defensive rings, past bastions, ravelins, and lunettes. Interpretive panels line the route. Each September, the "Palma alle Armi" historical reenactment draws over a thousand costumed participants in 16th-century dress.

visits.text: Villa Manin - This monumental complex of 8,500 square meters and 18 hectares was built in the 17th century by Friulian nobleman Antonio Manin. The forecourt, inspired by St. Peter's Square in Rome, opens onto a semicircular colonnade. The central building was redesigned in the 18th century by Giorgio Massari, one of Venice's great Settecento architects. Napoleon Bonaparte lodged here for two months in 1797 with Joséphine de Beauharnais, conducting negotiations that led to the Treaty of Campoformio signed October 17, 1797—ending a thousand years of Venetian Republic. Napoleon's bedroom and period bed remain on display.

Cappella Sant'Andrea - Built in 1708 by Domenico Rossi outside the square courtyard, this octagonal chapel adorned with marble statues by sculptor Pietro Baratta was connected by corridors to the villa's upper floors, allowing the Manin family to attend services without mingling with Passariano villagers.

Permanent collections - The former stables house a collection of antique carriages and a 350-piece armory from Udine's Casa della Contadinanza. Since 2004, the villa has also served as a Contemporary Art Center with rotating exhibitions featuring international artists and park installations.

activities.text: Stroll through the French-style gardens - Designed in 1714 by a French architect modeled on Versailles, the park blends centuries-old trees, small lakes, hillocks crowned with mythological statuary, and vast lawns. In spring, the central meadow carpets itself with yellow daffodils. Even Carlo Goldoni was dazzled by the site's magnificence during a visit.

map_keywords: Museo Diocesano Gallerie del Tiepolo Udine

visits.text: Piazza della Libertà - Considered the finest Venetian square outside Venice itself, it gathers the Loggia del Lionello, Loggia di San Giovanni, and the column crowned by the Lion of Saint Mark in one space—a striking summary of four centuries of Venetian rule over Friuli.

Loggia del Lionello - This Venetian Gothic town hall was commissioned in 1441 and built between 1448 and 1456. Its architect, Nicolò Lionello, wasn't a trained architect but a goldsmith, which explains the refinement of every detail. The façade measuring 35.5 meters long and 15.2 meters high alternates bands of white and pink stone, ten pointed arches at ground level, and a central balcony above. Ravaged by fire in 1876, it was reconstructed identically. The Gothic inscription "UTINUM" recalls Udine's Latin name.

Loggia di San Giovanni and Torre dell'Orologio - Facing the Loggia del Lionello, this Renaissance loggia shelters a clock tower inspired by—though squatter than—the one in St. Mark's Square in Venice. It underscores the Serenissima's constant influence on city life.

Arco Bollani and ascent to the Castello - Pass through the arch designed by Andrea Palladio in 1556: it grants access to the ramp climbing toward the castle hill. Along the way, walk beneath the Loggia del Lippomano, an elegant 16th-century portico.

Castello di Udine - Rebuilt in the mid-16th century after the 1511 earthquake, this palace-fortress atop the hill houses the Civic Museums: galleries of Venetian and Friulian paintings from the 14th to 19th centuries, archaeological collections, and prints. From the terrace, views sweep across the city rooftops and, on clear days, the snow-capped Julian and Carnic Alps.

Museo Diocesano e Gallerie del Tiepolo - Set in the Palazzo Patriarcale, residence of the Patriarchs of Aquileia from 1593 to 1751, this museum holds around 700 works. Young Giambattista Tiepolo painted masterful frescoes here between 1726 and 1730: The Fall of the Rebel Angels in the stairwell, Rachel Hiding the Idols in the Gallery of Guests, and The Judgment of Solomon in the Red Room. An entire floor displays polychrome wood sculptures from the 12th to 18th centuries.
