visits.text: Chianale - La Cianal in Occitan - clings to the mountainside at 1,800 meters in the upper Varaita valley, a stone's throw from the Colle dell'Agnello pass leading into France. The Varaita torrent splits the village in two halves connected by a stone bridge that forms the village's true heart. The slate houses with lauze stone roofs reflect an unbroken Occitan building tradition dating back to the tenth century, when Saracen raiders ravaged the region. In 1343, Chianale belonged to the five Free Escartons Republics - a Franco-Italian alpine confederation enjoying considerable autonomy granted by the last Dauphin of Viennois, Humbert II. The 1713 Treaty of Utrecht definitively attached the valley to Savoy.

The church of Sant'Antonio, erected in the fourteenth century, features a double-bay bell gable and a triple-crowned Romanesque portal. Inside, an eighteenth-century baroque altar of carved Swiss pine illustrates the richness of local craftsmanship. The Museum of Costume and Textile Crafts, opened in 2009 in the former eighteenth-century Capuchin Mission, exhibits the clothing and traditions of this alpine community.

Two kilometers from the village, the Alevè forest extends across 825 hectares between 1,500 and 2,500 meters elevation. It's the largest Swiss pine forest in the Alps and one of Europe's largest - trees several centuries old that Virgil already mentioned in Antiquity. Start from the hamlets of Castello or Villaretto for the 5-kilometer trail along Castello Lake, created by a dam built in the twentieth century.

activities.text: In summer, hike to Colle dell'Agnello (2,744m) with plunging views over the French Queyras and Monviso (3,841m), nicknamed the "Stone King." In winter, cross-country skiing and ski mountaineering from Chianale (15km of trails). Every January 17th, the blessing of the animals marks Saint Anthony's Day; in February, carnival perpetuates the wolf-return ritual with costumed men and nocturnal village celebrations.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate to difficult depending on route

visits.text: Ostana counts barely fifty permanent residents today - down from 1,200 in 1921 - yet this Occitan village perched at 1,250 meters beneath Monviso has become an international model of alpine renaissance. In 1985, mayor Giacomo Lombardo and architect Renato Maurino launched a restoration project following strict rules respecting original materials and volumes. This continuous effort transformed Ostana into an "open-air alpine architecture laboratory" in Legambiente's words, which awarded it the Bandiera Verde in 2005. In 2008, Ostana joined the Borghi più belli d'Italia network.

Wander through the hamlets scattered across the slope: La Villo (the administrative center with the town hall flying the Occitan flag), Champanho surrounded by maples and ash trees, Miribrart completely restored with European funds and home to a film school founded in 2012. In the small chapel of San Bernardo, a recently uncovered medieval fresco represents a rare testament to high-mountain sacred art. The ethnographic museum I Rënèis, housed in the town hall, presents traditional ways of life in the Po valley.

activities.text: Hike the Monviso Horizon route, a 50-kilometer loop connecting all the upper Po valley villages, also suitable for mountain biking. In winter, ski touring and snowshoeing at the foot of Monviso. Ostana is also a recognized climbing destination with summer routes and winter ice cascades.

visits.text: Barolo is a village of 700 souls whose name has traveled the world. Castello Falletti, with foundations dating to the tenth century, served as the residence of the powerful Falletti family until the nineteenth century. Juliette Colbert, who married Marquis Carlo Tancredi Falletti in 1807, transformed Barolo from a sweet wine into a dry, age-worthy red, then gifted 325 bottles to King Carlo Alberto of Savoy to introduce it at court - a founding gesture of its worldwide fame. In May 2015, Pope Francis recognized Juliette Colbert as Venerable in her beatification process.

The castle has housed the WiMu (Wine Museum) since September 2010, designed by French museographer François Confino. The route descends from the panoramic third floor to the ancient cellars, encountering multimedia installations that weave wine together with literature, painting, cinema, and gastronomy. The castle's ancestral cellars host the Regional Enoteca of Barolo, representing all 11 production communes of the appellation. For an unusual curiosity, the neighboring Corkscrew Museum displays hundreds of pieces from all eras.

Three kilometers away, climb to the village of La Morra (559m), nicknamed the "balcony of the Langhe." Its panoramic terrace offers one of the widest views over the hills classified as UNESCO World Heritage since 2014. Along the way, don't miss the Cappella Brunate - a small country chapel reinterpreted in contemporary art with vivid colors by two New York artists - and the majestic Cedar of Lebanon planted in 1856 before the chapel. The Big Bench Community project has installed giant benches in the surrounding vineyards, offering viewpoints at the height of the hills.

activities.text: Barolo DOCG tasting in the village's historic cellars or at the castle's Regional Enoteca. Barolo ages a minimum of 38 months in oak (62 months for Riserva) and develops aromas of faded roses, tar, and spices. The Barolo wine festival takes place every second Sunday in September.

visits.text: Monforte d'Alba - the Mons Fortis - takes its name from the ramparts that once crowned the hill at 480 meters. In 1028, Milan's Archbishop Aribert d'Intimiano besieged the village to eradicate a Cathar community. The deported population, condemned to the stake in Milan after refusing to recant, suffered an episode that preceded the Béziers massacres by two centuries. The medieval center has preserved its fan-shaped plan following the contour lines, with tortuous alleys bordered by carefully restored stone buildings.

The Auditorium Horszowski is an open-air venue carved into the hill's natural morphology - a 800-seat cavea whose exceptional acoustics are produced by the terrain configuration between the Romanesque bell tower (sole remnant of the old church demolished in the early twentieth century), the baroque oratory of Santa Elisabetta, and the walls of Palazzo Scarampi. Pianist Mieczysław Horszowski, then 91 years old, inaugurated it in 1986. Since then, the Monfortinjazz festival has welcomed internationally renowned artists every summer, from Paolo Conte to Caetano Veloso.

Palazzo Scarampi, built in the eighteenth century on the ruins of the medieval castle (itself rebuilt after 1706 by the Del Carretto family then restored in 1833), now houses the town hall. Its facade opens directly onto the auditorium and serves as a backdrop for concerts. Right beside it, the oratory of Saint Augustine and Saint Boniface presents a brick facade and a bronze portal with medieval symbols.

activities.text: Wine walk through the UNESCO-classified Langhe vineyards (2014) - Monforte is one of the 11 Barolo DOCG production communes. Local estates offer cellar visits with tastings of Barolo, Nebbiolo, and Dolcetto d'Alba.

visits.text: Guarene dominates the right bank of the Tanaro from a strategic position that allowed the Radicati to establish a fortress here in the Middle Ages. In the eighteenth century, Count Carlo Giacinto Roero di Guarene - friend and student of court architect Filippo Juvarra - decided to transform the fortress into a prestige residence. Work began on September 13, 1726, the date and hour recorded in the archives. The building, 25 meters high over three levels and surrounded by Italian gardens begun in 1740, is a rare example of Piedmontese baroque commissioned by an aristocrat who was himself an amateur architect.

In 1773, King Victor Amadeus III of Sardinia and the Queen visited the castle, leaving traces in the furnishings of several rooms. From the terraces, the view embraces 60 kilometers of UNESCO-classified territory - from the Langhe of Barolo to Barbaresco, the Monferrato, and the Alpine chain from the plain of Alba and beyond. Today converted into a luxury hotel and listed as a historic monument since 2011, the castle preserves all its period furniture, tapestries, and family portraits of the Roero.

On the hill of San Licerio, the Parco d'Arte presents works by Italian and international artists installed among Nebbiolo vines and woods. Born in 1975 from a local painting competition, the collection has grown to constitute a unique free open-air museum in the Roero.

activities.text: Tasting of Roero Arneis - Piedmont's great dry white - and Roero Rosso made from Nebbiolo in local cellars. Walk through the Roero vineyards along the "Rocche," spectacular sandy cliffs carved by erosion that characterize this north side of the Tanaro.

visits.text: Neive - the pais di sgnuret (village of little lords) - perches in the heart of the western Langhe, divided between an old town on the hilltop and Borgonuovo below. The upper old town was the chosen residence of nobility, who left a series of aristocratic palaces and mansions in terracotta along the steep alleys. The original castle was destroyed in 1276 during a war between the communes of Asti and Alba, but remnants of the medieval ricetto partially survive.

Piazza Italia, a true eighteenth-century drawing room bordered by elegant palaces, is the village heart. The thirteenth-century Clock Tower rises here - a Roman funerary plaque embedded in its wall testifies to the continuity of occupation since Antiquity. The fortified house of the Conti Cotti di Ceres, built in the thirteenth century near this tower by a banking family, houses the text by Francesco Cotti (late seventeenth century) that constitutes one of the oldest Piedmontese writings on vine cultivation. The church of the Archconfraternity of San Michele, built in the second half of the eighteenth century by architect Giovanni Antonio Borgese, is worth visiting for its interior stuccoes.

The Casa della Donna Selvatica museum, with free entry, traces local history and presents the production of Donna Selvatica grappa, the typical brandy of the Langhe. The hills around Neive produce the sector's four great DOCG wines: Barbaresco, Barbera, Moscato d'Asti, and Dolcetto d'Alba.

activities.text: Visit the cellars of Barbaresco (the "neighboring village" 5km away) where the Regional Enoteca of Barbaresco occupies the nave of the former church of San Donato. Barbaresco's medieval tower, 36 meters high and the tallest in Piedmont, offers in season a 360° panorama over the Alpine arc and the vines below.

map_keywords: Chiesa San Pietro in Vincoli Castagnole delle Lanze
Borgo San Pietro Castagnole delle Lanze
Via Ener Bettica Castagnole delle Lanze
Murales Vincenzo Piccato Castagnole delle Lanze

visits.text: Castagnole delle Lanze takes its name from the Lancia family - from the nickname Lancifero (lance-bearer) awarded to Lord Manfredo at Frederick Barbarossa's court in the twelfth century. Its history then follows that of the commune of Asti from the thirteenth century, then the Visconti in the fourteenth, finally Emanuele Filiberto of Savoy from 1573. The village sits in the UNESCO buffer zone of the Vineyard Landscapes of Langhe-Roero and Monferrato. Its vineyards primarily produce Moscato d'Asti, Barbera d'Asti, and Ruchè - a rare aromatic red wine from a grape variety typical of this zone.

Climb to the historic borgo of San Pietro, perched on the hill at 298 meters. The parish church of San Pietro in Vincoli, built in 1681 in Ligurian-Piedmontese baroque style, houses 230 carved cherubs, gilded stuccoes, and wooden altars inside - unexpectedly rich decor for a village of this size. The nineteenth-century panoramic tower, commissioned by Count Paolo Ballada di Saint Robert for his astronomical observations, dominates the Rimembranza park and offers a 360° view over the UNESCO buffer zone.

Along Via Ener Bettica, the arcades of the historic center are adorned with murals by local artist Vincenzo Piccato - thirteen arches in the colors of the seasons and wine constitute an open-air gallery on the nature of the Langhe and Monferrato. The church of the Confraternity of the Battuti Bianchi (1668), now deconsecrated, hosts exhibitions and concerts; it houses the Museum of Peasant Civilization of the Alto Monferrato.

activities.text: Tasting of Moscato d'Asti DOCG (sweet sparkling wine) and Barbera d'Asti in local cellars. The Adopt a Row of Vines initiative, launched in 2010 by the town hall, allows visitors to adopt ten meters of Barbera and receive ten bottles of wine - unique in Italy.

visits.text: Mombaldone is the only village in the Langa Astigiana still encircled by its intact original walls, with its urban plan unchanged since foundation. The first written mention dates to 991, in the foundation act of the abbey of San Quintino in Spigno Monferrato. In 1208, the fief passed to the Del Carretto family, Marquises of Finale Ligure. In the seventeenth century, the village alone resisted Napoleonic troops - armor and muskets from French soldiers are still found in the Bormida plain - while the rest of Piedmont opened its doors to the victorious army. A once-imposing square tower, now partially ruined, still dominates the village summit.

Walk through the single main street from which branch alleys and vaulted passages, interior courtyards and portals carved from centuries-old wood. Notice the engraved lintels and stone arches testifying to unchanged medieval craftsmanship. The Palazzo Fortezza, partially inhabited by descendants of the Del Carretto family - whose name spans more than eight centuries of continuous presence - faces the main street.

Leaving the village, climb a few hundred meters toward the surrounding countryside. The calanchi are deep ravines carved by erosion in the gray sandstone of the Langa Astigiana hills - a lunar and overwhelming landscape, unique in this wine region. Herds of goats whose milk produces Robiola di Roccaverano DOP graze these slopes.

activities.text: Hiking or cycling in the Bormida di Spigno valley, on trails crossing fields and vineyards. Canoeing on the Bormida for the more athletic, possible depending on water levels. In August, the Historia Montis Baudonis historical reenactment and classical music festival animate the village.

visits.text: Volpedo is the birthplace of Giuseppe Pellizza (1868-1907), divisionist painter whose work Il Quarto Stato - The Fourth Estate - became the worldwide icon of twentieth-century workers' struggle. Pellizza spent ten years on this monumental canvas (5.45m × 2.93m), completed between 1898 and 1901: he drew inspiration from his native village's Piazza Malaspina as the backdrop for a workers' strike. Bernardo Bertolucci chose this painting for the poster of Novecento (1978). The painting is now preserved at Milan's Museo del Novecento.

The Pellizza museum circuit comprises three sites: the painter's studio (via Rosano, donated to the town hall by his daughters in 1966), the didactic museum presenting preparatory studies for major works (including three large versions of the Fourth Estate), and the Romanesque parish church of San Pietro, built in the tenth century and remodeled in the fifteenth, which holds a painting of San Luigino painted by Pellizza in 1894. Marked trails connect the three sites.

The Romanesque Pieve of San Pietro, whose square bell tower has marked Volpedo's horizon since the tenth century, houses Renaissance frescoes in its side chapels. The historic village nestles on a small hill on the right bank of the Grue torrent, around which the medieval castrum walls were built - some remnants survive at the village edge.

activities.text: Walk through the rolling countryside of the Colli Tortonesi, production zone of Timorasso - a local white grape variety revived in the 1990s after nearly disappearing - and Barbera d'Asti. The vineyards extend to the hamlet of Vho (frazione of Tortona), 10km away.

map_keywords: Chiesa Santi Quirico e Giulitta Cella Monte
Ecomuseo della Pietra da Cantoni Cella Monte

visits.text: Cella Monte is mentioned as early as 1116 in a diploma of Emperor Henry V. Its name derives either from ancient wine cellars dug into the sandstone (cella in Latin), or from cells of small medieval monasteries that existed on these hills. Cella Monte's geological peculiarity is unique: 17 million years ago, the Monferrato was a sea, and house walls are literally built with marine sandstone containing fossils and shells still visible to the naked eye. This stone, called Pietra da Cantoni, has been the exclusive building material for all local construction since Antiquity.

The infernot are hypogean cellars carved entirely into this Pietra da Cantoni, without light or ventilation, by Monferrato peasants during winters since the late eighteenth century - the era when wine began to be bottled thanks to falling glass costs. These underground storage spaces maintain constant temperature and humidity ideal for conservation. Cella Monte sits in the central zone of the UNESCO Monferrato degli Infernot site, a component of the Piedmont Vineyard Landscapes inscribed in 2014. The Ecomuseum of Pietra da Cantoni, installed in Palazzo Volta (an extraordinary fifteenth-century loggia rediscovered during restoration), opens onto the history of this unique architecture.

The parish church of Santi Quirico e Giulitta, consecrated in 1633, preserves wall frescoes, canvases, and a precious polychrome marble altar. The Casaforte, a fourteenth-century fortified dwelling, and Palazzo Radicati - home of Pietro Secondo Radicati (1671-1729), bishop of Casale Monferrato - testify to the village's seigneurial past. In May, the Le Colline sono in Fiore event transforms facades into a flower competition.

activities.text: Guided visit of an infernot with a local producer, combined with tasting of Barbera d'Asti, Grignolino, and Freisa - the three main Monferrato wines. The first weekend of November, the White Truffle Fair of Valle Ghenza brings together producers and gourmets.

map_keywords: Abbazia Santa Maria di Vezzolano Albugnano

visits.text: Cocconato perches at 491 meters above the lower Astigiano, commanding valleys that extend to the Alps on clear days. Its exceptionally mild microclimate, due to protection from surrounding hills, allows palms, olive trees, and mimosas to flourish - hence the nineteenth-century nickname Riviera del Monferrato. The name likely derives from the Latin cum conatu, the effort required to climb to the summit. The counts Radicati family ruled Cocconato from the tenth to fifteenth centuries, granting it de facto quasi-autonomy.

The Torre dei Radicati, last remnant of the medieval castle rebuilt in the nineteenth century, served under Napoleon as an optical telegraph relay, then as a windmill - two successive functions engraved in the crenellated stone that make it the village symbol. The fifteenth-century Gothic Palazzo Comunale, with its elegant terracotta windows and ogival arcades, is one of the rare examples of civil Gothic style in Piedmont. A stone's throw away, the Pieve della Madonna della Neve (twelfth century), surrounded by vineyards, offers a striking panorama over the countryside.

Eight kilometers away, the abbey of Santa Maria di Vezzolano in Albugnano is a Romano-Gothic masterpiece whose legendary foundation is attributed to Charlemagne. The interior presents a carved fourteenth-century rood screen and a fresco cycle of exceptional artistic value - one of medieval Piedmont's most important monuments.

activities.text: Every last Sunday in September, the Palio degli Asini (donkey race) celebrates a tradition dating to the Middle Ages: legend has it that donkeys carried water to extinguish a fire at the castle. In June, the Cocco7 wine and food walk offers 15km through vineyards with seven tastings. Cocconato's Robiola - soft cow's milk cheese - and local prosciutto benefit from the village's particular microclimate.

to_next_leg.road_type: Canavese mountain roads then Val Soana

visits.text: Ingria, at 827 meters elevation in Val Soana, counts 46 permanent residents - but groups together some thirty hamlets scattered across the slopes, linked by ancient trails. This commune in Gran Paradiso National Park is one of Canavese's most isolated and best-preserved villages. The uncertain etymology suggests either the Lombard Ingrich, or the Latin ingredire, linked to the Roman bridge spanning the Soana torrent - true gateway to the valley. The local language, Valsoanin, is a variant of Arpitan (Franco-Provençal), still spoken in the hamlets.

The 1719 bell tower, standing opposite the parish church of San Giacomo, is the village symbol. The permanent outdoor exhibition Le case raccontano presents panels with old black-and-white photographs captioned in Arpitan and Italian, along the alleys. The most typical hamlets like Camprovardo (three- to four-story houses connected by exterior wooden staircases) and Pasturera (sixteenth-seventeenth century round-arch stone arcades) are discovered on foot.

activities.text: Hike to Punta Arbella or Monte Betassa from Ingria, in Gran Paradiso National Park - one of Italy's oldest parks (1922), created to protect the ibex after Carlo Felice's 1821 hunting ban. The Soana torrent is suitable for canoeing depending on water levels. In August, Cose d'altri tempi (Tchouse d'auti ten) is a Franco-Provençal festival with music, dance, and ancient crafts.

to_next_leg.road_type: alpine valley road then hill roads

visits.text: Rosazza is a village of about a hundred inhabitants in the upper Cervo valley (province of Biella), carved from local syenite. It owes its fame to Federico Rosazza Pistolet (1813-1899), Kingdom senator, Mazzinian philanthropist, and Grand Master of Biellese freemasonry. After the deaths of his wife and daughter, he devoted the last thirty years of his life to architecturally refounding his native village, in collaboration with painter-architect Giuseppe Maffei, according to an aesthetic blending Masonic symbolism and romantic neo-Gothic style. Today Rosazza is twinned with Rennes-le-Château in France.

Decipher the symbols carved into every building: compasses and squares, five-petaled roses (lodge emblem), hourglasses, five-pointed stars, and a swastika - a Neolithic cosmic symbol used by freemasons - adorn the facade of the parish church of Saints Peter and George, built between 1876 and 1880 on a former cemetery. Its interior ceiling is painted midnight blue and scattered with thousands of stars, like an invitation to spiritual elevation. The church connects to the village by a bridge decorated with crosses and five-pointed stars.

Federico Rosazza's neo-Gothic castle, completed in 1899 the year of his death, is a labyrinth of esoteric references with its false romantic ruins, high tower, and moss-covered walls - a composition seemingly extracted from a fantasized medieval tale. The monumental cemetery, accessible via a three-arch stone bridge adorned with hourglasses, houses the Rosazza family tombs in a setting of crypts and Masonic funerary symbolism. The Palazzo Comunale, where Rosazza held his lodge meetings, still houses period decor.

activities.text: From Rosazza, panoramic road to the Sanctuary of Oropa (one of Italy's most important Marian sanctuaries, at 891 meters) via a road built between 1889 and 1898 by Federico Rosazza himself, passing through a 367-meter gallery dug by hand. Hike to the alpine pastures and Alpe Desate.

visits.text: Rassa sits at the confluence of the Sorba and Gronda torrents in Valsesia, at 917 meters elevation. Since November 2024, it has joined the Borghi più belli d'Italia circuit - the first and only village in Vercelli province to receive this distinction. The village divides into five cantons each bearing its own name. Its territory forms a special nature protection zone. The region proudly carries the nickname La valle dei tremendi (the valley of the terrible ones) - a reputation built notably by the inhabitants' resistance to the troops of Fra Dolcino and his Dulcinian disciples, medieval heretics who took refuge in these valleys around 1304 and held out against the crusade of the bishop of Vercelli for two winters.

The parish church of Santa Croce, existing since 1596 and partially rebuilt in the nineteenth century after a fire, preserves eighteenth-century frescoes by Antonio Orgiazzi - an artist who also worked at the Sacro Monte of Varallo. The single-arch stone bridge over the Sorba is described by specialists as a jewel of alpine civil engineering. The Ecomuseum of Wood in Rassa is installed in the Brasei hydraulic sawmill, built in the seventeenth century and operating until the 1980s - last survivor of a series of sawmills that once paced the village economy. The Bottega del Patel, a small carpentry workshop with its period tools, completes the visit.

activities.text: Hike in Val Sorba and Val Gronda, both classified as special protection zones. The bike path linking Alpe di Mera to Sorbella and Rassa allows a cycling outing in an unaltered mountain landscape. Outside the village, an old lime kiln and a carbonera (wood carbonization oven) can be observed on the trail to rifugio Heidi Rassa.

map_keywords: Isola San Giulio Orta San Giulio
Basilica San Giulio Isola San Giulio
Via del Silenzio Isola San Giulio

visits.text: Orta San Giulio spreads across a wooded peninsula on the eastern shore of Lake Orta - the smallest of northern Italy's great lakes. Friedrich Nietzsche stayed here in 1882 and walked the hills arm-in-arm with Lou Andreas-Salomé; here he began conceiving Thus Spoke Zarathustra. Piazza Motta, closed to traffic and open to the lake, is bordered by the Palazzo della Comunità della Riviera (1582) whose first floor houses a baroque fresco representing the Virgin with Saints Francis and Giulio. The alleys that suddenly open onto the lake or interior courtyards form a preserved medieval labyrinth. The village ranks among the Borghi più belli d'Italia.

The island of San Giulio, 400 meters offshore (275m long, 140m wide), is reached by boat from Piazza Motta. Legend has it that the Greek missionary Giulio, wanting to build his hundredth church here in 390 AD, crossed the lake on his cloak, drove out the serpents and dragons infesting the island, and founded the edifice where he was buried. The Romanesque basilica of San Giulio, with foundations dating to the fourth century, houses salvaged ancient columns and medieval frescoes. The Benedictine abbey Mater Ecclesiae - a cloistered community - occupies most of the island. The perimeter path is marked by the Via del Silenzio and the Via della Meditazione.

activities.text: Navigate Lake Orta to reach San Giulio island (paid crossing, a few minutes). The regular navigation service (Navigazione Lago d'Orta) also connects Pella, Lagna, and San Filiberto on the opposite shore. Lake circuit by bike on the panoramic road. The sanctuary of Madonna del Sasso, perched west of the lake, offers a panorama over the entire body of water.

map_keywords: Sacro Monte di Orta Orta San Giulio
Chiesa Santa Maria Assunta Orta San Giulio
Palazzo della Comunità Orta San Giulio
Giardini Villa Motta Orta San Giulio

visits.text: The Sacro Monte of Orta, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2003, rises on the wooded hill dominating the village at 400 meters elevation. Commissioned by Capuchin friar architect Padre Cleto, the sanctuary was built between 1590 and 1760: it comprises 20 chapels decorated with frescoes and adorned with more than 900 life-size painted terracotta statues illustrating the life of Saint Francis of Assisi - from birth to canonization. It's the only alpine Sacro Monte entirely dedicated to a single saint. The church of San Nicolao at the summit, built in 1602-1607 modeled on the lower basilica of Assisi, houses a fourteenth-century wooden Pietà at the origin of local worship.

Back in the village, Via Bossi (n°11), the seventeenth-century Villa of the same name hides a coffered ceiling adorned with frescoes and a lakeside garden. The parish church of Santa Maria Assunta (fifteenth century, facade remodeled in 1940) presents a three-nave interior covered with seventeenth-eighteenth century frescoes. The Torre di Buccione, an isolated medieval tower in the woods on the eastern shore, is accessible on foot from the panoramic road. To the north, the Sanctuary of Madonna del Sasso (eighteenth century) dominates the wild western shore and offers a panorama over the entire body of water.

activities.text: Complete circuit of Lake Orta by car or bike (40km panoramic road). Swimming in the lake waters from Gozzano beach to the south. Garden enthusiasts can visit the villas and botanical gardens dotting the shores.

visits.text: Vogogna appears in a parchment dated 970 and preserves much older traces: a Celtic steatite mask from the third century BC (the Mascherone Celtico) and a Roman inscription commemorating the construction of the Ossola road in 196 BC are displayed in Palazzo Pretorio. Vogogna reached its apex in the fourteenth century: in 1348, Giovanni Visconti, bishop of Novara and lord of Milan, made it the administrative capital of Lower Ossola, rival to Domodossola for Upper Ossola.

Castello Visconteo, commissioned in 1348 by Giovanni Visconti, dominates the village with its semi-circular round tower. Its irregular plan follows the terrain's orography and its battlements still evoke the defensive role the duke assigned it to bar Swiss access to the lakes. In 1798, after three centuries under the Borromeo family, the castle passed to the municipality which used it as a prison until the nineteenth century. Restored from 1990, it now hosts permanent exhibitions on the Celts, Leonardo da Vinci, and the wolf's return.

Palazzo Pretorio (1348), with ogival arches on squat columns in the Lombard broletto tradition, was the seat of Lower Ossola government until 1819. It still preserves traces of original painted decoration, including the Visconti coat of arms on the facade. The mule trail Il respiro della storia starts from the castle, climbs to the hamlet of Genestredo with its oratory of San Martino, then to la Rocca (eleventh century) - a medieval watchtower from which the view over the Ossola valley and the Alps is striking.

activities.text: Hiking in Val Grande National Park - Italy's largest wilderness area, over 70,000 hectares without any permanent human infrastructure in the park's core. Marked trails from Vogogna cross the primeval forests. The Vogogna-Premosello geological trail, marked by the Park, descends to Premosello station. In November, the Montagna e Dintorni festival animates the valley's cultural life for ten days.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate to difficult for Val Grande core

seo.h1_title: Piedmont's Most Beautiful Villages
seo.intro_paragraph: Twenty days to traverse Piedmont in depth - from Garessio and its medieval gateways on the ancient salt road between Liguria and the plains, to Vogogna and its Visconti castle guarding the Ossola valley. Between them, a journey among villages officially ranked among Italy's most beautiful: Chianale with its Swiss pine forest, Ostana the Occitan alpine architecture laboratory, the UNESCO-inscribed hills of Barolo and Neive, the underground infernot of Cella Monte, the enigmatic Rosazza decorated with Masonic symbols, and Orta San Giulio facing its millennial island.
seo.seo_hook: Medieval villages, classified vineyards, and pristine alpine valleys - authentic Piedmont away from the crowds.
featured_list: Barolo: Castello Falletti and WiMu museum at the heart of the UNESCO Langhe
Orta San Giulio: mystical island and Sacro Monte on the lakeshore
Rosazza: Italy's most mysterious village, covered in Masonic symbols
Cella Monte: underground infernot carved from the Monferrato's marine stone
Ostana: worldwide model of contemporary Occitan alpine renaissance

places_taglines.IT::garessio: Where four medieval quarters converge on the ancient road between Piedmont and Liguria
places_taglines.IT::chianale: Stone-built Occitan village at 1,800 meters facing Monviso and ancient pine forests
places_taglines.IT::ostana: The mountain hamlet that wrote the playbook for alpine revival
places_taglines.IT::barolo: Where the king of Italian wines is born, beneath Castello Falletti's frescoed walls
places_taglines.IT::monforte-d-alba: A hilltop village cradling a natural amphitheater where jazz echoes off baroque stone
places_taglines.IT::guarene: Where an eighteenth-century castle surveys sixty kilometers of UNESCO vineyard country
places_taglines.IT::neive: Medieval stronghold of four great wines, built by minor nobles with major taste
places_taglines.IT::castagnole-delle-lanze: Where painted arcades celebrate Moscato and you can adopt your own vineyard row
places_taglines.IT::mombaldone: The last walled village of the Langa, standing defiant since the year 991
places_taglines.IT::volpedo: The piazza where Pellizza painted the world's most iconic vision of workers rising
places_taglines.IT::cella-monte: Where houses are built from seventeen-million-year-old seabed stone
places_taglines.IT::cocconato: The Riviera of Monferrato, where palms flourish and Gothic towers crown the hills
places_taglines.IT::ingria: Thirty Arpitan hamlets scattered across Gran Paradiso's wilderness
places_taglines.IT::rosazza: Where every stone whispers Masonic secrets beneath a starlit ceiling
places_taglines.IT::rassa: First and only Borgo in Vercelli province, carved from the valley of the terrible ones
places_taglines.IT::orta-san-giulio: Where Nietzsche dreamed of Zarathustra on medieval streets facing a sacred island
places_taglines.IT::vogogna: Medieval capital of lower Ossola, gateway to Italy's greatest wilderness

highlights: Harvest season in September-October in Langhe and Monferrato
White Truffle Fair of Alba in October-November
Alevè Forest (Chianale) accessible June through September
Monfortinjazz Festival at Auditorium Horszowski in July-August

itineraries.title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast
itineraries.subtitle: From Cori's Roman temples to Sperlonga's hidden coves—a journey through hilltop villages, Cistercian abbeys, and the shores of Ulysses' Riviera

map_keywords: Museo della Città e del Territorio Cori
Santuario della Madonna del Soccorso Cori

visits.text: The Temple of Hercules, built between 89 and 80 BC during Sulla's dictatorship, commands Cori's acropolis at 398 meters above sea level. Its Doric tetrastyle façade preserves eight intact columns with their entablature; on the door's architrave, the names of the two commissioning magistrates—Marcus Maglius and Lucius Turpilius—remain legible after more than 2,000 years. Originally covered in colored stucco, the structure so impressed Piranesi that he dedicated an entire series of 18th-century engravings to it. Raphael himself is said to have come to measure it when appointed architect of St. Peter's Basilica. The Church of San Pietro, built within the temple's cella, was destroyed by Allied bombing in 1944.

Of the Temple of the Dioscuri, constructed between the 4th and 2nd centuries BC and restored in Corinthian style around 100 BC, two columns of approximately 9 meters tall remain, still bearing a fragment of architrave. An inscription reveals the names of the magistrates who financed the restoration using the temple's treasury. The cella once housed colossal statues of Castor and Pollux in Parian marble, measuring 1.90 meters tall, depicted armed and holding the reins of 1.70-meter horses. The fragments are now displayed in the Museum of the City and Territory.

The Oratory of the Santissima Annunziata, a national monument built around 1400 commissioned by Spanish Cardinal Pedro Fernández de Frías, is nicknamed "the Scrovegni Chapel of southern Lazio." This small rectangle measuring 8 by 4 meters, vaulted with a pointed barrel vault, is entirely covered in late Gothic frescoes depicting scenes from the Old and New Testaments. On the façade, Cori's oldest coat of arms (circa 1440) sits beside the cardinal's inscription requesting a Pater Noster for his soul.

Step inside the Church of Sant'Oliva, built on the podium of a 3rd-1st century BC Roman temple, three columns of which are still visible in the nave. The complex blends Roman temple, 12th-century medieval basilica, 15th-century Augustinian convent, and the Cappella del Crocifisso (1467-1480) with 16th-century frescoes depicting stories from the Old and New Testaments. The Renaissance cloister, the work of Antonio da Como, features 27 different marble capitals. In the Middle Ages, this is where magistrates swore their oath of fidelity to the communal statutes.

The Museum of the City and Territory, inaugurated in 2000 in the former convent of Sant'Oliva, traces Cori's history from the Bronze Age to modern times. It houses a copy of the Capitoline Minerva from the Temple of the Dioscuri—the original is in Rome—gifted by the city of Rome in 1999, as well as a 1:75 scale model of the Temple of the Dioscuri reconstructing its original appearance.

activities.text: Stroll between Cori Monte and Cori Valle, the two distinct quarters separated by an olive grove: the upper borough climbs in a triangle from the Temple of Hercules (397 m), while the lower settlement centers around Santa Maria della Pietà (270-300 m). The medieval lanes, often covered by vaults, preserve 14th and 15th-century houses and a fountain where, according to tradition, Frederick Barbarossa bathed.

Climb to the Sanctuary of Madonna del Soccorso, whose esplanade offers sweeping views of the Lepini Mountains and the Pontine Plain stretching to the sea. The sanctuary commemorates the Virgin's appearance in Cori in 1521, celebrated each year in late June with the Carosello Storico dei Rioni, a historical reenactment in early 16th-century costume.

Pass through Piazza Pozzo Dorico, an ancient underground Roman cistern transformed into a silent, secret little square nestled among the alleyways, then continue along Via delle Colonne where vestiges of the ancient forum emerge between the houses.

map_keywords: Cattedrale di Santa Maria Assunta Sermoneta

visits.text: Castello Caetani, perched at Sermoneta's highest point at 237 meters altitude, was founded in the 13th century by the Annibaldi family with a 42-meter-tall keep (Maschio) and a counter-tower called Maschietto. In 1297, Pietro Caetani, nephew of Pope Boniface VIII, purchased the castle for 140,000 gold florins. The Caetani family added the Hall of the Barons in the 15th century—22 meters long with four bays separated by pointed arches—and the Camere Pinte, frescoed by an artist from Pinturicchio's school. In 1499, Pope Alexander VI Borgia excommunicated the Caetani and entrusted the castle to Lucrezia Borgia; Antonio da Sangallo then reinforced the walls. Charles V was received here on April 2, 1536, with 1,000 horsemen and 4,000 infantrymen. The guided tour (approximately 1 hour) passes through the prisons where detainees' graffiti still survive.

The Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta, built in the 13th century on the remains of a Roman temple dedicated to Cybele, preserves Quattrocento frescoes attributed to the school of Benozzo Gozzoli, remarkable for their vivid colors and meticulous detail. The Romanesque bell tower dominates the village's narrow streets.

The Loggia dei Mercanti, an arcade in the historic center, served as a filming location for "Non ci resta che piangere" with Roberto Benigni and Massimo Troisi. The nearby belvedere offers views across the Pontine Plain to the Circeo Promontory; a plaque commemorates Sermoneta's participation in the Battle of Lepanto in 1571, the victory over the Ottoman fleet still celebrated every second Sunday of October.

activities.text: Visit the Garden of Ninfa, an 8-hectare English garden created starting in 1921 by Gelasio Caetani on the ruins of the medieval city of Ninfa, destroyed in 1381. The town then had 1,300 inhabitants, seven churches, two monasteries, and a double ring of walls. Pope Alexander III was crowned here in 1159 in the church of Santa Maria Maggiore, whose ruins still stand. Three generations of Caetani women—Ada, Marguerite Chapin, and Lelia—planted more than 1,300 species from every continent. The New York Times called it "the most beautiful garden in the world." Declared a Natural Monument in 2000.

practical_info.booking: Advance booking required; open only on selected weekends from March to November

activities.text: Descend to Valvisciolo Abbey, founded in the 8th century by Greek Basilian monks and occupied by the Templars between the 12th and 14th centuries. In the stone cloister, you can spot a Templar cross carved near the central oculus of the rose window and the mysterious SATOR square, a Latin palindrome adopted by the Templars. According to legend, when Jacques de Molay, the last Grand Master, was burned in 1314, the architraves of Templar churches cracked—a fissure is still visible on the main portal.
Wander through Sermoneta's alleyways, passing artisan shops and the Church of San Giuseppe, built in 1525 by the Caetani family, Sermoneta's patron.

map_keywords: Cattedrale di Santa Maria Annunziata Priverno
Chiesa di San Giovanni Evangelista Priverno

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santa Maria Annunziata, reached by a broad staircase dominating the main square, is Priverno's mother church. It preserves elements of medieval architecture remodeled over the centuries. Several artists from Priverno worked here, including the painter Pietro Coleberti, active in the 15th century and author of frescoes throughout the region.

The Church of San Giovanni Evangelista preserves frescoes dating from the 13th to 15th centuries. The church testifies to Priverno's artistic activity during the Middle Ages, when the town supplied master builders to the region's major Cistercian construction projects.

The Archaeological Museum of Priverno, housed in the municipal palace, traces the territory's history from the first Volscian settlements to the Roman era through a collection of ceramics, inscriptions, and everyday objects from the excavations of Privernum.

The Archaeological Park of Privernum, a few kilometers from the village, lets you walk among the vestiges of the Roman city founded after the conquest of the Volscians. You can make out the mosaics of patrician domus, the bases of a theater, and the remains of a thermal complex, evidence of a prosperous city along the Via Appia.

activities.text: Visit Fossanova Abbey, 5.5 km south of Priverno. Founded in 1135 when Pope Innocent II ceded a Benedictine monastery to the Cistercians of Saint Bernard of Clairvaux, the new church was consecrated in 1208 by Innocent III. Built entirely of travertine, it represents the earliest example of Cistercian Gothic art in Italy. The central nave, with seven bays covered by ribbed vaults, is stripped of all decoration according to the austere Cistercian rule. The cloister has three Romanesque sides and a fourth Gothic side added in the 13th century by Roman marmorari. It was in the abbot's house that Saint Thomas Aquinas died on March 7, 1274, struck down by fever en route to the Council of Lyon; his cell is now a visitable chapel.

practical_info.booking: Free entry; guided tours by reservation

activities.text: Stroll through Priverno's alleyways, a village perched on a slope of the Lepini Mountains. The center preserves its medieval layout with narrow lanes, vaulted passages, and limestone palaces. The Svicolando festival brings the streets to life each summer with performances and art installations.

visits.text: Castello Baronale Caetani, a rare example of a fortress built on flat ground, rises on Fondi's perimeter walls. Its base is a parallelepiped mastio in opus quadratum dating from the late 12th century, completed in the 15th century by the Caetani with an imposing cylindrical tower and machicolated crenellations. The grand rocca has three round bastions. A passetto—a covered walkway reconstructed in wood and steel on the original model—connects the castle to the neighboring Palazzo Caetani, offering a bird's-eye view of the town and countryside. The castle houses the Civic Museum with artifacts from protohistoric to medieval times.

The Cathedral of San Pietro, the historic center's cathedral, was erected on the site of an ancient temple dedicated to Jupiter. Fondi is linked to the Western Schism: in the 14th century, a group of dissident cardinals elected the antipope Clement VII here, dividing Christendom for decades.

The Museum of Medieval Judaism, housed in the former synagogue called "Casa degli Spiriti" in the La Giudea quarter, traces the Jewish presence in Fondi over nearly a thousand years, from the 5th to the 16th century. The community included dyers, fabric makers, merchants, and moneylenders active in Fondi's economy. After the expulsion decrees of 1541 under Charles V and the malaria epidemic of 1633, the inhabitants dispersed. The museum occupies five rooms on two levels in the restored premises of the former synagogue.

activities.text: Walk through the La Giudea quarter, the former Jewish ghetto located in the northeast corner of the historic center. Its uniqueness lies in being integrated into the urban fabric rather than isolated like in most European cities. The low houses, on two floors, connect to one another: the ground floor served as shop or workshop, the upper floor as dwelling. The exterior staircases of the houses converge toward the square of the former synagogue.

Stroll around Lago di Fondi, the largest of the three lakes surrounding the town (along with Lago Lungo and Lago di San Puoto). The plain of Fondi, enclosed in a semicircle by the Ausoni Mountains, is one of the main market gardening centers of southern Lazio.

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Itri

visits.text: Monte San Biagio, a small village with labyrinthine alleyways climbing a hill between the Ausoni Mountains. The Church of San Giovanni Battista dominates the village. From Viale Littoria below, the panorama embraces the plain of Fondi, the three lakes, and the coast stretching to the Pontine Islands on clear days.

Castello di Itri, perched on Colle Sant'Angelo, has controlled the Via Appia since the 9th century. The first nucleus—a pentagonal tower—was erected in 882 by Duke Docibilis I of Gaeta. His grandson Marino I added around 950 a second tower, taller and wider. In the 13th century, the Caetani built the residential section and the cylindrical tower called the "Tower of the Crocodile": according to legend, prisoners condemned to death were thrown in as prey to a reptile kept in its waters. The walkway connects the towers and offers views to the coast.

The Church of San Michele Arcangelo, in Itri's upper section, is the village's oldest sacred building, dating back to the 11th century. In Arab-Norman style with three naves, it preserves 14th-century frescoes attributed to the Neapolitan Roberto Oderisio. Its square bell tower, decorated with colored majolica plates, is positioned centrally against the façade.

The Museum of Brigandage recounts the life of Michele Pezza, known as Fra' Diavolo, born in Itri on April 7, 1771. Initially wanted for murder, he became a colonel in the Neapolitan army against Napoleon's troops, using the castle's underground passages to ambush enemies on the Via Appia. Captured in 1806, he was hanged in Naples' Piazza del Mercato at age 35. Auber's opera "Fra Diavolo" (1830) and the Laurel and Hardy film (1933) made his name famous worldwide.

activities.text: Climb to the Sanctuary of Madonna della Civita, at 673 meters altitude on Monte Fusco, 13 km from the village in the Aurunci Mountains Park. According to tradition, two Basilian monks fleeing iconoclasm hid an icon of the Virgin and Child from Constantinople; a deaf-mute shepherd found it on the mountain and recovered his hearing and speech. The first historical document of the sanctuary dates from 1174. The icon, called Madonna Nera, was crowned in 1777 and 1877. From the forecourt, spectacular views of the mountains and the Gulf of Gaeta.

practical_info.distance: 13 km round trip by car + walk

activities.text: Hike along the Via Appia Antica: in the Gola di Sant'Andrea, between Itri and Fondi, a 2-km section of the ancient route has been excavated and enhanced. The Roman basalt paving winds between opus quadratum constructions and polygonal walls. On the ruins of a 1st-century BC Roman villa stands a fortino used by Fra' Diavolo as a rear base against the French in 1798.

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Campodimele

visits.text: Campodimele, perched at 647 meters between the Ausoni and Aurunci Mountains, holds the European title of "Village of Eternal Youth." In 2000, it counted 14 centenarians among 660 inhabitants. In 1985, the World Health Organization sent researchers on site: they discovered a cholesterol rate half the Italian average and very low blood pressure. A 2012 study by La Sapienza University in Rome identified a mutation of the ANGPTL3 gene that protects against cholesterol and diabetes. The village is accessible only on foot via its cobblestone lanes that wind between stone houses, covered passages, and small gardens.

The Church of San Michele Arcangelo, at the village's highest point, dates from the 11th century and was built on a former pagan worship site. Damaged by an earthquake, it was faithfully rebuilt. Inside, a tabernacle, ancient paintings, and wooden statues merit attention.

The Monastery of Sant'Onofrio, founded in the 11th century, suffered several destructions over the centuries before being restored in the 1980s. Its position below the fortified enclosure offers a viewpoint over the fertile valley below.

activities.text: Walk the medieval ramparts: the fortified enclosure, erected to protect inhabitants from Saracen raids, comprises twelve recently restored cylindrical towers. A path known to locals as the "Lovers' Walk" (Passeggiata dell'Amore) circles around it, offering panoramic views of the green valleys and Aurunci Mountains. On the Municipio square, an elm planted in 1789 allegedly commemorates the French Revolution.

Tasting of local products: the secret of longevity also lies in diet—little red meat, plenty of fruits, vegetables, and legumes from the garden, olive oil from the surrounding terraces. The village is part of "The Most Beautiful Villages in Italy" and has received the orange flag from the Italian Touring Club.

visits.text: Sperlonga's historic center clings its whitewashed houses to a rocky promontory between two sandy beaches. The name derives from the Latin spelunca (cave), referring to the natural cavities dotting the coast. The alleyways, flowering with bougainvillea, spiral upward to the rock's summit where you can embrace the Gulf of Gaeta on one side and Cape Circeo on the other.

Torre Truglia, a 16th-century watchtower planted on a rocky spur between the two beaches, is Sperlonga's symbol. Destroyed and rebuilt several times after Barbary raids, it surveys the horizon 360 degrees.

The Villa of Tiberius and its National Archaeological Museum: discovered in 1957 during construction of the coastal road, this imperial residence extended 300 meters along the beach. Its natural grotto, converted into a banquet hall, housed five monumental marble sculptural groups in Hellenistic style (circa 180 BC) narrating Ulysses' adventures—the blinding of Polyphemus, the attack of Scylla, the theft of the Palladium. The fragments bear the signatures of sculptors Agesander, Athenodorus, and Polydorus of Rhodes, the same authors of the Laocoön in the Vatican Museums. In 26 AD, a collapse during a banquet killed three slaves; Sejanus shielded the emperor with his body, who then left Sperlonga for Capri. The museum, built in 1963, displays reconstructions of the groups from 15,000 fragments.

activities.text: Swim on the white sandy beaches of the Riviera of Ulysses, within the eponymous Regional Park protecting 434 hectares including more than 100 of marine surface. The coves between the cliffs offer crystalline waters.
Boat excursion along the coast toward Gaeta, passing the cliffs and sea caves. The navigation allows you to see the Montagna Spaccata and Monte Orlando from the sea.

visits.text: Medieval Gaeta unfolds at the foot of the Castello Angioino-Aragonese, an imposing fortress dominating the town and gulf. The medieval quarter preserves a tight network of alleyways, stairways, and vaulted passages descending toward the port.

Via Indipendenza, the main artery of the former comune of Elena (Gaeta's lower section), lines up palaces and shops. Adjacent alleyways plunge toward the fishing port where maritime tradition endures.

The Church of Santissima Annunziata, in the historic center, is a jewel of Gothic architecture whose gilded 15th-century façade is one of southern Lazio's most photographed. The interior houses a Chapel of the Immaculate Conception covered in gold, nicknamed "Grotta d'Oro" (golden cave) for its extraordinary Renaissance decoration.

The Sanctuary of Montagna Spaccata is built in a spectacular fissure of Monte Orlando. According to tradition, the mountain split at Christ's death. You descend 35 steps carved into the rock to the Grotta del Turco, a cavity open to the sea where Saracen ships found refuge in the time of the Duchy of Gaeta. On the wall, a "Hand of the Turk"—a natural imprint in the rock—fueled local legends for centuries.

activities.text: Hike in the Parco di Monte Orlando, a nature reserve on Gaeta's promontory. Panoramic trails pass Bourbon fortifications (Polveriera Carolina), remains of Roman villas, and cliffs plunging into the sea where peregrine falcons nest.
Swim at Spiaggia di Serapo, a large fine-sand beach in the town center, one of the most famous on the southern Lazio coast. The sandy arc stretches in a crescent at the foot of Monte Orlando.

Detour to Castellone, Formia's medieval quarter, with its octagonal tower dominating the alleyways and the Cisternone Romano, a vast underground cistern from the 1st century BC that supplied water to the ancient city of Formiae.

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Pietro Apostolo Minturno

visits.text: The Archaeological Complex of Minturnae, on the Via Appia at km 156, preserves the vestiges of the Roman port-city founded in 295 BC on the ruins of an Auruncian town. The 1st-century AD Roman theater, built in opus reticulatum, accommodated more than 4,000 spectators; its restored cavea still serves as a setting for summer performances since 1960. Beneath the tiers, the Antiquarium displays statues, ex-votos, epigraphs, and coins recovered from the Garigliano River. An original section of the Via Appia, paved with basalt blocks, crosses the site. It was at Minturnae that General Gaius Marius, fleeing Sulla's assassins, hid in the marshes before being discovered—an episode recounted by Plutarch.

The Church of San Pietro Apostolo, the main church of Minturno's hilltop village, stands at the heart of the medieval historic center. Some of its marbles come directly from the ancient site of Minturnae, reused over the centuries in local constructions.

The Castello Baronale dominates the historic center from an elevated position. The village of Minturno was called Traetto until 1879, a name inherited from the Middle Ages when inhabitants of the ancient city, driven to the heights by the Lombards around 580 and by the swamping of the plain, rebuilt their village on the hill.

activities.text: Walk through Minturno's historic center (formerly Traetto), a hilltop village with picturesque alleyways and views over the Garigliano valley. The Memory Trail passes commemorative plaques recalling World War II events; near the archaeological site stands the British Commonwealth War Cemetery, where 2,049 soldiers who fell in 1943-1944 rest.

Pass by the Ponte Real Ferdinando over the Garigliano, Italy's first iron suspension bridge, designed by engineer Luigi Giura and inaugurated in 1832 by King Ferdinand II of the Two Sicilies. The bridge connected the Papal States to the Kingdom of Naples. Destroyed during World War II, it was restored in 1998 and can be visited.

seo.h1_title: Latina Province Circuit: Lepini Mountains and Coast
seo.intro_paragraph: Discover Cori and its Roman temples clinging to the Lepini Mountains, then descend to Sermoneta where the Caetani castle watches over the Garden of Ninfa. Cross through Priverno and its Cistercian abbey of Fossanova, reach Fondi and its thousand-year-old Jewish quarter. Pass through Itri, land of Fra' Diavolo, before arriving at Campodimele, the village of centenarians. The coast opens up at Sperlonga, white as the Cyclades, and continues to Gaeta and its split mountain, then Minturno and the vestiges of the Roman city of Minturnae. Southern Lazio alternates sea, stone, and open horizons.
seo.seo_hook: Where Roman temples grip mountainsides, Cistercian abbeys emerge from morning mist, Caetani fortresses overlook the Pontine Plain, and imperial caves face the Mediterranean—southern Lazio unfolds nine centuries of history between mountain and shore.
featured_list: Sermoneta: 13th-century Caetani castle commanding the Pontine Plain
Fossanova: Italy's oldest Cistercian Gothic abbey
Sperlonga: Tiberius' grotto and Hellenistic sculptures facing the sea
Gaeta: Split mountain and sanctuary carved into rock

places_taglines.IT::cori: Where two Roman temples have defied the centuries on the Lepini peaks
places_taglines.IT::sermoneta: Medieval village crowned by the Caetani fortress and Ninfa's gardens
places_taglines.IT::priverno: Hilltop town in the Lepini Mountains, gateway to Fossanova Abbey
places_taglines.IT::fondi: Fortified city on the plain sheltering a rare intact medieval Jewish quarter
places_taglines.IT::itri: Village at the gates of the Via Appia, homeland of the bandit Fra' Diavolo
places_taglines.IT::campodimele: Where the secret of long life hides in mountain air and ancient walls
places_taglines.IT::sperlonga: White village on a rocky promontory guarding Emperor Tiberius' cave
places_taglines.IT::gaeta: Fortified port of the gulf where legend says the mountain split in two
places_taglines.IT::minturno: Village overlooking the vestiges of ancient Minturnae and the Garigliano River

highlights: Temple of Hercules in Cori: Doric façade intact since the 1st century BC
Caetani Castle of Sermoneta and Garden of Ninfa, among the world's most beautiful
Fossanova Abbey, archetype of Cistercian Gothic in Italy
Villa of Tiberius in Sperlonga, original sculptures by the creators of the Laocoön

itineraries.title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani
itineraries.subtitle: From Bracciano's Renaissance castle to the volcanic lakes of the Castelli Romani, ten days through hilltop villages, noble villas, and ancient sanctuaries in the Roman countryside
specialties: Porchetta d'Ariccia IGP – whole roasted pig stuffed with rosemary and garlic
Fragoline di Nemi – wild strawberries cultivated since the 15th century
Frascati DOC – white wines from Malvasia and Trebbiano on volcanic soils
Zagarolo DOC – small-production white wines from the Prenestine hills
Pecorino Romano – aged sheep's milk cheese from Lazio shepherding traditions

visits.text: Castello Orsini-Odescalchi — A Renaissance fortress begun in 1470 under Napoleone Orsini's commission, likely built by the very masons working simultaneously on the Sistine Chapel. Napoleone's brother, Cardinal Latino Orsini, served as chamberlain to Pope Sixtus IV. Construction was completed in 1495 under his son Gentil Virginio's supervision. The complex incorporates five towers on five corners and three concentric defensive walls. In 1558, Duke Paolo Giordano Orsini married Isabella de' Medici and converted the fortress into a ducal palace, bringing in the Zuccari brothers to fresco several rooms. When the Orsini line died out in 1696, the castle passed to the Odescalchi family, nephews of Pope Innocent XI, who remain the owners today. The museum route covers twenty rooms displaying weapons collections, period furniture, and paintings spanning the 15th to 19th centuries.

Anguillara Sabazia — A lakeside village perched on a promontory of the Sabatini Mountains overlooking Lake Bracciano, with the 16th-century Collegiata dell'Assunzione and medieval lanes clinging to the hillside. From the belvedere in the central piazza, you look straight across the lake to the wooded shores of Parco Regionale di Bracciano-Martignano, designated a protected natural area in 1999.

Trevignano Romano — A small fishing village on the lake's northern shore, with a parish church built on the ruins of a medieval castrum. The main piazza, perched above the water, offers one of the most sweeping panoramas of Lake Bracciano's 56.5 km² expanse. These waters have supplied Rome since the 17th century via the Acquedotto Paolo.

activities.text: Swimming on the shores of Lake Bracciano — A volcanic crater lake 160 meters deep, ranked among the cleanest lakes in central Italy. The best access points are at Trevignano and Anguillara, where you'll find pebble beaches and picnic areas within the natural park.

map_keywords: Chiesa del Santissimo Nome di Gesù Calcata

visits.text: Monterano Antica — A ghost town abandoned after a malaria epidemic in the late 18th century. The ruins include a partially standing baroque church and an ornate fountain featuring a lion, attributed to Bernini's circle. The site sits within the Riserva Naturale Monterano, where collapsed houses disappear beneath advancing vegetation. The gradual abandonment accelerated after 1799 when French troops ravaged the area.

Calcata Vecchia — A medieval village perched on a red tuff spur in the Treja valley, declared uninhabitable by fascist decree in 1935 due to the risk of the cliff collapsing. Its residents were relocated to Calcata Nuova, built 2 km away. In the 1960s-70s, artists, intellectuals, and hippies from across Europe and America recolonized the abandoned houses, transforming the village into a creative community that thrives to this day. The 15th-century Palazzo Baronale degli Anguillara now houses the visitor center for Parco Valle del Treja. The small Chiesa del Santissimo Nome di Gesù, built in the 14th century and restored in 1793, preserves a reliquary in a 15th-century tabernacle. Local legend claims that the Holy Prepuce, stolen during the 1527 Sack of Rome, was kept here until the 1980s.

activities.text: Hiking in the Parco Regionale Valle del Treja — The Treja River, a tributary of the Tiber, has carved gorges through volcanic tuff for several kilometers. Trail 015 leads to the ruins of the ancient Faliscan city of Narce on the rocky spur opposite Calcata, with a necropolis dating to the 8th century BC. The Cascate di Monte Gelato, a series of small waterfalls and pools formed by the river, lie 5 km away.

Visiting artists' workshops in Calcata — The caves and cellars carved into the tuff house workshops for ceramics, sculpture, jewelry-making, and furniture restoration. Several artists welcome visitors into their working spaces, continuing the tradition that repopulated the village since the 1960s.

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Nicola di Bari Nerola
Parco Naturale Regionale dei Monti Lucretili

visits.text: Castello Orsini di Nerola — A medieval fortress overlooking the village from a hill in the Monti Lucretili, held by the powerful Orsini family from the 13th century. The castle witnessed the 1483 birth of Felice della Rovere, illegitimate daughter of Cardinal Giuliano della Rovere, the future Pope Julius II. Felice became a remarkable historical figure in her own right, serving as governor of the State of Bracciano and emerging as one of the most influential women in early 16th-century Rome.

Centro storico di Nerola — A compact village with medieval lanes, the Chiesa di San Nicola di Bari featuring a 13th-century Romanesque portal and bell tower, and several 16th-17th century noble palaces. The village preserves its medieval radial plan, with houses clustered tightly around the castle.

activities.text: Views over the Monti Lucretili from Nerola's heights — The vista stretches across the wooded ridges of Sabina, the Aniene valley, and on clear days, all the way to Rome. The Parco Naturale Regionale dei Monti Lucretili extends immediately to the north, its slopes cloaked in oak and hornbeam forests.

map_keywords: Parco Naturale Regionale dei Monti Simbruini

visits.text: Castello Massimo di Arsoli — A medieval fortress with origins tracing back to the 11th century, transformed into a noble residence in the 16th-17th centuries by the Massimo family, one of Rome's most ancient patrician houses, who claim descent from Fabius Maximus. The castle retains terraced gardens with centuries-old trees and a Renaissance loggia overlooking the Aniene valley. Arsoli sits at 473 m altitude on the eastern foothills of the Monti Simbruini.

Centro storico di Arsoli — A medieval village with cobbled lanes, the Chiesa di Santa Croce, and vestiges of fortifications. The village occupies a strategic position on the ancient Via Valeria, the Roman road linking Rome to the Adriatic via the Abruzzo.

activities.text: Hiking in the Monti Simbruini — The Parco Naturale Regionale dei Monti Simbruini is Lazio's largest natural park, covering 30,000 hectares of forests, high-altitude pastures, and springs. The Aniene River rises in this massif. Marked trails depart from Arsoli toward the wooded plateaus.

visits.text: Guadagnolo — Lazio's highest village at 1,218 m altitude in the Monti Prenestini, reached via a winding road from Capranica Prenestina. On clear days, the panoramic belvedere sweeps across a 180° arc covering Rome, the Tyrrhenian Sea, and the Apennines. The village's permanent population barely exceeds twenty residents.

Castel San Pietro Romano — A tiny village at 763 m overlooking Palestrina from the summit of Monte Ginestro, listed among the Borghi più Belli d'Italia. Medieval houses step along the rocky ridge, incorporating vestiges of ancient Praeneste's acropolis, with polygonal walls in opus cyclopeum identical to those of the sanctuary below.

Santuario della Fortuna Primigenia — The largest religious complex in Republican Roman Italy, built in the late 2nd century BC across seven cascading terraces on Monte Ginestro's flank. The sanctuary dedicated to Fortuna Primigenia—'Jupiter's firstborn' and oracular goddess—was consulted throughout the Empire. The oracle functioned through the drawing of wooden lots (sortes), and Cicero reported that Romans considered no site more suited to divination. The structure employed Roman concrete made with pozzolana on a grand scale for the first time, its durability proven by its preservation two millennia later. In 1944, Mussolini demolished several hundred medieval houses obstructing the complex, revealing the terraces in their current state.

Museo Archeologico Nazionale Prenestino and the Nile Mosaic — The museum, installed in Palazzo Barberini built directly atop the ancient structures, preserves on its third floor the Nile Mosaic, a Hellenistic masterpiece measuring 5.85 m × 4.31 m. Created in the 1st century BC by Alexandrian craftsmen, the mosaic depicts the Nile's course from the Ethiopian sources to the delta, populated with hippos, crocodiles, pygmy hunters, and a walled city flanked by four colossal statues. Discovered in 1507 still in place within the sanctuary, it was cut into 27 pieces in 1625 by Palestrina's bishop, transported to Rome, then returned to Palestrina by Cardinal Francesco Barberini in 1640 after several restorations. It has been displayed here since 1956.

activities.text: Walking through Palestrina's historic center — The medieval town developed directly atop the sanctuary's ramps and terraces, such that some cellars and basements of houses still reveal Roman masonry in opus incertum. The Cattedrale di Sant'Agapito, dedicated to the 2nd-century local martyr, preserves a paleo-Christian crypt.

map_keywords: Collegiata di San Lorenzo e Santa Maria Maddalena Zagarolo

visits.text: Palazzo Rospigliosi di Zagarolo — A 16th-century noble palace built by the Colonna family on the remains of a medieval citadel, acquired in 1629 by Camillo Rospigliosi, whose nephew became Pope Clement IX (1667-1669). The complex includes a palatine church and terraced gardens. The Porta di San Martino, a medieval tuff arch with crenellations, marks the entrance to the historic center.

Centro storico di Zagarolo — A medieval village on wooded hills with sloping lanes, several 16th-17th century noble palaces, and the 17th-century Collegiata di San Lorenzo e Santa Maria Maddalena. Zagarolo was a stop on the ancient Via Praenestina, the Roman road departing from Porta Praenestina in Rome.

activities.text: Tasting Zagarolo DOC wines — The Zagarolo wine appellation, one of Lazio's smallest, produces whites based on Malvasia and Trebbiano cultivated on the volcanic soils of the hills. Local cellars sell directly from the production.

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria del Popolo Nemi

visits.text: Lanuvio — A hilltop village at 324 m in the southern Colli Albani, with a 17th-century Collegiata dell'Assunzione and the vestiges of an ancient sanctuary dedicated to Juno Sospita. This cult was so important that newly elected Roman consuls traveled to Lanuvio to perform a sacrifice before taking office. 20th-century excavations unearthed ex-votos and coins spanning several centuries of devotion.

Nemi — A picturesque village at 521 m overlooking the crater lake from a rocky ridge, famous since antiquity for the sanctuary of Diana Nemorensis, whose priests obtained their position by killing their predecessor in single combat—a ritual documented by Strabo and Suetonius that inspired James Frazer's comparative anthropology of religions. The historic center preserves medieval lanes and the 15th-century Chiesa di Santa Maria del Popolo.

Museo delle Navi Romane di Nemi — A museum built between 1933 and 1939 specifically to house two ceremonial galleys commissioned by Caligula (37-41 AD) for Lake Nemi, measuring respectively 71.30 × 20 m and 73 × 24 m—extraordinary dimensions for lake vessels. Extracted from the lakebed in 1929-1931 after partial drainage of the lake, both original ships were entirely destroyed by fire on the night of May 31-June 1, 1944, under circumstances still debated (retreating German troops or Allied shells). The museum now displays 1:5 scale models, recovered fragments, anchors, lead pipes bearing Caligula's initials, and a partial reconstruction of the stern.

activities.text: Walking around Lake Nemi — A small volcanic lake of 1.67 km² and 33 m depth, surrounded by oak and chestnut forests. The ring road passes the old wild strawberry farms (fragoline di Nemi), cultivated since the 15th century and celebrated annually at the Sagra delle Fragole in early June.

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria dell'Assunzione Ariccia
Abbazia Greca di San Nilo Grottaferrata

visits.text: Ariccia — Piazza di Corte and Palazzo Chigi — A baroque ensemble designed by Gian Lorenzo Bernini for the Chigi family after they acquired the town in 1661, comprising Palazzo Chigi with its two curved wings framing the piazza, and the Chiesa di Santa Maria dell'Assunzione capped by a dome inspired by the Pantheon. Palazzo Chigi preserves a collection of 17th-18th century paintings and period-furnished apartments. To reach Ariccia's center, you cross the Ponte Monumentale, a viaduct 58 m high and 301 m long built in 1847-1854 over the Vallericcia gorge.

Frascati — Ville Tuscolane and Villa Aldobrandini — Frascati developed on the ruins of ancient Tusculum, destroyed by Roman armies in 1191. From the 16th century onward, cardinals and noble families built about a dozen country villas on its hills—the Ville Tuscolane—of which Villa Aldobrandini (1598-1603), designed by Giacomo della Porta and Carlo Maderno, is the most imposing. Its baroque-style terraced gardens with nymphaeum and cascades are open to the public. Cicero already owned a villa at Tusculum where he wrote the Tusculanae Disputationes.

Abbazia Greca di San Nilo at Grottaferrata — A monastery founded in 1004 by Saint Nilus of Rossano, a Basilian monk of Calabrian origin, fifty years before the Great Schism. It remains the last Byzantine-Greek rite monastery in Italy, still in communion with Rome. The abbey church, consecrated in 1024 by Pope John XIX, preserves a 13th-century mosaic on the triumphal arch depicting the Pentecost on a gold ground, attributed to the same workshops as Monreale Cathedral. The crypta ferrata—a Republican-era Roman burial chamber closed by iron grilles—gave Grottaferrata its name.

Palazzo Apostolico di Castel Gandolfo — The papal summer residence since Urban VIII first stayed here in spring 1626, after entrusting the conversion work to Carlo Maderno, architect of St. Peter's facade. The Ville Pontificali complex covers 55 extraterritorial hectares—a status granted by the 1929 Lateran Pacts—including 35 hectares of gardens and 20 hectares of farmland. In 2016, Pope Francis transformed the palace into a museum, ending four centuries of papal summer residence. The gardens include remains of Emperor Domitian's villa (81-96 AD).

activities.text: Walking along Lake Albano's shores from Castel Gandolfo — The largest lake in the Colli Albani at 6 km² surface area and 170 m depth, connected to the sea since antiquity via a 4th-century BC Roman emissary still in operation. The ring road follows the shore for 10 km with views of the tuff cliffs.

Tasting porchetta d'Ariccia IGP in the local fraschette — Ariccia porchetta has held IGP status since 2011. It's prepared with a whole deboned pig, stuffed with rosemary, garlic, and black pepper, rolled and slow-roasted in a wood oven. The fraschette—traditional osterias where you eat elbow-to-elbow—perpetuate a simple dining tradition documented since the 18th century in the Castelli Romani.

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Monte Porzio Catone
Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta Monte Compatri

visits.text: Monte Porzio Catone — A Castelli Romani village at 451 m with an astronomical observatory inaugurated in 1938, operated by INAF (National Institute for Astrophysics). The Cantina Sociale, a wine cooperative founded in 1934, is one of the most important producers of Frascati DOC, an appellation created in 1966.

Monte Compatri — A village at 576 m among the highest in the Colli Albani, with the 17th-century Chiesa di Santa Maria Assunta and a 360° belvedere covering Rome, Lake Albano, and the Apennines. The historic center, sheltered from winds by a natural spur, preserves quiet medieval streets.

Rocca di Papa — A village at the foot of Monte Cavo, the 949 m summit of the Colli Albani and the ancient mons Albanus—sacred site of the Latins where the Feriae Latinae were held, annual festivals of the Latin federation presided over by Rome since the 6th century BC. A Roman-paved via sacra still climbs Monte Cavo's flank from Rocca di Papa. The historic center rises in an amphitheater on the slope with 17th-18th century tuff houses.

activities.text: Hiking up Monte Cavo on foot from Rocca di Papa — A 5 km round-trip trail on the Roman paved via sacra, passing through the Parco dei Castelli Romani's oak forest to the 949 m summit where a radio antenna occupies the site of the ancient Temple of Jupiter. Panoramic views over Rome, both volcanic lakes, and the sea.

map_keywords: Cattedrale di San Pietro e Paolo Segni
Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta Carpineto Romano

visits.text: Norma and Parco Archeologico di Norba — Norma sits on the 'Rave,' a limestone spur at 433 m plunging over 400 m sheer above the Pontine Plain. One kilometer from the modern village lie the ruins of ancient Norba, founded in the 5th century BC by the Volsci as a fortress controlling the plain. The 2.5 km circuit of opus polygonale walls—massive limestone blocks dry-fitted without mortar—culminates in two acropolises. Norba sided with Marius against Sulla and was destroyed in reprisal around 82 BC; abandoned, it was never rebuilt. From the site, on clear days the panorama stretches to Nettuno, Terracina, and the Pontine Islands.

Segni — A village at 668 m in the Monti Lepini with Cyclopean walls from the Republican Roman era among the best preserved in Lazio. The Porta Saracena, a monumental arch in 3-meter-high polygonal blocks dating to the 4th-3rd century BC, was frequently reproduced by Grand Tour artists. The Cattedrale di San Pietro e Paolo preserves a 12th-century Romanesque apse.

Carpineto Romano — A village at 745 m in the heart of the Monti Lepini, a feudal holding of the Colonna from the 14th to 17th centuries, with a preserved medieval historic center of sloping lanes and several noble palaces. The 16th-century Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta preserves an altarpiece from the Roman school.

activities.text: Hiking the trails of the Monti Lepini around Carpineto — The limestone massif offers routes between 700 and 1,500 m altitude through beech and oak forests. The villages of Segni, Norma, Bassiano, and Sermoneta are all accessible via marked trails of the Comunità Montana dei Monti Lepini.

seo.h1_title: Hidden Villages Around Rome and the Castelli Romani
seo.intro_paragraph: The route opens on Lake Bracciano and the Castello Orsini-Odescalchi, a Renaissance fortress built by the same masons working on the Sistine Chapel. Calcata, a village declared uninhabitable in 1935 and reclaimed in the 1960s by artists from around the world, offers a journey outside time in the Treja gorges. Palestrina reveals Republican Rome's largest oracular sanctuary and its 5.85 m Nile Mosaic, a Hellenistic masterpiece. The Castelli Romani unfold through Nemi with Caligula's ships burned in 1944, Castel Gandolfo with its 55 hectares of papal gardens, and Grottaferrata with its Byzantine abbey founded in 1004. The circuit concludes on the Cyclopean walls of Norba and the heights of the Monti Lepini.
seo.seo_hook: Where Renaissance fortresses meet volcanic lakes, ghost villages harbor artists, and Rome's ancient oracular sanctuaries still stand guard over the countryside.
featured_list: Bracciano: Castello Orsini-Odescalchi built by the Sistine Chapel's masons (1470)
Calcata: Village declared uninhabitable in 1935, reclaimed by artists in the 1960s
Palestrina: 5.85 m Nile Mosaic and Sanctuary of Fortuna Primigenia from the 2nd century BC
Nemi: Caligula's ships (71 and 73 m) destroyed by fire in 1944
Castel Gandolfo: 55 hectares of extraterritorial papal gardens since 1626
Grottaferrata: Italy's last Byzantine-rite abbey, founded in 1004
Norba: 2.5 km circuit of Cyclopean opus polygonale walls, 1st century BC

places_taglines.IT::bracciano: Where a Renaissance fortress watches over a 160-meter-deep volcanic lake
places_taglines.IT::calcata: A tuff village condemned in 1935, reborn as an artists' haven perched on cliffs
places_taglines.IT::nerola: Where the Orsini fortress crowns the silent ridges of the Monti Lucretili
places_taglines.IT::arsoli: A mountain village on the edge of the Simbruini, guarded by the Massimo castle
places_taglines.IT::palestrina: Where Rome's greatest oracle spoke through a 5.85-meter mosaic of the Nile
places_taglines.IT::zagarolo: Medieval wine village wrapped around the Rospigliosi palace terraces
places_taglines.IT::nemi: A crater lake that once floated Caligula's palaces before they burned to ash
