The Castello Angioino-Aragonese dominates the town: 13th-century core (Charles of Anjou, 1279) expanded by Alfonso of Aragon (1440), fortified by Charles V (150,000 sq ft). Gaeta was 'the key to the Kingdom of Naples.' Pope Pius IX took refuge here from 1848 to 1850; Francis II of Bourbon capitulated here on February 13, 1861—one of the final acts of Italian unification. The Cattedrale (SS. Erasmo e Marciano) has an Arab-Norman campanile and a baroque crypt from 1620 with frescoes by Giacinto Brandi. The church of the Annunziata (1321, Gothic façade) houses the Cappelletta d'Oro, where Pius IX and John Paul II both prayed. The church of San Giovanni a Mare summarizes the architectural influences of the 'city of a hundred churches' with its Arab-inspired dome.

Montagna Spaccata and Grotta del Turco — On Monte Orlando (Riviera di Ulisse regional park). The Santuario della SS. Trinità (11th century, founded with a Benedictine convent) is built at the edge of three deep fissures in the limestone cliff: legend says they opened at Christ's death. Descending the main fissure, you see the 'Mano del Turco'—a handprint in the rock, linked to a legend of a skeptical sailor's conversion whose hand sank into the stone. Saint Philip Neri is said to have lived here; his stone 'bed' is visible. To the left of the church, a 300-step stairway descends to the Grotta del Turco, a sea cave with emerald waters (access sometimes closed for safety). At the summit of Monte Orlando: the Mausoleo di Lucio Munazio Planco, circular tomb of a general of Julius Caesar who died in Gaeta in 22 BC.

activities.text: Get lost in the medieval quarter of Sant'Erasmo: alleys, towers, stone staircases, 12th-13th century constructions. Lunch of tiella (octopus and escarole pie, eaten with hands) and Gaeta olives. Stroll along the lungomare Giovanni Caboto.

Descend into the Montagna Spaccata fissure, search for the Mano del Turco. If open, descend the 300 steps to the Grotta del Turco and its turquoise reflections. Climb to Plancus's Mausoleum for the panorama over the gulf.

visits.text: Villa of Tiberius and Cave — In 1957, coastal road builders stumbled upon an imperial villa identified as Tiberius's praetorium (emperor 14-37 AD), cited by Suetonius and Tacitus. The core dates to the late Republican era; the complex extended 985 feet with sea-facing terraces, baths, port and barracks. The centerpiece: a natural cave transformed into a mythological landscape, preceded by a large rectangular seawater fish pond with an artificial island serving as a summer dining room (caenatio). Inside the cave, a circular pool (40 feet diameter) was surrounded by monumental sculptural groups—a 'marble Odyssey.' Four groups identified: the blinding of Polyphemus by Ulysses, Scylla's assault on the ship, the theft of the Palladium by Ulysses and Diomedes, and Ulysses carrying Achilles's body. A Ganymede abducted by Zeus's eagle crowned the entrance. The sculptures bear the signatures of the same Rhodian artists as the Vatican's Laocoön (Agesander, Athenodorus, Polydorus). In 26 AD, Tiberius nearly died in a cave-in—Sejanus saved his life. The Julio-Claudian gens considered itself descended from Ulysses and Circe: the cave faced Monte Circeo, mythical home of the sorceress. The Museo Archeologico Nazionale (1963, Giorgio Zama) was a victory for residents who opposed transferring the works to Rome.

Borgo di Sperlonga — Listed among the 'borghi più belli d'Italia,' a whitewashed village with blue shutters clinging to a promontory between two beaches. Narrow lanes, arches, stairways, sea views at every turn. The Torre Truglia (1532), symbol of the village, was built on a coastal watchtower, destroyed by the corsair Barbarossa then by the Turks (1623), rebuilt each time. The spiaggia di Levante extends toward Tiberius's cave; the spiaggia di Ponente toward the north. Lago Lungo beach (sand spit with dunes separating an artificial lake from the sea) offers a wild atmosphere.

activities.text: Visit the archaeological museum: plaster reconstructions of complete groups, original Greek marble fragments, Faustinus inscription describing the works. Descend through the macchia toward the villa, fish ponds and cave. Observe the circular pool and the view toward Monte Circeo.
Wander the whitewashed alleys of the centro storico at sunset. Climb to Torre Truglia for the panorama. Dinner of fresh fish in a seaside trattoria.

visits.text: Crossing and Porto di Ponza — Ferry from Formia (2h30 on traghetto, 1h15 on aliscafo) to the largest of the Pontine Islands (2.9 sq mi, 928 feet at Monte La Guardia). The porto borbonico (1772-1793), built on a Roman port by Ferdinand IV's engineers (Antonio Winspeare and Francesco Carpi), is an amphitheater of colorful houses—one of the Mediterranean's most beautiful boat arrivals. The island, inhabited since the Neolithic, was colonized by the Phoenicians, then the Greeks (8th century BC), then the Romans (312 BC) who made it a resort and exile destination. Benedictine monks founded the abbey of Santa Maria, destroyed by Saracens. After centuries of pirate raids (the Turks blew up the port tower in 1655), the Bourbons repopulated the island in 1771 with settlers from Ischia (20 boats) and Torre del Greco (50 boats for coral fishing). Ischian surnames (Scotto, Migliaccio, Mazzella) and the Neapolitan dialect survive.

Grotte di Pilato and island tour — Hypogean and underwater archaeological complex from the 1st century AD: 5 caves carved into the tuff, connected by underwater tunnels and an external basin. Called 'Antico Murenaio Romano': the Romans bred moray eels here, a sacred fish. The main basin had 5 openings closed by sluice gates (saracinesche) to control water and transfer fish. In 1997, at the bottom of the main cave, a white marble statue (Apollo or Dionysus), a tuff altar and oil lamps were discovered—suggesting cultic use. The caves were connected by a stairway to Emperor Augustus's villa on the Collina della Madonna. The name 'Pilato' comes from a legend: young Pontius Pilate, a Roman noble, was sent to Ponza to quell a revolt and gained his surname before being sent to Palestine. The Cisterna della Dragonara (town center) is one of three large Roman cisterns. Continuing the tour by gozzo: Chiaia di Luna (white tuff cliff in a half-moon, 330 feet high), Piscine Naturali (volcanic rock pools), Cala Feola (only sandy beach), Faraglioni della Madonna.

activities.text: Climb the colorful lanes of the centro: church of SS. Silverio e Domitilla, Giardino Botanico. Rent a gozzo (~€80-150/day) or take an organized excursion for the island tour.

Gozzo tour with swimming stop at Grotte di Pilato (mask and snorkel to explore underwater tunnels). Chiaia di Luna for the monumental cliff. Swimming at Piscine Naturali. Fish dinner at sunset on the port.

visits.text: Inland Ponza: trails and heritage — The hilly interior (volcanic origin) is explored by a network of trails. The Faro della Guardia, at the southwestern tip (Monte La Guardia, 928 feet), is reached by an ancient path carved into rock: panorama over the open sea and Sardinia on clear days. The Roman tunnels (cunicoli from the 3rd century BC) cross the mountain between the port and Chiaia di Luna. Bourbon fortifications (1771-1793) dot the heights. The island served as confino under Mussolini: among political prisoners, Sandro Pertini (future president), Pietro Nenni and Giorgio Amendola. The church of SS. Silverio e Domitilla bears the inscription 'Cistercium Mater Nostra, Benedictus Pater' in memory of monasteries destroyed by pirates. Ferry return to Formia. If time permits, stop at the Tomba di Cicerone (traditional tomb of Cicero, assassinated in Formia in 43 BC).

activities.text: Morning hike to Faro della Guardia (2h round-trip). Explore the Roman tunnels (headlamp useful). Final swim in a cove before the return ferry.

seo.h1_title: Ulysses Riviera: Secret Gardens, Ancient Caves & Southern Lazio Islands
seo.intro_paragraph: From Ninfa, the medieval Pompeii drowned in roses, to Ponza's Roman caves carved to breed sacred moray eels: this 7-day circuit plunges into Lazio's deepest secrets. At Norba, the cyclopean walls of a city that chose mass suicide in 81 BC tower over the plain; at Circeo, Neanderthals preceded Ulysses and Circe by 50,000 years. Isola del Liri offers Italy's unique spectacle of an 89-foot waterfall in the town center, while Montecassino tells the story of destruction and resurrection of the West's oldest monastery. Gaeta hides in its Split Mountain the handprint of a converted Turkish sailor; Sperlonga reveals in Tiberius's cave an Odyssey sculpted in marble by the same artists as the Laocoön. The circuit ends at sea, on Ponza island and its gelato-colored Bourbon port.
seo.seo_hook: Ninfa Gardens (1,300 species on medieval ruins), cyclopean walls of Norba (1.5 miles, 94 acres), Grotta Guattari (11 Neanderthals), Cascata Grande of Isola del Liri (89 feet in town center), Montecassino (founded 529, bombed 1944), Split Mountain of Gaeta, Tiberius's Cave at Sperlonga (marble Odyssey), Grotte di Pilato at Ponza (Roman moray eel farm).
featured_list: Ninfa: English garden on a ghost medieval city, 1,300 botanical species
Sermoneta: Castello Caetani with 138-foot keep and 1470 painted chambers
Norba: Republican Pompeii of Lazio, cyclopean walls 33-40 feet high
San Felice Circeo: Circe's promontory, Grotta Guattari and 11 Neanderthals
Isola del Liri: Italy's only urban waterfall and paper-mill industrial heritage
Montecassino: monastery founded 529, bombed 1944, rebuilt identically
Gaeta: fortified medieval borgo, Split Mountain and Grotta del Turco
Sperlonga: Tiberius's Cave with marble Odyssey and white borgo above the sea
Ponza: colorful Bourbon port, Grotte di Pilato and island tour by gozzo

places_taglines.tagline: Where Caetani fortress towers guard a Pompeii of the Middle Ages
Circe's enchanted promontory where Neanderthals sheltered in stone
Where waterfalls thunder through the Manchester of Italy
Medieval pearl where mountains split at Christ's death
White labyrinth above the emperor's marble Odyssey
Rainbow harbor where Romans bred sacred eels in carved stone

highlights: Giardino di Ninfa: the medieval Pompeii beneath roses, classified Monumento Naturale
Castello Caetani: 7 centuries of history from the Annibaldi to the Borgias
Antica Norba: cyclopean walls and collective suicide of 81 BC
Grotta Guattari: 11 Neanderthals discovered on Circe's promontory
Isola del Liri: 89-foot waterfall in town center, unique in Italy
Montecassino: Italy's oldest monastery, 529-1944-1964
Gaeta: Split Mountain, Grotta del Turco and city of a hundred churches
Villa of Tiberius: marble Odyssey by the sculptors of the Laocoön
Ponza: Grotte di Pilato, Roman moray farm and Bourbon port

itineraries.title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio
itineraries.subtitle: From Sutri to Acquapendente, eight stages through Etruscan necropolises, papal palaces, and tufa villages perched on the volcanic hills of northern Lazio.
specialties: Tuscia extra virgin olive oil DOP

map_keywords: Mitreo Santa Maria del Parto Sutri

visits.text: Roman Amphitheatre of Sutri - Carved between the 2nd and 1st centuries BC directly into the tufa hillside, this elliptical amphitheatre measuring 49 by 40 meters could hold between 5,000 and 9,000 spectators across three tiers. Not a single masonry stone was used—everything was sculpted from volcanic rock. Buried under vegetation for centuries, locals rediscovered and excavated it between 1835 and 1838.

Etruscan Necropolis - Along the cliff below the town, 64 tombs carved into tufa date from the 6th to 4th centuries BC. You'll find niches for cinerary urns, chamber tombs with dromos entrances, and columbaria. A paved path winds between the sculpted rock faces. Sutri bore the title "Gateway to Etruria"—this monumental cemetery proves it.

Mithraeum and Church of Santa Maria del Parto - Three adjoining Etruscan tombs were transformed into a sanctuary dedicated to the god Mithras under the Roman Empire, then converted into a Christian church. Step inside to discover 14th-century frescoes depicting the Virgin and sacred scenes, painted directly onto the tufa walls. Local legend claims this cave was where Bertha, Charlemagne's sister, gave birth to Roland, the legendary paladin.

Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta - Built in the 12th century in Romanesque style, remodeled in the 17th century as Baroque. The original Romanesque bell tower and medieval crypt survive. In 1046, Emperor Henry III convened a council here to resolve the crisis of three rival popes.

Palazzo Doebbing - Former episcopal palace until 1986, restored between 2010 and 2018. The museum occupies five levels and blends ancient and contemporary art: sculptures, architectural fragments, and frescoes from surrounding churches.

activities.text: Walk through the Archaeological Park - Lazio's smallest regional nature park (7 hectares) connects all the sites via shaded trails winding between oaks and meadows. At the summit, the 17th-century Villa Savorelli overlooks the landscape, surrounded by an Italian garden featuring a boxwood labyrinth.

map_keywords: Duomo San Pietro e Santa Caterina Ronciglione

visits.text: Historic center of Ronciglione - Named "Borgo dei Borghi" by RAI television, this town of 8,500 inhabitants blends medieval and Renaissance architecture on a volcanic spur at 441 meters elevation. Climb Corso Umberto I (nicknamed "Montecalvo")—the steep alleys reveal palaces of the Farnese family, who transformed Ronciglione into a prosperous industrial hub with ironworks, paper mills, and armories along the Rio Vicano.
Fontana Grande - Attributed to architect Antonio Gentili da Faenza (school of Vignola), this monumental fountain marks the passage between the lower Renaissance town and the upper medieval quarter.

Museo delle Ferriere (Ironworks Museum) - Housed in the town's oldest forge, this museum traces Ronciglione's industrial epic. From the 15th century onward, 15 forges operated along the Rio Vicano. The last one closed only in 1992. The building preserves the hydraulic mechanisms that transformed water power into energy.

Cathedral of San Pietro e Santa Caterina - The cathedral dominates the main square. Its baroque interior houses 17th-century paintings. In 1728, Pope Benedict XIII elevated Ronciglione to the rank of "Città" (city).

activities.text: Lake Vico circuit - This volcanic crater lake, formed approximately 100,000 years ago, is Italy's highest and one of its best-preserved. Protected as a nature reserve since 1982, it shelters herons, falcons, and tortoises. According to legend, Hercules planted his club in the ground; when he pulled it out, water gushed forth and created the lake. Marked trails, equipped beaches, and picnic areas line the shores.

practical_info.duration: ~2h on foot or 30 min by car

map_keywords: Forum Cassii Santa Maria di Forcassi

visits.text: Historic center of Vetralla - A major stop on the Via Francigena, Vetralla occupies a hill inhabited since the 6th century BC. Enter through Piazza Marconi and its medieval fountain, then follow Via Cassia Interna: Palazzo Franciosoni (Vignola school), the 18th-century Duomo, and two baroque fountains on Piazza Umberto I punctuate the walk.

Church of San Francesco - Vetralla's oldest, erected in the 7th century then rebuilt in Romanesque style in 1207. The peperino stone facade incorporates Roman fragments from the nearby Forum Cassii. Inside: a Cosmatesque pavement, a marble tabernacle from Andrea Bregno's workshop (15th century), and the sarcophagus of condottiere Briobris, who died in 1353 at age 33.

Etruscan Necropolis of Norchia - 15 km from Vetralla, one of Etruria's most spectacular necropolises. The tombs (4th–2nd centuries BC) are carved into tufa cliffs: some reproduce Doric temple facades with pediments, friezes, and busts. The Lattanzi tomb, dated around 320 BC, featured a facade nearly 18 meters high with two levels of columns. The Via Cava Buia, a 400-meter gallery cut through rock with 10-meter-high walls, once led to the city. A semi-abandoned site—bring sturdy shoes.

activities.text: Forum Cassii and Santa Maria di Forcassi - 2 km from Vetralla, the ruins of the ancient Roman mansio (relay station on the Via Cassia) appear on the Tabula Peutingeriana, Europe's oldest road atlas. The medieval chapel preserves 14th-century frescoes beneath a partially collapsed roof. The Order of Hospitallers cared for pilgrims here during the Middle Ages.

visits.text: Palazzo dei Papi (Papal Palace) - Built from 1257 onward for Pope Alexander IV fleeing Rome. The Sala del Conclave hosted the longest papal election in history: 33 months (1268–1271). Exasperated, the Viterbese locked the cardinals in—hence the word "conclave" (cum clave, "with a key")—then tore off the roof to expose them to the elements. Gregory X was finally elected. The Loggia of Blessings (1267), with its trilobed Gothic arches, offers sweeping valley views.

Cathedral of San Lorenzo - A 12th-century Romanesque edifice, remodeled during the Renaissance and Baroque periods. Tradition holds it was built atop the foundations of a temple dedicated to Hercules. In 1277, part of the adjacent palace collapsed onto Pope John XXI, killing him—the only pope to die in a construction accident.

Museo del Colle del Duomo - The ticket grants access to the palace, cathedral, and museum. Collections include Etruscan and Roman archaeological finds, papal objects, reliquaries, and paintings. French audio guide available.

San Pellegrino Quarter - One of Italy's best-preserved medieval centers. The "case a ponte" (houses connected by upper-floor arches), profferli (external stone staircases), towers, and small squares have scarcely changed since the 13th century. Every September 3rd, the Macchina di Santa Rosa—a 30-meter tower weighing 5,100 kilograms—is carried through the streets by the facchini: this ritual is inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list.

Fontana Grande and Palazzo dei Priori - Viterbo's largest fountain stands on the piazza bearing its name. The Palazzo dei Priori (seat of civil power) houses halls adorned with Mannerist frescoes and the Cappella Palatina painted by Filippo Cavarozzi.

Villa Lante in Bagnaia - 5 km from Viterbo, this Renaissance garden attributed to Vignola (1560s) is considered one of Italy's most perfect. The central water axis descends 230 meters along four terraces: cascades, a chain of water sculpted in stone, a stone table crossed by a channel (to cool dishes during open-air dinners), and Giambologna's Fountain of the Four Moors. The 16th-century hydraulic system still functions without modern pumps.

activities.text: Bathing in the Bullicame thermal springs - Mentioned by Dante in Canto XIV of Inferno and sketched by Michelangelo, these sulfurous springs gush at 58°C from a natural crater. Already frequented by Etruscans and Romans, they attracted numerous popes: Nicholas V built a thermal palace in 1450. Several open-air pools are free to access (Piscine Carletti, Bullicame), open year-round.

map_keywords: Sacro Bosco Parco dei Mostri Bomarzo

visits.text: Sacro Bosco (Park of the Monsters) of Bomarzo - Created from 1552 onward by Prince Vicino Orsini, this 3-hectare park is the world's oldest modern sculpture garden. After his wife Giulia Farnese's death, Orsini commissioned architect Pirro Ligorio (who completed St. Peter's Basilica after Michelangelo) to create dozens of colossal creatures carved from peperino: an ogre with gaping mouth, an elephant bearing a combat tower, two wrestling giants, a dragon, sphinxes. The leaning house, built on a tilted block, disorients visitors. Abandoned for 400 years, the park was rediscovered by Salvador Dalí in 1938.

Tempietto of the Sacro Bosco - Atop the hill, this small temple attributed to Vignola crowns the route. Its dome echoes in miniature that of Santa Maria del Fiore in Florence. Vicino Orsini dedicated it to his wife's memory.

Etruscan Pyramid of Bomarzo - In the sacred wood, a peperino monolith carved into a stepped pyramid puzzles archaeologists. Likely a sacrificial altar or rock sanctuary, it remains shrouded in mystery. Access via a forest trail.

Vitorchiano, the "suspended village" - 15 minutes from Bomarzo, this grey peperino village seems to merge with the volcanic cliff. Classified among the Borghi più Belli d'Italia and awarded the Touring Club's Bandiera Arancione. The profferli (external staircases), the 13th-century fountain on Piazza Roma, and the Capitoline Wolf engraved on facades recall the unique status of "land faithful to Rome" granted in 1265. Director Mario Monicelli filmed "L'armata Brancaleone" here with Vittorio Gassman in 1966.

Moai of Vitorchiano - In 1990, eleven craftsmen from Easter Island (Rapa Nui) sculpted this 6-meter, 3-ton statue from a local peperino block—the only Moai in the world made outside Easter Island using ancestral techniques. Tuscia's volcanic peperino resembles Rapa Nui stone. The statue stands watch over the belvedere overlooking the Vezza valley.

activities.text: Vitorchiano belvedere - From Largo Padre Ettore Salimbeni (where the Moai stands), the view plunges over the borgo clinging to its cliff, the Vezza valley, and the oak forests of the Cimini Mountains. Tuscia's best photo spot, especially in late afternoon light.

map_keywords: Chiesa San Donato Civita di Bagnoregio

visits.text: Civita di Bagnoregio - Founded by Etruscans over 2,500 years ago, this "Città che Muore" ("dying city," a nickname given by writer Bonaventura Tecchi) counts only about ten permanent residents. The village sits atop a tufa and clay spur that erodes a little more each year. Of the five original medieval gates, only Porta Santa Maria survives. Inside, the Etruscan urban grid (cardo and decumanus) remains visible beneath the medieval overlay.

Civita footbridge - 300 meters long, 70 meters of elevation gain, 42 reinforced concrete pillars: this pedestrian bridge, built in 1965 after Germans destroyed the previous one in 1944, is the only access to the village. The steep climb transforms the approach into a kind of pilgrimage. Approximately 700,000 visitors cross it annually.

Church of San Donato - On the borgo's main square, this church houses a wooden crucifix venerated since the 15th century. According to tradition, it spoke to a pious woman in 1499 to end a plague epidemic. Every Good Friday, the crucifix is carried in procession across the bridge to Bagnoregio.

Cave of San Bonaventura and Bucaione - Beneath Civita's cliff face, an Etruscan chamber tomb bears the name of Saint Bonaventure: tradition holds that here Saint Francis healed the young Giovanni Fidanza, future Doctor of the Church. The "Bucaione," a deep Etruscan tunnel, pierces the rocky base and opens directly onto the Valley of the Calanchi.

activities.text: Sunset over the Valley of the Calanchi - From the Bagnoregio belvedere (before the bridge) or from Civita's Porta Sud, white clay ravines carve a lunar landscape between the Torbido and Chiaro watercourses. Constant erosion transforms this panorama season by season. Plan to be there around 6 PM in summer for raking light.

visits.text: Rocca dei Papi - Fortress built in the 13th century by Pope Innocent III at Montefiascone's highest point. A favored summer residence for several popes thanks to fresh breezes and an impregnable position. Archaeological excavations are uncovering vestiges of Etruscan occupation. Climb to the tower's summit: the panorama embraces all of Lake Bolsena, the volcanic domes, and Orvieto in the distance.

Cathedral of Santa Margherita - Its 17th-century dome, visible throughout the region, dominates Montefiascone's profile. Architect Michele Sanmicheli designed the general structure in the early 16th century. A fire ravaged it in 1670; it was restored and reopened in its current form in 1893. The neoclassical portal dates from the mid-19th century.

Church of San Flaviano - An original construction on two superimposed levels: Romanesque-Gothic arcades below, 16th-century loggia above—and the two churches face opposite directions. The walls preserve medieval frescoes. Near the entrance, look for the tombstone of German bishop Johannes Defuk (died 1113), origin of the Est! Est!! Est!!! wine legend.

activities.text: Panorama over Lake Bolsena - Europe's largest volcanic lake (114 km², 151 meters deep) spreads at Montefiascone's feet. Two small islands, Martana and Bisentina, emerge from the waters. From the Rocca dei Papi terrace, you can take in the entire caldera.

Est! Est!! Est!!! wine tasting - The DOC appellation (since 1966) takes its name from a 12th-century legend: Bishop Johannes Defuk sent a scout ahead to mark good wine inns with "Est!" (there is!) on the door. At Montefiascone, the scout wrote "Est!" three times, so pleased was he with the wine. The bishop settled there... and never left. The white wine, based on Trebbiano and Malvasia, is produced on volcanic soils around the lake.

visits.text: Basilica of the Holy Sepulchre - Nicknamed the "Jerusalem of Europe," this 9th-century basilica houses in its Romanesque crypt the world's oldest reproduction of Jerusalem's Holy Sepulchre. Legend tells that Matilda of Westphalia, mother of Emperor Otto I, was traveling to Rome with mules laden with gold when the animals refused to advance at Acquapendente. A dream commanded her to build the church here. The 11th-century crypt—24 columns with all-different capitals forming nine naves beneath groin vaults—is a true "stone forest." The current facade is the work of Nicola Salvi, architect of Rome's Trevi Fountain.

Historic center and Torre del Barbarossa - The former double circuit of walls preserves three gates, including the Torre Julia de Jacopo—named after a woman who in 1550 closed the gate under enemy fire to save the town from assault by Orsini troops. On the hill dominating the town, the clock tower is the last vestige of the fortress built by Emperor Henry IV in the 12th century.

Bosco del Sasseto - 12 km from Acquapendente, near the village of Torre Alfina (classified among the Borghi più Belli d'Italia), this monumental forest shelters centuries-old oaks, beeches, and hornbeams whose twisted trunks grow between enormous moss-covered lava boulders. The atmosphere evokes a fairy-tale setting. The neo-Gothic castle of Torre Alfina, restored by Sienese architect Giuseppe Partini, overlooks the wood.

activities.text: Via Francigena stage - Acquapendente is the first Lazio town for pilgrims coming from the north. The Pilgrim House, steps from the cathedral, still welcomes walkers with 25 beds managed by the Confraternity of San Rocco. This is where Saint Roch allegedly performed his first miracle by healing plague victims in the 14th century.

Pugnaloni and traditions - Every third Sunday in May, the Festival of the Madonna del Fiore commemorates the town's liberation from Frederick Barbarossa's yoke in 1166. The "Pugnaloni," giant mosaics composed of flower petals and leaves, are carried in procession through the streets. A tradition unique in all of Italy.

seo.h1_title: Tuscia Circuit: Etruscan Sites and Villages of Lazio
seo.intro_paragraph: From Sutri, the "Gateway to Etruria," to Acquapendente, the "Jerusalem of Europe," this eight-day circuit crosses the Viterbo Tuscia from south to north. A Roman amphitheatre carved into tufa, papal palaces where the word "conclave" was born, stone creatures of the Park of the Monsters of Bomarzo, the island-village of Civita di Bagnoregio suspended above its calanchi, thermal springs mentioned by Dante: northern Lazio reveals a little-known Italy, far from the crowds of Rome and Tuscany.
seo.seo_hook: Necropolises sculpted into tufa, 16th-century stone monsters, island-villages gnawed by erosion—Tuscia hides a secret Italy between Rome and Tuscany.
featured_list: Sutri: Roman amphitheatre entirely carved from tufa rock
Viterbo: the city where the papal conclave was born
Bomarzo: the Sacro Bosco and its giant sculptures
Civita di Bagnoregio: the island-village slowly eroding away
Montefiascone: volcanic vineyards and panorama over Lake Bolsena

places_taglines.IT::sutri: Where Romans carved an amphitheatre into living volcanic rock
places_taglines.IT::ronciglione: Medieval borgo perched above a volcanic crater lake
places_taglines.IT::vetralla: Gateway to Norchia's wild Etruscan cliff tombs
places_taglines.IT::viterbo: The city of popes where conclave was born in locked rooms
places_taglines.IT::bomarzo: Where Renaissance giants and monsters emerge from ancient stone
places_taglines.IT::civita-di-bagnoregio: An island in the sky, slowly crumbling into the ravines below
places_taglines.IT::montefiascone: Papal fortress gazing over Europe's largest volcanic lake
places_taglines.IT::acquapendente: First stop in Lazio on the Via Francigena pilgrimage route

highlights: Sutri amphitheatre carved from volcanic tufa
Palazzo dei Papi in Viterbo, birthplace of the conclave
Sacro Bosco of Bomarzo and its colossal 16th-century sculptures
Civita di Bagnoregio, island-village above the Valley of the Calanchi
Bullicame thermal springs mentioned by Dante
Wild Etruscan necropolis of Norchia
Lake Vico volcanic crater and nature reserve
Romanesque crypt of the Holy Sepulchre in Acquapendente

itineraries.title: Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 2 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From the medieval valley of Dolceacqua to the Alpine heights of Limone Piemonte, following the Riviera dei Fiori through Sanremo, Imperia, and Cuneo.

visits.text: Cross the Ponte Vecchio to enter the Terra quarter: this single-arch humpback bridge spanning 33 meters was built in the 15th century atop the ruins of an earlier structure. In 1884, Claude Monet was staying in Bordighera when he painted four canvases of it; in his letters, he described it as 'a jewel of lightness.' From the Ponte Nuovo a few meters downstream, the framing shows the bridge with the castle rising behind—exactly as Monet saw it.

Explore the Terra quarter through the caruggi—covered passages so narrow and dark that some require electric lighting even at midday. The neighborhood wraps in concentric rings around the castle, climbing six stories where space ran short. The Scasasse alley, connecting the bridge to the parish square, was once used by locals to move safely during conflicts. Emerge onto Piazza Mauro to see the Palazzo Doria, the noble residence where Napoleon Bonaparte stayed in 1794, and the small fountain dedicated to Rossese wine, where water flows red.

Climb up to the Castello dei Doria, built from 1151 by the Counts of Ventimiglia, then purchased in 1270 by Oberto Doria—the Genoese captain who had just crushed the Pisan fleet at the Battle of Meloria. The Doria family controlled the village for six centuries, until the 1887 earthquake severely damaged the castle and forced their departure. Inside, three museum rooms trace the history of the castle, the Doria genealogy, and the connection with Monet, including a reproduction of the painting held at the Musée Marmottan in Paris.

Visit the parish church of Sant'Antonio Abate, built in the 15th century by incorporating an angular tower from the old ramparts as the base of the bell tower. The Baroque interior houses a polyptych by Ludovico Brea (1515), leader of the Ligurian-Niçois painting school, depicting Saint Devota—patron saint of Monaco—with eight figures against a gold background. In the adjacent Oratory of San Sebastiano, a wooden statue of Saint Sebastian carved from fig wood by the Genoese Anton Maria Maragliano (1664-1739) is carried in procession every January 20th.

activities.text: Photo walk through the Borgo, the neighborhood on the right bank of the Nervia, more open and luminous than the Terra: from its lanes, the view of the Ponte Vecchio with the castle rising behind is the most photographed scene in the village. On the facade of a Borgo house, look for the fresco by Barbadirame, a student of Picasso, depicting Admiral Andrea Doria—born of Caracosa, a woman from Dolceacqua, who became the greatest sailor in the Mediterranean in the 16th century.

Rossese di Dolceacqua DOC wine tasting: designated in 1972 as the first controlled designation of origin in Liguria, this light, fruity red wine is produced on the hillsides of olive groves and vineyards surrounding the village. Local producers offer tastings in the cellars along Via Roma that connects the square to the castle entrance.

visits.text: Enter La Pigna through the Porta di Santo Stefano, a Gothic stone arch from the 14th century marking the boundary between the medieval town and the modern city. The quarter takes its name from its spiral form wrapping around the hillside, recalling the scales of a pine cone—a deliberately defensive design conceived after the 9th-century Saracen raids, ensuring that any invader entering the alleys would become lost without being able to advance. The Piazza della Cisterna, with its large underground medieval reservoir, shows how the quarter compensated for the absence of springs by collecting rainwater in a network of cisterns.

Climb to the Sanctuary of the Madonna della Costa, built in 1630 on the site of an earlier chapel, at the summit of the Pigna hill. The church was erected to celebrate Sanremo's liberation from the tutelage of Genoa's Doria family in 1361—an event the city still celebrates today. The Baroque interior houses wooden statues by Maragliano, paintings by Domenico Fiasella, and a 14th-century Madonna and Child. The facade is visible from the sea and has long served as a landmark for navigators.

Visit the Cathedral of San Siro, one of Sanremo's oldest churches, built in the 12th century in Romanesque style with round arches and an ashlar stone nave. It occupies the western edge of La Pigna, steps from the medieval quarter. In 1538, the Manara house at number 21 Via Palma hosted Pope Paul III, en route to Nice where he would sign a ten-year truce between Charles V and Francis I.

Spot the Saracen Tower, built between August and October 1550 in response to repeated attacks by Barbary corsairs. Cylindrical and slender in form, it was connected to the Pigna ramparts and its single vaulted interior room was occupied by guards during alerts. It now stands isolated from the rest of the quarter but remains visible from the base of the hill.

activities.text: Stroll through the lanes of La Pigna from Piazza Santo Stefano to the summit: take the Rivolte San Sebastiano, a covered alley with high, uneven steps, then Via Palma—the former main street of La Pigna where the most illustrious families lived. Push open the door of the Oratory of San Sebastiano, built after the 1502 plague and restored: the 1642 inscription on the entrance colonnade and the 18th-century interior frescoes are well preserved.

Evening walk along the Corso Imperatrice, named in honor of Tsarina Maria Alexandrovna who gifted the city with palm trees during a stay on the Riviera. These palms, some of which are the Tsarina's originals, still line this marble promenade adorned with a Spring statue by sculptor Vincenzo Pasquali. Steps away, the Russian Orthodox Church of Saint Basil, inaugurated on December 23, 1913 in the presence of the Tsar's ambassador, served Sanremo's Russian community, which then numbered a thousand people.

map_keywords: Pista Ciclabile della Riviera dei Fiori Sanremo

visits.text: Admire the exterior of the Casino di Sanremo, designed by French architect Eugène Ferret and inaugurated on January 12, 1905 under the name Kursaal. Mussolini's 1927 decree officially made it a casino. Sanremo is one of only four Italian cities authorized to have one, alongside Venice, Campione d'Italia, and Saint-Vincent. Its Art Nouveau halls have welcomed figures like King Farouk of Egypt (a regular in the 1950s after his exile), King Gustav of Sweden, and Princess Grace of Monaco. Poet Francesco Pastonchi created the tradition of Literary Mondays there.

Visit the park of Villa Ormond, on Corso Felice Cavallotti. The villa was built in 1889 by Swiss architect Émile Réverdin for Michel Louis Ormond, a wealthy Swiss entrepreneur, after the 1887 earthquake damaged the foundations of the original villa purchased in 1875. The municipality bought the villa and park in 1928. The garden is organized into themed zones: the palm grove, the orangery, the ficus area, and a Japanese garden in homage to Atami, Sanremo's sister city. Empress Elisabeth of Austria and the Princes of Prussia were among the Villa's guests.

Visit Villa Nobel, on the same Corso Felice Cavallotti, 200 meters from Villa Ormond. Alfred Nobel chose Sanremo in the late 19th century for health reasons and to set up a laboratory. The Moorish-style villa dates from 1870. It was here that he drafted the 1895 will in which he bequeathed his entire fortune to the foundation that would create the prize bearing his name. The salons, study, and bedroom are preserved with original furnishings; the exhibition traces the technological evolution of the 19th century and the role of his inventions.

Walk to the Fort of Santa Tecla, near the old port. Built by the Genoese in just eleven months and inaugurated on March 12, 1756, this star-shaped fort was erected to suppress Sanremo's independence aspirations vis-à-vis the Republic of Genoa. It now hosts cultural exhibitions and events.

activities.text: Bike rental and ride on the Pista Ciclabile Riviera dei Fiori, built on the former Genoa-Ventimiglia railway line relocated inland from the coast starting in 1998. The path follows the sea for 32 kilometers between Ospedaletti and San Lorenzo al Mare, passing through converted former stations and coastal tunnels. The first Sanremo-San Lorenzo al Mare section was inaugurated in 2009; the extension to Imperia was completed in 2025. Bike rental points available at Sanremo's former station.

Stroll along Corso Matteotti, Sanremo's main shopping street: on the sidewalk slabs, brass plaques engraved with the name of each Italian Song Festival winner since its creation. The Teatro Ariston, which has hosted the festival every year since the 1970s, faces this same street.

visits.text: Climb to the Parasio, the medieval promontory of Porto Maurizio, via the spiral road or the elevator from sea level. This quarter constitutes the original nucleus of Imperia: for centuries Porto Maurizio was an autonomous port and the Mediterranean's leading center for olive oil production and trade, under the protection of the Republic of Genoa. Only in 1923 did a government decree merge it with Oneglia—its Piedmontese rival separated by 3 kilometers—to form the municipality of Imperia. Inhabitants of the two towns still call each other 'ciantafourche' and 'cacelotti,' nicknames inherited from centuries of rivalry.

Enter the Basilica of San Maurizio, the largest church in Liguria, built between 1781 and 1838 on the site of an earlier chapel. Its neoclassical facade dominates the square at the Parasio's exit. Inside: frescoes, paintings, and 19th-century sculptures. On the adjacent Via Santa Caterina, visit the birthplace of San Leonardo da Porto Maurizio, born in 1676 in the Parasio and canonized in the 19th century: the house converted into a museum preserves his habit, his hair shirt, and the funeral cast of his face.

From the Logge di Santa Chiara, the panoramic loggias of the Convent of the Sisters of Santa Chiara atop the Parasio, the view embraces the Ligurian coast on both sides. Nearby, the Tower of Prarola rises directly from the sea west of the historic center: this cylindrical watchtower was built to monitor Saracen pirate raids, a constant threat on this coast until the 17th century.

activities.text: Walk the Passeggiata degli Innamorati, the path that follows the cliffs at the base of the Parasio and connects Porto Maurizio to Borgo Marina. This promenade suspended between the medieval quarter and the sea offers the best views of the coast, particularly at sunset.

Sketching or photography session by Porto Maurizio's harbor, equipped with a long protective pier whose lighthouse provides the best viewpoint over the entire coast. Since 1986, Imperia has periodically hosted the Vele d'Epoca, a gathering of historic sailboats over 20 meters arriving from around the world.

map_keywords: Basilica di San Giovanni Battista Oneglia

visits.text: Visit the Museo dell'Olivo Carlo Carli in Oneglia, housed in a Liberty-style palazzo and spread across eighteen rooms. The Carli brothers, olive oil industrialists, assembled a collection tracing olive cultivation techniques from Ancient Greece to the 20th century: Roman amphorae, parts of animal-drawn or hydraulic mills, documents on cosmetic and medicinal uses of oil. A reconstruction of a Roman ship's hold and a 17th-century press stand in the courtyard. The Taggiasca olive, cultivated in these hills since its Benedictine introduction in the 7th century, was the source of the entire region's historic wealth.

Visit the Basilica of San Giovanni Battista in Oneglia, consecrated in 1762 by architect Gaetano Amoretti and elevated to basilica status in 2017. This late Genoese Baroque building houses polychrome marbles, frescoes, and an 1874 three-keyboard organ. For centuries Oneglia was the main port of Piedmont under the Savoys, rival to Porto Maurizio which answered to Genoa—a rivalry so intense that in 1792, a French fleet bombarded Oneglia without touching neutral Porto Maurizio.

Stroll through Villa Grock, commissioned in the 1920s by Adrien Wettach—known as Grock—one of Europe's most famous clowns, born in Switzerland in 1880. The villa mixes Art Nouveau, references to the circus world, and elements recalling Dalí, Picasso, and Gaudí. It now serves as a museum of clowning and an architectural monument.

activities.text: Wander beneath the arcades of Piazza Dante in Oneglia: this lively square is Oneglia's commercial and social heart, surrounded by cafes and boutiques. Wednesday mornings host the local producers' market—Taggiasca olives, olive oil, vegetables, and cured meats from the hinterland.

Bike or walk a section of the Riviera dei Fiori cycle path between Imperia and San Lorenzo al Mare: the Imperia section, inaugurated in 2025, connects the two parts of the city and links to the existing route toward Sanremo. This stretch passes converted former stations and follows the sea for nearly the entire route.

map_keywords: Parco Fluviale Gesso e Stura Cuneo

visits.text: Begin at Piazza Galimberti, whose neoclassical design by Benedetto Brunati was approved in 1832 and implemented over fifty years. The square measures over 23,000 m² and is framed by ten identical arcaded palaces with round-arched vaults—a symmetrical arrangement inherited from the Napoleonic alignment plans of 1801, when the demolition of the ramparts finally permitted the city's southward expansion. Originally named Piazza Nizza, then Piazza Vittorio Emanuele II, it has borne since May 21, 1945 the name of Duccio Galimberti, a Cuneo lawyer and Resistance hero killed in 1944, who lived in Palazzo Osasco facing the square.

Walk the entire length of Via Roma: this medieval artery stretching over 800 meters, now pedestrianized, has always been Cuneo's commercial center. The porticos were gradually added to medieval facades to shelter activities under cover—in some buildings, you can still see the joint between the original masonry and the added arches. The 52-meter-high Civic Tower, built in 1317, and the Cathedral of Santa Maria del Bosco, whose dome and interior frescoes date from the 19th century, punctuate the route. From the tower's summit, accessible by elevator, guards would spot enemies and sound the alarm.

Explore Contrada Mondovì, Cuneo's former Jewish quarter: one of Piedmont's oldest ghettos since the 15th century, it now forms a picturesque pedestrian zone with several historic shops. Continue to the Church of San Francesco, built between the 13th and 15th centuries in Gothic style: its marble portal from 1418, crowned by a rosette pediment, is the city's most important medieval testimony. Its cloister houses the Civic Museum.

Visit the Museo Casa Galimberti, housed in the palace where Duccio Galimberti was born, an anti-fascist resistance fighter shot on December 3, 1944 at age 35. The museum includes a gallery of paintings by Piedmontese artists, the family library, and the hero's mementos. The building stands on Piazza Galimberti, at the very spot where the first call to armed resistance in Italy was pronounced on July 26, 1943.

activities.text: Walk through the Parco Fluviale Gesso e Stura, along the banks of the two torrents that define the plateau on which Cuneo was founded in 1198. Cuneo—whose name comes from the Piedmontese 'cùneo,' meaning wedge or point—occupies precisely the point of this natural triangle formed by the confluence of the Gesso and Stura di Demonte, at 534 meters altitude at the foot of the Maritime Alps.

Aperitivo beneath the porticos of Piazza Galimberti: the arcades allow you to travel from end to end of the square and Via Roma without ever being exposed to the weather—making Cuneo one of the European cities with the longest continuous covered gallery route. Taste the Cuneesi al rhum, small chocolates filled with meringue and rum, a local pastry specialty present in every shop window.

visits.text: Stroll through the historic center of Limone Piemonte, a village in the Vermenagna Valley at 1,000 meters altitude on one of the historic routes linking France, Piedmont, and the Ligurian coast—once traveled by convoys transporting salt from the Mediterranean inland. Inhabited since Roman times, Limone was one of the first villages in the Southern Alps to develop mountain tourism. Restaurants, shops, and refuges form a compact village center reachable from the train station within minutes on foot.

Visit the Museo dello Sci Agostino Bottero, opened in 2012, which traces the history of skiing in the Riserva Bianca from the first Alpine troops' training sessions in 1907 on the slopes of Monte Vecchio, to the 2006 Winter Olympics that modernized the resort. In 1937, 'lo slittone'—a large motorized sled—was the first mechanical lift installed in southern Piedmont, allowing riders to climb 700 meters of elevation.

activities.text: Skiing or snowboarding session on the Riserva Bianca slopes (season December to early April): the ski area extends from 1,050 to 2,085 meters altitude over 80 kilometers of slopes divided into 41 runs served by 16 lifts. The difficulty distribution is 25% beginner, 55% intermediate, and 20% red and black runs. From the summits—notably Mont Alpetta at 2,070 meters—on clear days, the Mediterranean and Corsica are visible to the south, the Matterhorn and Monte Rosa to the north. Italian and French national teams have conducted training camps here.

Outside ski season, snowshoe or hiking trek on one of the 12 marked trails around Limone and Limonetto, some leading to 19th-century military forts built around the Colle di Tenda—Fort Alto, Fort Pernante, Fort Giaura, and Fort Margherita to the west, Fort Taburda and Fort Pepino to the east. These defensive works of the Kingdom of Sardinia then the Kingdom of Italy controlled the historic pass between Italy and France.
Excursion to the Cascate del Saut, waterfalls located in the valley near Limone Piemonte, particularly impressive in winter under snow and in early spring during the melt.

seo.h1_title: Italy Road Trip from Nice: Riviera & Alps in 7 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: Depart Nice for Dolceacqua, a medieval village immortalized by Monet in 1884, where underground alleys descend beneath the humpback bridge over the Nervia. Continue up the coast to Sanremo—its pine-cone-shaped fortified quarter, its Art Nouveau casino from 1905, the villa where Alfred Nobel drafted his will. Then Imperia doubled: Porto Maurizio on its promontory of oil and stone, Oneglia with its olive museum in a Liberty palazzo. Next Cuneo beneath its kilometers of neoclassical porticos, before finishing at Limone Piemonte, an Alpine resort where skiers see the sea from the summits.
seo.seo_hook: A bridge painted by Monet, a casino opened in 1905, Nobel's last will, ski slopes with views to Corsica. Seven days between the Ligurian Riviera and Piedmont Alps.
featured_list: Dolceacqua: the Ponte Vecchio and medieval caruggi painted by Monet
Sanremo: the fortified Pigna, the Art Nouveau casino, and Villa Nobel
Imperia: the Parasio promontory and Oneglia's olive museum
Cuneo: neoclassical porticos and the Resistance quarter
Limone Piemonte: the Riserva Bianca and Mediterranean views from the peaks

places_taglines.IT::dolceacqua: Medieval Ligurian village where Monet captured the humpback bridge in 1884
places_taglines.IT::sanremo: Riviera town famed for its Art Nouveau casino, song festival, and spiral fortified old quarter
places_taglines.IT::imperia: City born in 1923 from two centuries-old rivals, Liguria's capital of Taggiasca olive oil
places_taglines.IT::cuneo: Piedmontese city founded in 1198 at the confluence of two torrents, renowned for its kilometers of neoclassical porticos
places_taglines.IT::limone-piemonte: Historic Alpine resort at 1,000 meters where peaks offer views across to the Mediterranean

highlights: Dolceacqua: medieval village labeled 'Most Beautiful Villages of Italy' and Orange Flag since 2000
Sanremo: La Pigna quarter founded around 1000 AD, Art Nouveau casino, and Villa Nobel
Imperia: two antagonistic historic centers united in 1923, olive museum spanning 18 rooms
Cuneo: neoclassical porticos extending over 800 continuous meters, Tuesday market among Italy's largest
Limone Piemonte: 80 km of slopes between 1,050 and 2,085 m, views of Mediterranean and Alps from summits

itineraries.title: Grand Tour of the Italian Lakes: Milan, Lakes Maggiore, Como and Verona
itineraries.subtitle: From Milan to Verona, an enchanting journey through the most beautiful lakes and art cities of northern Italy

map_keywords: San Maurizio al Monastero Maggiore Milano

visits.text: Let yourself be enchanted by the majestic Milan Cathedral, a masterpiece of Gothic architecture that dominates the square of the same name. With its 3,500 statues, soaring spires reaching towards the sky and the famous gilded Madonnina standing 4 metres tall, this cathedral dedicated to Santa Maria Nascente is the beating heart of the city. Climb to the terraces for a spectacular view over the metropolis and the Alps on clear days.

Walk through the splendid Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II, Milan's refined drawing room inaugurated in 1867. This elegant covered passage with its majestic iron and glass dome connects Piazza Duomo to Piazza della Scala, offering a unique experience among prestigious boutiques, historic cafés and exquisite mosaics. The architecture designed by Giuseppe Mengoni is a symbol of nineteenth-century Milanese ambition.

Explore the Museo del Novecento in the Palazzo dell'Arengario, a treasure trove of over 400 masterpieces of twentieth-century Italian art. From the celebrated Quarto Stato by Pellizza da Volpedo to works by Boccioni, Modigliani and Fontana, the chronological route guides you through Futurism, Metaphysical Art and Conceptual Art. The hall dedicated to Lucio Fontana offers a breathtaking view of the Duomo through the large windows.

Admire Leonardo da Vinci's Last Supper, one of the most famous paintings in the world housed in the refectory of Santa Maria delle Grazie. Created between 1494 and 1498 using an innovative technique, this depiction of the Last Supper reveals Leonardo's genius in its dramatic composition and the psychological depth of the figures. A deeply moving experience that requires advance booking.

Discover the Pinacoteca di Brera, one of Italy's most prestigious museums founded in 1776. The collection includes absolute masterpieces such as Caravaggio's Supper at Emmaus, Mantegna's Dead Christ, Piero della Francesca's Brera Altarpiece and Raphael's Marriage of the Virgin. Hayez's celebrated Kiss is the icon of Italian Romanticism.

Immerse yourself in the atmosphere of the Church of San Maurizio al Monastero Maggiore, known as Milan's Sistine Chapel. The vivid frescoes extend across no fewer than 4,000 square metres and are the work of the greatest masters of sixteenth-century Lombard painting, including Bernardino Luini. The plain façade on Corso Magenta conceals an interior of astonishing beauty.

activities.text: Stroll along the romantic Navigli, the ancient canal system designed in part by Leonardo da Vinci to connect Milan to the lakes and the sea. The recently renovated Darsena offers picturesque views, artisan workshops and a uniquely bohemian atmosphere in the heart of the city.

Explore the Castello Sforzesco, one of Europe's largest fortresses housing priceless treasures including Michelangelo's Pietà Rondanini and Leonardo's frescoes. Behind it stretches the Parco Sempione, the city's green lung and the perfect setting for a relaxing walk to the Arch of Peace.

visits.text: Admire the Como Cathedral, a splendid church blending Gothic and Renaissance elements in the heart of the city. Built from 1396 and completed in the eighteenth century with the majestic dome designed by Filippo Juvarra, it is one of Lombardy's most important religious monuments. The richly decorated façade and solemn interiors bear witness to centuries of devotion and art.

Visit the Tempio Voltiano, an elegant Neoclassical museum dedicated to Alessandro Volta, Como's illustrious citizen and inventor of the electric battery. Set on the lakefront in a panoramic position, the museum houses original scientific instruments and documents tracing the revolutionary discoveries of the scientist who changed the course of history.

Ride the funicular up to Brunate, the balcony of the Alps offering breathtaking panoramas of the lake and the Alpine chain. This charming village at 720 metres altitude is reachable in just 7 minutes of scenic travel and rewards visitors with unforgettable views, particularly captivating at sunset.

activities.text: Stroll along the Como lakefront, an elegant promenade lined with historic villas, lush gardens and panoramic cafés. The crystal-clear waters of the Lario and the mountain profiles create a setting of incomparable beauty that has inspired poets and artists through the ages.

visits.text: Explore Punta Spartivento, the panoramic point where Lake Como divides into two branches creating its characteristic Y shape. From this legendary promontory you can admire a breathtaking view immortalised by Alessandro Manzoni in The Betrothed, with the mountains reflected in the crystal-clear waters.

Visit the Church of San Giacomo, a gem of Romanesque-Lombard art built in the twelfth century using the characteristic grey stone of Moltrasio. Inside you can admire precious frescoes and sculptures from different historical periods, while the bell tower bears witness to the village's ancient defensive system.

Wander through the grounds of Villa Serbelloni, a magnificent garden of nearly 21 hectares stretching across a steep promontory. This historic residence, now owned by the Rockefeller Foundation, boasts 18 km of paths among rare plants and panoramic vistas that have enchanted distinguished guests over the centuries, including Leonardo da Vinci and Alessandro Manzoni.

Discover the Gardens of Villa Melzi d'Eril, a botanical paradise on the western lakefront created in the early nineteenth century by the noble Milanese family. Among exotic plants, ancient trees, antique statues and archaeological finds, you can admire the famous statue of Dante and Beatrice that inspired Franz Liszt to compose his Dante Sonata.

activities.text: Walk up the Salita Serbelloni, a picturesque stairway connecting the lakefront to the old town through boutiques and characteristic establishments. Once known as Il Fossato for its medieval defensive role, it now features a commemorative plaque to the composer Franz Liszt, who stayed at length in this enchanting village.

visits.text: Admire the Church of San Giorgio, a splendid example of thirteenth-century medieval Lombard architecture built on the foundations of an ancient Roman temple. The grey stone façade is dominated by a majestic bell tower, while inside the 1822 Baroque high altar and a precious cycle of frescoes stand out. The floor of fine black marble completes the elegance of this sacred place.

Explore Villa Monastero, an evocative sixteenth-century architectural complex that was once a Cistercian convent. Today it is one of the lake's main attractions with its house museum and extraordinary botanical gardens hosting hundreds of plant species from around the world, along a path winding beside the lakeshore.

Visit Villa Cipressi, an elegant late-Renaissance gem with terraces cascading down towards the lake. This historic complex offers romantic views and manicured gardens delivering unforgettable panoramas over the waters of the Lario and the surrounding mountains.

activities.text: Walk the Passeggiata degli Innamorati (Lovers' Walk), a romantic walkway of about 500 metres suspended on the cliff face above the lake. This arched structure, draped in climbing plants that burst into a riot of colour and fragrance in the warmer months, connects the landing stage to the colourful houses of the old town in a route of incomparable beauty.

Climb to the Castello di Vezio, an ancient fortress overlooking Varenna from above with spectacular views of the lake and surrounding villages. You can explore the gardens, the atmospheric underground passages and the square watchtower housing a permanent art exhibition.

visits.text: Stroll along the Menaggio lakefront, an elegant Belle Époque promenade lined with refined cafés and Art Nouveau palms. This golden ribbon facing the waters of the Lario offers picturesque views of historic villas such as Villa Mylius Vigoni and Villa Govone, with the majestic Alps as a natural backdrop.

Explore the old town of Menaggio, characterised by cobbled lanes and lively little squares where you can breathe an atmosphere of times gone by. The bell tower with its copper dome dominates the village, while the pastel-coloured façades of the traditional houses create an environment of rare lakeside elegance.

activities.text: Take advantage of Menaggio's strategic position as a gateway to the Val Sanagra and Monte Grona for panoramic hikes, or head to the Golf Club Menaggio e Cadenabbia, one of Italy's oldest founded in 1907, set in an Alpine landscape of extraordinary beauty.

map_keywords: Chiesa San Vittore il Moro Porlezza

visits.text: Explore the Grotte di Rescia, a fascinating system of natural cavities rich in stalactites and stalagmites bearing witness to millennia of geological activity. This underground spectacle offers a unique experience among limestone formations of bizarre and evocative shapes, wrapped in an atmosphere of mystery and natural wonder.

Visit the Church of San Vittore il Moro, the religious heart of the Porlezza community built in the seventeenth century over a pre-existing fifth-century structure. Inside you can admire the frescoes by Giulio Quaglio di Laino depicting episodes from the saint's life, a precious Baroque altar, eighteenth-century stuccowork and fine wooden sculptures.

Discover the Oratory of San Maurizio Martire, a small church set just outside the centre that still preserves its original layout. This gem of religious architecture is a precious testimony to local devotion through the centuries.

activities.text: Enjoy the Lombard shore of Lake Lugano, a body of water shared between Italy and Switzerland offering views of rare beauty. Porlezza is a renowned holiday resort prized for its crystal-clear waters, equipped beaches and relaxed atmosphere that draws visitors from all over Europe.

visits.text: Admire the Palazzo Verbania, an Art Nouveau architectural jewel built in the nineteenth century that regularly hosts art exhibitions and cultural events. This elegant historic building is the cultural heart of the town, bearing witness to the period of great prosperity Luino enjoyed as a major trading centre on Lake Maggiore.

Visit the Church of San Pietro, one of the most significant religious buildings in the old town preserving precious artistic testimonies of local devotion. Together with the Church of San Giuseppe, it forms the sacred heritage of this fascinating lakeside town.

Don't miss the famous Luino Market, one of the largest open-air markets in the area and among the oldest in Europe. Every Wednesday the town centre streets come alive with stalls offering local produce, handicrafts and gastronomic specialities, attracting visitors from across the region and nearby Switzerland.

activities.text: Stroll along the magnificent Luino lakefront, one of Lake Maggiore's most beautiful promenades winding between elegant historic buildings and lush green spaces. The view over the crystal-clear waters and surrounding mountains invites moments of pure relaxation and contemplation.

Venture onto the panoramic trails around Luino, such as the walk to Colmegna along the lake or the hike to Monte Bedea and the charming Laghetto di Moncucco, immersed in Alpine scenery of extraordinary beauty.

map_keywords: Chiesa Santi Filippo e Giacomo Laveno-Mombello

visits.text: Visit the prepositural Church of Saints Philip and James, known as the old church, a religious building dating from 1315 that is the historic spiritual heart of Laveno. Beside it stands the Church of Sant'Ambrogio, built in 1940 by architect Paolo Mezzanotte in a modern style, together forming the sacred heritage of this charming lakeside town.

Discover Villa De Angeli Frua, an elegant eighteenth-century residence donated to the municipality and now home to the public library. The magnificent Parco delle Terrazze surrounding it offers breathtaking views of Lake Maggiore, with an evocative entrance tunnel that in spring is draped in blooming wisteria, creating a fairy-tale setting.

Ride the cable car up to Poggio Sant'Elsa for a privileged perspective of Lake Maggiore. This panoramic excursion offers spectacular views of the blue lake waters, the Borromean Islands in the distance and the Alpine skyline, delivering an unforgettable experience for nature and photography lovers.

activities.text: Stroll along the Laveno lakefront, one of Lake Maggiore's most evocative promenades offering views of the town, Stresa on the opposite shore and the famous Borromean Islands. From here ferries depart for Verbania, giving you the chance to explore both sides of the lake.

map_keywords: Isola di San Giulio Orta San Giulio
Basilica San Giulio Orta San Giulio
Salita della Motta Orta San Giulio
Sacro Monte di Orta Orta San Giulio

visits.text: Take a motorboat to the Island of San Giulio, a vision of pure poetry floating on the lake waters. Legend has it that in the fourth century the Greek holy traveller Giulio drove out a serpent that ruled the island. Walk the Via del Silenzio, a ten-minute loop dotted with meditative signs inviting reflection and introspection.

Climb the Salita della Motta, a picturesque cobbled lane lined with frescoed houses leading to the Church of Santa Maria Assunta. From this elevated point you can admire one of the lake's most evocative panoramas, with the Island of San Giulio seemingly floating on the crystal-clear waters.

Visit the small Church of San Rocco on the main street, a hidden gem that wins you over with its welcoming and spiritual atmosphere. Beside it stands the Palazzo Comunale with its wonderful flower garden overlooking the lake, a corner of paradise in the heart of the village.

Explore the Sacro Monte di Orta, a UNESCO World Heritage Site rising on the hill above the village. This sixteenth- to seventeenth-century devotional complex comprises twenty frescoed chapels dedicated to the life of Saint Francis of Assisi, set in an ancient wood with spectacular views of the lake and island.

activities.text: Wander through the cobbled lanes to Piazza Motta, the beating heart of the village overlooked by the sixteenth-century Palazzotto with its elegant arcades. From here public motorboats depart for the Island of San Giulio, offering unforgettable photographic vistas from the very moment of boarding.

Treat yourself to a lakeside stroll admiring the ancient frescoed palaces and breathing in the romantic atmosphere that also captivated Nietzsche, who arrived here almost by chance and was left spellbound by the beauty of the place. Villa Crespi, a Moorish-style masterpiece, is home to Antonino Cannavacciuolo's celebrated Michelin-starred restaurant.

visits.text: Explore Isola Bella, the jewel of the Borromean Islands housing the splendid Palazzo Borromeo, a perfectly preserved noble residence with precious paintings, tapestries and antique furnishings. The magnificent Borromean Gardens, created in the seventeenth century in pure Italian Baroque style, cascade over ten terraces towards the lake with an extraordinary variety of floral species and monumental statues.

Visit Isola Madre, the largest and most evocative of the Borromean Islands, wrapped in a quiet, suspended atmosphere. The sixteenth-century palace houses vast collections of period objects and a curious display of historic puppets, while the lush botanical garden is celebrated for its magnificent camellias, azaleas and exotic plants.

Admire Villa Pallavicino, an elegant nineteenth-century residence surrounded by a vast English-style garden hosting a fascinating zoological park. Among magnificent avenues lined with ancient trees and a wonderful botanical garden, you can encounter kangaroos, fallow deer, zebras and llamas in a beautifully kept natural setting, ideal for families with children.

Discover the Church of Sant'Ambrogio in the heart of Stresa, an elegant example of Neoclassical architecture with a Greek-cross plan completed in 1790. Inside lie the relics of San Vitaliano, a young Christian martyr venerated as protector of children, whose cult was introduced in 1835 following the transfer of his remains from the Roman catacombs.

activities.text: Ride the cable car to the summit of the Mottarone at 1,491 metres, from where you can admire one of the most spectacular panoramas in the Alps. The view stretches from Lake Maggiore to the Po Plain, from the peaks of Monte Rosa to the charming Giardino Botanico Alpinia, creating an unforgettable experience in every season.

Stroll along the romantic Stresa lakefront, lined with magnificent noble villas, exclusive historic hotels and manicured gardens lapped by the waters of Lake Maggiore. From here boats depart for the enchanting Borromean Islands and the extraordinary panorama of the Alps reflected in the lake.

map_keywords: Santuario Madonna del Buon Rimedio Verbania

visits.text: Lose yourself in the magnificent Botanical Gardens of Villa Taranto, twenty hectares of pure wonder on the shores of Lake Maggiore. Created by Scottish Captain McEacharn from 1931, these enchanting English-style gardens feature labyrinths of dahlias, water lily pools, valleys of rhododendrons, waterfalls and fountains in a place where care and love for nature are palpable at every turn.

Visit Villa San Remigio on the Colle della Castagnola, built in the early twentieth century by the poet and musician Marquis Silvio della Valle di Casanova and the painter Sofia Browne. Their dream garden is a true hymn to love, composed of several themed gardens overlooking the lake in a romantic atmosphere suspended in time.

Discover Villa Giulia and its park in the heart of Pallanza, built in the mid-nineteenth century by the Branca family, inventors of the famous Fernet. Now municipal property, it hosts temporary exhibitions while the park, always open and free, offers a privileged view of the lake and the picturesque village of Pallanza.

activities.text: Walk the lakeside promenade from Verbania to Pallanza, a pedestrian and cycle path of about 2 km offering breathtaking panoramas, ancient villas and dream palaces. An itinerary for daydreaming of a less hectic life on the lakefront, imagining yourself in an age of noble titles and grand balls.

Venture along the path to the Church of Buon Rimedio, an ancient mule track through jasmine-scented woods to a small eighteenth-century church. From this natural balcony overlooking Lake Maggiore you can enjoy breathtaking panoramic views and picnic at the equipped area with lakeside tables.

Explore the historic village of Pallanza with the Museo del Paesaggio housed in the Palazzo Viani Dugnani, rich in paintings, sculptures and archaeological finds. Then treat yourself to a stop at one of the lakeside terraces on the main square to savour the relaxed atmosphere of this enchanting corner of Piedmont.

visits.text: Ride the funicular up to Città Alta, one of Italy's best-preserved medieval villages enclosed within the majestic Venetian Walls, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Wandering through the cobbled streets you'll discover centuries of history and Renaissance art in a timeless atmosphere that has captivated travellers of every era.

Admire Piazza Vecchia, the true civic drawing room overlooked by the Palazzo della Ragione, the Civic Tower (Campanone) and the Angelo Mai Library. Every evening the Campanone bell tolls one hundred times, just as in Venetian times when it signalled the closing of the city gates, evoking an atmosphere steeped in historical charm.

Visit the Basilica of Santa Maria Maggiore with its richly Baroque interiors adorned with tapestries and gilded stuccowork, and the adjacent Cappella Colleoni, a Renaissance masterpiece built as a mausoleum for the condottiere Bartolomeo Colleoni. The chapel's polychrome marble façades rank among the finest in Italy.

Explore the Accademia Carrara, one of Italy's most prestigious art galleries with over 1,800 paintings by artists such as Botticelli, Raphael, Mantegna, Titian and Canaletto. Directly opposite stands the GAMeC, the Gallery of Modern and Contemporary Art, creating a unique dialogue between tradition and modernity.

activities.text: Ride even higher on the San Vigilio Funicular to the Castle, crossing breathtaking landscapes with spectacular views of the city rooftops, medieval towers and the Lombard plain. From the castle ramparts you can enjoy one of the most beautiful sunsets in Lombardy.

Lose yourself in the Lorenzo Rota Botanical Garden, a small urban Eden of 2,400 sq m on the panoramic terraces of Città Alta. From here you can admire a spectacular view of the city and surrounding mountains, with the Venetian Walls seeming to merge with the landscape in perfect harmony.

visits.text: Explore the Museo di Santa Giulia, an extraordinary monastic complex and the city's main museum housing treasures of exceptional historical and artistic importance. From the ninth-century Cross of Desiderius to the incredible frescoes of the Nuns' Choir, from the remains of ancient Roman Domus houses to the marvellous interiors of the Basilica of San Salvatore, it is part of the UNESCO site in the heart of Brescia.

Admire the Roman Archaeological Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site comprising Piazza del Foro, the Capitolium with the hall dedicated to the Winged Victory, the Republican Sanctuary, the ancient theatre and the Roman Basilica. This extraordinary complex bears witness to the greatness of Brixia, one of northern Italy's most important cities in the Roman era.

Visit Piazza Loggia, the beating heart of Brescia characterised by a splendid blend of styles with a clear Venetian imprint. The Palazzo della Loggia, built between 1492 and 1570 with contributions from Palladio and Sansovino, dominates the square alongside the Clock Tower with its ancient astronomical clock.

Discover the Pinacoteca Tosio Martinengo, one of northern Italy's most important galleries with works by Raphael, Foppa, Moretto, Romanino, Lotto, Ceruti and Hayez. The route through 21 exhibition halls offers an intense journey from late Gothic to the early nineteenth century through the great Brescian Renaissance painting.

activities.text: Climb to Brescia Castle on the Colle Cidneo, a great fortress built between the fourteenth and sixteenth centuries in a commanding position over the city. A favourite destination for relaxing walks in the greenery, it houses the Luigi Marzoli Arms Museum and offers breathtaking views of the city and the Bergamo valleys.

Stroll through Piazza Paolo VI admiring the Duomo Vecchio, a rare example of Romanesque architecture with a circular plan, and the Duomo Nuovo completed in 1825. Then lose yourself in the ancient Carmine quarter with its evocative lanes, traditional taverns and bohemian atmosphere.

map_keywords: Chiesa San Pietro in Mavino Sirmione

visits.text: Admire the Scaliger Castle, a majestic medieval fortress built after the mid-fourteenth century standing at the entrance to the old town. Surrounded by the crystal-clear waters of Lake Garda, the castle with its dock, courtyard and corridors evokes the poignant atmosphere of the Della Scala era, when it served as a strategic defensive position for the entire area.

Explore the Grotte di Catullo, the evocative remains of an ancient Roman villa from the first to second century AD in a spectacular position at the tip of the peninsula. Set among ancient olive trees and overlooking the lake panorama, the ruins are linked by popular tradition to the poet Catullus. The adjacent museum houses precious artefacts from Sirmione's and Lake Garda's history.

Visit the Church of San Pietro in Mavino, the oldest in the area dating from the eighth century AD. Nestled among olive trees on the hill of the same name, it offers an atmosphere of peace and silence with its Romanesque bell tower renovated in 1070 and evocative fourteenth-century frescoes, including a fine Madonna Enthroned with Child and Saints.

Discover the Palazzo Callas Exhibitions, dedicated to the memory of the Divine Maria Callas who chose Sirmione as her Italian refuge. Follow the evocative Callas Arie On Air trail, which through ten stops at the peninsula's most characteristic locations lets you relive the unique atmosphere of the most beautiful arias performed by the celebrated soprano.

activities.text: Relax at Spiaggia Giamaica, an exotic-feeling spot at the tip of Sirmione below the Grotte di Catullo. Formed of smooth white rocks polished by the waves, surrounded by crystal-clear waters and wild vegetation, it offers spectacular views towards the Rocca di Manerba and the northern part of the lake.

Treat yourself to a boat trip around the Sirmione peninsula to admire the harbour, Scaliger Castle, the Grotte di Catullo, the thermal facilities and Villa Callas from a unique perspective, interspersed with wonderful natural panoramas and lush Mediterranean vegetation.

Stroll through the old village from the Scaliger Castle to the Grotte di Catullo, immersing yourself in local history through evocative lanes, the remains of the Church of San Salvatore and Roman-era sites, with refreshing stops in the parks and characteristic establishments of the old town.

map_keywords: Castello di Desenzano Desenzano del Garda
Duomo Santa Maria Maddalena Desenzano del Garda
Museo Archeologico Rambotti Desenzano del Garda

visits.text: Explore the Castle of Desenzano del Garda, an imposing fortress erected in the early Middle Ages probably on the remains of an ancient Roman castrum. Of the original construction, portions of walls and truncated corner towers survive; from the panoramic observatory at the top you can enjoy a spectacular view of Lake Garda that amply rewards the climb.

Visit the Cathedral of Santa Maria Maddalena, a splendid example of late Brescian Renaissance built between the late sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries. Inside it houses important works by Andrea Celesti, and in the Chapel of the Blessed Sacrament you can admire G.B. Tiepolo's celebrated Last Supper from 1738, a masterpiece of eighteenth-century Venetian painting.

Discover the Roman Villa, the most important testimony to the great late-antique villas of northern Italy. Built between the first and fourth centuries AD and accidentally discovered in 1921, it preserves over 240 sq m of extraordinary polychrome mosaics featuring cupids, maenads, satyrs and wild animals, bearing witness to the refinement of Roman life on the lake.

Admire the Giovanni Rambotti Archaeological Museum, rich in prehistoric finds from the Lake Garda area dating mainly from the Bronze Age. Among the treasures it houses is what is considered the oldest wooden plough in the world, a precious testimony to the first farming communities that inhabited these shores.

activities.text: Stroll through the picturesque Porto Vecchio, one of Desenzano's most evocative corners overlooked by the broad arcades of Palazzo Todeschini. The dock took shape in the days of the Venetian Republic; don't miss the curious Stone of the Unemployed from 1555, where town criers and those condemned for bankruptcy would stand.

map_keywords: Fortezza di Peschiera Peschiera del Garda
Santuario Madonna del Frassino Peschiera del Garda

visits.text: Explore the Fortress of Peschiera del Garda, an extraordinary example of military architecture enclosing the entire old town. Its walls, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2017, bear witness to centuries of history: from Roman fort to Scaliger stronghold, from sixteenth-century Venetian fortress to Habsburg citadel. Enter through the monumental Porta Verona or Porta Brescia to discover this defensive gem.

Visit the Sanctuary of the Madonna del Frassino, a splendid monumental church nestled in nature at the foot of the Lake Garda hills. Built between 1511 and 1514 on the spot where the Madonna appeared to the farmer Bartolomeo Broglia, the sanctuary kept by the Franciscan Friars Minor features a single nave and two marvellous frescoed chapels.

activities.text: Stroll along the Lungolago Mazzini, a wonderful lakeside promenade stretching about 700 metres from the old town to the Lido dei Cappuccini. This panoramic route, perfect at sunset, is lined with elegant establishments and offers spectacular views of the crystal-clear waters of Lake Garda.

Relax at the Lido Cappuccini, one of the lake's most popular bathing establishments where you can hire sun loungers and pedalos, swim in the clear waters and enjoy a refreshing break on the pebble beach, with lifeguard service and a restaurant right on the waterfront.

visits.text: Let yourself be enchanted by the Arena di Verona, a majestic Roman amphitheatre built in 30 AD and splendidly preserved. Originally capable of seating up to 30,000 spectators, today it is one of the world's most prestigious venues for open-air opera and concerts, where the perfect acoustics and magical atmosphere make every performance unforgettable.

Visit Juliet's House on Via Cappello, the iconic symbol of Shakespearean romance with its famous balcony and courtyard that have inspired the timeless love story. In the courtyard you can admire the bronze statue of Juliet by Nereo Costantini, which tradition says brings luck in love to those who touch it.

Explore Castelvecchio and the Ponte Scaligero, an imposing medieval fortress with a magnificent fortified bridge offering breathtaking views over the River Adige. The museum inside houses extraordinary collections of medieval and Renaissance art, sculptures, paintings and the city's ancient bells displayed across 29 unique rooms.

Admire the Basilica of San Zeno Maggiore, a masterpiece of Romanesque architecture built between 967 and 1398 AD.
