activities.text: Visit to Villa Cetinale: Built in the late 17th century for the Chigi family by architect Carlo Fontana, this villa is accompanied by a citrus garden and the Thebaid Park—a wood crossed by a meditation path leading to chapels, grottoes, ponds, and statues. The Hermitage of the Scala, accessible by 300 penitential steps carved in stone, dominates the whole.

map_keywords: Complesso Santa Maria della Scala Siena

visits.text: Piazza del Campo: Laid out between 1293 and 1349, this shell-shaped square is divided into nine sectors symbolizing the Council of Nine that governed Siena at the height of its power. The red brick paving is bordered by eleven lanes converging toward the center—an urban composition unmatched in Italy. The Palazzo Pubblico (13th century) closes the concave arc and the Torre del Mangia rises to 102 meters, built from 1338 to 1348, the third tallest medieval tower in Italy.

Civic Museum (Palazzo Pubblico): On the palace's first and second floors, the Hall of the Mappamondo and the Hall of Peace preserve Ambrogio Lorenzetti's frescoes on the Effects of Good and Bad Government (1338-1340)—one of the first secular painting cycles in Western history, representing the city of Siena itself.

Santa Maria della Scala Complex: A former pilgrims' hospital founded in the 11th century, one of Europe's oldest, this 19,000 m² complex is now a vast museum. The Sala del Pellegrinaio, painted between 1440 and 1444 by Domenico di Bartolo and Vecchietta, documents medieval hospital life with striking documentary precision.

activities.text: Walk through the contrade: Siena is divided into 17 contrade (districts), each with its own church, museum, and animal emblem. Getting lost in the steep lanes between Campo and Duomo—passing by the 12th-century Fonte Branda and Via di Città—reveals a remarkably preserved Gothic city, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

visits.text: Cathedral (Duomo di Siena): Built in white Carrara marble and black Montagnola marble, the polychrome 13th-14th century façade is one of the peaks of Italian Gothic. The interior, with three naves of 50 alternating white and black marble columns, houses a floor in marquetry of 56 inlaid marble panels created between 1369 and 1547, long covered to protect it. The Piccolomini Library (1495), painted by Pinturicchio, documents the life of Pope Pius II in ten large scenes of exceptional Renaissance realism.

Baptistery of San Giovanni: Attached to the cathedral's apse, this 14th-century Gothic baptistery houses the monumental baptismal font (1427-1430), a collective work by Jacopo della Quercia, Donatello, Lorenzo Ghiberti, and other Florentine and Sienese masters—a unique synthesis between the two schools.

National Picture Gallery: Housed in the 14th-century Palazzo Buonsignori, this collection traces 400 years of Sienese painting, from the gold backgrounds of Duccio di Buoninsegna to Simone Martini, Ambrogio Lorenzetti, and the 15th-century masters. Nowhere else can you measure so clearly the specificity of the Sienese school versus Florence.

Sanctuary of Saint Catherine: On St. Anthony's Hill, the old dyers' quarter houses the birthplace of Catherine Benincasa (1347-1380), Doctor of the Church. The ensemble of chapels, oratories, and loggias built around the family home in the 15th century constitutes one of Siena's most intimate places of devotion.

activities.text: Palio di Siena: Italy's most famous horse race pits ten of the seventeen contrade against each other each year on July 2 and August 16 on the Piazza del Campo covered with sand. Documented origins date back to the 14th century. The silk banner bearing the image of the Madonna—the 'Cencio'—rewards the victorious district. Book grandstand seats several months in advance.

practical_info.booking: Grandstand reservations 6 months in advance minimum

map_keywords: Abbazia di Monte Oliveto Maggiore Asciano

visits.text: Palazzo Corboli Museum: Housed in a 12th-century palace that belonged to the noble Bandinelli family, this museum unfolds over three floors a collection of sacred art and archaeology. The archaeological section gathers Etruscan and Roman remains from the territory, including Ambrogio Lorenzetti's Badia a Rofeno triptych and the remarkable 14th-century Wheel of Barlaam.

Torre della Mencia and Piazza del Grano: Standing 25 meters high, this medieval tower dominates Asciano's oldest square, once Siena's granary. The Fontana della Mengia at the tower's base, with its stone and bronze Gorgon head, is accompanied by a legend: whoever drinks from this fountain always returns to Asciano.

Abbey of Monte Oliveto Maggiore: Founded in 1313 by Bernardo Tolomei, a Sienese nobleman renouncing his fortune for a hermit's life, this red brick Benedictine abbey emerges from the woods of the Crete Senesi. Its Great Cloister is entirely decorated with frescoes by Luca Signorelli (late 15th century) and Il Sodoma (early 16th century) on the life of Saint Benedict—one of the masterpieces of Italian Renaissance painting. The monks still chant Gregorian vespers.

activities.text: Hiking in the Crete Senesi Park: The Crete Senesi Life Park covers the Asciano territory with marked, not-difficult trails through the clay hills. Trail No. 2, starting from Asciano, offers the widest views of the biancane—these characteristic whitish clay formations—and rejoins the historic center to finish at the Corboli Museum.
Palio dei Ciuchi: Every Sunday in September, Asciano's seven districts compete through the village streets—on donkeyback rather than horseback, a popular wink at Siena's Palio.

map_keywords: Museo d'Arte Sacra della Val d'Arbia Buonconvento
Chiesa dei Santi Pietro e Paolo Buonconvento

visits.text: Porta Senese and ramparts: Buonconvento is one of the Italian villages that best preserves its medieval ramparts. Built from the late 13th century by Siena to protect this strategic node of Val d'Arbia on the Via Cassia, the walls still completely encircle the center. The Porta Senese, to the north, preserves its massive original wooden doors—the Porta Romana on the south side was destroyed in 1944 during the German retreat to delay the Allied advance.

Museum of Sacred Art of Val d'Arbia: Housed in the Palazzo Ricci Socini, a 19th-century building restored in Art Nouveau style in 1907 by architect Gino Chierici, this museum is a double curiosity—Sienese paintings from the 13th to 19th centuries displayed in a still-intact Liberty interior. The collection founded in 1926 by Father Don Crescenzio Massari includes the Madonna of Buonconvento by Duccio di Buoninsegna (1290-1295), Pietro Lorenzetti, Luca di Tommé, and canvases from the 17th-century Sienese school.

Church of Saints Peter and Paul and Palazzo Podestarile: The 14th-century church, rebuilt in the 18th century, was according to tradition the death place of Emperor Henry VII of Luxembourg in 1313—an engraved marble stone on its left façade bears the date 1103, possibly that of its foundation. The neighboring Palazzo Podestarile, with its Clock Tower and 25 podestà coats of arms, recalls that Buonconvento was Val d'Arbia's most important administrative center.

activities.text: Nova Eroica: Every spring, Buonconvento hosts this amateur cycling ride on dirt roads (strade bianche) with routes for all levels, including families and children—an accessible version of Gaiole's Eroica.

map_keywords: Museo Civico e Diocesano d'Arte Sacra Montalcino

visits.text: Fortress of Montalcino: Built in 1361 by reusing earlier 13th-century structures, this pentagonal fortress was the last bastion to resist during the fall of the Republic of Siena to the Medici in 1555—Sienese refugees held out here until 1559. Its interior enoteca allows you to taste Brunello in the very symbol of republican resistance. From the ramparts, the view stretches across Val d'Orcia, Monte Amiata, and the Maremma.

Piazza del Popolo and Palazzo dei Priori: The 13th-century communal palace, adorned with heraldic coats of arms from Montalcino's history, has a slender bell tower whose carillons still punctuate the life of the town. It's the central square of a network of narrow, steep lanes typical of Sienese medieval urbanism.

Civic and Diocesan Museum of Sacred Art: Housed in the former 14th-century convent of Sant'Agostino (with its two 16th-century cloisters), this museum preserves over 200 works on three floors, including the 12th-century Crucifix of Sant'Antimo, a Madonna by Simone Martini, and the Coronation of the Virgin by Bartolo di Fredi.

Abbey of Sant'Antimo: Ten kilometers south via the SP55, in a valley hollow planted with cypresses and olive trees, this Benedictine Romanesque abbey is mentioned as early as 813 in an act of Louis the Pious. The main church, built in the 12th century in the Cistercian Burgundian style, is one of the finest examples of French Romanesque architecture in Italy. Since 1992, a community of Premonstratensian canons has sung the Gregorian offices—vespers at 6:30 PM can be heard from afar.

activities.text: Brunello di Montalcino tasting: Brunello is produced exclusively from Sangiovese Grosso on the commune's territory. Clemente Santi was the first to get it recognized in 1869. The fortress cellars or surrounding estates (Poggio di Sotto, Ciacci Piccolomini) offer tastings of the current vintage and riserva.
Sagra del Tordo: Every last Sunday in October, Montalcino's four districts compete in archery in this medieval festival commemorating the region's hunting traditions.

map_keywords: Collegiata dei Santi Quirico e Giulitta San Quirico d'Orcia
Palazzo Chigi Zondadari San Quirico d'Orcia
Cappella della Madonna di Vitaleta San Quirico d'Orcia
Via Dante Alighieri San Quirico d'Orcia

visits.text: Collegiate Church of Saints Quirico and Giulitta: One of the masterpieces of Romanesque architecture in Val d'Orcia, first mentioned in 712 during a dispute between the bishoprics of Siena and Arezzo. Its three portals are of different styles and periods: the main 12th-century Romanesque portal, supported by two columns on two lions with crocodiles in the architrave; the 13th-century Lombard portal attributed to Giovanni Pisano (Gothic inscription 'Iohes' in the spire); and a third Baroque one. Inside, a polyptych by Lucas Cranach the Elder offered by Frederick III of Germany in 1504.

Horti Leonini: An Italian garden created around 1581 by Diomede Leoni on land offered by Francesco I de' Medici. The diamond-shaped plan of the lower part, with its boxwood hedges and triangular parterres, is one of the rare Renaissance public garden achievements open to travelers—Leoni had wanted it accessible to Via Francigena pilgrims. At the center, a statue of Cosimo III de' Medici by Giuseppe Mazzuoli (1688). Above, a Roman-era double-headed Janus marks the boundary between the geometric garden and the wild ragnaia.

Palazzo Chigi Zondadari: Built in the second half of the 17th century by Cardinal Flavio Chigi, nephew of Pope Alexander VII, this palazzo faces the Collegiate Church and dominates Via Dante Alighieri. Seriously damaged during World War II, it now belongs to the commune.

Chapel of the Madonna di Vitaleta: Three kilometers southeast of San Quirico, at the end of a marked 800-meter dirt path, this small 16th-century chapel surrounded by cypresses is one of the most photographed images of Val d'Orcia. The cypress avenue is most evocative at sunrise.

activities.text: Festa del Barbarossa: Every last weekend in June, San Quirico commemorates the 1155 meeting between Emperor Frederick Barbarossa and Pope Adrian IV's emissaries. Medieval costume parades, flag throwing, and archery competitions.

Val d'Orcia hiking trails: San Quirico lies at the intersection of stage 35 and stage 36 of the Tuscan Via Francigena. The section toward Bagno Vignoni (4 km) runs along cypress fields and vineyards of the Orcia DOC appellation.

visits.text: Piazza Pio II and Renaissance ensemble: In 1459, Pope Pius II—born Enea Silvio Piccolomini in the village of Corsignano—commissioned Bernardo Rossellino, a student of Leon Battista Alberti, to transform his birthplace into a model city. In just three years (1459-1462), Rossellino erected the cathedral, Palazzo Piccolomini, Palazzo Borgia, and the trapezoidal Piazza—the first concrete realization of Renaissance humanist urban ideals. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1996.

Cathedral of the Assumption: Built between 1459 and 1462 by Rossellino, its exterior façade is purely Renaissance in travertine, while the three-nave interior draws on the late Gothic of South German churches—a compromise wanted by Pius II, influenced by German cardinal-philosopher Nicholas of Cusa. It houses the Assumption by Il Vecchietta, the Madonna in Majesty by Matteo di Giovanni, and Flemish tapestries in the basement.

Palazzo Piccolomini: Pope Pius II's summer residence built in 1459 by Rossellino on the model of Florence's Palazzo Rucellai. The triple-level loggia on the south side opens onto a hanging garden with panoramic views of Val d'Orcia, Monte Amiata, and Enea Silvio's birthplace, Corsignano. The interior preserves period furniture, a library, and a weapons collection.

activities.text: Fiera del Cacio: On the first Sunday in September, Pienza's six districts compete in the game of cacio al fuso—rolling a wheel of Pecorino di Pienza as far as possible—along with tastings and markets.
Pecorino di Pienza tasting: Pienza's sheep cheese comes in fresh, semi-stagionato, and stagionato varieties. The shops on Corso Rossellino offer tastings with Val d'Orcia DOC wines.

visits.text: Piazza Grande: At the hilltop (605 m), this Renaissance square is surrounded by the 15th-century Palazzo Comunale (a miniature Sienese version of Florence's Palazzo Vecchio, with panoramic tower), the Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta begun in 1592 by Ippolito Scalza, Palazzo Contucci by Antonio da Sangallo, and Palazzo Tarugi. At the center, the Pozzo dei Grifi e dei Leoni, a Renaissance well designed by Sangallo.

Historic cellars of Vino Nobile: Beneath the Renaissance palaces of the center, a network of tunnels carved into tuff and brick vaults houses the oak barrels in which Vino Nobile di Montepulciano DOCG rests. The Contucci family has produced wine here since the 11th century; the Cantina De' Ricci extends over several medieval levels. Vino Nobile, based on Prugnolo (local Sangiovese), was already being exported to the Medici court in the 15th century.

Tempio di San Biagio: Five hundred meters below the ramparts, this Renaissance church begun in 1518 by Antonio da Sangallo the Elder is one of the finest examples of central-plan Renaissance architecture in Italy—inspired by Bramante's Tempietto. The travertine façade dominates the Valdichiana vineyards.

Strade bianche and Val d'Orcia: Montepulciano is the natural starting point for exploring the white roads from Val di Chiana to Val d'Orcia. The road toward Monticchiello (4 km) runs along vineyards and cypresses with views of classic Tuscan panoramas.

activities.text: Vino Nobile DOCG tasting: The cellars in the center offer guided tours of the tunnels carved into tuff with comparative tastings of Rosso di Montepulciano DOC, Vino Nobile DOCG, and Vino Nobile Riserva.

Bravio delle Botti: Every last Sunday in August, Montepulciano's eight districts compete by pushing 80 kg barrels along Via Gracciano nel Corso to Piazza Grande—a tradition documented since the 14th century.

visits.text: Castello Manenti: Built in the 12th-13th centuries and restructured in the 15th century, the castle is surrounded by a double wall enclosing a square keep and round towers. It was a Sienese bastion throughout the Middle Ages. Francesco Todeschini Piccolomini was born in Sarteano in 1439—he became pope for less than a month in 1503 as Pius III, immediate successor to Alexander VI.

Civic Archaeological Museum: Housed in the 16th-century Palazzo Gabrielli (whose structure dates to the Middle Ages), this museum inaugurated in 1997 traces Sarteano's history from the 9th to the 1st century BC from the surrounding Etruscan necropolises. The lower floor reconstructs in life-size—a technique unique in Italy—the 4th-century BC Tomb of the Infernal Quadriga, whose frescoes depict the god Charun driving a chariot pulled by two lions and two griffins, followed by a three-headed serpent and a funeral banquet scene.

Pianacce Necropolis: One kilometer from the center, this necropolis discovered in 1954—with major revelations in 2003—contains 21 tombs carved into travertine. Visits to the original Tomb of the Infernal Quadriga are done only with the archaeological museum, which provides guides.

activities.text: Sarteano thermal baths: Freshwater thermal pools (23-28°C) are found in the Sarteano area, in a natural setting among vineyards and olive groves of Val di Chiana.

map_keywords: Museo Civico per la Preistoria del Monte Cetona Cetona

visits.text: Rocca di Cetona: The first mentions of the castle date to the 10th century. In the Middle Ages, Cetona first belonged to the Republic of Orvieto then to that of Siena, which fortified it in the 15th century. The fortress, surrounded by centuries-old holm oaks and now private property, overlooks Piazza Garibaldi—a large oval square laid out in the 16th century, surrounded by the Terrosi, Vitelli palaces and two churches, Santissima Annunziata and San Michele Arcangelo.

Civic Museum of Prehistory of Monte Cetona: Housed in the Palazzo Comunale, this museum documents human occupation of the territory from the Paleolithic to the Bronze Age, from discoveries made in caves and archaeological sites of Monte Cetona during the 20th century. The adjacent natural archaeological park, called Belvédère, includes travertine cavities that served as places of worship and burial.

Collegiate Church of the Santissima Trinità: A 12th-13th century Gothic church that houses a fresco by Pinturicchio, one of the painters of the Sistine Chapel. The San Francesco convent (2 km from the village), founded according to tradition by Saint Francis of Assisi in 1212, marks the border between Tuscany and Umbria.

activities.text: Hike on Monte Cetona (1,148 m): The volcanic mountain dominating the village offers views west toward Val d'Orcia and Monte Amiata, and east toward Valdichiana and Umbria. The first section is drivable, then an hour's walk to the summit.

practical_info.distance: 8 km round trip from the village

map_keywords: Santuario etrusco-romano del Bagno Grande San Casciano dei Bagni
Scavi archeologici Bagno Grande San Casciano dei Bagni
Terme di San Casciano San Casciano dei Bagni

visits.text: Etruscan-Roman Sanctuary of Bagno Grande: Excavations conducted from 2019 uncovered in November 2022 more than twenty-four bronze statues dated from 200 BC to 100 AD, perfectly preserved in the mud of thermal springs—including five nearly a meter tall representing Apollo, Hygieia, and matrons. With over 5,000 pieces in gold, silver, and bronze, Jacopo Tabolli's team (University for Foreigners of Siena) called this discovery 'the most important since the Riace bronzes in 1972.' The sanctuary, founded in the 3rd century BC by the Etruscans, was developed by the Romans under the name Balnea Clusinae. The thermal water (42°C) preserved the Etruscan and Latin inscriptions of donor families.

Archaeological site of Bagno Grande: The bronzes from the 2022-2024 excavations are circulating in a traveling exhibition (Quirinale, Naples, Reggio Calabria, Berlin in 2025) while the permanent museum—planned in a 16th-century palace in the historic center—is under construction, with a first inauguration expected in late 2026. Meanwhile, the site itself is visitable via guided tours organized every Saturday in summer by the municipal archaeological group, departing from the Stanze Cassianensi at the Palazzo Comunale.

activities.text: Thermal baths: The springs of San Casciano dei Bagni have been known since the 9th century BC and are still used today for their therapeutic properties. Modern thermal establishments welcome visitors year-round.

visits.text: Fortress of Radicofani: Perched at 896 meters on a basalt cliff, this is one of the most spectacular positions in all of Tuscany. First mentioned in 973, transferred to the Carolingians in the 9th century, it passed to the Papal State in 1153 then to Siena in the late 15th century. In 1297, Messer Ghino di Tacco—a gentleman bandit from Torrita di Siena, mentioned by both Dante in the Divine Comedy and Boccaccio in the Decameron—seized it and made it his base for years of benevolent brigandage. The Cassero Museum inside traces the site's history from the Bronze Age to the 16th century.

Medieval village and Medici Post: The town at the Rocca's foot preserves stone lanes and palaces including the Palazzo Pretorio and the 16th-century Villa Medici. The Casale della Posta (Osteria Grossa), built by the Medici in the 1500s and framed by a double row of cypresses, served as a postal relay for Via Francigena travelers until the 19th century—Casanova, Stendhal, Dickens, and Goethe stayed here.

activities.text: View from the Rocca tower: The view from the summit extends to Monte Cetona, Val d'Orcia, Monte Amiata, and on clear days, Lakes Trasimeno and Bolsena. Radicofani is one of the five Val d'Orcia communes listed as UNESCO World Heritage Sites since 2004.

Bagni San Filippo thermal baths: Twenty kilometers northwest of Radicofani, these natural pools of 48°C sulfurous water are freely accessible in a forest setting. The white travertine waterfalls have shaped natural basins known since antiquity.

visits.text: Rocca Aldobrandesca: A quadrilateral fortress in lava stone first mentioned in 1194 in a diploma of Emperor Henry VI, it successively belonged to the Abbey of San Salvatore, the Aldobrandeschi family, the Orsini of Pitigliano, then the Republic of Siena which seized it between 1415 and 1430. Its two towers—the large one serving as keep, and the small one defending the gate—are characteristic of Aldobrandeschi fortresses on Amiata. Now a museum, it offers panoramic views of the Paglia valley.

Church of San Francesco and Palazzo Bourbon del Monte: The church, from a 15th-century convent, has a façade preceded by a simple loggia and preserves fragments of frescoes from the 14th-century Sienese school. The Palazzo Bourbon del Monte, at the village's edge, was the residence of feudal lords from 1601 to the late 18th century, when Grand Duke Leopold I made Piancastagnaio the capital of an autonomous community.

activities.text: Hiking in the chestnut woods of Monte Amiata: The forests surrounding Piancastagnaio are dominated by the Amiata chestnut, a protected species. Trails depart from the village for loops of 5 to 10 km up to the first slopes of the extinct volcano (1,738 m).

seo.h1_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Siena Province
seo.intro_paragraph: Circle the medieval ramparts of Monteriggioni, fictional birthplace of Ezio Auditore, then explore the fortified cellars of Castellina and the Grand Duke's ice house in Radda. Cross Colle Val d'Elsa, world capital of crystal and birthplace of Arnolfo di Cambio, before diving into medieval Siena with its seventeen contrade and its Palio. The Crete Senesi lead to Asciano and the Abbey of Monte Oliveto, then the UNESCO-listed Val d'Orcia unfolds its landscapes from Montalcino to Pienza. To the south, Monte Amiata and its Aldobrandeschi fortresses guard Tuscany's borders.
seo.seo_hook: Stone walls crowning medieval hilltop towns, Chianti vineyards cradling centuries of winemaking, clay landscapes of the Crete Senesi, volcanic fortresses of Monte Amiata—the province of Siena, where time stands still.
featured_list: Siena: medieval capital with seventeen contrade and the legendary Palio
Monteriggioni: fortress village crowned by fourteen medieval towers
Pienza: the Renaissance's first ideal city, a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Montalcino: last bastion of the Sienese Republic and capital of Brunello
San Casciano dei Bagni: site of the 21st century's most exceptional Etruscan bronzes

places_taglines.IT::monteriggioni: Where fourteen thirteenth-century towers crown a perfect circle of stone
places_taglines.IT::castellina-in-chianti: Chianti's fortified heart, crowned by a Florentine fortress above vaulted streets
places_taglines.IT::radda-in-chianti: Former capital of the Chianti League, where heraldic arms adorn the podestà's palace
places_taglines.IT::colle-val-elsa: Where Italy's crystal empire was born in the shadow of Arnolfo's tower
places_taglines.IT::casole-elsa: An elliptical fortress village guarded by Martini's Renaissance towers
places_taglines.IT::sovicille: Where medieval bishops ruled beneath the Montagnola's slopes
places_taglines.IT::siena: Gothic city of seventeen contrade, where the Palio thunders twice each summer
places_taglines.IT::asciano: Gateway to the Crete Senesi and the frescoed cloisters of Monte Oliveto
places_taglines.IT::buonconvento: Where intact medieval walls guard a Madonna by Duccio
places_taglines.IT::montalcino: Last fortress of Sienese freedom, where Brunello ages in pentagonal walls
places_taglines.IT::san-quirico-orcia: Where Romanesque portals and Renaissance gardens frame the Via Francigena
places_taglines.IT::pienza: The Renaissance's dream made stone, Pius II's vision of the perfect city
places_taglines.IT::radicofani: Where a gentleman bandit's basalt fortress towers above the Val d'Orcia
places_taglines.IT::piancastagnaio: Ancient stronghold beneath Monte Amiata's chestnut forests
places_taglines.IT::san-casciano-bagni: Where thermal springs preserved twenty-four bronze gods for two millennia
places_taglines.IT::cetona: From Paleolithic caves to Etruscan tombs beneath a volcanic peak
places_taglines.IT::sarteano: Where the Infernal Quadriga races across fourth-century Etruscan walls
places_taglines.IT::montepulciano: Renaissance citadel above tuff-carved cellars aging Italy's noble wine

highlights: Val d'Orcia landscapes, a UNESCO World Heritage Site
Palio di Siena (July 2 and August 16)—the world's most famous horse race
Brunello di Montalcino and Vino Nobile di Montepulciano, two of Italy's most prestigious red wines
Etruscan bronzes of San Casciano dei Bagni, major archaeological discovery of 2022
Pienza, the Renaissance's first ideal city according to Alberti's canons

itineraries.title: The Finest Stops Around Florence in Two Weeks
itineraries.subtitle: From Fiesole and its Etruscan legacy to the forests of Vallombrosa, via the Medici villas of Poggio a Caiano and Artimino, Leonardo's workshops in Vinci, the Chianti hills and the Valdarno—a grand loop through the Florentine countryside.
specialties: Chianti Classico and Carmignano DOCG wines
Extra virgin olive oil from Tuscan hills

visits.text: The archaeological area of Fiesole—Roman theater, thermal baths, Etruscan-Roman temple, and museum—spreads across three hectares in the heart of the hillside. Fiesole (ancient Faesulae) was founded by the Etruscans between the 9th and 8th centuries BC and only became a Roman colony in the 1st century BC, after the Romans had already settled in the valley below to establish Florence. The theater, carved into the hillside in the 1st century BC, still hosts performances each summer during the Estate Fiesolana festival. The layered Etruscan-Roman temple—first dedicated to Minerva (a bronze owl attests to this), then converted to a Roman temple in the 1st century BC—was destroyed by fire following the Roman conquest. Cyclopean Etruscan walls remain visible on the site's northern edge. The museum, built in 1914 as an Ionic temple, preserves a fragment of a bronze Capitoline wolf and the Costantini collection of Greek and Etruscan ceramics.

The Villa Medici in Fiesole, built between 1451 and 1457 by Michelozzo for Giovanni di Cosimo de' Medici—son of Cosimo the Elder—is the first pleasure villa of the Renaissance: it broke definitively with the medieval fortress model by making the loggia and panorama the pivots of its architecture. Lorenzo the Magnificent inherited it in 1469 and turned it into a meeting place for the Platonic Academy: Marsilio Ficino, Pico della Mirandola, and Poliziano philosophized here. The villa has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2013. A singular detail: this is where the Medici first introduced citrus cultivation to Tuscany, imported from southern Italy. An assassination attempt on Lorenzo the Magnificent by the Pazzi family took place during a gathering within these walls.

Villa Peyron al Bosco di Fontelucente, 2 km from central Fiesole, is a 20th-century Italian garden designed between 1934 and 1960 by Paolo Peyron—without an architect, following only his own instinct—across 40 hectares of wooded estate. Thirty terraced levels descend toward Florence, linked by double-hedged boxwood parterres, 29 fountains fed by a 16th-century spring, statues in Vicenza stone (replacing originals destroyed during the war), and an artificial lake. Bequeathed to the Bardini-Peyron Foundation in 1998, the complex is classified as an exemplary 20th-century garden by Florence's Monuments Authority.

activities.text: Panoramic views of Florence from Piazza Mino da Fiesole or from the Villa Medici gardens, with vistas over Brunelleschi's dome, the bell towers of Santa Croce, and the Arno Valley extending to the Apennines. Evening light is particularly striking.
Hike the Sentiero dei Borghi trail between Fiesole and Settignano, through olive groves and historic villa gardens. The path follows the Etruscan walls for part of the route.

visits.text: The Parco Mediceo di Pratolino (Villa Demidoff), a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2013, is the only major Medici park whose original villa was demolished—in 1821 by the Lorraine dynasty, who found the upkeep too expensive. The 30-hectare estate was created by Francesco I de' Medici beginning in 1568: he commissioned Bernardo Buontalenti to design a 'garden of marvels' with hydraulic surprise fountains, artificial grottoes, and water features that would spread the fashion for scherzi d'acqua (water tricks) throughout Europe. Montaigne visited Pratolino in 1580-1581 and left an enthusiastic description in his Travel Journal.

The Colossus of the Apennines, carved from rock between 1579 and 1583 by Jean de Boulogne (Giambologna), stands 14 meters tall. The statue embodies the Apennine mountain chain: its skin covered in simulated stalactites and algae shows it emerging from the mountain itself. The interior contains three levels of secret rooms: a lower grotto once adorned with frescoes and mother-of-pearl, two mid-level salons where musicians performed during Medici festivities, and a fireplace in the skull—when lit, smoke would pour from the giant's nostrils. Construction reportedly cost double that of the Uffizi. An old Italian rhyme laments that such a masterpiece is hidden in the woods rather than displayed in the city.

The Buontalenti Chapel, with its hexagonal plan surrounded by a loggia, is one of the few original buildings from the Medici estate still standing. The Cupid Grotto, also by Buontalenti (1577), once contained a pivoting bronze statue that would spray visitors who stepped on a hidden trigger plate. The Statue of the Mugnone, personifying the local river by Giambologna, was reconstructed around 1830.

activities.text: Free walking in the park among centuries-old trees—oaks, cedars, horse chestnuts. The park is open free of charge on weekends and certain weekdays; deer, foxes, and hares roam semi-wild within its grounds.

map_keywords: Oratorio della Madonna dei Terremoti Scarperia

visits.text: The Palazzo dei Vicari has dominated Scarperia's main square since the town's foundation by the Florentine Republic on September 7, 1306. Conceived as a military outpost on the only road between Florence and Bologna, it became in 1415 the seat of the Florentine Vicariate administering all of Mugello. The facade bears the stone and Della Robbia majolica coats of arms of all the vicars who served here. Inside: a fresco from the school of Ghirlandaio (1501) depicting Saint Barnabas offering Scarperia to the Virgin surrounded by Florence's patron saints, and the ancient bell tower clock documented as the work of Brunelleschi—the only proof of his activity in clockmaking.

The Museum of Cutting Blades (Museo dei Ferri Taglienti), installed in the Palazzo's north wing restored in 1999, traces 700 years of knife-making in Scarperia. Since the town's founding in 1306, its strategic position on the trans-Apennine route ensured the spread of local knives throughout Europe. By the 15th century, Scarperia's reputation was international. In 1908, a law prohibiting knives exceeding the palm of the hand nearly ruined the craft; it survived and has experienced a revival since the 1990s. The museum presents the different phases of production—forging, tempering, sharpening, handle assembly.

activities.text: Visit an artisan knife workshop in the historic center. Several independent cutleries offer demonstrations of forging and assembly using traditional Mugello techniques.

The Palio del Diotto, every September 8: the town's neighborhoods compete in Renaissance costumes on the main square, celebrating the Nativity of the Virgin. The evening before features Renaissance Days with processions, falconers, and fire spectacles.

map_keywords: Pieve di San Giovanni Battista Vicchio

visits.text: Vicchio is the birthplace of two painting giants: Giotto di Bondone (born around 1267 in Vespignano) and Fra Angelico (born around 1395 in Vicchio). The Giotto House-Museum in Vespignano—a medieval farmhouse on the hill where, according to tradition, Cimabue discovered him drawing his sheep on stones—is now a cultural space open on weekends and during events. Vasari recounts this tradition and turned it into an emblematic story of artistic transmission. A bronze statue of Giotto with palette and brushes stands in Vicchio's main square.

The Museum of Sacred Art and Popular Religiosity Beato Angelico, inaugurated in 2000, was created in the 1960s to save artworks from rural churches abandoned during agricultural exodus. It preserves paintings and sculptures from the Middle Ages to the 18th century: a glazed terracotta Madonna and Child attributed to Andrea della Robbia, a Saint John the Baptist from the same workshop, and Gothic altarpieces from about fifteen Mugello churches. Of note: the former house of Benvenuto Cellini, who stayed in Vicchio between 1559 and 1571, now houses a goldsmithing school.

activities.text: Painters' Trail: a marked hiking route linking Vespignano to the Cimabue Bridge, a late 13th-century medieval bridge over the Sieve River, symbolic site of the legendary meeting between Cimabue and the young Giotto. The route crosses the same landscapes of cultivated hills that inspired the painters of Mugello.

map_keywords: Pieve di San Lorenzo Borgo San Lorenzo
Villa Pecori Giraldi Borgo San Lorenzo

visits.text: The Pieve di San Lorenzo, founded in 941, is the largest rural Romanesque building in the Florentine countryside. Its hexagonal bell tower with a semi-dodecagonal brick base, placed directly on the apse, is unique in Tuscany. The interior preserves a Madonna attributed to Giotto, a Crucifix from Giotto's school, a Virgin Enthroned attributed to Agnolo Gaddi, and a Virgin with Saints by Matteo Rosselli. The apse is entirely frescoed by Galileo Chini. On the right side facade, a polychrome terracotta tabernacle by Fornaci Chini (1926), created for the centenary of Saint Francis's death, is one of the finest street pieces of the Art Nouveau movement in Tuscany.

The Chini Museum in Villa Pecori Giraldi traces the history of the Fornaci San Lorenzo manufactory, founded in 1906 by Galileo and Chino Chini, two cousins born in Borgo San Lorenzo. Galileo Chini was one of the leading representatives of Italian Art Nouveau: his ceramics—first with floral motifs inspired by Botticelli, then with metallic-luster Deco designs—adorn the Salsomaggiore thermal baths, several Florentine palaces, and Borgo San Lorenzo's town hall. In 1911-1913, the King of Thailand invited him to Bangkok to decorate the Sathon Throne. The villa's rooms are frescoed by the Chini family: a high-quality Saint George cycle, medieval geometric motifs, and coats of arms of Florentine nobility.

activities.text: Art Nouveau Trail of Mugello: a walking or driving route among Chini works scattered throughout the town—Misericordia facade, mayor's office decorated on floors and windows, Santissimo Crocifisso oratory. Borgo San Lorenzo was named 'Città della Ceramica' (City of Ceramics) by the Italian Ministry of Economic Development in 2017.

visits.text: The Museo Leonardiano occupies two buildings: the Palazzina Uzielli and the 11th-century Castello dei Conti Guidi, given to the municipality in 1919 for the 400th anniversary of Leonardo's death and opened to the public in 1953. It presents Leonardo's machines reconstructed from his notebooks—war engines, hydraulic devices, flying mechanisms—as well as a section dedicated to the Battle of Anghiari, the unfinished mural work Leonardo designed for the Salone dei Cinquecento in Palazzo Vecchio in Florence, known only through contemporary copies. The Leonardian Library, founded in 1883, preserves manuscripts and facsimiles of the codices.

The birthplace in Anchiano, 3 km from central Vinci, is the rural farmhouse where Leonardo was born on April 15, 1452. The birth was noted by his paternal grandfather Antonio da Vinci on the last page of his family notebook: 'A grandson was born to me, son of my son Ser Piero, on April 15, a Saturday at three in the morning. He received the name Lionardo.' The house, documented since 1427, was donated to the municipality in 1952 by Count Giovanni Rasini. Since 2012, a life-size holographic installation of the aging Leonardo, speaking from Amboise while looking back toward his homeland, greets visitors. The landscape of vineyards and olive groves of Montalbano has remained very close to what Leonardo knew as a child.

activities.text: Strada Verde (Green Route): a marked 3 km trail linking Vinci to Anchiano through the vineyards and olive groves of Montalbano, in the same landscape that inspired Leonardo. Details from the landscape of the Mona Lisa have been identified in this area.

visits.text: The Villa Medici 'La Ferdinanda,' built in just four years from 1596 to 1600 by Bernardo Buontalenti for Grand Duke Ferdinand I de' Medici, is the last great Medici villa and the masterwork of Buontalenti's old age—too old to travel, he directed the works from his house on Via Maggio in Florence by correspondence. Its nickname, 'Villa of the Hundred Chimneys,' comes from Ferdinand I's desire to equip each room with its own hearth for winter hunting seasons: the chimneys, all different, bristle from the roof like a crown. The interiors are frescoed by Domenico Cresti (Il Passignano) and Bernardino Poccetti. The villa has been a UNESCO site since 2013.

The Archaeological Museum of Artimino 'Francesco Nicosia,' reinstalled since 2011 in the former grand-ducal stables of the village, displays Etruscan finds from the region: tumuli of Montefortini and Boschetti at Comeana (7th century BC, Orientalizing period), necropolis of Prato Rosello in the Artimino hills, and a 4th-century BC Etruscan red-figure krater with its perfectly preserved bronze symposium set. The Pietramarina archaeological area completes the Carmignano Archaeological Park.

The Pieve di San Leonardo ad Artimino, at the village gates, is one of the most significant examples of Romano-Lombard architecture in Tuscany. Its foundation is attributed to Countess Matilda of Canossa. The sober, compact building contrasts with the Medici Villa facing it.

activities.text: Tasting of Carmignano DOCG in a cellar in the Artimino hills. This red wine produced exclusively in the municipality of Carmignano is one of Italy's oldest appellation wines: Cosimo III de' Medici officially delimited its production zone in 1716. The dominant grape is Sangiovese, often blended with Cabernet Franc or Cabernet Sauvignon introduced to the region as early as the 17th century.

map_keywords: Salone di Leone X Poggio a Caiano
Giardino della Villa Medicea Poggio a Caiano
Museo della Natura Morta Poggio a Caiano

visits.text: The Villa Medici at Poggio a Caiano, designed by Giuliano da Sangallo for Lorenzo the Magnificent beginning in 1485 and completed under Pope Leo X in 1519, is the prototype of the Renaissance villa: raised on an arcaded podium with an Ionic loggia on the facade, it established the relationship between building and landscape that would influence all European villas for two centuries, including Palladio's. A UNESCO site since 2013, it was the scene of three historic Medici marriages: Alessandro de' Medici and Margaret of Austria (1536), Cosimo I and Eleanor of Toledo (1539), Francesco I and Bianca Cappello (1579).

The Salone di Leone X, on the first floor, houses one of the most important fresco cycles of Florentine Mannerism. The painters were summoned under Leo X (son of Lorenzo the Magnificent) around 1520 according to a program by Paolo Giovio: Pontormo painted Vertumnus and Pomona (an allegory of the seasons and agriculture), Franciabigio the Triumph of Cicero, Andrea del Sarto the Tribute to Caesar (alluding to gifts received by Lorenzo from the Sultan of Egypt in 1487). Leo X died in 1521, the works stopped. They were only completed in 1578-1582 by Alessandro Allori under Francesco I. Since 2007, the second floor houses the Museum of Still Lifes: 200 paintings from the late 16th to mid-18th century from the Medici collections.

activities.text: Walk in the villa park and visit the former stables renovated in 2000. The garden preserves rare plant species and terracotta statues. The complex includes the Cascine di Tavola—Medici agricultural estate—which defined the model of the 'villa-azienda' combining prestigious residence with productive rural exploitation.

visits.text: The Cathedral of Santo Stefano in Prato is the only Italian cathedral with an outdoor pulpit—on the right corner of the facade, under a bronze canopy by Michelozzo, the 'Pergamo del Santo Cingolo'—from which the Holy Belt of the Virgin is solemnly displayed five times a year to the people of Prato. The relic, brought from Jerusalem in 1141 by merchant Michele Dagomari, triggered construction of the Chapel of the Belt (14th century), frescoed by Agnolo Gaddi. The main chapel houses Filippo Lippi's fresco cycle (1452-1466): Stories of Saint Stephen and Saint John the Baptist, which Vasari judged 'the most excellent of all his things.' During these years, Lippi painted Lucrezia Buti, a nun from the convent of Santa Margherita, as his model for the Virgin—and abducted her on the very day of the Belt procession in 1456. Cosimo de' Medici intervened with Pope Pius II to obtain their pardon.

The Imperial Castle of Frederick II is the only medieval building of this type preserved in Tuscany and the sole example of a Swabian fortress north of the Alps. Built between 1237 and 1248 for Holy Roman Emperor Frederick II of Hohenstaufen, it served to collect taxes and duties on the territory and to symbolize imperial power against papal authority. Square plan, four corner towers, no ornamentation: an architecture of power.

The Textile Museum traces 700 years of Prato's cloth-making history, which made Prato the European capital of textiles in the Middle Ages. The city owes its medieval wealth to merchant Francesco Datini (1335-1410)—whose house-museum is also open to the public—who built one of Europe's first multinational enterprises with branches in Barcelona, Valencia, Genoa, Paris, and London. His 150,000 business letters preserved in the Prato State Archives form the world's most important medieval commercial archive.

activities.text: Luigi Pecci Center for Contemporary Art, on Prato's outskirts: one of the few Italian museums entirely dedicated to contemporary art since 1988. The ring-shaped 2016 extension by Dutch architect Maurice Nio doubles the exhibition space to 3,000 m².

map_keywords: Chiesa dei Santi Michele e Francesco Carmignano

visits.text: Pontormo's Visitation (1528-1530) is preserved in the Church of Saints Michael and Francis in Carmignano—currently undergoing restoration for water infiltration. During the works, the painting is temporarily displayed at the Villa Medici of Poggio a Caiano. The painting (202 × 156 cm, oil on wood panel) depicts the meeting of Mary and Elizabeth in a city street, surrounded by two witnesses with fixed, vacant gazes. Acid colors—reds, greens, and iridescent grays—elongated bodies apparently lifted on tiptoe, monumental drapery: Florentine Mannerism at its most accomplished. American artist Bill Viola drew inspiration from it for his video The Greeting (1995).

The Tumuli of Montefortini and Boschetti at Comeana, in the hills below the village of Artimino, are monumental tombs from the Orientalizing period (7th century BC) among the best preserved in central Tuscany. The Montefortini tumulus, about 60 meters in diameter, houses two burial chambers accessible for visits.

activities.text: Hiking or cycling through the vineyards of the Carmignano DOCG route, between the medieval village of Artimino and the wine estates of the Montalbano hills. In October the Palio dei Ciuchi takes place, a traditional race of the town's four neighborhoods on donkeys in medieval costumes.

visits.text: The Basilica-Sanctuary of Santa Maria all'Impruneta was consecrated on January 3, 1060, on the site of an Etruscan place of worship. Its foundation rests on the legend of the discovery of an icon of the Virgin during construction: the oxen carrying the materials knelt at a precise point, indicating the site of the sanctuary. This Madonna—a 12th-century painted panel—has since been the principal Marian devotion of Tuscany. In 1711, she was proclaimed co-patron of Florence. The left chapel preserves a Madonna and Child by Giovanni Pisano (1317); the Treasury Museum houses a cross attributed to Ghiberti and illuminated choir books from the 14th-16th centuries.

Impruneta has been the world's principal center of terracotta production since at least 1098 (first documented mention). The local clay, refractory and extremely durable, was used by Brunelleschi for the tiles of the dome of Santa Maria del Fiore, and by the Della Robbia workshops for their glazed ceramics. The oil jars and garden vases of Impruneta adorn the gardens of Tuscany's greatest villas.

activities.text: Visit an active terracotta workshop in Impruneta: throwing, drying, firing, and glazing according to traditional techniques. Production covers jars, tiles, sculptures, and garden pots—the same ones that adorned the Uffizi and Medici palaces.

The Fair of Saint Luke, October 18 in Impruneta, is one of Tuscany's oldest fairs, attested since the Middle Ages: originally a cattle fair linked to the Madonna procession, it now brings together markets, banquets, and horse races on Piazza Buondelmonti.

map_keywords: Borgo di Montefioralle Greve in Chianti
Museo del Vino Greve in Chianti

visits.text: Piazza Matteotti in Greve in Chianti has been the commercial center of the vineyard for five centuries. Its atypical triangular shape—surrounded by arcades under which merchants displayed wines and oils—is crossed by the Via Chiantigiana, the only road between Florence and Siena until the 18th century. At the center stands a statue of Giovanni da Verrazzano, born in 1485 at the nearby Castello di Verrazzano, who discovered New York Bay in 1524—his name is preserved in the Verrazano-Narrows Bridge between Brooklyn and Staten Island. In 1716, Cosimo III de' Medici officially delimited the Chianti production zone here, in the first European legal act delimiting a wine appellation.

The medieval village of Montefioralle, 2 km from Greve, is classified among Italy's Most Beautiful Villages. Still enclosed by its medieval walls, it preserves a compact plan of stone houses and streets virtually unchanged since the 13th century. The family of Amerigo Vespucci, the Florentine navigator who gave his name to America, originated from this village.

activities.text: Tasting of Chianti Classico DOCG in cellars of hillside estates. In 1906, major landowners founded the Chianti Wine Union here, precursor to today's Consorzio. The Gallo Nero (Black Rooster) label, emblem of the town, certifies the strictly local origin of the wines. The Chianti Classico Expo, every second weekend of September, brings together about sixty producers in the main square.

The Abbey of Badia a Passignano, 8 km northwest of Greve, is a Vallombrosian abbey founded in the 11th century. The Antinori group vinifies Chianti Classico Riserva Badia a Passignano in its medieval cellars, and produces its Tignanello nearby—one of the first Super Tuscans. Estate visits and tastings can be reserved.

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Figline Valdarno

visits.text: Figline Valdarno is the birthplace of Marsilio Ficino (1433-1499), the Neoplatonist philosopher who, under the patronage of Cosimo de' Medici then Lorenzo the Magnificent, translated into Latin the entire works of Plato (1482 edition), Plotinus's Enneads, and the Corpus Hermeticum—thus restoring to the West texts lost since Antiquity. He directed the Platonic Academy of Florence, founded by Cosimo de' Medici in 1459. The main square bears his name. The Collegiate Church of Santa Maria (founded 1257) houses the Madonna Enthroned by the Master of Figline (1317) and a terracotta Saint Joseph attributed to Andrea della Robbia.

The Castello di Sammezzano, 8 km north in Leccio (municipality of Reggello), is a Moorish castle transformed between 1853 and 1889 by Marquis Ferdinando Panciatichi Ximenes d'Aragona—a Florentine noble of Spanish origin. Inspired by the Alhambra in Granada, he had 365 rooms built decorated with arabesque ceilings, stalactite vaults, polychrome mosaics, and horseshoe arches, employing only local artisans. In 1878, King Umberto I was received here. The castle is currently closed to the public for restoration work (new ownership since April 2025, reopening envisioned at the earliest in 2028). The 65-hectare park—one of Tuscany's largest, with Italy's largest group of giant sequoias—remains accessible.

activities.text: Hiking in Castello di Sammezzano park among giant sequoias, romantic alleys, and Moorish architectural elements of the garden (bridge, artificial grotto with statue of Venus, pools). Ideal in spring and autumn.

visits.text: The Abbey of Vallombrosa (Santa Maria di Vallombrosa) was founded in 1036 by Giovanni Gualberto—a Florentine nobleman who had renounced his court life after granting forgiveness to his brother's murderer before the Crucifix of San Miniato al Monte, seeing Christ bow his head in approval. He retreated to these Apennine forests at 1,000 meters altitude to found the Vallombrosian Congregation, recognized by Pope Urban II in 1090. The abbey took its current form in the 15th century, with expansions in the 16th and 17th centuries. The church preserves Lorenzo Lippi's Trinity, a glazed terracotta altarpiece from Andrea della Robbia's workshop, and the reliquary of the arm of Saint John Gualberto by Florentine goldsmith Paolo Sogliano (1500). Perugino's Assumption (1498-1500), once on the altar, is now in Florence's Accademia Gallery. John Milton visited the abbey in 1638 and evoked its forests in Paradise Lost. Lamartine dedicated a poem to it in his Harmonies (1830).

activities.text: Forest walk in Vallombrosa forest, a biogenetic nature reserve since 1973: centuries-old beeches, white firs, and Scots pines in a dense, silent undergrowth. The Sentiero del Calvario climbs from the abbey toward the Hermitage of Paradisino (11th century), where anchorite monks retreated to isolated cells—panoramic view over the Arno valley.

practical_info.altitude: 1,000 m - road may be closed in winter

map_keywords: Torre di Federico II San Miniato
Area Archeologica di San Genesio San Miniato

visits.text: The Tower of Frederick II in San Miniato, rebuilt in 1958 after 1944 bombings, dominates from the highest point of the town. In the Middle Ages, San Miniato was called 'San Miniato al Tedesco': Otto I of Saxony made it the center of German imperial administration in central Italy in 962, and Frederick II of Swabia installed tax collection for all central Italy here in 1218. Bishop Sigeric of Canterbury stopped here in 990 during his pilgrimage to Rome on the Via Francigena. From the tower, the view embraces the Valdarno, the hills of Volterra, and, on clear days, the Apennines.

The Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta and San Genesio, begun in the 12th century, has a sober facade adorned with 26 ceramic bowls mainly of Tunisian origin—the originals are in the Diocesan Museum. The baroque interior preserves altarpieces by Francesco d'Agnolo called Lo Spillo and Cosimo Gamberucci. On July 22, 1944, an American artillery shell killed 55 civilians sheltering in the church, gathered there by German soldiers—one of the darkest pages of Tuscany's liberation.

The archaeological area of San Genesio, a few kilometers from the center, is one of the historic sites of the Via Francigena mentioned by Bishop Sigeric around 990 AD. Recent excavations have uncovered the foundations of an important medieval town.

activities.text: White truffle hunting in the forests around San Miniato with a guide and his dogs. The white truffle of San Miniato (Tuber Magnatum Pico) represents about one-fifth of national production. The National White Truffle Fair takes place the last three weekends of November since 1969; in 1954, a truffle weighing over 2 kg was presented to President Eisenhower.
Tasting in the historic center: tagliatelle with white truffle, mallegato (medieval charcuterie still produced by local farming families). San Miniato is a member of the Slow Food movement.

seo.h1_title: Treasures Around Florence: A 15-Stop Loop
seo.intro_paragraph: Just 8 kilometers from Florence, Fiesole conceals beneath its lanes a 1st-century Roman theater still in use and the first Renaissance villa, where Lorenzo the Magnificent gathered Plato and Ficino. From there, the loop opens northward through the wooded hills of Mugello—Pratolino and its 14-meter stone giant, Scarperia and its knives forged since 1306, Vicchio birthplace of Giotto and Fra Angelico—before heading west through Vinci and Leonardo's birthplace, Artimino with its villa of a hundred chimneys, Poggio a Caiano and its Pontormo frescoes. Prato and its Filippo Lippi, Carmignano and its Visitation, Impruneta and its terracotta, Greve and its Chianti Classico, Sammezzano and its Moorish halls locked in secret, Vallombrosa and its thousand-year firs, San Miniato and its white truffle: fifteen stops within a thirty-kilometer radius, a territory that shaped the Renaissance.
seo.seo_hook: UNESCO Medici villas, Giambologna's stone giant hidden in forest, Pontormo guarded by a sleeping village, abbey woods and white truffles—the Florentine hills haven't given up all their secrets.
featured_list: Villa Medici of Poggio a Caiano: prototype Renaissance villa with Pontormo frescoes
Pratolino: Giambologna's 14-meter colossus with secret rooms in the skull
Greve in Chianti: gateway to Chianti Classico DOCG, homeland of navigator Verrazzano
Vallombrosa Abbey: thousand-year forest at 1,000 m altitude visited by Milton in 1638

places_taglines.IT::fiesole: Where Etruscan walls meet Renaissance vision above Florence's rooftops
places_taglines.IT::pratolino: A Medici park hiding Giambologna's giant with rooms inside his stone skull
places_taglines.IT::scarperia: Seven centuries of blades forged by hand in medieval Mugello
places_taglines.IT::vicchio: Where Giotto sketched sheep on stones and Fra Angelico learned to see light
places_taglines.IT::borgo-san-lorenzo: Art Nouveau ceramics bloom in the capital of Mugello
places_taglines.IT::vinci: Hillside cradle of Leonardo, where genius drew its first breath
places_taglines.IT::artimino: A crown of hundred chimneys on Buontalenti's final masterpiece
places_taglines.IT::poggio-a-caiano: Where Lorenzo's Renaissance dream stands frozen in Pontormo's colors
places_taglines.IT::prato: Medieval textile empire guarding Filippo Lippi's greatest frescoes
places_taglines.IT::carmignano: Pontormo's Visitation waits in Montalbano's wine-dark hills
places_taglines.IT::impruneta: Where Brunelleschi's dome was born, one terracotta tile at a time
places_taglines.IT::greve-in-chianti: Triangular heart of Chianti, where Verrazzano dreamed of distant shores
places_taglines.IT::figline-valdarno: Birthplace of Ficino, who gave Plato back to the Renaissance
places_taglines.IT::vallombrosa: Where Milton walked among firs and forgiveness founded an abbey
places_taglines.IT::san-miniato: Imperial towers and autumn earth yielding Italy's finest white truffles

highlights: UNESCO Medici Villas: Fiesole, Artimino, and Poggio a Caiano—three architectural masterpieces not to miss
Pratolino: Giambologna's Colossus, a little-known monumental sculpture with secret rooms, free access on weekends
Chianti and Carmignano: two historic appellations with tastings at estates and cellars
San Miniato: white truffles in November, Italy's most prized harvest

itineraries.title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province
itineraries.subtitle: From medieval mining towns to Etruscan tufa cliffs, from Spanish coastal forts to granite islands: ten stops through the deep Maremma.

map_keywords: Cattedrale di San Cerbone Massa Marittima
Museo di Arte Sacra Massa Marittima

visits.text: San Cerbone Cathedral sits diagonally across Piazza Garibaldi—a deliberate perspective trick: the architect spaced the facade pilasters unevenly so it appears perfectly symmetrical when viewed from the square's center. Built from the 11th century in Pisan Romanesque style, Giovanni Pisano redesigned the third register of the facade in 1287 and extended the apse in the Gothic forms we see today. It measures 58.72 meters long by 18 meters wide. Inside, the Ark of San Cerbone (1324) by Goro di Gregorio stands as one of the pinnacles of Italian Gothic sculpture. The Maestà attributed to Duccio di Buoninsegna (1316) hangs in the left chapel, while the travertine baptismal font by Giroldo da Arogno dates from 1267.

Piazza Garibaldi, formerly Piazza del Duomo, is the Maremma's most cohesive medieval square. It brings together the Palazzo del Podestà (early 13th century, its facade covered with coats of arms of successive podestas), the Palazzo del Comune, and the Loggia del Mercato. Massa Marittima was a free city in the 13th century before being conquered by Siena in 1335—precisely when the Sienese demolished the upper third of the Torre del Candeliere to punish the rebellious inhabitants.

The Palazzo del Podestà houses Massa Marittima's Archaeological Museum, with a prehistoric section featuring a reconstructed cave and an Etruscan section displaying artifacts from local sites. The town owes its medieval wealth to its argentiferous lead mines in the Metalliferous Hills—the Latin name Massa Metallorum bears witness.

The Museum of Sacred Art in San Pietro all'Orto, adjacent to the Palazzo del Podestà, has housed since 2005 the Maestà by Ambrogio Lorenzetti (c. 1335), a masterpiece of Sienese painting, along with the original Giovanni Pisano sculptures that once adorned the cathedral facade before their removal. The neighboring Augustinian church of Sant'Agostino (late 13th century) features a remarkable pentagonal apse and preserves relics of Saint Bernardino of Siena.

activities.text: The Fonti dell'Abbondanza, a public fountain built in 1265 under Pisan podestà Ildebrandino, served as the walled city's only water source. During restoration in 1999, workers discovered beneath several layers of plaster a 5 x 6 meter fresco dating between 1265 and 1335: the Albero della Fecondità (Tree of Fertility), depicting a tree whose fruits are twenty-five phalluses that women are trying to pick. Interpreted as an apotropaic allegory of abundance, it's one of the rare examples of 13th-century secular painting to survive.

The Torre del Candeliere (Candlestick Tower), erected in the 13th century as a symbol of communal authority, takes its name from the custom of maintaining a permanent fire that made it resemble a candle. After the Sienese conquest of 1335, its top was demolished—today it stands a third shorter than originally. The arch connecting it to the Cassero Senese (Sienese fortress) was built by Siena to watch over the inhabitants. From the tower, on clear days, the view reaches as far as Elba Island.

The Museo della Miniera, installed in a reconstructed underground gallery beneath the town, traces the millennial history of ore extraction in the Metalliferous Hills. The guided tour descends into the galleries and presents the tools and techniques for extracting argentiferous lead, copper, and pyrite that made Massa's fortune from the 9th to 20th centuries.

map_keywords: Area Archeologica di Vetulonia Castiglione della Pescaia
Museo Civico Archeologico Isidoro Falchi Vetulonia

visits.text: Vetulonia—Vatluna in Etruscan—was one of the twelve cities of the Etruscan League, prospering from the 9th to 3rd centuries BC through metalwork and maritime trade via Lake Prile, now silted by alluvium. The city was rediscovered in 1880 by physician Isidoro Falchi, who identified the site beneath the medieval village of Colonna di Buriano. The archaeological zone of Poggiarello Renzetti, below the village, reveals the remains of the main street and Etruscan shops. The cyclopean walls of the Mura dell'Arce, which encircled a living space of about 16 hectares, are partially visible in the village center.

The necropolis spreads across the hills at the village's foot along the Via dei Sepolcri. The Tomba della Pietrera is the site's largest funerary monument: two tholoi stacked one atop the other, surrounded by a tumulus 60 meters in diameter, dating from the 7th century BC. One of the eight statues that once decorated it—a mourning figure—is now visible in Florence. The Tomba del Diavolino II, accessible through a long partially uncovered corridor, is another major tumulus from the same period. The panorama from the tombs embraces the Grosseto plain, the opposite hill of Roselle, and the sea in the distance.

The Civic Archaeological Museum Isidoro Falchi, dedicated to the archaeologist who rediscovered the city, houses a remarkable collection of objects from excavations: bronze banquet ware, jewelry, helmets, Greek and Phoenician imports testifying to Vetulonia's commercial openness across the entire Mediterranean.

activities.text: Walk along the Mura dell'Arce walls and through the medieval village's narrow streets perched on its hill, with views over the Maremma plain and the Tyrrhenian Sea. The village retains a preserved atmosphere, far removed from the usual tourist circuits.

map_keywords: Pieve delle Sante Flora e Lucilla Santa Fiora
Chiesa della Madonna delle Nevi Santa Fiora

visits.text: The Pieve delle Sante Flora e Lucilla, a parish church built before the year 1000 and reconstructed in the 13th century, preserves one of the world's most important collections of glazed terracotta from Andrea della Robbia's workshop. The pieces, created between 1464 and 1490, include a Coronation of the Virgin, a Saint Francis Receiving the Stigmata, a Madonna della Cintola, and several polychrome altarpieces with the characteristic blue background. The reliquary of Saints Flora and Lucilla, jointly offered by the Sforza and Aldobrandeschi families, bears the arms of both houses and materializes the county's passage from one dynasty to the other in 1439.

The Palazzo Sforza Cesarini, erected in 1575 on the foundations of the Aldobrandeschi fortress whose two medieval towers still survive on Piazza Garibaldi, now serves as the town hall. Dante mentioned Santa Fiora in Purgatorio (VI, 111) when the Ghibelline county of the Aldobrandeschi was prey to pillaging—the plaque affixed to the square reproduces this verse. Since 2002, the palace has housed the Museum of Mercury Mines of Monte Amiata, with 22 panels documenting the technical and social history of mercury extraction, the dominant activity on the massif between the 19th and mid-20th centuries.

The church of Madonna della Neve, a small Renaissance building from the 15th century adjoining the Peschiera, contains a unique feature: its floor is a transparent glass slab beneath which flows the official source of the Fiora River. The river literally springs to life beneath visitors' feet. The interior frescoes, attributed to Francesco Nasini, and a glazed terracotta bas-relief by Della Robbia complete the ensemble.

activities.text: La Peschiera, a medieval basin redesigned in the 16th century by the Sforza for trout farming in the Fiora's waters, was transformed into an English garden in the 19th century. Art critic Cesare Brandi wrote that it alone justified the journey. From its shaded banks, the village walls reflect in the water. It's one of the most photographed sites in Grosseto province, virtually unknown outside Italy.

Hiking in the beech forests of Monte Amiata, an extinct volcano reaching 1,733 meters, the highest mountain in southern Tuscany. The massif is geothermally active—steam columns from geothermal plants (including Bagnore 3 and 4) punctuate the landscape. Marked trails depart from Santa Fiora toward the state forest of Selva.

visits.text: Saturnia rises on a travertine promontory at 294 meters elevation, near the remains of an Etruscan necropolis at Pian di Palma. The Porta Romana, dating from the 2nd century BC, is built over preexisting Etruscan defensive structures. In the village center, the Archaeological Museum of Saturnia preserves artifacts from local excavations spanning prehistory to the Roman era. According to mythology, the town's name derives from the god Saturn himself: angered at mankind, he struck the ground with his thunderbolt, causing the thermal spring to gush forth.

Montemerano, a hamlet of Manciano municipality 7 km from Saturnia, ranks among Italy's "Most Beautiful Villages." Its historic center, developed around the early 11th-century Aldobrandeschi fortress (of which a square tower survives), is a labyrinth of perfectly preserved paved alleys. The church of San Giorgio, built in the 13th century on Piazza del Castello, houses a polyptych by Sano di Pietro, a 15th-century Sienese painter, along with precious medieval frescoes.

activities.text: The Cascate del Mulino (Mill Waterfalls) are a system of natural travertine pools created by Saturnia's thermal spring. The water, laden with sulfur, bicarbonate, and calcium, gushes at 37.5°C with a flow of 800 liters per second and has carved into the rock tiered pools with turquoise waters. Used since Etruscan and then Roman antiquity—a square Roman pool is still visible near the church of Maddalena—these cascades are freely accessible 24/7, year-round. Arrive early morning to enjoy the pools before the summer crowds.

Tasting of Morellino di Scansano DOCG wines in the cellars between Saturnia and Montemerano: this Sangiovese-based red produced in the Grosseto hinterland is one of the most renowned Tuscan reds outside the Chianti circuits.

map_keywords: Cattedrale dei Santi Pietro e Paolo Pitigliano

visits.text: Pitigliano bursts into view around the road's bend like a vision: houses grafted onto the edge of a volcanic tufa spur, the whole carried by the Medici aqueduct that springs toward the town. This aqueduct, commissioned by the Orsini and completed by the Medici between 1636 and 1639, forms 15 arches—two monumental and thirteen smaller—and constitutes the site's postcard image. It terminates at Piazza della Repubblica with the Fontana delle Sette Cannelle (16th century), whose seven spouts shaped as animal heads represented the seven historic quarters.

The Palazzo Orsini, a former Aldobrandeschi fortress transformed into a noble residence by the Orsini family between the 14th and 16th centuries, houses two museums: the Civic Archaeological Museum (with finds from the Poggio Buco necropolis) and the Palazzo Orsini Museum (21 rooms of frescoes, 15th-century paintings, goldsmith work, antique furniture, textiles donated by local parishes). The Orsini coat of arms above the portal depicts a bear—but the Maremman sculptor who executed it had never seen one; the animal rather resembles a dog.

The Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul, whose origins trace back to the 13th century, presents an 18th-century Baroque facade and a 16th-century Renaissance portal. The interior blends several styles accumulated through restorations. The church of San Rocco, the town's oldest construction, and the sanctuary of Madonna delle Grazie outside the walls complete the village's religious circuit.

The Jewish ghetto and Little Jerusalem: first protected by the Orsini who refused to close its gates despite papal directives, Pitigliano's Jewish community settled in the 15th century, fleeing persecution from the Papal States. It prospered until Italian unification. The synagogue, built in 1598, was restored in 1995 and now serves as a museum of local Jewish culture. Underground, you can visit the unleavened bread oven (forno delle azzime), the kosher wine cellar, the butcher shop, and the women's ritual bath (mikvé), all carved into the tufa. The sfratto—a dry honey-and-walnut biscuit—is the local specialty of Jewish origin: in the 17th century, Medici soldiers would knock (sfrattare = to evict) on ghetto doors at sunset to force Jews to return home. The pastry takes the shape of a soldier's staff.

activities.text: Walk through the Vie Cave around Pitigliano: a network of paths carved by hand into tufa rock by the Etruscans, with walls sometimes reaching 20 meters high over several kilometers. The Via Cava di San Giuseppe and Via Cava di Fratenuti, west of the village on either side of the river, are the most accessible. These passages served as communication routes between settlements and necropolises, probably also as a defensive system against cavalry attacks.

Tasting of Bianco di Pitigliano DOC, a Trebbiano Toscano-based white wine produced in natural caves carved into the tufa beneath the village. The underground cellars ensure stable temperature and humidity—ideal conditions for vinification.

map_keywords: Via Cava di San Rocco Sorano

visits.text: Sorano, the "Matera of Tuscany," clings to a volcanic tufa cliff above the gorges of the Lente River. The Rocca degli Orsini, of medieval Aldobrandeschi origin and entirely renovated in 1552, is a multi-level fortress whose underground galleries partially date back to Etruscan times. It houses the Civic and Archaeological Museum. At the village's opposite end, the Masso Leopoldino—a fortress built by order of the Lorraine family in the 18th century—offers from its panoramic terrace a 360° view over the Tufa Valley, the Albegna and Fiora valleys, and neighboring villages. Its crenellated tower and clock make it the village's visual landmark.

The Cathedral of San Pietro in Sovana (a hamlet of 350 inhabitants 15 km from Sorano) is considered one of the most important Romanesque monuments in the Maremma. The building, whose origins trace back to the 7th century, exemplifies the transition from Romanesque to Gothic. Its 8th-century ciborium—a rare survivor from this era—ranks among the oldest in Tuscany. Sovana was also the birthplace of Pope Gregory VII (Ildebrando di Soana, c. 1020-1085—his exact birth date remains uncertain, historians place it between 1015 and 1025), whose Gregorian Reform transformed the medieval Church.

The Etruscan necropolis of Sovana, within the Città del Tufo Archaeological Park, is the region's most varied for the quality of its monumental tombs carved into tufa. The Tomba Ildebranda (3rd-2nd century BC) is the most spectacular: a temple with fluted columns of which only one of eight survives, all sculpted directly from the cliff. The Tomba della Sirena, whose entrance is adorned with a bas-relief depicting a siren holding two figures, and the Tomba del Tifone complete the circuit. All are accessible from the park's forest trails.

activities.text: Hike along the Via Cava di San Rocco from Sorano: this Etruscan path carved into tufa descends toward the San Rocco necropolises with walls reaching 20 meters in places. It leads to a panoramic viewpoint over the Lente Valley and Sorano below. A trail then allows walking to Sovana on foot through the countryside.

Visit the site of Vitozza (5 km from Sorano, 2 km from San Quirico): a cave village abandoned in the 18th century, one of Italy's largest troglodyte sites with over 200 caves carved into a hill's tufa. Inhabited since at least the 11th century, and probably from Etruscan times, it preserves the remains of a fortress, several churches, and a medieval columbarium for pigeon breeding.

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Giovanni Battista Magliano in Toscana
Olivo della Strega Magliano in Toscana
Abbazia di San Bruzio Magliano in Toscana

visits.text: Magliano in Toscana is surrounded by one of Tuscany's best-preserved medieval walls, built in the 9th-10th centuries by the Aldobrandeschi then considerably reinforced by the Republic of Siena in the 15th century. The walkway runs the entire perimeter. Two monumental gates frame Corso Garibaldi: the Porta di San Martino to the north offers a remarkable view over the Maremman countryside; the Porta San Giovanni to the south guards access to the vineyards. The village occupies the site of ancient Roman Heba, itself heir to an Etruscan settlement whose several necropolises have been unearthed in the surroundings.

The church of San Giovanni Battista, on Piazza della Repubblica, has been documented since the 11th century. Its facade was restored in the Sienese style in 1471, and its interior preserves stucco statues and a Renaissance baptismal font from 1493. The church of Pieve di San Martino, older, presents characteristic Romanesque austerity. A few minutes' walk away, the small Civic Archaeological Museum houses the famous Magliano Disc—an Etruscan lead artifact bearing a long circular inscription in both directions, of still-debated interpretation—whose original is preserved at the Archaeological Museum in Florence.

activities.text: The Olivo della Strega (Witch's Olive Tree), just outside Magliano's walls in an olive grove behind the Oratory of Santissima Annunziata, is a monumental tree whose circumference exceeds 8 meters. Medieval tradition held it to be the nocturnal meeting place of women accused of witchcraft. It's one of the oldest and most massive trees in Tuscany.

The ruins of San Bruzio Benedictine abbey, 5 km from Magliano in an olive grove, are accessible on foot from the village. Founded around the year 1000, this abbey retains an intact sacred aura despite its ruined state: its gray travertine nave, its arcades open to the sky, and its centuries-worn capitals make it one of the most melancholic and beautiful places in the Maremma.

Tasting of Morellino di Scansano DOCG at wineries in the Colli di Maremma zone: this Sangiovese-dominated red, produced on the hills around Magliano and Scansano, has enjoyed DOCG status since 2006. The Vinellando, a wine festival held every year in late August through Magliano's streets, offers the opportunity to discover all the producers in one evening.

visits.text: Capalbio is a medieval village whose urban layout has remained virtually intact since the 15th century. Its double-walled ramparts with walkway, built between the 11th and 15th centuries, can be walked entirely. The Porta Senese preserves its original massive wooden doors from the 15th century. Inside, the Rocca Aldobrandesca adjoins the Palazzo Collacchioni, a richly frescoed Renaissance noble residence. The village was given in 805 by Charlemagne to the abbey of Saints Anastasius and Vincent alle Tre Fontane in Rome—one of the oldest donation acts in the Tuscan territory.

The church of San Nicola, built in the Middle Ages atop the hill around which the village wraps, is an architectural palimpsest: Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance are layered one upon another. It preserves a fresco cycle from the Umbrian-Sienese school, and the Oratory of Providence outside the walls contains 18th-century frescoes attributed to Pinturicchio's workshop.

Talamone, 25 km north on the coastal road, is a tiny port dominated by the Rocca degli Aldobrandeschi, a medieval fortress in excellent condition perched on a rocky promontory plunging into the sea. In 1860, Garibaldi stopped here with his Thousand to resupply arms before sailing to Sicily—a plaque commemorates the event. The village retains the atmosphere of an authentic fishing port, with a few hundred permanent residents.

activities.text: The Tarot Garden (Giardino dei Tarocchi), 2 km from Capalbio at the foot of the hamlet of Capalbiole, is a sculpture park created by Franco-American artist Niki de Saint Phalle starting in 1979, inspired by Gaudí's Park Güell in Barcelona. The 22 major arcana of the tarot are represented by monumental sculptures covered in ceramic mosaics, mirrors, and colored glass. Saint Phalle lived for several years inside the Judgement sculpture, transformed into an apartment. The garden opened to the public in 1998.

Walk along Capalbio's rampart walkway with panoramas over the Maremman countryside, the Tyrrhenian Sea, and on clear days Giglio Island and Monte Argentario. Local legend reports that Tiburzi, the Maremma's most famous brigand, spent much of his 19th-century flight here.

visits.text: Porto Ercole is guarded by three Spanish fortresses built in the 16th century during the Hispanic domination of the State of Presidi. Forte Stella—whose six-pointed star shape allowed enemy targeting from each point—was begun in 1548 by Giovan Battista Belluzzi and completed by Giovanni Camerini under Cosimo I de' Medici; it's the most accessible and most photographed. The Rocca Spagnola, an irregular four-bastion star form, dominates the borgo antico. Forte Filippo, on the northern promontory, was built for Philip II of Spain according to a design by Giovanni Camerini.

The borgo antico of Porto Ercole, accessible only on foot from the Porta Pisana topped by a clock tower, is a labyrinth of alleys and staircases clinging to the hillside. Piazza Santa Barbara, above the bastion of the same name, offers the village's most iconic panorama over the bay and fortresses. The Palazzo dei Governatori, built in the early 16th century under Siena, served as residence of the Spanish commander and then the summer governor of the State of Presidi. The church of Sant'Erasmo (17th century), at the village's highest point, is the tallest building.

Caravaggio died in Porto Ercole on July 18, 1610, at age 39, in circumstances still debated. Fleeing a death sentence for murder, he had left Naples with three paintings intended for Cardinal Scipione Borghese, the price of an expected papal pardon. His death certificate, found in 2001 in the Sant'Erasmo parish register, indicates he died at the Hospital of Santa Maria Ausiliatrice, probably from malaria. A monument honors him in the historic center.

activities.text: The Duna Feniglia Nature Reserve is a wooded dune 6 km long separating the open sea from the Orbetello lagoon. A dirt path traverses it under pines, inhabited by semi-wild fallow deer.
