According to local tradition, it was on this beach that Caravaggio was found dying in 1610—though his death certificate found in 2001 indicates he died in hospital, the exact circumstances remaining debated. Car access is prohibited: the dune is explored on foot or by bicycle.

Orbetello, a town perched on the narrow sand strip between the two tomboli of Argentario, is a discovery in its own right: 16th-century Spanish walls, compact old town, lagoon classified as a nature reserve (flamingos, herons, egrets). From the lagoon's edge, the view of Monte Argentario's green promontory rising from the sea is one of the most beautiful on the Tuscan coast.

Spiaggia Lunga beach, accessible from Porto Ercole by a steep path descending behind Forte Stella. Sand and pebble beach below the fortress, surrounded by Mediterranean scrub. Further along the same trail, the Acqua Dolce beach, accessible behind the Hotel Pellicano, is one of Argentario's most preserved coves.

practical_info.difficulty: Steep path about 200 m to descend to the beach

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Pietro Apostolo Giglio Castello
Spiaggia del Campese Isola del Giglio

visits.text: Giglio Castello, a fortified medieval village at 400 meters elevation in the island's center, is surrounded by 13th-century walls whose certain sections exceed 10 meters in height. Giglio Island, whose name derives not from the "lily" (giglio) but from the Greek aigulion ("goat"), was given by Charlemagne to a Roman abbey in 805 before passing to the Aldobrandeschi, the Pisans, the Orsini, the Piccolomini, then to Eleanor of Toledo, wife of Cosimo I, in 1558.

The Rocca Pisana (also called Rocca Aldobrandesca), a fortress built by the Pisans in the 12th century at the village's highest point, was designed to withstand long sieges: inside were a deep cistern, a vegetable garden, food stores, a poultry yard, and a dovecote, all ensuring self-sufficiency in case of attack. It communicated with the coast by fire and smoke signals.

The church of San Pietro Apostolo (15th century), in the alleys of Giglio Castello, houses the relics of San Mamiliano, patron saint of the island whose cult is one of the oldest and most vibrant in the Tuscan archipelago, as well as a gold chalice offered by Pope Innocent XIII. A Corinthian capital from the Roman villa of Giglio Porto is embedded in the walls.

Giglio Porto, the landing point for ferries from Porto Santo Stefano (crossing about 1 hour), retains the atmosphere of an authentic fishing port with its colorful houses and the medieval Torre del Saraceno. In the Cala del Saraceno behind the Medici tower, the Cetariae—fish-farming basins carved into rock by the Romans in the 1st century AD for the Domizi Enobarbi family—are visible in the port's clear waters.

activities.text: Ferry crossing from Porto Santo Stefano to Isola del Giglio. The crossing takes about an hour and offers views of Monte Argentario, the Tuscan archipelago, and on clear days, Corsica and Montecristo Island.

practical_info.booking: Reservation recommended in season, especially with vehicle

activities.text: Hiking trail Giglio Porto - Giglio Castello: a 45-minute climb on a stone-paved path traversing Mediterranean scrub (garrigue, mastic, strawberry trees). The view over the coast and archipelago widens as you ascend. The same trail continues to Giglio Campese (west coast) with its large sandy beach and the 18th-century Torre di Campese—last remnant of a pirate attack in 1799.

Campese beach, the largest beach in the Tuscan archipelago, in an open bay on the island's west side. Fine sand, clear waters, sunset facing the sea. In late afternoon, the same ferries return to Porto Santo Stefano.

seo.h1_title: The Most Beautiful Villages of Grosseto Province
seo.intro_paragraph: Grosseto province is the Tuscany few travelers truly dare: Massa Marittima with its Gothic cathedral on a stone square, Vetulonia the ancient Etruscan city forgotten on its hill, Santa Fiora with its Della Robbia treasures nestled at Monte Amiata's foot. Then the tufa country with Pitigliano the Little Jerusalem clinging to its cliff, Sorano the Tuscan Matera, Sovana and its Etruscan tombs in the forest. Further down, Magliano in Toscana and its witch's olive tree, Capalbio and the Tarot Garden. The coast with Porto Ercole and its Spanish fortresses, where Caravaggio died. And Giglio Island to finish, granite and scrub in the middle of the Tyrrhenian Sea.
seo.seo_hook: Ten villages from the Metalliferous Hills to the Tuscan archipelago: the deep Maremma reveals its tufa towns, medieval mines, and Spanish fortresses.
featured_list: Massa Marittima: Gothic cathedral and scandalous 13th-century fresco
Pitigliano: Little Jerusalem suspended from tufa cliffs
Saturnia: natural thermal baths at 37°C with free access
Porto Ercole: Spanish fortresses where Caravaggio died
Sovana: Etruscan necropolis and rock-carved tombs
Giglio Castello: fortified medieval village in the heart of the Tuscan archipelago

places_taglines.IT::massa-marittima: Where Gothic treasures hide in a medieval mining town beneath the Metalliferous Hills
places_taglines.IT::vetulonia: An ancient Etruscan city rediscovered in the 19th century with haunting necropolises
places_taglines.IT::santa-fiora: Mountain village at the foot of Monte Amiata guarding the world's finest Della Robbia collection
places_taglines.IT::saturnia: Where thermal cascades at 37°C flow freely through travertine pools carved by time
places_taglines.IT::pitigliano: Little Jerusalem carved from volcanic tufa with an intact 16th-century Jewish ghetto
places_taglines.IT::sorano: The Matera of Tuscany, where medieval houses merge with towering tufa cliffs
places_taglines.IT::magliano-in-toscana: Medieval walls encircling the legendary thousand-year-old witch's olive tree
places_taglines.IT::capalbio: Where perfectly preserved medieval ramparts meet Niki de Saint Phalle's visionary Tarot Garden
places_taglines.IT::porto-ercole: Spanish star fortresses guarding the bay where Caravaggio breathed his last
places_taglines.IT::giglio-castello: Granite walls rising 400 meters above the Tyrrhenian on a wild island refuge

highlights: The Cascate del Mulino in Saturnia, natural thermal baths at 37°C freely accessible year-round
The Pitigliano-Sorano-Sovana trio in the Tufa Country, with Etruscan Vie Cave and necropolises
San Cerbone Cathedral in Massa Marittima and the medieval Tree of Fertility fresco
Porto Ercole and its three 16th-century Spanish fortresses, Caravaggio's final refuge
Giglio Island accessible by ferry from Porto Santo Stefano, with Giglio Castello and granite beaches

itineraries.title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries
itineraries.subtitle: From the lakeside villages of Umbria to the sacred forests of the Casentino, through the frescoes of Piero della Francesca and the medieval hilltop towns of the Valtiberina

map_keywords: Rocca del Leone Castiglione del Lago
Palazzo della Corgna Castiglione del Lago

visits.text: Rocca del Leone: pentagonal fortress built from the 12th century on the ruins of a fortification destroyed in 1091 by Henry IV of Franconia. The triangular keep rises nearly 30 meters and offers a 360° view over the entire lake. Designed by Arnolfo di Cambio — according to some sources — or by the Perugian architects who reinforced it in 1297, the Rocca perfectly illustrates medieval Umbrian military architecture.

Palazzo della Corgna: 16th-century Renaissance palace commissioned by Ascanio della Corgna, nephew of Pope Julius III, who governed the marquisate of Castiglione from 1550 to 1571 and died at the Battle of Lepanto. The mythological interior frescoes are attributed to Giovanni Antonio Pandolfi. A covered corridor connects the palace directly to the Rocca del Leone. Machiavelli and Leonardo da Vinci are said to have been among its guests.

Historic center of Castiglione del Lago: a limestone promontory that in Etruscan times was an island in the lake, gradually absorbed by sediment. Castiglione fell under Chiusi's territory (Clusium Novum for the Romans) before coming under Perugian control in the 12th century. The 12th-century medieval ramparts are still visible, pierced by three gates — Perugia, Siena and Fiorentina — reorganized in 1563 along a plan inspired by the theories of Vignola, the Mannerist architect then in high demand in Rome.

Passignano sul Trasimeno: a village on the lake's north shore whose name likely derives from "Passo di Giano," the ancient god of passages. The 14th-century medieval Rocca dominates the historic center. Livy reports that it was between Tuoro and Passignano, where the road narrowed between the lake and the hills, that Hannibal set the largest ambush in antiquity in 217 BC, slaughtering 40,000 Roman soldiers under Consul Flaminius.

activities.text: Ferry crossing to the islands of Lake Trasimeno: three distinct islands, each accessible from different docks. Isola Maggiore, the only one permanently inhabited, preserves an Irish lace tradition introduced in the late 19th century. Isola Polvese, the largest, is now a science park managed by the Province of Perugia.

Trasimeno cycle path: a roughly 60-km route circling the entire lake, passing through Castiglione, Cortona, Panicale and Passignano. Sections along the southern shore cross the Oasi La Valle, a wetland where herons, storks and ospreys winter.

visits.text: MAEC — Museum of the Etruscan Academy and City of Cortona: housed in the 14th-century Palazzo Casali, the museum founded in 1727 was one of the first in Europe to open its collections to the public. The centerpiece is a 4th-century BC Etruscan bronze chandelier with 16 burners adorned with Achelous heads and dolphins, from a sanctuary in the Orvieto area. The Tabula Cortonensis, a bronze plate in eight fragments discovered in 1992, is the third-longest Etruscan text in the world — a legal document recording the transfer of vineyard property. A section is dedicated to Gino Severini, the Futurist painter born in Cortona, who personally bequeathed his works to the city.

Diocesan Museum: housed in the former church of the Gesù, it holds the Annunciation by Fra Angelico, painted around 1430 for the church of San Domenico. A tempera on panel measuring 175 × 180 cm, the work is regarded as the first undisputed masterpiece by the artist and served as a model for a whole series of later variations. It was commissioned by Giovanni di Cola di Cecco, a cloth merchant and member of the San Domenico Confraternity. The landscape in the background depicts Lake Trasimeno as Fra Angelico saw it from the heights of Cortona. The museum also holds nine panels by Luca Signorelli — a Cortona native — including the Compianto (1502) and the Communion of the Apostles (1512).

Etruscan walls and archaeological park: the massive polygonal masonry walls encircling the town date in part from the 4th century BC and constitute one of the best-preserved Etruscan fortified perimeters in Italy. Outside the walls, the funerary tumuli known as Meloni di Sodo contain remarkable burial chambers: Tumulus II of Sodo features a spectacular stairway-terrace decorated with sculptural groups suggesting Eastern influences.

Girifalco Fortress: a Medici fortress perched at the top of the hill, from which the view sweeps across the Valdichiana, Lake Trasimeno and, on a clear day, as far as Monte Amiata. Below, Cortona was in the Middle Ages a rival city to Arezzo and Perugia for control of the lake's eastern shore.

activities.text: Cortona On The Move photography festival: every year from mid-July to early October, the festival transforms the squares and lanes of the center into open-air galleries, with exhibitions by international artists. Founded in 2011, it's one of the most important photography events in Italy.

Cortonantiquaria: one of Italy's oldest antique fairs, held annually in August–September at the Palazzo Vagnotti. Since 1964, it has brought together dealers specializing in antiques from the Middle Ages to Art Deco.

Walk along the Via Romea between Castiglion Fiorentino and Cortona: a 24-km stage crossing the Sodo Archaeological Park, allowing you to see the Etruscan tumuli and the remains of Roman centuriation in situ before entering the town through its ancient walls.

visits.text: Cortona Cathedral — Cattedrale di San Donato: a Renaissance cathedral with a remarkable interior. It houses a painting by Pietro da Cortona depicting the Nativity, a marble ciborium attributed to the local sculptor Ciuccio di Nuccio, and a mosaic by the Cortona-born Gino Severini depicting the Sacred Heart. The building adjoins the Museo Diocesano, both facing the 2nd-century Roman sarcophagus that, according to Vasari, specifically prompted Donatello and Brunelleschi to travel to Cortona to study it.

Church of San Domenico: a 14th-century Gothic building that originally housed, before their transfer to the Diocesan Museum, Fra Angelico's Annunciation and Triptych. On the exterior portal, a recently restored lunette by Fra Angelico is again visible. Inside, a Luca Signorelli altarpiece and, in the main chapel, a polyptych commissioned by the San Domenico Confraternity remain.

Tanella di Pitagora: an Etruscan tomb in rotunda form from the 2nd century BC, located outside the walls by a stream. Its fanciful name invokes Pythagoras with no historical basis — 15th-century oral tradition held that the Greek philosopher had passed through Cortona. The well-preserved corbelled dome is a rare example of this type of construction in the region.

Farneta Abbey: a Benedictine abbey founded in the 9th century, a few kilometers south of Cortona. It contains a pre-Romanesque crypt among the oldest in Tuscany and a paleontological museum housing fossil remains of Pleistocene fauna discovered nearby, including dwarf elephants and hippopotamuses from the Farneta fauna, which populated the Valdichiana over a million years ago.

activities.text: Panoramic viewpoints over the Valdichiana: from the upper lanes of the historic center, the gaze sweeps across the fertile Valdichiana plain to the profile of Arezzo to the north and, on a clear day, the Sienese hills to the west. The upper piazza offers one of the widest panoramas in all of eastern Tuscany.

map_keywords: Collegiata di San Michele Arcangelo Lucignano

visits.text: Elliptical layout of Lucignano: the village, Etruscan in origin and developed between the 13th and 16th centuries, is one of the best-preserved examples of medieval town planning in Tuscany. Its streets coil in four concentric elliptical rings around the hilltop, like a snail shell. This singular layout earned it the Bandiera Arancione label from the Italian Touring Club. Its position on the boundary of the former territories of Florence, Arezzo, Siena and Perugia gave it a strategic importance still visible in the thickness of its ramparts.

Municipal Museum — the Albero d'Oro: on the ground floor of the former Palazzo Pretorio, this 2.70-meter gilded copper reliquary is one of the masterworks of Italian goldsmithing. Created between 1350 and 1471 — a 120-year project — thanks to a bequest from one Madonna Giacoma, it was intended for the church of San Francesco and held Franciscan relics and splinters of the Cross of Christ. Its tree form draws on the Lignum Vitae (1259–1260) by Saint Bonaventure. The tree is displayed in the Sala delle Udienze, the former tribunal hall decorated with a fresco cycle (1410–1479) depicting illustrious figures from Antiquity, from Solomon to Aristotle. The museum, founded in 1924, also holds two panels by Luca Signorelli and works from the 14th-century Sienese school.

Church of San Francesco: built in the 12th century, recognizable by its travertine and local sandstone facade. Inside, a polyptych attributed to Luca di Tommè depicting a Madonna holding the castle of Lucignano recalls the town's role as a marquisate contested between Siena and Florence until the Battle of Scannagallo in the 16th century.

activities.text: Walk through the concentric rings: following the four elliptical streets that encircle the village from the outside is the only way to perceive the exceptional urban layout. The Medici Fortress, built in the 16th century on orders from Cosimo I de' Medici, offers a panorama over the Valdichiana and Lake Trasimeno.

Palio delle Contrade of Lucignano: a medieval historical reenactment in costume held every year in May. In September, the Festa degli Sposi commemorates the symbolic link between the Albero d'Oro and love, celebrated since the Japanese designer Yumi Katsura chose Lucignano for a bridal gown presentation.

map_keywords: Castello di Montecchio Vesponi Castiglion Fiorentino
Chiesa di San Francesco Castiglion Fiorentino

visits.text: Torre del Cassero: imposing tower rebuilt around 1350 by the Perugians — the town was then called Castiglion Perugino — on an Etruscan oppidum on the acropolis. Originally 50 meters tall (slightly reduced after a 19th-century lightning strike), it dominates the medieval center ringed by 13th-century ramparts. In 1384, Florence purchased the town from Charles of Durazzo, who needed cash to fund his Hungarian mercenary troops, and renamed it Castiglion Fiorentino.

Vasari Loggia: built in 1513 by the craftsmen Bernardo di Ghieba and Filippo da Bellinzona, it was reworked between 1560 and 1570 by Giorgio Vasari, who had the Medici coat of arms in pietra serena placed on it. Three arches open onto the valley, creating a unique architectural balcony over the Val di Chio, between the Valdichiana and Valtiberina. The interior walls are adorned with polychrome Della Robbia terracotta coats of arms of the Florentine commissioners and podestàs.

Municipal Art Gallery and Museum of Sacred Art of the Pieve: the gallery, housed in the former 12th-century church of Sant'Angelo al Cassero, preserves works by Tuscan masters — Taddeo Gaddi, Bartolomeo della Gatta, Ghirlandaio and Margarito d'Arezzo. The Pieve museum holds the fresco Compianto sul Cristo Morto by Luca Signorelli and a Della Robbia school altarpiece depicting the Baptism of Christ. The church of San Francesco houses a 1548 canvas by Giorgio Vasari — the Virgin with Saint Anne, Saint Anthony and Saint Sylvester — whose preparatory drawing is at the Louvre.

Castello di Montecchio Vesponi: a medieval fortress 3 km south of the town, documented since the 10th century. It was seized and redesigned by Florence in the late 13th century before losing its military function. The enclosure features eight turrets along 263 meters, with a 30-meter keep. Abandoned then repurchased in 1860 by the banker Giacomo Servadio, it's one of the best-preserved medieval fortresses in the province of Arezzo.

activities.text: Palio dei Rioni: an equestrian competition pitting the three historic quarters — Cassero, Porta Fiorentina and Porta Romana — against each other every third Sunday of June on the Piazzale Garibaldi. The tradition dates back to 1864 but the current version was established in 1932.

visits.text: Piazza Grande: Arezzo's central square since the 13th century, tilted with a 10-meter drop from top to bottom. It is bordered by the apse of Santa Maria della Pieve (12th century), the Palazzo della Fraternità dei Laici (Gothic and Renaissance facade, 14th–15th centuries), the Palazzo Pretorio (14th century) and Vasari's Logge, designed in 1573 and completed in 1595. During its construction in 1553, the Medici fortress unearthed the Chimera of Arezzo — a 78.5-cm-tall Etruscan bronze ex-voto, now in the National Archaeological Museum of Florence. Replicas adorn fountains near the station and at Porta San Lorentino.

Basilica of San Francesco and Piero della Francesca's cycle: a Gothic building erected between the late 13th and late 14th centuries, made famous by the Bacci Chapel, whose decoration was entrusted to Piero della Francesca after the death of Bicci di Lorenzo in 1452. Piero executed the 15 scenes of the Legend of the True Cross between 1452 and 1466, covering the three registers of the side walls. The iconographic program, drawn from the Golden Legend of Jacobus de Voragine, follows not chronological order but formal and symbolic correspondences between scenes: the meeting of the Queen of Sheba and King Solomon mirrors the battle of Heraclius, and the two battle scenes echo each other. A ten-year restoration completed in 2000 returned the frescoes to their original colors.

Cathedral of San Donato: one of the most representative examples of Italian Gothic architecture, its construction spanning from the 13th to the 16th century. It houses the stained-glass windows of Guillaume de Marcillat — including the 1524 rose window depicting Honorius III's approval of the Indulgence of Saint Francis — as well as Piero della Francesca's Mary Magdalene fresco, tucked at the back of the left nave, and a painting by Pietro da Cortona depicting the Nativity.

Church of San Domenico and Cimabue's Crucifix: a medieval church founded in the 13th century with austere Gothic architecture. The Cimabue Crucifix — dated by scholars to the decade 1265–1275 — hangs above the high altar. It's one of the Florentine painter's earliest works, already revealing his break from the Byzantine manner: the body of Christ begins to curve naturally, foreshadowing the humanism of Giotto.

Giorgio Vasari House-Museum: the personal residence that the architect, painter and first art critic of the Renaissance — born in Arezzo in 1511 — decorated himself with frescoes of allegories and portraits of great artists. The building, acquired in 1541 and transformed through 1548, reflects Vasari's taste for Mannerist decoration and his relationship to the history of art that he would codify in his Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects (1550).

activities.text: Giostra del Saracino: a jousting tournament rooted in the Middle Ages, revived in 1931 as a 14th-century reenactment. It takes place twice a year on the Piazza Grande: the penultimate Saturday of June (Joust of San Donato) and the first Sunday of September. The four quarters — Porta Santo Spirito, Porta Sant'Andrea, Porta del Foro and Porta Crucifera — compete in a tournament where mounted knights charge and strike the shield of a pivoting Saracen effigy. The procession mobilizes over 350 costumed participants and 27 horses.

practical_info.period: Penultimate Saturday of June and 1st Sunday of September

activities.text: Arezzo Antique Fair: one of the largest antique fairs in Europe, created in 1968 by Ivan Bruschi. On the first Saturday and Sunday of every month, the Piazza Grande and surrounding streets host more than 500 dealers from across Italy, specializing in furniture, paintings, objects and antique jewelry.

practical_info.period: 1st Saturday and Sunday of each month

visits.text: Santa Maria della Pieve: a 12th-century Romanesque church whose facade of stacked pilasters is adorned with sculptures attributed to Lombard masters. The interior preserves the polyptych by Pietro Lorenzetti (1320–1324), Madonna and Child between Saint John the Baptist, Saint Donatus, Saint John the Evangelist and Saint Matthew, on the high altar. The campanile of the Hundred Holes, pierced by 40 windows across five levels, is one of the most distinctive Romanesque bell towers in Tuscany.

National Archaeological Museum Mecenate: housed in the former convent of San Bernardo, built against the 1st–2nd-century Roman amphitheater. It brings together the Etruscan and Roman collections of the region, including sigillata corallium ceramics — the distinctive production of Arretium whose coral-red color characterized Roman exports — and Etruscan bronzes. The amphitheater exterior, with its surviving arc of seating, can be freely explored.

House of Petrarch and the medieval historic center: Francesco Petrarca, born in Arezzo in 1304, is alongside Dante and Boccaccio one of the three founders of the Italian literary language. His birthplace, at the end of Via dell'Orto, provides access to a biographical museum. The historic center also reveals the medieval Lappoli tower-house on Piazza Grande, and the heritage of a city that was in the Middle Ages a powerful bishopric and a Ghibelline stronghold that resisted the Guelph forces of Florence until the Battle of Campaldino (1289).

activities.text: Filming locations for Life Is Beautiful: Roberto Benigni's 1997 film, which won three Academy Awards including Best Foreign Language Film, was largely shot in Arezzo. The Piazza Grande serves as the film's central square, and Via Vittorio Veneto was used as the set for the main character's bookshop. Guided film-location tours retrace these sites.

map_keywords: Museo della Madonna del Parto Monterchi

visits.text: Museum of the Madonna del Parto: a 260 × 203 cm fresco painted around 1460 by Piero della Francesca for the church of Santa Maria di Momentana, located away from the village — a site linked to traditions of Marian devotion and fertility rites. It was painted for the hometown of the painter's mother, according to Vasari during a return to Sansepolcro upon her death. Analysis of the fresco suggests it was executed in roughly seven days. It depicts the pregnant Virgin standing, opening her mantle with one hand, flanked by two symmetrical angels produced from the same cartoon reversed. The church was demolished in 1785 to make way for a cemetery; the fresco, forgotten in the masonry of a funerary chapel, was only rediscovered in 1889. After restoration, it was transferred in 1993 to the former village primary school, now converted into a museum. A legal dispute between the municipality of Monterchi, the Catholic Church and the Ministry of Culture over its ownership remains unresolved.

Historic center of Monterchi: perched on an isolated hill on the border with Umbria, the village retains a typically medieval character despite several earthquakes in the 19th and 20th centuries. Its name derives from Mons Herculis — Hill of Hercules — recalling presumed ancient cults on this promontory. The Museum of the Balance, housed in the center, holds one of Europe's most important collections of antique scales and weights, with an interactive educational trail.

activities.text: Exploring the Valtiberina countryside: the surroundings of Monterchi are dotted with Romanesque parish churches, castles and rural hamlets typical of the upper Tiber valley landscape. The road between Arezzo and Monterchi passes through several well-preserved farming villages, with panoramas over the wooded hills that herald the Tuscan Valtiberina.

visits.text: Fortified village of Anghiari: ranked among the most beautiful villages in Italy, Anghiari rises on a hill overlooking the Valtiberina plain, protected by bastions of which a portion survives. The Torre del Campano, dating from the 13th century and rebuilt in the 16th century after its destruction by Vitellozzo Vitelli in 1502, marks the village's silhouette. The Palazzo Pretorio on Piazza del Popolo displays a facade covered in the coats of arms of the Florentine vicars who administered the town after 1440, typical of fortress-towns governed from Florence.

Museum of the Battle of Anghiari — Palazzo del Marzocco: the museum, opened in 2000 and expanded in 2007, traces the history of the battle of June 29, 1440, in which the Florentines led by Micheletto Attendolo defeated the Milanese under Niccolò Piccinino, securing Florence's independence. The battle would have remained a local episode were it not for Leonardo da Vinci, commissioned in 1503 by Gonfalonier Piero Soderini to paint it in the Hall of the Five Hundred of the Palazzo Vecchio. Using experimental encaustic, Leonardo managed only to sketch the "Battle for the Standard" before leaving Florence in 1506; Vasari covered the unfinished work in 1563. The museum holds a 17th-century copy by the Flemish engraver Gérard Edelinck, as well as engravings by Dürer and Chagall inspired by Leonardo's sketches.

Museo Statale di Palazzo Taglieschi: a national museum housed in a Renaissance palace, devoted to the history of Anghiari and the Valtiberina from the Middle Ages to the 18th century. It brings together local sculpture and painting as well as documentation on the tradition of firearm manufacture — Anghiari was in the 18th century a center renowned for the fineness of its arms decorations.

activities.text: Trail of the Battle of Anghiari to the Maestà della Vittoria: a roughly 1-hour hike from the historic center to the exact spot where the Florentines defeated the Milanese in June 1440. The path follows the ancient straight road built in the late 14th century linking Anghiari to Sansepolcro, along which the former Byzantine church of Santo Stefano and traces of Roman centuriation are still visible.

visits.text: Civic Museum of Sansepolcro — The Resurrection: Piero della Francesca was born in Sansepolcro around 1412 and died there on October 12, 1492. The Museo Civico, housed in the Palazzo della Residenza whose current structure dates from 1456, is the only museum in the world to hold more than three works by the master. The Resurrection — a 225 × 200 cm fresco from the 1460s — was painted directly on the wall of the audience hall where the town council sat. Aldous Huxley called it in 1925 "the greatest painting in the world." In 1944, a British officer, Anthony Clarke, remembering that essay, halted the order to bomb Sansepolcro, then believed occupied by German soldiers — saving the fresco. The Polyptych of the Misericordia (commissioned in 1445) is Piero's first documented work.

Historic center and cathedral of Sansepolcro: the town, founded according to tradition by two pilgrims returning from the Holy Land in the 9th century with relics of the Holy Sepulchre, retains the character of a Renaissance art city with its stone palaces and 14th–16th-century buildings. The cathedral, rebuilt between the 14th and 16th centuries, houses a 12th-century wooden crucifix and frescoes. The birthplace of Piero della Francesca, in present-day Via Aggiunti, can be glimpsed from outside.

activities.text: Palio della Balestra: a crossbow competition pitting Sansepolcro against Gubbio, held every second Sunday of September on Piazza Torre di Berta since 1594. In medieval dress, the balestrieri of the two rival towns compete at shooting a wooden target — one of the oldest crossbow tournaments in Italy.

map_keywords: Chiesa di Santa Maria degli Angeli La Verna

visits.text: Sanctuary of La Verna — Basilica and Chapel of the Stigmata: Mount Alverne was given to Saint Francis in 1213 by Count Orlando Cattani di Chiusi, who described it as "a solitary and wild place suited to those who wish to do penance." It was here, on September 17, 1224 — two years before his death — that Francis received the stigmata of the Passion, becoming the first stigmatist officially recognized by the Catholic Church. The Chapel of the Stigmata marks the exact spot of the miracle, indicated by a tablet in the floor. The great Basilica dedicated to Santa Maria Assunta was built from 1348 and completed in 1509 thanks to donations from the Senate of Florence. It was raised to the rank of minor basilica by Pope Benedict XV in 1921. Inside, 15th-century glazed terracottas by Andrea della Robbia decorate the chapel and the basilica — including the large Crucifixion altarpiece, one of the master's last works.

Small church of Santa Maria degli Angeli: the original nucleus of the complex, commissioned by Francis himself in 1216. This is the chapel where the eremitic life on the mount began. It is the starting point of the daily 3 PM procession to the Chapel of the Stigmata.

activities.text: Monumental forest of La Verna: protected by the Franciscan brothers for centuries, this forest of ancient beeches and firs includes trees reaching 50 meters in height. Marked trails cross the forest and lead to the summit of Monte Penna (1,283 m), from which the view extends over the entire Casentino and Valtiberina. Deer, wild boar, wolves and numerous bird species inhabit these woods.

practical_info.difficulty: Easy to moderate depending on route chosen

to_next_leg.road_type: Route de montagne sinueuse en descente depuis La Verna vers Bibbiena

map_keywords: Propositura dei Santi Ippolito e Donato Bibbiena
Santuario di Santa Maria del Sasso Bibbiena

visits.text: Historic center of Bibbiena: the administrative capital of the Casentino, Bibbiena is documented since 979 and developed as a medieval fortress of the bishops of Arezzo atop a hill overlooking the Archiano river. After the Battle of Campaldino (1289), the Florentine Guelphs sacked it for eight days. Rebuilt, it became one of the strategic castles of the Tarlati da Pietramala in the 14th century before passing to Florence in 1384. Of the original walls, the Tarlati Tower on Piazza Tarlati survives as the sole remnant of the medieval castle. The square offers a view over the entire Casentino valley.

Church of San Lorenzo: erected in the 15th century, it preserves two remarkable glazed terracottas by Andrea della Robbia — a Deposition and an Adoration. These late 15th-century works rank among the finest examples of Della Robbia art in the Casentino.

Church of Saints Ippolito and Donato (Propositura): the main religious building in the historic center, built in the early 12th century on the site of the Tarlati castle chapel, consecrated in 1314. The interior houses a 13th-century wooden sculpture of the Madonna and Child, a triptych by Bicci di Lorenzo and an Annunciation by Balducci.

Casentino Archaeological Museum Piero Albertoni — Palazzo Niccolini: the museum traces the valley's history from prehistory to the Middle Ages through local discoveries. The highlight is the collection of Etruscan bronzes from the Lago degli Idoli (Lake of the Idols), an artificial lake on the flank of Monte Falterona where over 14,000 Etruscan ex-votos were excavated, some of which are now in the British Museum and the Louvre.

activities.text: Sanctuary of Santa Maria del Sasso: a Renaissance complex 1 km from the historic center, built to the design of Giuliano da Maiano in Renaissance style on the site of a Marian apparition in 1347. The sanctuary contains a Madonna and Child by Bicci di Lorenzo, a Nativity of the Virgin by Ligozzi, an Assumption by Fra' Bartolomeo and Fra' Paolino, and a remarkable Renaissance cloister.

visits.text: Castle of the Conti Guidi of Poppi: first mentioned in writing in 1169, when it belonged to the abbey of San Fidele di Strumi. By the late 12th century, it had passed to the Conti Guidi, a feudal family whose territories stretched from the banks of the Po to the Apennines. The tower and keep were erected in the second half of the 13th century — the architect Arnolfo di Cambio is said to have used it as a prototype for the Palazzo Vecchio in Florence, which he designed shortly after. The Guidi Chapel is covered in frescoes by Taddeo Gaddi, a pupil of Giotto. Dante took refuge in the castle of Poppi during his exile, in 1307 and again in 1311; tradition holds that he composed Canto XXXIII of the Inferno here, dedicated to Count Ugolino della Gherardesca. A bust of the poet on the square before the castle recalls this connection. In 1440, the Guidi sided with the Milanese at Anghiari and were exiled, ending 350 years of dominion over the Casentino.

Pieve di San Pietro a Romena: one of the finest Romanesque parish churches in the Casentino, 5 km from Poppi in the commune of Pratovecchio. Tradition holds it was built at the request of Countess Matilda of Canossa. Dante mentions it in the Divine Comedy (Canto XXX of the Inferno), in connection with the counterfeiting of gold florins carried out at the nearby castle of Romena by Master Adam of Brescia on behalf of the Guidi di Romena — around 1280 at least. The sandstone building, with its three apses and plain facade, is a representative example of Tuscan Romanesque architecture.

Castello di Romena: a Conti Guidi di Romena fortress founded by the Marquises of Spoleto probably in the 11th century, atop a 626-meter hill. The castle reached its peak in the 13th century, in Dante's time. It retains three large fortified towers and the remains of three concentric walls. In 1902, the poet Gabriele d'Annunzio stayed there as a guest of the Goretti counts and wrote part of Alcyone.

Pratovecchio: birthplace of Paolo di Dono, known as Paolo Uccello (c. 1397), master of Renaissance perspective. The medieval village houses two convents of Camaldolese and Dominican nuns, and several remarkable artworks in its churches. The castle of Porciano, a 13th-century Guidi fortress, commands the valley from a neighboring hill.

activities.text: Hike in the Casentino Forests National Park from Poppi: the park, covering the forested massifs between Tuscany and Romagna, is one of the best-preserved forests in central Italy. Deer, wild boar, wolves and golden eagles inhabit these beech and fir woods. The Sasso Fratino Integral Reserve, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is off-limits to the public, but peripheral trails cross ancient-growth forests.

practical_info.difficulty: Easy to moderate depending on trail chosen

map_keywords: Pieve di Santa Maria Assunta Stia

visits.text: Piazza Tanucci and Pieve di Santa Maria Assunta: the heart of Stia is an irregular, gently sloping square lined with porticoes and decorated balconies — one of the most charming in the entire Casentino. It is named after Bernardo Tanucci, born in Stia in 1698, a jurist and royal adviser to the Kingdom of Naples. The Pieve di Santa Maria Assunta, erected in Romanesque style in the 12th century with a later-modified facade, preserves a glazed-terracotta Madonna and Child from the Della Robbia workshop.

Museum of the Art of Wool — Lanificio di Stia: housed in the former Stia wool mill — one of the most important in Italy in the first half of the 20th century, employing over 500 people — the museum traces the history of the Panno Casentino, a woolen cloth with its famous looped texture produced in the valley since the 14th century. The mill bell set the rhythm of village life for decades. The factory ceased production in 2000 before being converted into a museum. Samples, period photographs and pre-mechanization machines illustrate the evolution of this fabric, which became an internationally recognized fashion article in the 20th century.

activities.text: Hike to the source of the Arno from Passo della Calla: drive or cycle to the pass on the Romagna border, then about 2 hours' walk round trip to reach the source of the Arno on the slopes of Monte Falterona, at around 1,350 meters. The river, which will go on to flow through Florence before reaching the sea at Pisa after 241 km, begins here as a thin stream among the beeches.

practical_info.distance: ~7 km round trip from Passo della Calla

activities.text: Castle of Porciano: an 11th-century Conti Guidi di Romena fortress commanding the upper Casentino valley from a hill near Stia. The 13th-century crenellated tower and keep are accessible. Local legend says Dante Alighieri found refuge here during his exile from Florence. The castle was abandoned, then restored from the 1960s onward.

to_next_leg.road_type: Route de montagne pour rejoindre Camaldoli

visits.text: Monastery of Camaldoli: founded in 1046, roughly twenty years after the hermitage, on a former pilgrim hostel called Fontebuona for the quality of its water. The current monastic building was rebuilt from the 16th century and completed in 1611. The church contains seven panels by Giorgio Vasari, including a main altarpiece depicting the Deposition from the Cross. The library, founded in 1622 with a coffered ceiling painted with the Evangelists, held hundreds of codices and incunabula before the Napoleonic suppression of 1810. The monastery's historic pharmacy, heir to a tradition dating back to the Camaldolese origins, produces and sells natural remedies, liqueurs and body-care products according to recipes handed down for centuries.

Eremo di San Romualdo: hermitage founded around 1023 by Saint Romuald of Ravenna at 1,100 meters, 3 km from the communal monastery — the very distance representing the intended separation between eremitic and cenobitic life. Romuald installed the first five hermit cells and the oratory dedicated to the Holy Savior Transfigured, consecrated in 1027 by the Bishop of Arezzo. The current enclosure contains twenty cells. Among the spaces open to visitors: Saint Romuald's former cell, the 16th-century chapter house, and the church whose baroque facade with two towers dates from 1713–1714. The Camaldolese order, a Benedictine branch combining eremitism and cenobitism, is distinguished from other Benedictines by its white habit and full beard.

activities.text: Hike through the Camaldoli forest: the monumental forest surrounding the hermitage is composed mainly of centuries-old white firs planted by the monks for their commercial value — their timber was used notably for the construction of the dome of Florence's Duomo and the fleet of Pisa and Livorno. Marked trails lead to the Laghetto Traversari, an artificial pond dug in the early 15th century by the monks to raise fish, now naturalized and incorporated into the Camaldoli biogenetic reserve.

seo.h1_title: Casentino Circuit: Art, Villages and Sanctuaries
seo.intro_paragraph: From Lake Trasimeno and its Umbrian hilltop towns to the sacred forests of the Casentino, this circuit crosses two regions and ten centuries of history. Cortona and its Etruscan walls, the frescoes of Piero della Francesca in Arezzo and Monterchi, the battlefield where Leonardo da Vinci sought his greatest painting at Anghiari, the sanctuary where Saint Francis received the stigmata at La Verna, and the hermitage where Saint Romuald founded the Camaldolese order in 1023 — all woven together by medieval lanes, Romanesque churches and Renaissance palaces.
seo.seo_hook: Renaissance frescoes with their colors restored, crenellated towers of the Conti Guidi on their hilltops, monastery bells echoing through ancient forests — the Casentino is the Tuscany that tourism forgot.
featured_list: Arezzo: Piero della Francesca's fresco cycle of the Legend of the True Cross
Cortona: Etruscan art and Fra Angelico facing Lake Trasimeno
La Verna: the sanctuary where Saint Francis received the stigmata
Sansepolcro: the Resurrection that saved the town in 1944
Poppi: Dante's castle and the Conti Guidi stronghold
Camaldoli: the Benedictine hermitage founded in 1023

places_taglines.IT::castiglione-del-lago: Etruscan hilltop town on a lakeside promontory, medieval fortress facing the islands of Trasimeno
places_taglines.IT::cortona: Walled Etruscan city perched above Tuscany, keeper of a 4th-century BC bronze chandelier
places_taglines.IT::lucignano: Medieval village spiraling in concentric ellipses, famous for its 2.70-meter golden reliquary
places_taglines.IT::castiglion-fiorentino: Etruscan and medieval hilltop town, crowned by a tower and a loggia redesigned by Vasari
places_taglines.IT::arezzo: Renaissance city housing the greatest fresco cycle by Piero della Francesca
places_taglines.IT::monterchi: Birthplace of Piero della Francesca's mother, guardian of a pregnant Madonna unique in the world
places_taglines.IT::anghiari: Fortified medieval hilltop town where Leonardo da Vinci sought to paint his greatest battle
places_taglines.IT::sansepolcro: Birthplace of Piero della Francesca, home to the Resurrection called the greatest painting in the world
places_taglines.IT::la-verna: Franciscan sanctuary at 1,128 meters, site of Saint Francis's stigmata in 1224
places_taglines.IT::bibbiena: Capital of the Casentino, gateway to the national forests and keeper of Della Robbia terracottas
places_taglines.IT::poppi: Medieval hilltop village overlooking the Casentino, Dante's castle and Conti Guidi seat since the 12th century
places_taglines.IT::stia: Birthplace of the Casentino cloth since the 14th century, at the foot of the Arno's headwaters
places_taglines.IT::camaldoli: Benedictine hermitage founded in 1023 by Saint Romuald, immersed in a millennial fir forest

highlights: Piero della Francesca's fresco cycle in Arezzo, restored over ten years and reopened in 2000 — 15 scenes of the Legend of the True Cross painted between 1452 and 1466
The sanctuary of La Verna, where Saint Francis received the stigmata on September 17, 1224 — Della Robbia terracottas and a monumental forest of centuries-old firs
The Albero d'Oro of Lucignano, a 2.70-meter gilded copper reliquary crafted over 120 years (1350–1471) — one of the masterpieces of Italian goldsmithing
The Conti Guidi castle at Poppi, Dante's refuge in exile and alleged prototype for the Palazzo Vecchio in Florence
The Giostra del Saracino in Arezzo, a medieval jousting tournament with 350 costumed participants and 27 horses

itineraries.title: Lucca Province Circuit: Hill Towns and Coast
itineraries.subtitle: From medieval Lucca to the perched villages of Garfagnana, through the marble workshops of Versilia to the golden beaches of the Mediterranean.
specialties: Colline Lucchesi DOC wines (Trebbiano whites, Sangiovese reds)
Buccellato di Lucca (traditional anise-fennel sweet bread)
Lardo di Colonnata (marble-aged cured pork fat)

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Michele in Foro Lucca

visits.text: Piazza dell'Anfiteatro: Lucca's oval plaza is no reconstructed set piece—it traces the exact perimeter of a 2nd-century AD Roman amphitheatre that once held 10,000 spectators. Medieval residents built their houses directly against the ancient stone tiers, erasing the arena but preserving its elliptical form. The 19th-century restoration by architect Lorenzo Nottolini cleared the square and opened the four arched passages that still produce the element of surprise described by Grand Tour travelers. Enter through one of the vaulted passages to understand how an entire city grew from the bones of a Roman monument.

San Martino Cathedral: Founded in the 6th century by Bishop Fridian, rebuilt from the 11th century onward, and completed in the 16th–17th centuries, the cathedral displays a distinctive asymmetrical façade—the architect had to narrow the final arcade to fit the pre-existing bell tower, creating an elegant dissonance. Inside, the main treasure is the Volto Santo, a 13th-century wooden crucifix traditionally attributed to Nicodemus and carried in a candlelit procession every September 13th since the Middle Ages. In the sacristy, the tomb of Ilaria del Carretto (1406) sculpted by Jacopo della Quercia shows the young wife of Paolo Guinigi lying with a dog at her feet—a symbol of marital fidelity—in a style that would influence Donatello.

Torre Guinigi: The Guinigi family's red brick tower, erected in the late 14th century, reaches 44.25 meters. Its unique feature is a garden of holm oaks planted at the summit—a symbol of rebirth chosen by the Guinigi, then lords of Lucca. Paolo Guinigi, who ruled the city from 1400 to 1430, made this tower the emblem of his mercantile and banking power. Climb the 230 steps for sweeping views of tiled rooftops, ramparts, and, on clear days, the Apuan Alps.

San Michele in Foro Church: Built on the site of the ancient Roman forum, San Michele displays a 12th-century Pisan-Lucchese façade with four tiers of sculpted columns, each different from the next. Throughout the Middle Ages, the building also served as the courthouse for the Republic of Lucca. Inside: a 14th-century frescoed Virgin and Child and a glazed ceramic Madonna by Luca della Robbia.

Puccini's Birthplace (Museo Puccini): Giacomo Puccini was born on December 22, 1858, in this apartment on Corte San Lorenzo, where the Puccini family had served for five generations as organists and chapel masters at the cathedral. The museum preserves the Steinway & Sons piano purchased in 1901 on which he composed Turandot, opera manuscripts, costumes, and stage photographs. Puccini bought back the family apartment after his sister sold it—proof of the composer's attachment to his native city.

activities.text: Bike ride on the ramparts: Built between 1513 and 1650 along a 4.2 km circuit, Lucca's Renaissance walls constitute one of the best-preserved examples of modern fortifications in Europe. Thirty meters wide at the base and 12 meters high, they never saw combat—Lucca maintained its independence through diplomacy until 1799. Today they're transformed into a tree-lined promenade bordered by plane trees and oaks, accessible on foot or by bike. Rentals are available at the main gates.

Colline Lucchesi DOC wine tasting: The Colline Lucchesi appellation covers the hills immediately north and east of Lucca. It produces Trebbiano-based whites and Sangiovese-based reds cultivated on these slopes since Roman times. Several estates in the province offer tastings.

visits.text: Palazzo Mansi - National Museum: Built in the 17th century for a wealthy family of Lucchese merchants, this palace preserves its state apartments almost intact—music room with allegorical frescoes, hall of mirrors, painting collections from the Medici and Grand Duchy holdings. Lucca built its fortune between the 13th and 15th centuries on silk trade, exporting fabrics as far as England and Flanders. This palace is the direct architectural expression of that wealth.

Palazzo Pfanner and Gardens: Built in 1660 for the Moricani family, then purchased in the mid-19th century by the Austrian-origin Pfanner family—who still own it. It opens onto one of the rare Italian-style gardens still preserved within the walls, with a central fountain surrounded by statues of deities and seasons. Jane Campion's film Portrait of a Lady was partly shot here.

Via Fillungo and Historic Center: The main artery of the pedestrian center concentrates historic cafés, artisan shops, and fragments of medieval architecture. The Torre delle Ore, whose first clock dates to 1390, marks its northern end. Puccini frequented Caffè Di Simo, an establishment opened in 1875 whose Art Nouveau storefront is still visible.

Villa Guinigi - National Museum: Former country residence of Paolo Guinigi, lord of Lucca from 1400 to 1430, built outside the medieval walls in the style of Venetian Palladian villas with an arcaded loggia. The museum houses collections ranging from 8th-century BC Etruscan discoveries to medieval sculptures from churches suppressed during the Napoleonic era.

activities.text: Antiques market: Every third Sunday of the month, the antiques market spreads across the main squares of the historic center—Piazza San Martino, Piazza San Giovanni, Piazza Bernardini—with several hundred vendors. It's one of the most frequented antiques markets in Tuscany.

map_keywords: Pieve di San Piero in Campo Montecarlo

visits.text: Rocca di Cerruglio: The village of Montecarlo owes its name to Charles IV of Luxembourg—Mons Charoli in Latin—who contributed in 1333 to liberating Lucca from Pisan occupation. The Republic of Lucca then had the destroyed village rebuilt on Cerruglio hill and erected this three-tower fortress: the semi-circular keep, the Tower of the Apparition, and the Tower of Saint Barbara, the latter named for a legend that the Virgin appeared to defend the village during a 15th-century Pisan siege. The fortress is open to visitors with views over the plain of Lucca and the Valdinievole.

Collegiata di Sant'Andrea: In the heart of the village, this collegiate church founded in the 14th century preserves its original Gothic façade and portal, remodeled in Baroque style in the 18th century. Inside, a fresco of the 15th-century Florentine school depicts the Madonna del Soccorso snatching a child from the devil's claws. Two plaques commemorate visits by poet Giuseppe Ungaretti in the 1960s and composer Giacomo Puccini.

Teatro dei Rassicurati: Built in the early 18th century, this tiny Italian-style theater ranks among the five smallest in Italy. It takes its name from the Academy of the Rassicurati, a local learned society founded in 1690. Puccini visited several times. The auditorium, with its lacquered wooden boxes and ceiling frescoes, has remained almost unchanged since construction.

Pieve di San Piero in Campo: At the foot of the hill, this 12th-century Romanesque parish church with three naves is one of the oldest Christian buildings in Lucca province. Its sober limestone façade and austere interior contrast with the Baroque exuberance of the collegiate church perched atop the village.

activities.text: Montecarlo DOC tasting at family estates: Montecarlo's winemaking tradition dates to Roman times, but it was in 1870 that a local landowner imported French grape varieties from Bordeaux (Sauvignon, Sémillon, Cabernet, Merlot) and Burgundy (Pinot Blanc, Pinot Gris) to blend with Tuscan Trebbiano. Montecarlo Bianco DOC—recognized in 1969—has been celebrated since the 15th century: Pope Gregory XII praised it in the early 1400s. Village estates welcome visitors year-round.

map_keywords: Ponte della Maddalena Borgo a Mozzano
Ponte del Diavolo Borgo a Mozzano

visits.text: Ponte della Maddalena (Devil's Bridge): This humpback bridge spans the Serchio with a main arch rising 18.50 meters flanked by four smaller asymmetric arches—a silhouette that has intrigued travelers since the 16th century, notably Michel de Montaigne who described it in his 1581 Travel Journal. The structure probably dates to the 11th century, but its current appearance is due to Castruccio Castracani, lord of Lucca, who had it rebuilt between 1324 and 1327. On the Via Francigena pilgrimage route from Canterbury to Rome, the bridge held during World War II: retreating German troops had mined it but it was never destroyed, which proved crucial for the Allies. Legend tells that the mason, despairing of floods from the Serchio, made a pact with the devil to finish the work in one night—in exchange for the first soul to cross the bridge. The village priest sent a dog across, outwitting the devil. They say this white dog still walks the bridge on October evenings.

map_keywords: Real Casino Ponte a Serraglio Bagni di Lucca
Ponte delle Catene Bagni di Lucca
Orrido di Botri Bagni di Lucca

visits.text: Historic Thermal Center and Terme Bagni di Lucca: Known since Roman times, Bagni di Lucca's hot springs took off in the 11th century thanks to Countess Matilda of Canossa, before becoming in the 19th century one of Europe's most fashionable spa resorts. Elisa Baciocchi, Napoleon's sister and Princess of Lucca, had the Bernabò establishment built here in 1810, where waters emerge naturally at 39.2°C. The thermal complex has welcomed Byron, Shelley, Montale, Henry James, and Puccini. The English called the place "the Switzerland of Tuscany."

Real Casino of Ponte a Serraglio: Inaugurated in 1839, this casino was one of the very first in Europe—some sources credit Bagni di Lucca with inventing roulette around 1837. The Art Nouveau building, restored and reopened in 2009 after years of closure, overlooks the banks of the Lima and constitutes one of the best-preserved testimonies of 19th-century aristocratic tourism.

Ponte delle Catene (Chain Bridge): This iron suspension bridge designed by Lorenzo Nottolini—the same architect who redesigned Lucca's Piazza dell'Anfiteatro—is one of the oldest metal bridges still standing in Italy. It spans the Lima between the hamlets of Ponte a Serraglio and Villa Fiori.

Italy's first Anglican church and English cemetery: The British community established in Bagni di Lucca from the early 19th century built the country's first Anglican church here. The adjoining cemetery holds the graves of English, American, and other nationalities who chose to end their days in this valley.

activities.text: Hiking in Orrido di Botri: A few kilometers north, this canyon carved by the Pelago torrent through the Apennines offers vertical limestone gorges and waterfalls. The ascent follows the riverbed for several kilometers; a protective helmet is recommended. Access is regulated and best done with a local guide.
Thermal treatments: The Bagni Caldi thermal establishments offer pools, natural steam grottos, and treatments using sulfurous waters whose natural temperature approaches 39°C.

map_keywords: Duomo dei Santi Pietro e Paolo Castelnuovo di Garfagnana
Fortezza di Mont'Alfonso Castelnuovo di Garfagnana

visits.text: Rocca Ariostesca: This trapezoidal fortress has dominated Piazza Umberto I since the 10th century, but its 14th-century reconstruction by Castruccio Castracani gave it its current form. It takes its name from poet Ludovico Ariosto—author of Orlando Furioso—who resided here from 1522 to 1525 as Este governor for Alfonso I d'Este, Duke of Ferrara. Ariosto, who called Garfagnana "land of wolves and brigands," nevertheless wrote several of his Satires here. The central tower, equipped with a civic clock, and the terrace added in 1675 overlooking the square make it one of the most complete examples of communal fortification in the region. Every Thursday, the weekly market is held at the foot of the rocca—a tradition so ancient that a ducal decree once forbade arresting criminals on that day.

Cathedral of Santi Pietro e Paolo: Built in the 15th century in Renaissance style on an 11th-century Romanesque church, then largely remodeled in the Baroque era. The austere gray stone façade contrasts with the richness of the interior: apse redone in the 19th century, Baroque altars, and series of 15th–16th century paintings. The church is a waypoint on the Via Matildica del Volto Santo linking Mantua to Lucca.

Medieval Historic Center and ramparts: The fortified heart of Castelnuovo, at the confluence of the Serchio and Turrite Secca, preserves its medieval alleys, arched gates (Porta dei Borselli), and Clock Tower. The town is the administrative capital of Garfagnana and the meeting point of two historic pilgrimage routes.

activities.text: Sentiero dell'Ariosto to Fortezza di Mont'Alfonso: A 6 km hiking trail dedicated to the poet, starting from Rocca Ariostesca, crossing the bridge over the Turrite Secca and climbing toward the village of Torrite then the bastioned fortress of Mont'Alfonso, built by Alfonso II d'Este between 1579 and 1586, with panoramas over the Apuan Alps and Apennines.
Thursday weekly market: Castelnuovo's market is the largest in Garfagnana, with cheeses, charcuterie (biroldo della Garfagnana, Colonnata lard), and mountain products.

map_keywords: Riserva Naturale dell'Orecchiella Castelnuovo di Garfagnana
Parco Regionale delle Alpi Apuane Castelnuovo di Garfagnana

visits.text: Orecchiella Nature Reserve: Created in 1980, this 500-hectare reserve in the Tuscan-Emilian Apennines shelters chamois, deer, foxes, and vegetation of beech forests and alpine meadows. The Corfino visitor center offers exhibitions on local fauna.

Lago di Vagli and the sunken village of Fabbriche di Careggine: The artificial lake created in 1953 for a hydroelectric plant submerged the village of Fabbriche di Careggine. During the decennial draining of the dam, the ruins reappear—a phenomenon that attracts thousands of visitors each time. The site offers panoramas over the Apuan Alps.

activities.text: Hiking in the Apuan Alps (Parco Regionale): The regional park protects the white marble peaks dominating Versilia. Trails of varying difficulty lead toward Pania della Croce (1858 m). CAI (Italian Alpine Club) refuges allow outings of 4 to 8 hours.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate to difficult depending on route
practical_info.booking: IGM maps available at tourist office

visits.text: Duomo di San Cristoforo: Perched at the village's highest point (410 m), this Romanesque cathedral was built in stages from the 11th to 16th centuries in blocks of alberese, a local gray-cream limestone. Its 13th-century marble pulpit, the work of Guido Bigarelli, is supported by four columns, two resting on recumbent lions. Inside: a ciborium of the Holy Oils by Andrea della Robbia, a 14th-century Madonna del Molino, and a medieval polychrome wooden statue of San Cristoforo. From the churchyard, views extend over the entire historic center and, on clear days, to the Apuan Alps.

Palazzo Pretorio and Museo Civico del Territorio: Residence of Florentine commissioners from 1341 to 1859—when Barga chose to submit to Florence rather than Lucca, obtaining exemption from numerous taxes. The palace houses the municipal museum with archaeological collections (prehistory, Ligurians, Romans) and artworks from the Middle Ages and Renaissance. The old medieval prisons are still visitable.

Loggia dei Mercanti and Piazza Salvo Salvi: This loggia with slender columns was built in 1546 on orders from Cosimo I de' Medici to shelter Barga's market, whose main products were silk and salt. The stone lion (Marzocco) on the façade symbolizes Florence's political guardianship. Palazzo Pancrazi, seat of the town hall, displays a diamond-bossed façade characteristic of 16th-century Florentine architecture.

Teatro dei Differenti: Inaugurated in 1689 and rebuilt in 1795, this Italian-style theater is one of the best preserved in Tuscany. It was here that Giovanni Pascoli delivered on November 26, 1911, his famous speech La grande proletaria si è mossa in favor of the Italo-Turkish War in Libya.

activities.text: Barga Jazz Festival (August): Since 1986, this internationally renowned jazz festival has animated the squares and alleys of the medieval historic center each summer. Musicians from around the world perform in this medieval setting for a week in August.

visits.text: Casa Museo Giovanni Pascoli in Castelvecchio (4 km from Barga): Giovanni Pascoli, major poet of Italian Symbolism, purchased this 18th-century country house in 1895 and lived here with his sister Mariù until his death in 1912. He composed here his most celebrated collections, including Myricae and Canti di Castelvecchio, directly inspired by the surrounding landscapes. The museum preserves books, manuscripts, and everyday objects in the state the family left them. Pascoli is buried in the garden chapel, beneath cypresses and wisteria.

Medieval Historic Center of Barga: Classified among "Borghi più belli d'Italia" and labeled Cittaslow (slow cities network for quality of life), Barga has kept its 12th–14th century communal urban fabric almost intact. Enter through Porta Reale—so renamed after Grand Duke Leopold of Lorraine's visit—and climb the cobbled alleys to the Duomo. The presence of a significant Scottish community, heirs of hundreds of Barghigiani who left to settle in Glasgow between the 19th and 20th centuries as ice cream vendors and fish and chips shop managers, has earned Barga the nickname "most Scottish town in Italy."

activities.text: Via dei Poeti and Castelvecchio surroundings: Marked trail linking the main sites associated with Pascoli in the countryside around Castelvecchio, with views of the Apuan Alps.

map_keywords: Museo della Figurina di Gesso Coreglia Antelminelli
Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Coreglia Antelminelli
Torre di Castruccio Castracani Coreglia Antelminelli

visits.text: Museum of the Plaster Figurine and Emigration (Palazzo Vanni): From the 16th century until the 20th, the figurinai of Coreglia Antelminelli crafted plaster statuettes—religious subjects, mythological characters, celebrity busts—and left to sell them in the great cities of Europe and beyond. This migratory phenomenon, unique to this village, led to the founding in 1975 of the museum that today houses 1,300 pieces divided into two sections: one dedicated to productions from the 17th–20th centuries, the other to artisan emigration with period passports, letters, and documents. Among the most unusual pieces: 18th-century cats colored with candle smoke.

San Michele Arcangelo Church and Tower of Castruccio Castracani: Built in the 12th century on the site of an old Antelminelli fortress, San Michele church preserves an Annunciation from the Pisan school (1352), a 14th-century painting of the Archangel Saint Michael, and a pre-Romanesque ambo depicting a cow. The imposing adjoining bell tower—former watchtower of Castruccio Castracani, erected in the 11th century and approximately 25 meters high—dominates the village from the ridge at 595 meters altitude.

Historic Center and panorama over the Serchio valley: The medieval village, classified among "Borghi più belli d'Italia," preserves its vaulted streets, arched gates, and 16th-century palaces (Palazzo Antonimi, Palazzo Vincenti, 1572 town hall). From the square outside the walls, views extend over the entire Serchio valley and the silhouette of the Apuan Alps to the west.

activities.text: Medieval Day on August 15th: Every August 15th, Coreglia Antelminelli organizes a historical reenactment with a procession of halberdiers, artisan market, tastings of local products, and street performances in the medieval village lit by torches.

map_keywords: Pieve dei Santi Giovanni e Stefano Camaiore

visits.text: Collegiata Santa Maria Assunta: Founded in the 13th century and profoundly remodeled in the Baroque era, this collegiate church is Camaiore's central monument. Its Romanesque façade contrasts with the decorative richness of the Baroque interior. The adjoining sacred art museum collections preserve furnishings, objects, and paintings from the 14th–16th centuries, including several noteworthy works of local provenance.

San Pietro Abbey: Founded by Benedictine monks between the 8th and 13th centuries, this three-nave Romanesque abbey with bell tower constitutes one of the oldest testimonies of Christianity in Versilia. Accessible on foot from the center via an olive-lined path, it's approached through a 14th-century gate preserving part of the original convent walls. The sober architecture in local limestone reflects Benedictine austerity.

Pieve dei Santi Giovanni e Stefano: Erected in the 12th century at the junction of the Via Francigena (of which Camaiore is the 26th stage), this Romanesque parish church has a bell tower with twin windows on three levels. Inside, the baptismal font was carved from a 3rd-century BC Roman sarcophagus—a characteristic reuse of medieval religious architecture.

Archaeological Museum of Palazzo Tori Massoni: Installed in a Renaissance palace, this museum preserves discoveries covering prehistoric, Ligurian, Etruscan, Roman, and medieval periods. The Camaiore region (from the Roman Campus Major, the great plain) was inhabited since the Neolithic, as attested by finds from the woods of Lucese.

activities.text: Hiking in the hills of Versilia around Camaiore: The hills rising toward the Apuan Alps offer routes through vineyards, olive groves, and perched villages like Monteggiori, Casoli, or Gombitelli, with views of the sea and countryside. Camaiore is an ideal starting point for hikes toward Monte Prana.

practical_info.difficulty: Easy to moderate depending on route

visits.text: Piazza del Duomo and Duomo San Martino: Pietrasanta's rectangular square is the artistic heart of the town. San Martino Cathedral, whose construction dates to the 14th century, presents a façade clad in local marble and an interior enriched with 19th-century frescoes and artworks from preceding centuries. The brick bell tower, commissioned around 1520 from Donato Benti—a collaborator of Michelangelo—conceals an extraordinary self-supporting helical staircase in brick, long overlooked. It was during the period when Michelangelo stayed in Pietrasanta to extract marble destined for the façade of San Lorenzo in Florence that this staircase was designed, according to most historians.

Museo dei Bozzetti (Museum of Sketches) - Chiostro di Sant'Agostino: Created in 1984 in the former convent of Sant'Agostino (14th century), this museum unique in the world documents the creative process of more than 350 Italian and international sculptors who have worked in Pietrasanta. It preserves over 700 maquettes and plaster models that preceded marble or bronze works now dispersed in museums and public spaces worldwide. Botero, Mitoraj, Folon, Cascella, Pomodoro, Miró, and Henry Moore are represented.

Marble and bronze workshops - Parco Internazionale della Scultura Contemporanea: Pietrasanta houses some fifty marble sculpture workshops and artistic bronze foundries. The town, nicknamed "Little Athens," has welcomed since the 15th century—when Michelangelo stayed here to work marble from nearby quarries—sculptors from around the world. Henry Moore was among the first modern foreigners to discover Pietrasanta in the 1950s; he was followed by Noguchi, Miró, Mitoraj, and Botero. The International Park of Contemporary Sculpture presents over 70 monumental works donated to the town, visible in the streets and squares.

Museo Archeologico Bruno Antonucci (Palazzo Moroni, 16th century): Located on Piazza del Duomo, this museum presents collections ranging from prehistory to the medieval era, with Etruscan and Roman pieces discovered in the Versilia territory.

activities.text: Stroll among art galleries and sculptors' workshops: Pietrasanta counts more than one gallery per 1,200 inhabitants—a record density even in Tuscany. Several workshops open their doors to the public, allowing observation of artisans working marble or bronze.

Thursday weekly market and antiques market (1st Sunday of month): The Thursday market covers Piazza del Duomo and adjacent streets with food, crafts, and local products. The first Sunday of the month, antique dealers set up in the same squares.

map_keywords: Parco della Versiliana Marina di Pietrasanta

visits.text: Fortino (Fort Lorenz): At the center of Piazza Garibaldi, this quadrangular fort was built in the late 16th century on orders of Grand Duke Pietro Leopoldo of Tuscany, initially to protect the coastline and store marble from the Apuan Alps before loading it onto ships. The town takes its name from this fort—Forte dei Marmi literally means "fort of the marbles." Until April 26, 1914, Forte dei Marmi was attached to Pietrasanta; it became an independent municipality on that date. The fort today houses the Museum of Satire and Caricature, founded on the collections of the Political Satire Prize created in 1973.

Pontile (historic pier): Built between 1867 and 1877 to load marble from the Apuan Alps directly onto ships, this wharf equipped with rails and a steam crane was destroyed during World War II and rebuilt in 1955. The walk on the jetty, with the Apuan Alps behind and the Tyrrhenian Sea ahead, is the emblematic panorama of Forte dei Marmi.

Parco della Versiliana: On the border between Forte dei Marmi and Marina di Pietrasanta, this park of umbrella pines spanning several dozen hectares was frequented by literary and artistic figures of the 19th and 20th centuries. It now hosts a renowned summer cultural festival (theater, concerts, exhibitions) throughout Italy.

activities.text: Beach and swimming at Versilia: The beach establishments of Forte dei Marmi, with their white-striped tents and wooden cabanas, are set on a fine sand beach of about 5 km. The sandy seabed slopes gently into the Tyrrhenian Sea.

Excursion to the marble quarries of Carrara: About 20 km north, the quarries of the Apuan Alps have been exploited since Roman times. Michelangelo came to personally select his blocks. Guided tours by all-terrain vehicle allow approach to active quarry faces.

visits.text: Pontile di Lido di Camaiore: The wooden pier of Lido di Camaiore, symbol of the seaside resort, extends into the Tyrrhenian Sea facing the Apuan Alps. Less frequented than those of Forte dei Marmi or Viareggio, this promenade on stilts offers a peaceful panorama over the Versilia coastline.

activities.text: Beach and waterfront: Lido di Camaiore stretches for several kilometers of fine sand between the Tyrrhenian Sea and the pine forest. Less fashionable than Forte dei Marmi, the resort offers family beach establishments with all the infrastructure of traditional Italian beach culture. The coastal bike path links Lido di Camaiore to Forte dei Marmi in the north and Viareggio in the south.

Versilia bike path: The path runs along the waterfront for over 20 km, from Viareggio to Marina di Pietrasanta, allowing you to connect the main seaside resorts of Versilia by bike while riding directly along the sea.

seo.h1_title: Lucca Province Circuit: Medieval Villages, Garfagnana and Versilia Coast
seo.intro_paragraph: From Lucca and its Renaissance ramparts, the circuit climbs to the wine hills of Montecarlo, descends toward the Devil's Bridge at Borgo a Mozzano and the thermal baths of Bagni di Lucca before plunging into medieval Garfagnana: Castelnuovo where Ariosto served as governor, Barga the town of Giovanni Pascoli, Coreglia Antelminelli and its figurinai exporting plaster art. The descent to the sea passes through Camaiore and its Benedictine abbeys, Pietrasanta the Little Athens of marble where Michelangelo, Botero, and Mitoraj worked, Forte dei Marmi and its pier from which Carrara blocks departed. Mountain and coastline, stone and marble, poetry and sculpture.
seo.seo_hook: Intact Renaissance ramparts, a Roman amphitheatre turned village square, the Devil's Bridge over the Serchio, a village submerged beneath a reservoir lake, marble workshops frequented by Michelangelo. Lucca province reveals a Tuscany the crowds don't know.
featured_list: Lucca: Renaissance city encircled by ramparts intact since 1513
Barga: medieval village classified among Italy's most beautiful, homeland of Pascoli
Pietrasanta: world capital of marble sculpture since Michelangelo
Castelnuovo di Garfagnana: medieval fortress residence of Ariosto

places_taglines.IT::lucca: Where a Roman amphitheatre became a living piazza and Renaissance walls never saw battle
places_taglines.IT::montecarlo-toscana: Hilltop village perched above vineyards that popes praised five centuries ago
places_taglines.IT::borgo-a-mozzano: Where the Devil built a bridge in one night and a clever priest saved the town's soul
places_taglines.IT::bagni-di-lucca: Byron's Switzerland of Tuscany, where thermal waters flow at 39 degrees since Roman days
places_taglines.IT::castelnuovo-garfagnana: Stone fortress where Ariosto governed wolves and brigands and wrote poetry anyway
places_taglines.IT::barga: Scotland's Italian sister city, where Pascoli found his muse in Apuan mountain light
places_taglines.IT::coreglia-antelminelli: Village of plaster artisans who carried their craft from the Serchio to the world
places_taglines.IT::camaiore: Where pilgrims on the Via Francigena found shelter in Benedictine stone since the 8th century
places_taglines.IT::pietrasanta: Little Athens where Michelangelo chose his marble and Botero still shapes bronze
places_taglines.IT::forte-dei-marmi: Where marble met the Mediterranean and a fort became Italy's most elegant beach
places_taglines.IT::lido-di-camaiore: The family beach where the Apuan Alps watch over the Tyrrhenian from the shore

highlights: Lucca and its 4.2 km of Renaissance ramparts accessible on foot and by bike
Preserved Garfagnana: medieval villages, chestnut forests and hiking in the Apuan Alps
Pietrasanta and its marble workshops where sculptors from around the world work
Versilia: fine sand beaches between Mediterranean and Apuan Alps

itineraries.title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns & Thermal Spas
itineraries.subtitle: From medieval Pistoia to Art Nouveau thermal baths, through mountain fortresses and hilltop villages of the Valdinievole valley to Pinocchio's birthplace.

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Giovanni Fuorcivitas Pistoia

visits.text: Piazza del Duomo brings together the four pillars of medieval power in a single theatrical space: San Zeno Cathedral, the Baptistery, Palazzo del Podestà, and Palazzo dei Vescovi. The campanile tells the city's history in stone—originally a Lombard defensive tower, then heightened in the 12th and 13th centuries in Pisan Romanesque style. Each floor marks a different era, from the austere lower masonry to the elegant Pisan arcading at the summit.

San Zeno Cathedral shelters one of medieval Europe's most extraordinary goldsmithing treasures: the Silver Altar of Saint James, begun in 1287 and enriched over two centuries by Tuscany's finest goldsmiths, including a young Filippo Brunelleschi who worked on it around 1399-1400. The crypt holds the relics of Saint Zeno, while Coppo di Marcovaldo's monumental Crucifix from the 1270s dominates the central nave with its hieratic figures against a shimmering gold background.

The Ospedale del Ceppo, founded according to tradition in 1277—its name comes from a dry tree stump that miraculously flowered to mark the site—operated as a hospital for over seven centuries until 2013. Its early 16th-century loggia bears one of the Renaissance's finest glazed terracotta friezes: commissioned from 1511 by the monk Leonardo Buonafede, it united the rival workshops of the Della Robbia and Buglioni families. Giovanni della Robbia created the 1525 roundels depicting scenes from Mary's life; the narrative frieze illustrates the Seven Works of Mercy in 16th-century settings and costumes, a vivid documentary of daily life in Renaissance Pistoia.

The Hospital Museum del Ceppo, installed in the former wards since 2017, displays several hundred surgical instruments from the 17th to early 20th centuries, exhibited in bed-shaped vitrines along the old men's ward. The visit culminates in the neoclassical oval Anatomical Theater, built 1785-1787, where medical students learned anatomy. A wooden and leather birthing machine, loaned to London's Science Museum in 1913 and returned to Pistoia a century later, is now on display.

San Giovanni Fuorcivitas—"outside the walls," as it was originally built beyond the first Lombard ramparts—presents one of the most spectacular side facades in Pisan Romanesque architecture: a densely packed alternation of white marble and green Prato serpentine bands, constructed from the 12th to 14th centuries. The north portal's architrave, carved in 1166 by Gruamonte with the Last Supper, is one of the earliest examples of Pistoiese Romanesque sculpture. Inside, three masterpieces await: Fra Guglielmo da Pisa's pulpit (1270), disciple of Nicola Pisano; the central holy water font attributed to Nicola Pisano and his workshop; and Luca della Robbia's Visitation in glazed terracotta (1445), the first surviving freestanding sculpture created by the Della Robbia workshop using the invetriatura technique.

The Basilica della Madonna dell'Umiltà traces its origin to a miraculous image of the Virgin dated 1490. The foundation stone was laid in 1495 following plans by Giuliano da Sangallo. Cosimo I later commissioned Giorgio Vasari to roof the building: the dome he constructed from 1561, visible across the entire city, deliberately references Brunelleschi's in Florence and ranks among Tuscany's largest domes.

activities.text: Pistoia Sotterranea: beneath the Ospedale del Ceppo's forecourt runs a network of brick-vaulted tunnels following the ancient course of the Brana torrent, channeled and covered as the city expanded. The public route covers about 650 meters of the total 1,200-meter network, with panels showing the successive phases of urban expansion and the remnants of walls, towers and bridges integrated into the underground.

practical_info.booking: Advance booking recommended, departures from Ospedale del Ceppo

activities.text: The Pieve di Sant'Andrea houses the absolute masterpiece of Pistoiese pulpits: Giovanni Pisano's pulpit, completed in 1301. Its reliefs of shattering expressiveness—the Massacre of the Innocents, the Nativity, the Crucifixion—achieve a sculptural depth and dramatic tension unmatched in early 14th-century Tuscany.
