Pistoia is the only city in the world to possess so many major medieval pulpits within such a compact area.

The Civic Museum of Ancient Art (Palazzo del Podestà) preserves works from the 13th to 16th centuries, including the famous altarpiece by Giuliano da Rimini and panels by Pistoiese masters. The 13th-century palace facade, with its rounded arches and massive tower, stands as one of Tuscany's best-preserved examples of communal palace architecture.

Wander through the daily market in Piazza della Sala, where stalls cluster around a medieval well surrounded by arcades. Here Pistoia shows its living face, amid displays of citrus fruits, cheeses and cured meats from the Pistoiese Apennines.

map_keywords: Chiesa di San Bartolomeo in Pantano Pistoia

visits.text: The Antico Palazzo dei Vescovi, facing the cathedral across Piazza del Duomo, houses the Cathedral Museum with its collections of sacred goldwork and medieval art. Excavations in the basements have uncovered superimposed Roman and Lombard remains, visible in the underground archaeological route.

San Bartolomeo in Pantano—"in the swamp," as it was built on marshy ground outside the first ramparts in the 8th century—preserves the third great Pistoiese pulpit: created in 1250 by Guido da Como, it's the oldest of the three major medieval pulpits surviving in the city, with reliefs of the Annunciation, Nativity and Adoration of the Magi set against polychrome marble panels.

Marliana, a farming village at 500 meters altitude on the north slope of Montalbano, is surrounded by olive groves and vineyards producing the oils and wines of the Pistoiese hills. Its medieval Rocca commands views across the Pistoia plain to the Apennines. The village long served as one of the wine-producing outposts supplying Pistoia's merchants.

activities.text: Pistoia Zoo, founded in 1970, occupies 7 hectares in the northern part of the city and is home to over 500 animals of 100 species. It's known for its endangered species conservation programs and naturalistic habitats, ranking among central Italy's most active zoological institutions.

Walk through the nursery district around Pistoia: the plain between Pistoia and Pescia forms Italy's main ornamental plant nursery zone, exporting millions of plants annually throughout Europe. Marked paths wind through greenhouses and ornamental plant fields from October to April.

The Embroidery Museum (Palazzo Rospigliosi) traces the history of Pistoiese embroidery, a craft of excellence that dressed European courts from the 17th century. The Rospigliosi family, one of whose members became Pope Clement IX in 1667, were among the great patrons of this art.

map_keywords: Oratorio della Vergine Assunta Serravalle Pistoiese
Pieve di Santo Stefano Serravalle Pistoiese
Chiesa di San Michele Arcangelo Serravalle Pistoiese

visits.text: The Torre del Barbarossa rises 42 meters at the village's highest point, on the ruins of the Rocca Vecchia built by Pistoia in the 12th century. Its name remains enigmatic: no historical document connects it to Frederick I Barbarossa. What's certain is its function: from its summit, sentries watched over the Valdinievole to Lucca and the sea, communicating by light signals with neighboring towers at Monsummano Alto and Montecatini Alto. The entrance door sits 5.20 meters above ground level, following medieval military architecture standards.

The Rocca Nuova, also called Castruccio's fortress, was built in 1302 after Serravalle's conquest by the Lucchese, under the direction of Uguccione della Faggiola, then completed by Castruccio Castracani. It preserves its powerful walls, a central hexagonal-base tower and a corbelled turret connected by a parapet walk. The Gabella Gate—so named for its medieval toll-collection role—opens through a rounded arch on molded consoles. Entry is free.

The Oratorio della Vergine Assunta is considered by specialists a masterpiece of late Trecento Pistoiese painting. The frescoes on its walls, discovered by chance in the 1980s, reveal a Crucifixion facing the entrance, a Last Judgment on the right wall with the damned below and apostles at mid-height, and the Virgin with Christ in glory at the summit—colors of startling brilliance and expressions of rare intensity for the period.

The Pieve di Santo Stefano and San Michele Arcangelo church (12th-13th centuries) form with the two towers the characteristic panorama of Serravalle, visible from the Firenze-Mare motorway. San Michele preserves a 14th-century fresco of the Miracle of San Biagio and a triptych by Bartolomeo di Andrea Bocchi dated 1438.

activities.text: Walk along the ramparts and through Serravalle's medieval lanes: the entire village can be explored by following the single main street linking Porta Gabella to Torre del Barbarossa, passing through stone houses with arched doorways decorated with coats of arms, remnants of the 13th-century prosperity when Serravalle controlled the obligatory passage between Pistoia and Lucca on the ancient Via Cassia.

Visit Serra Pistoiese (850m altitude) and the Museum of Peasant Culture: 15km to the northeast, this small Apennine village presents in its ethnographic museum the tools and farming practices of the Pistoiese mountains, from charcoal production to high-altitude haymaking.

map_keywords: Palazzo dei Capitani della Montagna Cutigliano
Chiesa della Madonna di Piazza Cutigliano

visits.text: The Palazzo dei Capitani della Montagna, built in 1377 to house the Florentine governor charged with administering the Pistoiese mountains, displays 95 stone and polychrome terracotta coats of arms on its facade—the heraldic shields of each successive Captain, dated from 1444 to 1742. The first to reside here was Baldo Altoviti, a Florentine nobleman. The Captains' Loggia opposite was where justice was dispensed and notarial acts signed. The Marzocco column, bearing Florence's lion with the Republic's lily, recalls that Cutigliano was an outpost of Florentine power in the mountains.

The Chiesa della Madonna di Piazza (15th century), steps from the Palazzo, preserves a terracotta by Benedetto Buglioni representing the Virgin and Child with Saint Anthony Abbot and Saint Bernardino of Siena. San Bartolomeo church, whose campanile rises from an ancient Lombard watchtower, houses 14th-century works.

activities.text: Stroll through the stone lanes of Cutigliano (678m altitude), certified among Italy's most beautiful villages (Bandiera Arancione). The village preserves its intact medieval layout: green sandstone fountains, vaults, covered passages and hand-cut stone facades.

visits.text: In Gavinana, the Museo Ferrucciano honors Francesco Ferrucci, condottiere of the Florentine Republic killed here on August 3, 1530, in the final battle of the Florentine republican resistance against Charles V and the Medici. The house on the square—according to tradition, the very threshold where Ferrucci was finished off with dagger blows by Fabrizio Maramaldo as he lay mortally wounded and told him: "You kill a dead man"—was transformed into a museum in 1931. The enemy commander Filiberto di Chalon, Prince of Orange, also died that day beneath Gavinana's walls, struck by two arquebuses.

activities.text: The Ponte Sospeso delle Ferriere connects Mammiano Basso to Popiglio via a steel-cable footbridge inaugurated in June 1923: 227 meters long, 36 meters above the Lima torrent, only 80 centimeters wide. It was built on the idea of Vincenzo Douglas Scotti, director of the Mammiano Basso rolling mill, to allow workers from Popiglio to reach the factories in 3 minutes instead of a 6-kilometer detour. In 1990, the Guinness Book of Records certified it as the world's longest pedestrian suspension bridge—a title it held until 2006, when it was surpassed by Japan's Kokonoe Yume bridge.

visits.text: The Terme Tettuccio constitutes the most representative building of European Belle Époque thermalism: rebuilt in the 1920s by architect Ugo Giovannozzi, it combines a circular colonnade, an English-style garden with fountains, and four drinking galleries where the waters of the Tettuccio, Rinfresco, Regina and Leopoldina springs flow. The interior frescoes and mosaics are the work of Galileo Chini, Italy's leading Art Nouveau artist. Montecatini has been inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list since 2021 in the "Great Spas of Europe" category.

Montecatini Alto, accessible via the funicular inaugurated in 1898—the oldest still in service according to Wikipedia—is the ancient medieval village perched at 300 meters altitude above the spa town. The town walls were razed in 1554 by Cosimo I's Florentines during their conquest of the territory. The village preserves some cobbled lanes, the Rocca, and panoramic views across the entire Valdinievole. The 8-minute journey aboard the historic red carriages "Gigio" and "Gigia" is an experience in itself.

The Viale Verdi and its "Passi di Gloria": since 2011, over 200 bronze plaques embedded in the sidewalks between Piazza del Popolo and the Terme Tettuccio trace the visits of the most notable personalities—from Giuseppe Verdi to Puccini, from Ruggero Leoncavallo to Marie Curie, from Gary Cooper to Audrey Hepburn, all who came to take the waters. The early 20th-century Palazzo Municipale, designed by Raffaello Brizzi, borders this promenade.

activities.text: Thermal cure or wellness session at Terme Tettuccio or Terme Excelsior: Montecatini's four springs, which rise from depths of over 60 meters, produce sulfate-saline bicarbonate-sodium waters, traditionally used for digestive, hepatic and metabolic conditions. The thermal season runs from May to October.
The Teatro Verdi, built in 1950 in the thermal park, hosts the summer music season. Puccini, who stayed regularly in Montecatini, reportedly said it was one of his favorite places to compose.

map_keywords: Chiesa della Madonna della Salute Buggiano

visits.text: The Palazzo Pretorio of Buggiano Castello, a building from the first half of the 14th century, displays on its restored facade 57 coats of arms of the podestàs who governed the commune from the 14th to 17th centuries. It houses the municipal historical archive and 15th-century frescoes in its halls. It's one of the most complete examples of medieval civic architecture in the Valdinievole.

The church of Madonna della Salute e San Nicolao, founded as a Benedictine monastery in the 11th century, features an austere 12th-century Romanesque campanile. The three-nave interior preserves a 13th-century baptismal font, 16th-century paintings by G. Brina, and the remains of an adjoining cloister. The castle is documented from 1038 when the "da Buggiano" family founded the abbey there.

The Rocca and its clock tower, at the village's highest point, are the remnants of the defensive structure erected by the "da Buggiano" family who, in 1167, obtained from the Emperor a diploma confirming their possessions. The clock tower was completed with its mechanism in 1510.

Uzzano Castello, 3km from Buggiano, is a hilltop village perched on the hill dominating the Pescia valley. It's renowned for producing Bianco della Valdinievole, a DOC white wine from Trebbiano Toscano grapes. The medieval castle and its white limestone lanes can be visited in an hour.

activities.text: Walk through the citrus gardens of Buggiano Castello: the exceptionally mild microclimate of the village—sheltered from the north and open to the south—has favored since the Middle Ages the cultivation of orange, lemon and citron trees in private gardens, hence the nickname "Village of Citrus." Every two years, the village cultural association organizes "La campagna dentro le mura" (The countryside within the walls), opening these secret gardens to the public.

practical_info.period: Biennial spring event; gardens accessible from lanes even outside events

activities.text: Olive oil and wine tasting from the Buggiano hills: the terraced olive groves surrounding the village produce a recognized IGP olive oil. Several estates offer cellar visits and farm tastings.

visits.text: Pescia Cathedral is mentioned as early as 951 in the form "Sita loco Piscia." Elevated to episcopal status by Pope Benedict XIII in 1726, it preserves its sober baroque architecture. Pescia was declared a noble city by Cosimo III de' Medici in 1699 and received on that occasion the Porta Fiorentina, a commemorative triumphal arch with the peculiarity of not being connected to any walls—built solely to celebrate the elevation in status.

The Palazzo del Vicario and Piazza Grande form the heart of Pescia's civic center, structured in the 13th century in "fifths"—four quarters on the right bank of the Pescia river and one on the left bank—with a fortified bridge ensuring the connection. This double town center (religious cathedral pole / civic palazzo pole) is an urban organization preserved intact since the Middle Ages.

San Francesco church preserves a collection of 14th-15th century paintings including the Triptych by Bonaventura Berlinghieri (1235), one of the earliest representations of Saint Francis of Assisi painted during his lifetime or shortly after his death, commissioned by the Franciscans of Pescia.

activities.text: Pescia's flower market: the city is Italy's capital of cut flowers, primarily carnations. The tradition dates back to the 1920s-1930s when, after the decline of silk manufactures and paper mills, farmers converted their land to horticulture. The first covered reinforced concrete market, built in 1950-1951 on plans that won awards in Brazil in 1953, is now reopened as a multipurpose cultural space. The region exports millions of flowers and indoor plants throughout Europe.

practical_info.note: Wholesale market is active early morning; public market accessible all day

activities.text: Stroll along the lungofiume of the Pescia: the river flows through the city between the two medieval poles, beneath ancient bridges. The landscaped banks offer a pleasant walk between gardens and palace facades.

visits.text: The Historic Garden of Villa Garzoni is one of Europe's best-preserved Italian baroque gardens, classified as a National Monument in 1909 and 1912, and integrated in 2020 into the European Cultural Route of Historic Gardens. The Garzoni family, originally from Pescia, bought the castle in 1333; major garden works began in 1652 under Romano Garzoni. The steep hillside was transformed into a theatrical green amphitheater: terraces, monumental staircases, artificial grottoes, white mythological statues, water jets, a boxwood theater and labyrinth succeed each other in rigorous baroque staging. Napoleon Bonaparte, King Victor Emmanuel III and the American ambassador number among the villa's illustrious visitors. This is where Angiolina Orzali, Carlo Lorenzini's mother, worked and met her future husband.

The Pinocchio Monumental Park was inaugurated in 1953 for the 70th anniversary of the book's publication, thanks to fundraising among Italian schoolchildren initiated by Pescia mayor Rolando Anzillotti. The bronze sculptures of characters from the tale are signed by major artists including Emilio Greco and Pietro Consagra; architectural installations were entrusted to Marco Zanuso in the 1960s-1970s. The world's tallest wooden Pinocchio, 16 meters high, was inaugurated in 2009 by the Fondazione Collodi.

The Pieve di San Bartolomeo, at the highest point of the medieval village of Collodi (244m altitude), is mentioned from the 12th century. The steeply sloping village—stone houses clustered like a cascade from the pieve down to the Villa Garzoni gardens below—stood on the border between the Republic of Lucca and the Grand Duchy of Tuscany, who disputed it for centuries before Collodi became a Lucchese possession in 1442.

activities.text: Casa delle Farfalle (Butterfly House): a tropical greenhouse installed in the Garzoni garden, where hundreds of butterflies from Asia, Africa and South America fly freely in vegetation recreating their natural habitat. Included in the combined Pinocchio Park ticket.

practical_info.booking: Combined ticket Pinocchio Park + Garzoni Garden + Casa delle Farfalle available online

activities.text: Wander through Collodi's medieval village: lanes too narrow for cars, limestone staircases and stone arches lead through the traffic-free village from the gardens below to the pieve above. Carlo Lorenzini himself described the village as "a long streak of old houses blackened by time climbing up the back of a steep high hill."

seo.h1_title: Pistoia Province Circuit: Hill Towns, Thermal Spas & Mountains
seo.intro_paragraph: Pistoia and its five medieval pulpits open this journey, before climbing to Serravalle Pistoiese and its rival towers. The Pistoiese mountains reveal Cutigliano with its coat-of-arms-covered Palazzo, then San Marcello Piteglio and its vertiginous suspension bridge. The descent to Montecatini and its UNESCO Art Nouveau thermal baths leads to the hilltop villages of the Valdinievole—Buggiano, flower-filled Pescia—before the final stop at Collodi, the stone village clinging to its hillside that gave its pen name to Pinocchio's creator.
seo.seo_hook: Medieval pulpits, condottiere fortresses, Belle Époque UNESCO thermal spas, citrus villages and Pinocchio's baroque garden. Pistoia province holds all of Tuscany in a single circuit.
featured_list: Pistoia: Tuscany's capital of medieval pulpits
Montecatini Terme: UNESCO-listed Art Nouveau thermal spa town
Serravalle Pistoiese: medieval village of two rival towers
Collodi: birthplace of Pinocchio author's pen name

places_taglines.IT::pistoia: Where five medieval pulpits tell Tuscany's story in stone and silver
places_taglines.IT::serravalle-pistoiese: Twin towers standing sentinel over the ancient road from Pistoia to Lucca
places_taglines.IT::cutigliano: A mountain village whose palace wears ninety-five governors' coats of arms
places_taglines.IT::san-marcello-piteglio: Where a 1923 suspension bridge floats above the Lima gorge
places_taglines.IT::montecatini-terme: Art Nouveau temples of wellness crowned by a medieval village in the clouds
places_taglines.IT::buggiano-castello: Stone lanes perfumed by secret citrus gardens within medieval walls
places_taglines.IT::pescia: Where millions of carnations bloom and a river splits the medieval heart
places_taglines.IT::collodi: The stone cascade village that gave Pinocchio's creator his name

highlights: Pistoia: one of Tuscany's best-preserved medieval cities, off the mass tourism circuit
Montecatini Terme: UNESCO-listed Art Nouveau thermal baths, historic 1898 funicular and medieval village of Montecatini Alto
Pistoiese Mountains: Cutigliano and San Marcello Piteglio for Apennine landscapes, the Ponte Sospeso and stone villages
Collodi: Villa Garzoni's baroque garden and Pinocchio Park, ideal family destination

itineraries.title: From Lunigiana to Cinque Terre: Between Tuscany and Liguria
itineraries.subtitle: A 6-day journey through prehistoric statue-menhirs, white marble quarries, the Gulf of Poets, and colourful villages clinging to the cliffs of Cinque Terre.
itineraries.notes: Cinque Terre trails may close after heavy rain - check trail status on the National Park website. Trains are the most convenient way to travel between the five villages. The road to Portovenere is a dead-end often congested in summer - take the boat from La Spezia or arrive early.

visits.text: Pontremoli is Tuscany's northernmost town, nestled at the foot of the Apennines on the border with Liguria and Emilia-Romagna. This ancient stop on the Via Francigena - the medieval pilgrimage route between Canterbury and Rome - retains its historic centre crossed by the Magra river and its tributaries, spanned by medieval stone bridges. Start by climbing to the Castello del Piagnaro, whose name comes from the 'piagne', sandstone slabs used for roofing in Lunigiana. Built around the year 1000 to control the mountain passes, this castle dominates the village and houses the Museum of Lunigiana Statue-Menhirs, founded in 1975 by Professor Augusto Cesare Ambrosi.

The museum displays over 80 statue-menhirs, human figures carved from sandstone between the 4th and 1st millennium BC - one of Europe's most important collections of megalithic art. These abstract silhouettes, with their 'carabiniere hat' shaped heads and U-shaped faces, represent men armed with daggers and women adorned with jewellery. Their exact meaning remains partly enigmatic: protective deities? Ancestors? Territorial markers? The museum staging, renovated in 2015, plunges the statues into semi-darkness with lighting that reveals every detail - allow a good hour to explore thoroughly.

Descend into Pontremoli's centre to visit the Duomo, designed in the 17th century by Alessandro Capra from Cremona, with its broad dome and Latin cross plan. On the edge of town, stop at the church of the Santissima Annunziata, which preserves a small polychrome marble temple attributed to Jacopo Sansovino. Then wander through the medieval alleys, beneath the arcades, among the stone bridges and towers - Pontremoli has retained its authentic atmosphere as a pilgrims' staging post on the road to Rome.

activities.text: Spend your evening in Pontremoli savouring testaroli, Lunigiana's absolute speciality. These are thick pancakes made from flour and water, cooked between two scorching cast-iron plates (the 'testi'), then cut and reheated in boiling water like pasta, served with pesto, olive oil or pecorino cheese. It's one of Italy's most ancient dishes - some consider it the ancestor of all pasta.

practical_info.tip: Order testaroli al pesto at any trattoria in Pontremoli - it's the town's signature dish

activities.text: If you arrive early in the day and weather permits, push on to the Giaredo gorges just outside Pontremoli - a succession of canyons where the Gordana torrent has carved the rock for dozens of metres. The path follows the cliff walls and offers total escapism. Allow 1.5 hours return for the section accessible without equipment.

practical_info.tip: Hiking boots essential - the path is damp and rocky

visits.text: Before leaving Lunigiana, stop at Bagnone, on the route to Carrara (15 minutes' detour from Pontremoli). This small medieval village clings to a hillside above the torrent of the same name, which rushes down from the Apennines forming natural waterfalls. The castle, dominated by an imposing cylindrical keep, was built in the 14th century by the Malaspina - the powerful feudal family who controlled all of Lunigiana for centuries and sheltered Dante during his exile. The village divides into two parts: the upper section around the castle, and the lower, the old merchant quarter called 'Gutula', with its arcades and artisan workshops. Half an hour suffices to wander the alleys and admire the 14th-century medieval bridge over the torrent.

Arriving in Carrara, head up to the quarries of the Fantiscritti basin. Since Roman times (the port of Luna already exported marmor Lunensis under Julius Caesar), the Apuan Alps have been carved by quarries that supplied the raw material for some of the greatest works of Western sculpture. Michelangelo came personally to choose his blocks - it was Carrara marble that gave birth to the David (now in Florence) and the Pietà (St Peter's Basilica, Rome). Donatello, Bernini and Canova used it after him. The open-air museum at Fantiscritti, free of charge, traces the evolution of extraction techniques. The spectacle of white walls cut into giant tiers, like an amphitheatre carved into the mountain, is breathtaking.

Continue to the village of Colonnata, 8 km from Carrara, perched on the mountainside amid the quarries. This hamlet of 300 inhabitants is famous throughout Italy for its lardo - not simple lard, but an exceptional cured meat prepared since around the year 1000. The fat layer from the pig's back is seasoned with aromatic herbs and sea salt, then matured for 6 to 10 months in basins carved from marble, the 'conche'. In the past, this was the quarrymen's meal, which they slipped into their bread loaves with tomatoes to face the steep climbs and exhausting extraction work. Taste it sliced paper-thin on lightly warmed bread.

Descend into Carrara's historic centre to Piazza Alberica, lined with noble palaces with marble facades, then the Duomo (Cathedral of Sant'Andrea), built between the 11th and 14th centuries in local white marble, with its Gothic rose window. The town also preserves the Malaspina-Cybo Castle, transformed into a princely residence during the Renaissance.

activities.text: Tour operators offer guided 4x4 visits into the active quarries, including underground quarries - veritable cathedrals carved into the mountain. The Ravaccione gallery, accessible through the old marble railway tunnels (the 'Marmifera'), is where Michelangelo allegedly spent three years searching for the perfect block for the Pietà. Allow 2 to 3 hours for a complete visit with lardo tasting in Colonnata.

practical_info.tip: Closed shoes mandatory - marble dust is everywhere

visits.text: Today, dedicate the entire day to Portovenere and Palmaria island - take your time. Portovenere is UNESCO-listed alongside Cinque Terre. A practical tip: the access road is a dead-end often jammed in summer. Arrive early morning, or better still, take the boat from La Spezia (regular shuttles, 30 minutes). The village stretches along a row of medieval tower-houses with colourful facades, squeezed together facing the harbour. This 'palazzata' - continuous facade of fortified houses - served as a defensive wall on the sea side.

Climb to the church of San Pietro, perched on a rocky spur at the tip of the promontory - built in the 13th century in black and white marble in the Ligurian Gothic style, it overlooks the sea with Palmaria island as backdrop. The spot is extraordinary at sunset, but even in daylight the light on the rocks and sea is striking. Below, the Grotta Arpaia (known as 'Byron's Cave') is a natural cavity where the English poet Lord Byron came to meditate and swim - he swam across the gulf to Lerici, according to legend.

Climb up to the Castello Doria, a 12th-century Genoese fortress dominating the village from the hilltop. The view from the ramparts embraces the entire Gulf of Poets, Palmaria island and, on clear days, the Cinque Terre coast to the north. Descending, stroll along Via Capellini, the village's main street, lined with shops and trattorias.

activities.text: In the afternoon, take the boat from Portovenere harbour to Palmaria island (5-minute crossing). It's the largest of the Ligurian islands, UNESCO-listed. The island offers hiking trails through Mediterranean maquis, swimming coves accessible on foot, and the Grotta Azzurra (Blue Grotto), visitable by boat. The path circling the island takes about 2 hours - otherwise, just enjoy the cove on the Portovenere side for a swim. Last boat back around 6pm in summer.

practical_info.tip: Boats depart regularly from Portovenere harbour - buy the return ticket at the quayside booth

activities.text: Dine in Portovenere as evening falls, facing the harbour. Ligurian specialities are essential: focaccia, trofie with pesto, fritto misto of gulf fish, Monterosso anchovies. The waterfront terraces are magical when the lights from the tower-houses reflect in the harbour.

practical_info.tip: Book in high season - restaurants facing the harbour are small and highly sought-after

visits.text: Before reaching Cinque Terre, stop at Lerici in the morning - it's on the route, on the other side of the Gulf of Poets. This fishing village is dominated by an imposing 13th-century castle, built by the Pisans and enlarged by the Genoese, which today houses a palaeontology museum. This is where Percy Bysshe Shelley spent his final months before drowning in the gulf in 1822, aged 29. The small bay of San Terenzo, nearby, preserves Villa Magni where the poet resided. Stroll along the seafront and at the castle's foot - allow 45 minutes.

Park your car in La Spezia or Riomaggiore (car parks uphill, traffic is banned from the villages) and take the train. Riomaggiore is the first Cinque Terre village coming from the south: its pastel-coloured tower-houses tumble down a narrow gorge to a tiny harbour wedged between rocks. Climb to the upper part of the village for the church of San Giovanni Battista (1340) and the castle ruins, where the coastal view is spectacular. In the evening, descend to the harbour to see the fishing boats hauled onto the concrete ramp - the atmosphere is particularly beautiful in the golden light.

Take the train (5 minutes) to Manarola, perhaps the most photographed village in Cinque Terre. Its pink, yellow and orange houses stack up on a rocky spur above a natural harbour. The most famous viewpoint is reached by following the path towards Corniglia: from there, you embrace the entire village with the sea as backdrop. Manarola is also the heart of Sciacchetrà production, a rare sweet wine from grapes dried on the terraces - if you find some, taste it, production is tiny.

activities.text: Swim from the rocks at Riomaggiore or Manarola - there are no sandy beaches, but the water is crystal-clear and swimming spots are easily accessible. The best areas are the harbour ramps in both villages. Bring water shoes.

practical_info.tip: Buy the Cinque Terre Card at Riomaggiore station - unlimited trains between the 5 villages + trail access

activities.text: If the path is open, the Riomaggiore → Manarola section (the famous Via dell'Amore, often closed for works) is short and easy. Otherwise, take the train without regret - 5 minutes and you're there. In the evening, dine in Riomaggiore at one of the trattorias in the upper village, with views over the rooftops and sea.

practical_info.tip: Check the status of Via dell'Amore on the National Park website before relying on it

visits.text: Start the day with Corniglia, the only Cinque Terre village not at sea level - it's perched at 100 metres altitude, accessible via the Lardarina, a staircase of 382 steps from the station (or by shuttle bus, check timetables). Best to go in the morning, fresh and rested. Quieter than its neighbours, Corniglia is surrounded by vineyards and orchards. Via Fieschi, its main street, crosses the village end to end to a panoramic terrace overlooking the sea. It's the most 'authentic' of the five villages, with fewer tourists and more locals.

Train to Vernazza (7 minutes), often considered the jewel of Cinque Terre. It's the only village with a true natural harbour, bordered by a tiny sandy beach squeezed between houses. The medieval towers rising above the rooftops recall that Vernazza was a Genoese naval base in the 11th century. Climb to the Castello Doria (11th century), perched on the promontory - the circular view of the harbour, pastel houses and rocky coast is the iconic image of Cinque Terre. Descending, pass by the church of Santa Margherita d'Antiochia (1318), built on a rock above the sea.

Final village: Monterosso al Mare, the largest and most 'beach resort' of the five. It's divided into two parts: the old village (narrow streets, medieval Aurora tower, Capuchin convent housing a painting attributed to Van Dyck) and Fegina, the modern part, which has the only real sandy beach in Cinque Terre. On the promontory separating the two, look for the giant statue of Neptune (Il Gigante), 14 metres high, sculpted in reinforced concrete in 1910.

activities.text: The Vernazza-Monterosso path is the most spectacular section of the Sentiero Azzurro - about 2 hours' walking with climbs and descents through maquis, vertiginous panoramas over the sea and terraces. It's the most difficult section but also the most beautiful. Leave from Vernazza after your visit, you'll arrive at Monterosso in time for the beach.

practical_info.tip: Bring plenty of water and sunscreen - there's virtually no shade on the trail

activities.text: Enjoy Fegina beach in Monterosso - it's the only place in Cinque Terre where you can lay your towel on sand. Parts of the beach are free access (at the eastern end, near Neptune), others equipped with deckchairs. Late afternoon, take the train back to Riomaggiore - the journey takes just 15 minutes.

practical_info.tip: If you do the Vernazza-Monterosso hike, take the train for other sections - combining both gets tiring quickly

visits.text: Levanto is the Ligurian coast's well-kept secret - the northern gateway to Cinque Terre, but with a far more relaxed atmosphere. The town has a beautiful sandy beach (rare in the region), a medieval historic centre with frescoed noble houses, and the church of Sant'Andrea (13th century), built in white marble and green serpentine in the Ligurian Gothic style, with a carved rose window and portal. Stroll along Via Garibaldi and its arcades, then climb to the medieval castle for views over the bay.

From Levanto, the path to Monterosso via Punta Mesco is one of the most beautiful hikes on the Ligurian coast. The promontory of Punta Mesco, with the ruins of the hermitage of Sant'Antonio, offers panoramic views over the entire Cinque Terre coast on one side and the Gulf of Levanto on the other. The path (about 3 hours return) crosses Mediterranean maquis, with maritime pines and constant panoramas over the turquoise sea below.

activities.text: Spend the morning on Levanto beach - a beautiful sandy stretch with free-access sections and equipped zones. The waves are regular, making it a popular surf spot. The water is clear and the view of hills covered with terraced vineyards completes the picture.

practical_info.tip: Levanto is 4 minutes by train from Monterosso - handy for alternating beach and villages

activities.text: In the afternoon, grab a bike for the Levanto-Bonassola-Framura cycle path - a spectacular 5.5 km seaside route, through old railway tunnels transformed into a trail, alternating lit galleries and open views onto coves. Bike hire available in Levanto. The path is flat and accessible to all - ideal in late afternoon for the golden light.

practical_info.tip: The tunnels are cool even in summer - bring a light jacket if you feel the cold

seo.h1_title: From Lunigiana to Cinque Terre: Between Tuscany and Liguria
seo.intro_paragraph: This 6-day itinerary explores the lesser-known border between Tuscany and Liguria, from the mysterious prehistoric statue-menhirs of Pontremoli to the white marble quarries of Carrara, then the Gulf of Poets where Shelley and Byron found inspiration at Portovenere, before reaching the five colourful villages of Cinque Terre clinging to their cliffs above the Mediterranean.
seo.seo_hook: From Lunigiana's prehistoric statues to Michelangelo's marble, from the Gulf of Poets to Cinque Terre's colourful villages: 6 days between Tuscany and Liguria.
featured_list: Pontremoli: 80 prehistoric statue-menhirs in the Piagnaro castle, megalithic art from the 4th millennium BC
Carrara: white marble quarries exploited since antiquity, where Michelangelo selected his blocks
Portovenere: UNESCO-listed Genoese fortified village, facing Palmaria island and the Gulf of Poets
Cinque Terre: five colourful fishing villages linked by coastal paths above the Ligurian Sea

places_taglines.it::pontremoli: Where prehistoric statue-menhirs guard the medieval crossroads of the Via Francigena.
places_taglines.it::carrara: Where Michelangelo carved history from mountains of white marble.
places_taglines.it::portovenere: A Genoese fortress village where Byron swam and poets found their muse.
places_taglines.it::riomaggiore: Pastel tower-houses tumbling down a gorge to the turquoise embrace of the sea.
places_taglines.it::levanto: The Cinque Terre gateway that kept its sandy beach and laid-back soul.

highlights: The statue-menhirs of Pontremoli, 80 prehistoric sculptures from the 4th to 1st millennium BC in the Piagnaro castle
The marble quarries of Carrara, exploited since Roman antiquity, where Michelangelo chose his blocks for the David and Pietà
Portovenere and Palmaria island facing the Gulf of Poets, UNESCO World Heritage sites
The five colourful villages of Cinque Terre linked by spectacular coastal paths above the Mediterranean

itineraries.title: Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages
itineraries.subtitle: From Pisa to the towers of San Gimignano, from Chianti vineyards to the suspended villages of the Cinque Terre—a journey through medieval Tuscany, the Etruscan coast, and the Ligurian Riviera.
specialties: Sciacchetrà dessert wine from Cinque Terre

map_keywords: Piazzale delle Baleari Marina di Pisa

visits.text: Bocca d'Arno - Where the Arno River meets the Tyrrhenian Sea, this engineered river mouth owes its existence to Ferdinand I de' Medici, who ordered it relocated roughly 1,500 meters north in 1606 to protect Pisa from flooding. Along the riverbank, traditional fishing nets called 'retoni' hang like portals above the water—relics of an activity that has shaped local life for centuries. On clear days, Corsica, Elba, and the island of Gorgona materialize on the horizon.

Piazzale delle Baleari and the Art Nouveau Waterfront - Marina di Pisa emerged as a seaside resort in the late 19th century when Gaetano Ceccherini commissioned the first wave of villas here after 1869. The seafront is lined with some of the finest Liberty-style buildings on the Tuscan coast, including Villa Bondi, built in 1909 on Piazza Baleari for Angelo Bondi, a Florentine terracotta industrialist. Poet Gabriele D'Annunzio stayed here and drew inspiration from the nearby pine forest to compose 'La pioggia nel pineto' (Rain in the Pine Grove), one of the most celebrated Italian poems of the 20th century.

Piazza dei Miracoli - The Leaning Tower of Pisa, begun on August 9, 1173 and completed in 1372 after two centuries of construction, stands 56 meters tall on its lowest side and weighs approximately 14,500 tons. It began tilting during construction of the third floor around 1178 because the soft alluvial soil couldn't support its weight. Stabilization work between 1990 and 2001 reduced the tilt by 44 centimeters, bringing it to roughly 4 degrees. The Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta rises to 77 meters and towers over the campanile. The entire Piazza dei Miracoli has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987.

Baptistery of San Giovanni in Pisa - Started in 1152 by architect Diotisalvi, who also designed the tower, the Baptistery of Pisa is Italy's largest at 54 meters high and 107 meters in circumference. Its construction spanned two centuries, which explains the coexistence of Romanesque and Gothic styles in its galleries. Its acoustic properties are remarkable: the double-shell dome creates a reverberation lasting several seconds, allowing a single voice to form a complete chord.

Lungarno of Pisa - The ochre and terracotta facades lining the Arno in Pisa form one of the most photographed urban panoramas in Tuscany. Legend has it that Galileo, born in Pisa in 1564, observed the oscillations of a chandelier in the nearby cathedral—an experience traditionally presented as the starting point of his pendulum research.

Orto Botanico di Pisa - Founded in 1544 by Cosimo I de' Medici on the proposal of botanist Luca Ghini, the Botanical Garden of Pisa is one of the world's oldest scientific gardens still in operation. It covers about 3 hectares in the historic center and houses a collection of over 3,000 plant species, including a magnolia planted in the 18th century that stands as one of Europe's oldest specimens.

activities.text: Bike ride Marina di Pisa → Pisa via the Ciclopista del Trammino - This 13 km bike path follows the Arno along the former route of the steam tram inaugurated in 1892, which connected Pisa to the sea and ceased operation in 1932. The route passes through the pine forest of San Rossore Regional Park before reaching downtown Pisa.

visits.text: Lucca - morning stop en route to Florence (Lucca sits on the A11 highway, halfway between Pisa and Florence). City Walls - Passeggiata sulle Mura: Lucca's ramparts form a 4.2-kilometer Renaissance enclosure built between 1504 and 1645. Unlike most Italian fortifications, they were never breached and remain virtually intact. Their flat crown, 30 meters wide and shaded by centuries-old plane trees, was converted into a public promenade in the 19th century. Eleven star-shaped bastions punctuate the circuit, designed to withstand artillery using the military engineering techniques of the era.

Torre Guinigi - Built in the second half of the 14th century by Paolo Guinigi, Lord of Lucca from 1400 to 1430, the Guinigi Tower rises 45 meters in red Romano-Gothic brick. Its absolute singularity is the hanging garden at its summit: seven holm oaks (Quercus ilex), symbols of rebirth in the Guinigi family tradition, grow in a soil-filled chamber 45 meters above ground. In the early 15th century, Lucca counted nearly 250 towers; the Guinigi is one of the few still standing. Local legend claims that the tallest tree was planted by Paolo Guinigi himself, and that it lost all its leaves when the lord was imprisoned by Francesco Sforza. The 230 steps lead to a panorama over the entire medieval center.

Torre delle Ore - Lucca's medieval public clock tower dates from the 14th century and was one of Tuscany's first mechanical public clocks. The climb is optional but offers a different view over the rooftops and the nearby Torre Guinigi.

San Michele in Foro - Built on the site of the Roman forum, the church of San Michele in Foro is one of the finest achievements of Pisan Romanesque architecture in Lucca. Its four-tiered facade, adorned with twisted columns, marble lozenges, and polychrome inlays, was erected between the 11th and 14th centuries. The facade is deliberately higher than the nave to impress merchants in the square.

Piazza dell'Anfiteatro - This elliptical square, unique in the world, is built on the foundations of a 1st-century AD Roman amphitheater that could hold up to 10,000 spectators. The tiers were gradually integrated into medieval houses, whose facades still follow the exact curve of the ancient arena. Four vaulted passages, heirs to the Roman entrances, are the only access points to the enclosed square.

Cathedral of San Martino - The Cathedral of Lucca preserves one of the most venerated relics of the Middle Ages: the Volto Santo, a cedar wood crucifix representing the face of Christ, which tradition attributes to Nicodemus, a disciple of Jesus. The tomb of Ilaria del Carretto (1406), a masterpiece by Jacopo della Quercia, is one of the most moving examples of Italian Gothic sculpture.

activities.text: Bike ride on Lucca's ramparts - The complete circuit of the ramparts is 4.2 km and takes 30 to 45 minutes by bike. Rental shops are located at the main city gates.

visits.text: Ponte Vecchio and Lungarno - The Ponte Vecchio owes its existence to an earlier Roman bridge built around 120 AD on the Via Cassia. Destroyed by a flood in 1333, it was rebuilt in stone in 1345 by Taddeo Gaddi and Neri di Fioravante with three arches, the largest measuring 30 meters. In 1593, Ferdinand I de' Medici, bothered by the smells of butchers and fishmongers when crossing the Vasari Corridor above, ordered that only jewelers and goldsmiths could keep shops there—a regulation still in effect today. The Vasari Corridor, built in 1565, runs above the bridge for nearly a kilometer.

Piazza del Duomo - Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore, Brunelleschi's Dome, Giotto's Campanile - The Cathedral of Florence, whose construction began in 1296 under Arnolfo di Cambio, is crowned by Brunelleschi's dome completed in 1436: with its 45-meter diameter and 91-meter height, it remains the world's largest brick dome. Brunelleschi had to invent new construction techniques because no contemporary knew how to close such a cupola. Giotto's Campanile, begun in 1334, reaches 84 meters and offers from its 414 steps a direct view of the dome. The Baptistery of San Giovanni, whose 'Gates of Paradise' were sculpted by Ghiberti between 1425 and 1452, is one of the city's oldest buildings.

Mercato Centrale (San Lorenzo) - The halls of the Mercato Centrale, built in iron and glass between 1870 and 1874 by architect Giuseppe Mengoni (also architect of Milan's Galleria Vittorio Emanuele), constitute one of Tuscany's liveliest covered food markets.

Piazza della Signoria - Loggia dei Lanzi - The Palazzo Vecchio, whose construction began in 1299 under Arnolfo di Cambio, was the seat of the Florentine Republic and later of the Grand Duchy of Tuscany. Its 94-meter tower is offset from the facade because it incorporates a pre-existing medieval tower. In the square, a circular plaque marks the spot where Savonarola was hanged and burned in 1498. The Loggia dei Lanzi houses Benvenuto Cellini's Perseus (1554) and Giambologna's Rape of the Sabine Women (1583).

Palazzo Pitti and Boboli Gardens - The Palazzo Pitti, whose construction began in 1458 for banker Luca Pitti on a design attributed to Brunelleschi, was acquired by the Medici in 1560. It's the largest palace in Florence with a 205-meter facade. The Boboli Gardens, laid out from 1549, constitute one of the first examples of Italian-style gardens with their geometric paths, fountains, and statues.

Oltrarno Quarter - The neighborhood situated beyond the Arno retains a more authentic atmosphere. The Basilica of Santo Spirito, designed by Brunelleschi in 1436, and the alleys around Piazza Santo Spirito concentrate artisan workshops, antique dealers, and trattorias frequented by Florentines.

activities.text: Walking loop through Florence's historic center (Duomo → Signoria → Ponte Vecchio → Oltrarno) - The entire historic center of Florence has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1982. The loop covers approximately 3 km.

map_keywords: Via delle Volte Castellina in Chianti
Palazzo del Podestà Radda in Chianti
Castello di Brolio Gaiole in Chianti

visits.text: Greve in Chianti - Piazza Matteotti - Considered the gateway to Chianti from Florence, Greve has been inhabited since Etruscan times. Its triangular square, bordered by asymmetrical arcades that testify to different owners over the centuries, served as the central market for the entire region since the Middle Ages. The Castello di Verrazzano, visible in the surroundings, is linked to the memory of Giovanni da Verrazzano, navigator born here around 1485 who explored the east coast of North America in 1524 and discovered the bay now occupied by New York.

Castellina in Chianti - Via delle Volte - Perched on its hill at the historic border between Florence and Siena, Castellina possesses a 15th-century medieval fortress. Its Via delle Volte, a vaulted medieval corridor carved into the ramparts for about 150 meters, served as a defensive passage and interior route. The archaeological museum of Chianti preserves Etruscan objects from the 7th century BC found in the tumulus of Montecalvario.

Radda in Chianti - Former capital of the Chianti League - Radda in Chianti was the capital of the Chianti League, a military organization founded in the 12th century to defend the region against incursions from Siena. The League's emblem, the Gallo Nero (black rooster), is today the official label of Chianti Classico DOCG. The Palazzo del Podestà, whose facade is covered with coats of arms of successive governors, dominates the main square.

Gaiole in Chianti - Birthplace of Chianti Classico - For centuries, Gaiole was the supply market for the surrounding castles. The Castello di Brolio, property of the Ricasoli family since the 12th century, is Italy's oldest winery still in operation. It was Bettino Ricasoli, future Prime Minister of Italy, who codified the recipe for Chianti Classico in 1872.

activities.text: Panoramic drive on SR222 Chiantigiana - The Strada Chiantigiana (SR222) connects Florence to Siena over 69 kilometers through the Chianti hills. Winding between Sangiovese vineyards, cypress trees, and sleeping castles, it traverses a territory shaped by the centuries-old rivalry between Florence and Siena.

Chianti Classico DOCG tasting at a winery - The cellars around Gaiole (Castello di Brolio, Castello di Ama, Badia a Coltibuono) offer tours and tastings. Chianti Classico is a DOCG requiring a minimum of 80% Sangiovese.

visits.text: Piazza del Campo - Torre del Mangia - Palazzo Pubblico - The Piazza del Campo, shaped like a scallop shell sloping toward the Palazzo Pubblico, was laid out from 1293 under the government of the Nine Lords. Its red brick floor laid in a herringbone pattern is divided into nine sectors by white stone ribs. The Torre del Mangia, built between 1325 and 1348, measures 87 meters (102 with the lightning rod) in cotto (red Tuscan brick) except for the travertine crowning. Its name comes from the nickname of its first bell-ringer, Giovanni di Duccio, called 'Mangiaguadagni' because he immediately spent what he earned. The Cappella di Piazza was erected in 1352 by Sienese survivors of the Black Death. The Palio is run twice a year (July 2 and August 16) on this square covered with earth.

Duomo di Siena - Built between 1136 and 1382, the Cathedral of Our Lady of the Assumption of Siena is characterized by alternating bands of white and dark green marble. Its floor entirely covered with figurative marble marquetry—56 panels created between the 14th and 16th centuries—is one of the most ambitious achievements of the Renaissance. The Piccolomini Library (1492-1502) is accessible from the nave. In 1339, Siena had decided to enlarge its cathedral to make it the largest in the Christian world: the project was abandoned in 1348 after the Black Death.

Baptistery of San Giovanni in Siena - Built between 1316 and 1325 to support the enlarged choir of the cathedral above, the Baptistery of Siena houses the hexagonal baptismal font, the most important of the early Italian Renaissance. Its gilt bronze decoration (1417-1431) was created by Donatello, Lorenzo Ghiberti, and Jacopo della Quercia.

Basilica of San Domenico and panorama over Siena - Erected between 1226 and 1465, the Basilica of San Domenico preserves the relics of Saint Catherine of Siena (1347-1380), patron saint of Italy and Doctor of the Church. Her mummified head is displayed in a side chapel. Set on a promontory, the bare brick facade offers one of the most striking views of the Duomo and medieval rooftops.

activities.text: Wandering through Siena's contrade - Siena is divided into 17 contrade (historic neighborhoods), each with its own animal emblem, church, museum, and colors. The oratories and fountains of the contrade, the emblems painted on walls, and the flags testify to an identity transmitted from generation to generation.

visits.text: Siena - Palazzo Pubblico and Museo Civico - The Palazzo Pubblico, built between 1288 and 1342, is one of Italy's finest Gothic communal palaces. Its Museo Civico preserves Ambrogio Lorenzetti's Allegory of Good and Bad Government (1338-1339), considered the first secular representation of a city and countryside in Western painting. The Sala del Mappamondo houses Simone Martini's Maestà (1315).

Monteriggioni - Ramparts, 14 towers, and Piazza Roma - Monteriggioni was founded between 1213 and 1219 by the Republic of Siena to watch over the Florentine border. Its ramparts of approximately 570 meters perimeter, pierced by two gates, are flanked by 14 square towers still standing. The walls reach 10 meters high. Dante Alighieri evoked these towers in the Inferno (canto XXXI) to describe the giants guarding the entrance to the last circle. In 1554, Captain Giovanni Zeti surrendered the fortress to the Florentines, sealing the end of the Republic of Siena. The village counts barely 50 houses within the ramparts.

Monteriggioni in Arme - This museum offers a collection of reproductions of medieval and Renaissance armor and weapons, providing insight into siege and defense techniques. The ticket includes access to both sections of the walkway.

activities.text: Walk on Monteriggioni's ramparts - The complete circuit of the walkway is about 500 meters and offers a panoramic view over the Tuscan countryside. Monteriggioni is also a major stop on the Via Francigena, the pilgrimage route connecting Canterbury to Rome.

map_keywords: Collegiata di Santa Maria Assunta San Gimignano

visits.text: San Gimignano - Piazza della Cisterna and Piazza del Duomo - In the 14th century, San Gimignano counted about 72 tower-houses erected by noble families who competed in height as symbols of wealth. Fourteen remain today, including the Torre Grossa (54 m), built in 1311. The historic center has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1990. The Piazza della Cisterna takes its name from the octagonal public cistern of 1287 that still occupies its center. Dante Alighieri stopped in this town on May 18, 1300 to plead in favor of the Tuscan Guelph league.

San Gimignano - Collegiata Santa Maria Assunta - The collegiate church, a 12th-century Romanesque building, houses fresco cycles from the 14th and 15th centuries, including the cycle of Saint Fina by Domenico Ghirlandaio (1477-1478), considered among the most graceful frescoes of the Tuscan Renaissance.

Volterra - Piazza dei Priori and Palazzo dei Priori - Perched at 550 meters altitude, Volterra was founded by the Etruscans under the name Velathri in the 7th century BC. The Palazzo dei Priori, begun in 1208 and completed in 1257, is the oldest town hall in Tuscany—it's believed to have served as a model for Florence's Palazzo Vecchio.

Porta all'Arco - An Etruscan gate from the 4th-3rd century BC, the Porta all'Arco is one of the best preserved in Italy. Three heads sculpted in basalt, probably protective deities of the city, adorn its arch. During World War II, residents formed a human chain in front of retreating German soldiers to prevent its destruction.

Roman Theater of Volterra - Built in the late 1st century BC and rediscovered during excavations in the 1950s, this Roman theater is one of the best preserved in central Italy. The adjacent 2nd-century AD Roman baths complete the complex.

Museo Etrusco Guarnacci - Founded in 1761 by Mario Guarnacci, who donated his private collection to the city, this museum is one of Europe's oldest public museums. It preserves over 600 Etruscan funerary urns. The most famous piece is the Ombra della Sera (Shadow of the Evening), a bronze votive statuette with extreme elongation close to Giacometti sculptures, as well as the Urna degli Sposi representing an elderly married couple.

activities.text: Scenic drive Monteriggioni → San Gimignano → Volterra - This route crosses the hills between the Val d'Elsa and the heights of Volterra. San Gimignano stands out on the horizon from several kilometers away, its medieval towers emerging from the Tuscan scrubland.

map_keywords: Castello di Castagneto Carducci Castagneto Carducci

visits.text: Castagneto Carducci - Medieval village - Perched on a hill overlooking the Etruscan Coast, Castagneto Carducci bears the name of poet Giosuè Carducci (1835-1907), Italy's first Nobel Prize winner in Literature in 1906, who spent his childhood here from 1838 to 1848. The medieval village preserves its ramparts and stone alleyways. The municipality is surrounded by vineyards producing Bolgheri and Bolgheri Sassicaia DOC wines.

Bolgheri - Viale dei Cipressi and medieval borgo - The avenue of cypress trees leading to Bolgheri from San Guido, planted in 1831, was immortalized by Giosuè Carducci in his poem 'Davanti San Guido' (1874). The cypresses, now centuries old, form a vegetal corridor nearly 5 kilometers long. Inside the medieval village, small shops sell Bolgheri DOC wines including Sassicaia, created in 1948 and which became the first Super Tuscan classified in an individual appellation in 1994.

Vada and beaches of the Etruscan Coast - Vada was the ancient port of Volterra from the 4th century BC and was mentioned in Cicero's writings. Amphorae discovered on the seabed testify to the intense commercial activity of this port. The beach of Vada, stretching 5 kilometers of fine sand, has regularly received the Blue Flag certification.

activities.text: Walk on the Viale dei Cipressi of Bolgheri - The 4.7 km cypress avenue between San Guido and Bolgheri can be walked or cycled in both directions. The road is lightly trafficked and shaded.
Swimming at Vada - The beaches of Vada offer accessible swimming areas. The northern zones (Secche di Vada) are rockier and appreciated by divers; the southern zones are sandy.

visits.text: Populonia - Medieval village and Etruscan acropolis - Populonia is the only one of the twelve great Etruscan cities built directly on the sea, on the Piombino promontory overlooking the Gulf of Baratti. Founded around the 6th century BC, it became the wealthiest mining city in the ancient Mediterranean thanks to iron processing extracted from Elba Island. The medieval village built on the Etruscan acropolis offers a 180° view over the gulf and the islands of the Tuscan Archipelago.

Archaeological Park of Baratti and Populonia - This 80-hectare park preserves Etruscan and Roman remains, accessible via developed trails. The necropolis of San Cerbone is the only Etruscan necropolis built by the sea. The 7th-century BC Tumulo dei Carri with its bronze chariot is particularly well preserved. Iron slag accumulated during centuries of metallurgical activity still forms black dunes on the beach.

Gulf of Baratti - The beach of the Gulf of Baratti, nestled between wooded hills and the sea, is one of the most preserved beach sites on the Etruscan coast. Submerged remains from the Roman era have been discovered offshore.

activities.text: Hiking in the archaeological park of Baratti and Populonia - The main trail (necropolis circuit) covers about 3 km through the necropolis, ancient quarries, and Mediterranean forests of the promontory.
Swimming in the Gulf of Baratti - Fine sand and crystalline water protected by the promontory. Free access from the park parking lot.

visits.text: Castiglioncello - Baia del Quercetano and Castello Pasquini - Originally an Etruscan village (4th-3rd century BC), Castiglioncello owes its modern fame to patron Diego Martelli, who established his residence here (Castello Pasquini, 1889) and hosted the Macchiaioli painters (Giovanni Fattori, Odoardo Borrani, Telemaco Signorini), Italian precursors of French Impressionism. These artists depicted the coves, pine forest, and red cliffs of Castiglioncello in hundreds of paintings.
Coastal path Punta Righini and coves - The coastal path follows the characteristic red limestone cliffs, overlooking turquoise, shallow coves. Some coves are accessible only on foot or by swimming.

Archaeological Museum of Castiglioncello - Installed in the Palazzo Bombardieri of Rosignano Marittimo since 1996, this museum preserves collections from a necropolis of over 350 Etruscan and Roman tombs discovered in Castiglioncello, testifying to connections with the city of Volterra in the 3rd-2nd centuries BC.

activities.text: Swimming in the coves of Castiglioncello - The coves between Punta Righini and Porticciolo offer clear waters appreciated for snorkeling. Baia del Quercetano and Cala delle Morelle are accessible on foot from the village center.
Coastal hike Castiglioncello - The panoramic trail along the cliffs from Punta Righini offers views of the islands of the Tuscan Archipelago. About 5 km round trip.

visits.text: Technical Naval Museum of the Italian Navy - Considered one of the world's oldest naval museums, the Museo Tecnico Navale is housed in the military naval arsenal built in 1869. Across more than 3,000 square meters, it preserves ship models, navigation instruments, 28 figureheads (the oldest dating to 1738), and a section dedicated to Guglielmo Marconi, who conducted his first long-distance wireless communication experiments in the Gulf of La Spezia in 1897.

Castello San Giorgio and Archaeological Museum - Documented from the second half of the 14th century, the Castello San Giorgio houses the Civic Archaeological Museum with collections from Lunigiana from Prehistory to the Middle Ages, notably the famous anthropomorphic statue-menhirs from the Copper and Bronze Ages. The ramparts offer a panorama over the Gulf of La Spezia.

Via del Prione and historic center - The main pedestrian artery of La Spezia's historic center is lined with arcades and 19th-century palaces. The Museo Civico Amedeo Lia, installed in a 17th-century Franciscan convent, preserves a collection spanning the 13th to 18th centuries including works by Tintoretto and Titian.

Harbor promenade and Passeggiata Morin - From the Molo Italia, a panorama extends over the Cinque Terre. The Gulf of La Spezia, nicknamed the 'Gulf of Poets' since Dante, Shelley, and Byron stayed here, is one of the largest protected natural gulfs on the Ligurian coast.

activities.text: Logistics reconnaissance for the Cinque Terre - La Spezia Centrale station is the main departure point for the Cinque Terre. The regional train serves all five villages in a maximum of 20 minutes. The Cinque Terre Card (train + trail access) is available at the station.

visits.text: Riomaggiore - Marina and stairway alleys - The southernmost village of the Cinque Terre was reportedly founded in the 8th century by Greek refugees. Its five- to six-story houses, painted in ochre, yellow, and terracotta, are built directly on rock—an ancient technique that allowed sailors to recognize their home from the sea. The Via dell'Amore, a ledge carved into the cliff during the construction of railway tunnels in the 1920s, is partially reopened.

Manarola - Belvedere and postcard view - Manarola is renowned for its production of Sciacchetrà, a sweet, amber-colored white wine obtained by drying the grapes. Its belvedere on the rocky promontory is the most famous photographic viewpoint of the Cinque Terre. The church of San Lorenzo, built in 1338, preserves a medieval rose window and bell. The giant Christmas nativity scene installed on the cliffs every year since 1961 is visible from several kilometers at sea.

Corniglia - Village perched 100 meters above the sea - Corniglia is the only one of the five villages without direct access to the sea. Perched at 100 meters altitude, it is preceded by the Lardarina, a staircase of 382 steps from the station. The 14th-century church of San Pietro is adorned with a rose window in white Carrara marble. Less crowded than its neighbors, Corniglia offers the calmest atmosphere of the Cinque Terre.

activities.text: Cinque Terre Card train between villages (south) - The regional train connects La Spezia to Riomaggiore, Manarola, and Corniglia in less than 15 minutes. The Cinque Terre Card allows unlimited boarding.

practical_info.booking: Cinque Terre Card available at the station

activities.text: Hike Manarola → Corniglia (trail 586/506) - The route takes the heights from Manarola, crossing terraced vineyards, holm oak forest, and a pass at 360 meters before descending to Corniglia. Allow 2h30-3h.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate to difficult, significant elevation gain

visits.text: Vernazza - Port, Piazza Marconi, and Castello Doria - Vernazza is regularly cited among Italy's most beautiful villages. Its natural port, protected by a medieval breakwater, is the only true mooring harbor of the Cinque Terre. The Castello Doria, built in the 11th century on the rocky ledge, is one of the oldest fortifications of the Cinque Terre and offers the most iconic view of the village and harbor.

Monterosso al Mare - Medieval borgo and Fegina beach - Monterosso is the largest of the five villages, divided into two parts: the medieval borgo (Colle di San Cristoforo) and the beach resort of Fegina, which has the only true sandy beach of the Cinque Terre. The statue called Il Gigante, a 14-meter reinforced concrete sculpture representing Neptune, was erected in 1910. The 14th-century church of San Giovanni Battista in Ligurian Gothic style striped with white and green marble is the largest in the Cinque Terre. The hinterland of Monterosso produces the Monterosso lemon, a local variety used for limoncino.

activities.text: Hike Sentiero Azzurro Monterosso → Vernazza - This 3.6 km section is one of the most spectacular and demanding of the Sentiero Azzurro. The trail climbs through terraces of lemon and olive trees, skirts cliffs above the drop, crosses the hamlet of Prevo, then descends toward Vernazza by a steep staircase. Total elevation gain of about 400 meters. Allow 1h30-2h.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate, some steep passages, Cinque Terre Card required

activities.text: Cinque Terre Card train between villages (north) - The train connects Vernazza and Monterosso to La Spezia in 20 minutes.

visits.text: Santa Margherita Ligure - Port and waterfront - Santa Margherita Ligure developed its tourist vocation from the 19th century when aristocrats from across Europe discovered its Gulf of Tigullio. The town was long known as Pescino before being renamed in 1863. Its marina, Belle Époque villas, and tree-lined promenade form the backdrop of a resort that maintains its elegance without Portofino's ostentation. The town is included in the Portofino Regional Natural Park, created in 1935.

Portofino - Piazzetta and harbor - Portofino, founded in the 10th century as a simple fishing port, became in the 19th century one of the most sought-after destinations on the Italian Riviera. Its Piazzetta, surrounded by pastel houses overlooking the horseshoe-shaped harbor, concentrates cafés, boutiques, and prestige yachts. Lord Byron swam in the bay. The Castello Brown, a medieval fortress renovated in the 16th century and purchased by British consul Montague Yeats Brown in the 19th century, offers the most complete panorama over the village.

Paraggi - Protected bay and beach - Nestled between Santa Margherita and Portofino, the bay of Paraggi is one of the rare sandy beaches on this Ligurian coast. Its clear, shallow waters, protected by the Portofino promontory, make it one of the most frequented swimming spots on the Riviera.

activities.text: Coastal trail Santa Margherita → Portofino via Paraggi - This 5.3 km trail follows the coast overlooking the sea, passing through the bay of Paraggi before descending to Portofino. Elevation gain of only 47 meters: one of the most accessible coastal routes on the Riviera. Local boats provide the return service.

map_keywords: Abbazia di San Fruttuoso San Fruttuoso

visits.text: Camogli - Port and waterfront - Camogli is one of the most preserved fishing villages on the Ligurian Riviera. Its name reportedly comes from 'Casa dei Mogli' (house of wives). Its five- to six-story houses, painted in bright colors to be visible to sailors, overlook a lively harbor. The Basilica of Santa Maria Assunta, built directly at the water's edge on rocks, was rebuilt several times after storms. Camogli is known for the Sagra del Pesce, fish festival celebrated on the second Sunday of May since 1952, when thousands of sardines are fried in a giant 4-meter diameter pan.

Abbey of San Fruttuoso - Accessible only by boat or on foot, the abbey occupies a secret cove between Camogli and Portofino. Its foundation dates to the 7th century, when Greek monks fleeing the Muslim conquest of Hispania brought the relics of Saint Fructuosus of Tarragona. The current structure dates largely from the 11th century, built at the request of Adelaide of Burgundy, widow of Emperor Otto I. In the 13th century, the Doria family added the Gothic cloister and family tombs in black and white marble. A defensive tower was erected in 1562 to repel pirates. In 1954, the Christ of the Abyss statue was submerged at 17 meters depth—a 2.5-meter bronze created by Guido Galletti in memory of diver Dario Gonzatti who died here in 1947. The FAI has managed the site since 1983.

activities.text: Boat crossing Camogli → San Fruttuoso - Golfo Paradiso company boats connect Camogli to San Fruttuoso in about 30 minutes. The crossing follows the Portofino promontory.

Hike San Rocco di Camogli → San Fruttuoso - The trail descends through the holm oak forest of Portofino Natural Park to the bay of San Fruttuoso. The descent takes about 1h30 but the climb back is demanding—prefer the boat for the return.

practical_info.difficulty: Moderate to difficult on the ascent

seo.h1_title: Tuscany & Cinque Terre Road Trip: Renaissance Art, Chianti Hills & Coastal Villages
seo.intro_paragraph: From the Leaning Tower of Pisa to the Art Nouveau ramparts of Marina di Pisa, from the wine-growing hills of Chianti to the medieval alleys of Siena, this 15-day road trip crosses Tuscany in all its depth before pivoting to Liguria. Etruscan Volterra, San Gimignano and its towers, the wild Etruscan coast between Vada and Populonia, then the five suspended villages of the Cinque Terre and the Riviera from Portofino to Camogli.
seo.seo_hook: Where vineyard-covered hills cascade toward turquoise seas, medieval towers pierce the clouds, and villages cling to cliffs above the Mediterranean—Tuscany sings of heritage, the Cinque Terre whisper coastal poetry.
featured_list: Florence: Renaissance galleries and Brunelleschi's dome
Chianti: medieval villages and Classico DOCG vineyards
Siena: Palio, Torre del Mangia, and Gothic Duomo
Volterra: perched Etruscan city and Guarnacci Museum
Cinque Terre: five UNESCO villages clinging to the cliffs
Portofino and Camogli: Ligurian Riviera and San Fruttuoso Abbey

places_taglines.IT::marina-di-pisa: Where the Arno meets the sea beneath 19th-century Liberty villas
places_taglines.IT::firenze: Cradle of the Renaissance, birthplace of Brunelleschi's dome and Medici power
places_taglines.IT::siena: Gothic medieval city of the shell-shaped Piazza del Campo and the legendary Palio
places_taglines.IT::gaiole-in-chianti: Heart of Chianti Classico country, home of Castello di Brolio and the Sangiovese vine
places_taglines.IT::monteriggioni: A crown of 14 towers guarding walls that Dante immortalized in the Inferno
places_taglines.IT::volterra: Etruscan citadel perched at 550 meters, alabaster capital since ancient times
places_taglines.IT::vada: Where golden sands meet Etruscan heritage along Tuscany's western shore
places_taglines.IT::la-spezia: The Gulf of Poets, maritime gateway to the timeless Cinque Terre
places_taglines.IT::santa-margherita-ligure: Belle Époque elegance on the Riviera, where palms meet pastel harbors and Portofino beckons

highlights: Piazza dei Miracoli in Pisa and Leaning Tower (1173-1372)
Renaissance ramparts of Lucca and Torre Guinigi with its hanging garden
Brunelleschi's Dome and Ponte Vecchio in Florence
Chiantigiana SR222 route through Chianti Classico vineyards
Piazza del Campo and Torre del Mangia in Siena - home of the Palio
Monteriggioni: 13th-century fortified village cited by Dante
Etruscan Volterra: Porta all'Arco (4th century BC) and Guarnacci Museum
San Gimignano and its 14 UNESCO-listed medieval towers
Populonia and Baratti Archaeological Park (Etruscan necropolis)
Castiglioncello: coves and legacy of the Macchiaioli painters
Cinque Terre: 5 UNESCO villages, Sentiero Azzurro, panoramic train
Portofino, Santa Margherita, and San Fruttuoso Abbey by boat

itineraries.title: Umbria Itinerary: Medieval Villages & Rolling Hills
itineraries.subtitle: From the tuff city of Orvieto to the shores of Lake Trasimeno, ten stages through Umbria's most unspoiled medieval hamlets.
specialties: Orvieto Classico DOC - dry white wine aged in tuff cellars since Etruscan times
Lenticchie di Castelluccio IGP - tiny lentils grown at 1,500m altitude with paper-thin skins
Sagrantino di Montefalco DOCG - one of Italy's most tannic and powerful red wines
Black truffles from Norcia and the Valnerina
Porchetta - slow-roasted pork seasoned with wild fennel and garlic

map_keywords: Necropoli del Crocifisso del Tufo Orvieto

visits.text: Duomo di Orvieto - Pope Nicholas IV laid the first stone of this Gothic cathedral on November 13, 1290, to house the corporal stained with blood from the miracle of Bolsena (1263). Its construction spanned three centuries, involving Fra Bevignate, Lorenzo Maitani (who arrived in 1308), Andrea Pisano, and Andrea Orcagna. The triptych façade—one of Italy's most spectacular—is divided into three superimposed registers of bas-reliefs, golden mosaics, and four pilasters carved by Maitani between 1325 and 1330. The central rose window, Orcagna's work, is framed by 52 sculpted heads symbolizing the weeks of the year. The flanks alternate white marble and black basalt, a legacy of the Sienese tradition. Inside, the Cappella San Brizio preserves Luca Signorelli's fresco cycle of the Last Judgment (1499-1502), considered one of the Italian Renaissance's major works. Sigmund Freud mentions this chapel in The Psychopathology of Everyday Life, recounting how he forgot Signorelli's name.

Pozzo di San Patrizio - In 1527, after the Sack of Rome by Charles V's troops, Pope Clement VII took refuge in Orvieto and ordered this well's construction to guarantee water during a siege. Florentine architect Antonio da Sangallo the Younger dug between 1527 and 1537 a cylinder 13 meters in diameter and 54 meters deep into the volcanic tuff of the plateau. His technical solution is remarkable: two independent helical ramps of 248 steps each wind around the central well, illuminated by 72 large windows, allowing mules to descend and ascend with water jars without ever crossing paths. The well was never actually used for military purposes: Clement VII reconciled with Charles V before its completion. Its current name, given in the 19th century by monks from the neighboring Servite convent, was inspired by the legend of St. Patrick's Purgatory in Ireland.

Cappella Nova (San Brizio) - Accessible from the cathedral, this chapel in the right transept (built from 1408) is the jewel of the visit. Fra Angelico began its vault in 1447 before being recalled to Rome by the pope. In 1499, Luca Signorelli took over and spent two years covering the walls with scenes from the Apocalypse and the Last Judgment of unprecedented violence and anatomical precision. Michelangelo drew direct inspiration from it for the Sistine Chapel.

Orvieto Sotterranea - Beneath the upper town's streets, more than 1,200 artificial cavities carved into the tuff constitute a network whose origins go back 2,500 years. Local speleologists began exploring and documenting it in the late 1970s; about 440 cavities have since been mapped and studied. Guided tours traverse Etruscan cisterns from the 5th century BC, water collection tunnels, medieval olive presses, and waste pits that have yielded exceptional stratigraphy of daily life over two millennia. The temperature remains constant throughout the year.

Necropolis of the Crucifix of Tuff - At the foot of the cliffs supporting the city, this Etruscan necropolis (6th-3rd century BC) aligns over 200 tuff tombs arranged in an orthogonal plan, like a miniature city of the dead. Each tomb bears the name of the deceased and their clan carved into the lintel. The Etruscan city that overlooked these tombs was called Velzna; the Romans destroyed it in 264 BC and deported its inhabitants to the site of present-day Bolsena.

activities.text: Orvieto Classico DOC tasting in a tuff-carved cellar - Orvieto's vineyards have been producing wine since antiquity (one still has an Etruscan cellar). Wine cellars in the historic center offer tastings of the dry white Orvieto Classico DOC, a blend of Grechetto and Trebbiano, which Pope Paul III Farnese particularly appreciated.

Panoramic views from the ramparts of Rocca Albornoz - The medieval fortress built by papal legate Albornoz in the 14th century offers circular views of the tuff plateau, the Paglia valley, and the Tuscan hills. The circuit from Porta Rocca to the northern ramparts takes about 40 minutes.

visits.text: Rocca Albornoz - This imposing fortress was built in the 14th century by Cardinal-Legate Egidio Albornoz to establish papal dominance over central Italy. Perched at the edge of the rocky promontory dominating the town and the Nera river, it presents a quadrilateral plan flanked by four corner towers. Its thick walls and ramparts offer panoramic views of the Nera valley and the Narni hills. It served for a long time as a prison; in the 16th century, condemned prisoners whose names remain carved into the walls were detained there.

Centro storico di Narni - The medieval town, founded on the site of ancient Roman Narnia (from which C.S. Lewis took the name Narnia for his novel), is organized around Piazza dei Priori with its 13th-century Palazzo del Podestà and its loggia. Stone alleyways span vaulted passages, fountains, and small silent squares. Narni once controlled the Via Flaminia, the strategic road axis linking Rome to the Adriatic: whoever held Narni held the road to Rome.

Narni Sotterranea - In May 1979, six young speleologists from Narni climbed a wall on the edge of San Bernardo park and discovered, behind a walled-up door indicated by an old gardener, a 12th-13th century church entirely covered with frescoes, forgotten beneath the San Domenico convent. Thirty years of research in the Vatican and Trinity College Dublin archives identified, in an adjacent cell, graffiti in Masonic-alchemical symbols left by an Inquisition prisoner named Giuseppe Lombardini—persecuted by the Dominican order that precisely managed this convent. The guided tour (1h30) also traverses a Roman cistern from the Republican era and the Hall of Torments, where the Holy Office Tribunal interrogated its suspects between the 16th and 19th centuries. Only one visit is possible at a time; reservation required.

Ponte d'Augusto - At the entrance to the lower town, a single arch 30 meters in span, remnant of a 1st-century AD Roman bridge that once crossed the Nera in five arches, still stands in the vegetation. It's one of the best-preserved testimonies of Roman road engineering on the Via Flaminia.

activities.text: Swimming at the Mole di Narni (Stifone) - 5 km from Narni, the Nera river forms remarkably transparent turquoise pools among white limestone rocks. This natural site, once a Roman shipyard area according to ancient sources, is accessible by a path descending from the village of Stifone. The water, fed by karst springs, remains cool even in summer.

visits.text: Borgo di Castelluccio - This village of about 30 inhabitants perched at 1,452 meters altitude is Umbria's highest inhabited settlement. It dominates the Pian Grande, a 15 km² karst plateau formed by the drying of a lake about one million years ago. The October 2016 earthquake (magnitude 6.5) destroyed most of the village; its reconstruction spans several years but access to the plateau is restored. 360° panoramic views of the Sibillini Mountains, including the Monte Vettore summit (2,476 m), the watershed between Umbria and Marche.

Piana di Castelluccio - This high karst plateau comprises three plains: Pian Grande, Pian Piccolo, and Pian Perduto. Between late May and mid-July, the simultaneous blooming of lentils (white flowers with bluish reflections), poppies, cornflowers, wild mustard, and Venus's looking-glass covers the 20 km² of cultivated land with a mosaic of colors changing week by week. The phenomenon is due to pesticide-free agriculture practiced since antiquity: farmers have preserved production methods unchanged for 3,000 years (Castelluccio lentil seeds have been found in Neolithic tombs). Each species blooms at a slightly different period, which modifies the dominant colors of the landscape throughout the season.
